lines: This manual describes how to use the Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution tool to perform gate-level power-grid analysis on an Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC) design to determine whether the power grid will be adequate.
This manual covers the following topics:

- Product and Licensing Information
- Getting Started
- Data Preparation
- Using the Graphical User Interface
- Importing the Design
- Distributed Processing
- Extensively Parallel Processing
- Design Sanity Checks
- Power Grid Library Generation
- Static Power, IRdrop and EM Analysis
- Dynamic Power and IRDrop Analysis
- Hierarchical Power Integrity Analysis with Extreme Modeling
- ESD Analysis
- Package Analysis
- What-If Rail Analysis
- Power Gate (Switch) Analysis
- Through-Silicon Via and System-in-Package
- IR Drop Impact on Timing
- IR Drop Aware Placement
- Signal ElectroMigration
- Self-Heating Effect Analysis
- Statistical ElectroMigration Budgeting
- File Formats
This document uses the following syntactical conventions:

- Commands and parameters used on the command line are given in Courier font: 
 command_name
- Menu commands and the fields in dialog boxes in the graphical user interface are given in Helvetica italic font: 
 File – Save 
 An en dash (–) separates the menu name and the command name.
- Variables for which you are to substitute a value are given in Courier 
 italic font: 
 filename , cellname 
, 
 layer_name
- File names are given in Courier font: 
 library.lib
- Angle brackets enclose optional parameters: 
 < options >
- Vertical bars ( | ) in commands indicate choices.
- Square brackets indicate that you must select one of the choices: 
 [a|b|c]
- Square brackets followed by a plus sign mean that you can choose more than one of the choices, but you must select at least one: 
 [a|b|c]+
- Use white space (tabs or spaces) to separate a command and its arguments.

Each Cadence® Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution product is sold as part of a product package. Product packages may also include product options. The options provide advanced features and capabilities.
Each product and product option has a corresponding license. The software uses licenses to determine the features that are available when the software runs.
You can run Voltus from Innovus and Tempus but this can only be done using Voltus licenses. However, you can run the following Voltus features from Innovus without the need of a Voltus license:

- Static power analysis
- Early Rail Analysis (ERA) with static power
ERA with dynamic power requires VTS-XL.

title: About Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution Products and Product Options

lines: This release of the Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution software includes the following product package and option:

- Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution
- Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution Product Options
Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution
This package includes the products listed in Table 1-1 . To start any of these products, type the following UNIX/Linux command:
voltus

data: Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution - L

Provides static and dynamic IR Drop and Electromigration analysis. Includes vector-driven and vectorless power and power distribution calculation, what-if analysis, low power optimization, and chip-package co-design. It allows tight integration with Innovus and Tempus. 
 
 Voltus-L can only run on 1 CPU, that is, it does not support multi-CPU processing.
Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution - XL

Provides all Voltus-L functions plus Hierarchical Analysis and Hotspot Debugger functions. 
 
 Voltus-XL license is enabled for multi-CPU usage and each license can run up to 8 CPUs.
Provides all Voltus-L and Voltus-AA functions. 1 license runs up to 50K instances and can be stacked to 2 licenses for a total of 100K instances.

lines: Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution Product Options

The product options provide the extensibility and cost-effective access to additional advanced technologies, such as electrostatic discharge analysis, on-chip voltage regulator, effective resistance calculator, and multi-die rail analysis.
The Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution includes the following product options:

data: Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution Advanced Analysis GXL Option

Adds advanced features such as Statistical Leakage Power Analysis (SLPA), effective resistance calculator, on-chip voltage regulator support, clock and signal jitter analysis, qrcTechFile with features on 20nm or below process nodes, power network optimization, and Sigrity Package Analysis (SPA).
Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution - XM

The Voltus Extreme Modeling or Voltus-XM license enables modeling of large digital blocks (xPGV model generation), and use these models in the top-level full-chip analysis. It is an option to baseline license Voltus-XL. The licensing use model for Voltus-XM is: 
 
 One Voltus-XM license is required for generating one xPGV model, irrespective of the number of times the specific xPGV model is instantiated (xPGV with multiple instances). That is, if a CPU core's xPGV is instantiated four times, the Extreme Modeling flow would still require only 1 XM license. 
 The number of Voltus-XM licenses are limited to a maximum of 10 for a design with multiple blocks. For example, if a top-level run has to generate 12 xPGV models, you do not require 12 XM licenses. That is, 10 XM licenses will be able to run a design instantiating 12 xPGV models.
Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution - ESD

The Voltus Electrostatic Discharge Analysis or Voltus-ESD license enables ESD analysis resistance checks and current density analysis. It is an option to baseline licenses, Voltus-L or Voltus-XL or VDS-P.
Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution - MP (Massive Parallel Option to Voltus XL)

Adds 8 more CPUs per additional VTS-MP license. Voltus-MP is a license accelerator that only works with the base license of Voltus-XL. Voltus-L does not have multi-CPU capability and hence Voltus-MP does not work with it.
Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution - Sigrity Package Analysis

Enables Sigrity Package Analysis for Voltus. It provides integrated “Voltus + Sigrity” solutions for IC-centric Chip-PKG co-analysis. It is a product suite that contains both Sigrity XtractIM and Sigrity PowerDC but functions per “1-Product-1-License”. 
 
 
Note 
: The license requirement is for the SPA flow is Voltus-L/XL + Voltus-AA + Voltus-SPA.
Tempus Timing Signoff Solution - Power Integrity
The Tempus Power Integrity license enables identification of IR-sensitive critical paths. It is an option to the baseline licenses, Tempus-XL or Voltus-XL.

title: About Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution Licenses

lines: When you run a command to invoke a product or product option, a license is checked out. Each product and product option has a unique license string (also called a license key).

To fix the above error, restart the license server using the latest 
cdslmd
 and 
lmgrd
 versions included in the release tree.
Licensing Terminology
The following terminology is useful in understanding licenses.
Base license

The license that is checked out when the software starts. Only a full-fledged product license can be used as a base license. You cannot use a product option license as a base license to start the software. For example, Voltus has VTS-L and VTS-XL as base licenses.
Dynamic license

A license for a product option that is not checked out until a feature provided by the product option is needed. You can check out more than one dynamic license per base license. For more information on dynamic licenses, see the following section "Checking Out Licenses for Product Options".
Multi-CPU license

A license that enables additional CPUs for multithreading or distributed processing. Multi-CPU licenses can be checked out after the base license is checked out. You can check out more than one multi-CPU license per base license. For base license of VTS-XL, you can also stack it up to access more CPUs.
Checking Out Licenses for Product Options

The following command specifies the product options to be checked out when you invoke the software. The product options specified with the -lic_startup_options parameter are checked out immediately and the product options specified with the -lic_options parameter are checked out dynamically. 
 voltus -lic_startup_options "option1 option2 …" -lic_options "option1 option2 …"
If you do not want any product options to be checked out dynamically, use empty quotes with the -lic_options parameter, as follows:
voltus -lic_startup_options "option1 option2 …" -lic_options ""

The following command can be used to check out product options after you have invoked the software. The product option specified with the -checkout parameter is checked out immediately and the product options specified with the -optionList parameter are checked out dynamically.
set_license_check -checkout option -optionList "option1 option2 …"

With the checkout parameter, you can specify only one product option. If you do not want any product options to be checked out dynamically, use empty quotes with the - optionList parameter, as follows:
set_license_check -checkout option -optionList ""
You cannot check out a license for a product option if you have not checked out a base license.

You can check out more than one multi-CPU license per base license. For base license of VTS-XL, you can also stack it up to access more CPUs.

 voltus -lic_startup_options "option1 option2 …" -lic_options "option1 option2 …"
If you do not want any product options to be checked out dynamically, use empty quotes with the -lic_options parameter, as follows:
voltus -lic_startup_options "option1 option2 …" -lic_options ""

The following command can be used to check out product options after you have invoked the software. The product option specified with the -checkout parameter is checked out immediately and the product options specified with the -optionList parameter are checked out dynamically.
set_license_check -checkout option -optionList "option1 option2 …"

With the checkout parameter, you can specify only one product option. If you do not want any product options to be checked out dynamically, use empty quotes with the - optionList parameter, as follows:
set_license_check -checkout option -optionList ""
You cannot check out a license for a product option if you have not checked out a base license.

Product and Installation Information
For product, release, and installation information, see the README file at any of the following locations:

- downloads.cadence.com, where you can review the 
README
 before you download the software
- In the software installation, where it is also available when you are using or running the software
For information about Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution licenses, see "About Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution Licenses" in the Product and Licensing Information chapter.
Setting the Run-Time Environment
If install_dir is the location of your Voltus installation, you should set up your run-time environment like this:

- Add the install_dir/bin directory to your path. The bin directory has links to all the public executables in the install hierarchy.
- If you want the legacy Voltus man pages to be available from the Unix man command, you can add install_dir/share/voltus/man to your MANPATH envar.
- If you want the Common UI Voltus man pages to be available from the Unix man command, you can add install_dir/share/voltus/stylus/man to your MANPATH envar.
- If you want the Tcl man pages to be available from the Unix man command, you can add install_dir/share/tcltools/man to your MANPATH envar.
For example, you might add this to your startup shell script:
Supported and Compatible Platforms
The README file lists the supported and compatible platforms for this release.
Temporary File Locations
Each Voltus session creates its own temporary directory to store temporary files at the beginning of the run.
By default, tmp_dir is created in /tmp . If the Unix envar TMPDIR is set, then the tmp_dir directory is created inside TMPDIR .
The name of the tmp_dir directory will look like:
voltus_temp_[pid]_[hostname]_[user]_xxxxxx
Where _xxxxxx is a string added to make the directory unique. For example:
voltus_temp_10233_farm254_bob_nfp9ez
The temporary directory is automatically removed on exit or if the run terminated with a catchable signal (e.g. SIGSEGV).
Launching the Voltus Console

When you start Voltus in the non-GUI environment using the -no_gui parameter, the UNIX window (shell tool, xterm, and so on) where you start the Voltus session is called the Voltus console. This is where you enter all Voltus text commands and where the software displays messages. When a session is active, this console displays the following prompt:
voltus>
When you start Voltus in the GUI environment, the Voltus console is displayed within the main Voltus window.
If you use the console for other actions--for example, to use the vi editor--the session suspends until you finish the action.
If you suspend the session by typing Control-z , the voltus> prompt is no longer displayed. To return to the Voltus session, type fg , which brings the session to the foreground.
Tab Completing Command Names, Parameter Names, and Enum Values
You can use the Tab key within the software console to complete text command names.

After you type a partial text command name and press the Tab key, the software displays the exact command name that completes or matches the text you typed (if the string is unique to one text command) or all the commands that match the text you typed. For example, if you type set_power_ and press the Tab key, the software displays the following commands:
If you type set_power_p<Tab> , the software completes the command name as follows:
This function supports all Tcl commands, and the man first argument also supports it (for example, man set_power_p<Tab> ).
Tab Completing Parameter Names
The Tab completion capability is also available for parameter names starting with " - " for Voltus commands or for user commands registered with define_proc .
For example,
If you type set_pg_library_mode -enable_m<Tab> , the software completes the parameter name as follows:
To view all parameters of a command, type the command name followed by - and press Tab .
For example:
Tab Completing Enum Type Values for Parameters
Tab completion can be used to view or complete enum values.
For example, if you type set_pg_library_mode -cell_type followed by a space and press the Tab key, the software displays the following:
If you type set_pg_library_mode -cell_type t<Tab> , the software completes the value as follows:

If you type a < Tab > in any other context, the Tcl shell will look for Unix file and directory names that match the string.

Command-Line Editing

The Voltus software provides a GNU Emacs-like editing interface. You can edit a line before it is sent to the calling program by typing control characters. A control character, shown below as a caret followed by a letter, is typed by holding down the 
Control
 key when typing the character.

Most editing commands can be given a repeat count, 
n
, where 
n
 is a number. To enter a repeat count, press the 
Esc
 key, the number, and then the command to execute. For example, 
Esc 4 ^f
 moves forward four characters. If a command can be given a repeat count, the text 
[
 
n
 
]
 is shown at the end of its description.

You can type an editing command anywhere on the line, not just at the beginning. You can press 
Return
 anywhere on the line, not just at the end.

Note: 
Editing commands are case sensitive: 
Esc F
 is not the same as 
Esc f
.
Control (^) Characters

data: Move to the beginning of the line
Kill to end of line (or column [n])
Search backward (forward if [n]) through history for text; must start line if text begins with an up arrow
Insert next character, even if it is an edit command
Move forward to next character c

lines: Setting Preferences

You set preferences at the beginning of a new design import. You can assign special characters for the design import parser for Verilog®, DEF, and PDEF files, and control the display of the Physical view window. You can also change the hierarchical delimiter character in the netlist before importing the design, and change the DEF hierarchical default character and the PDEF bus default delimiter before loading the file.
For information on setting design preferences, refer to the " Set Preferences " section in the View Menu chapter in Voltus Menu Reference .
The Voltus Power Integrity Solution software uses the following initialization files for setting preferences:

data: Used for setting Tcl parameters or adding user-defined Tcl commands. If different versions of this file exist in the installation, home, or working directories, the file in the working directory takes precedence.

Contains design preferences set using the Design and Console tabs in the Preferences form in the GUI. 
 
 
Note: 
By default, Voltus saves changes that you make to your preferences to the 
ssv.pref.tcl
 file in the working directory.
Contains design preferences set using the Console tab in the Preferences form in the GUI.

lines: The initialization files are read in the following sequence:

1. .ssvrc
 in the home directory
2. .ssvrc
 in the working directory
3. ssv.pref.tcl 
in the working directory
4. .ssv
 in the home directory

Note: 
If initialization files contain conflicting information, the last file read takes precedence.
Starting the Software

To start a Voltus session, type the following command with the appropriate parameters on the UNIX/Linux command line. If you type a command without parameters, the software starts in GUI mode and creates a log file and a command file. The system attempts to check out the license with the most functionality, then the license with the next most functionality, and so on.
voltus Starts one of the following products:

- Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution L
- Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution XL
For an overview of the products and product licensing, see Product and Licensing Information .
Accessing Documentation and Help
You can access the Voltus documentation and help system by using the following methods:

- Launching Cadence Help From the Command Prompt
- Accessing Documentation and Help From the GUI
- Using the man and help Commands on the Text Command Line
- Other Sources of Information
Launching Cadence Help From the Command Prompt

1. Change to the following directory: 
 installation_dir/tools/bin
2. Enter the following command: 
 ./cdnshelp

After launching Cadence® Help, press 
F1
 or choose 
Help
 - 
Contents
 to display the help page for Cadence Help.
For more information see the Cadence Help manual.
Accessing Documentation and Help From the GUI
The software provides the following two methods to access documentation and help from the GUI:
Select Help on the Main Menu
Click Help on the main menu and then select Documentation Library to open the Cadence Help window. The Cadence Help window provides access to all the documentation shipped with the release.

Alternatively, select 
Help
, and then click on any of the following options to open a document directly:

- Text Command Reference

Opens the Table of Contents page of the text command reference.
- User Guide

Opens the Table of Contents page of the user guide.
- What’s New

Opens the Table of Contents page of the what’s new document
Select Help on a Voltus Form

Click the Help button in the bottom right corner of a form. Clicking the Help button opens the 
 
 Voltus Menu Reference entry for the form in the Cadence Help window.
Using the man and help Commands on the Text Command Line
Using the help Command to View the Command Syntax
To see syntax information for a command, type the following command in the software console:
help command_name

For example, to see syntax information for the 
 read_lib
 command, type the following command:
help set_power_include_file
The software displays the following text:
To see the entire list of Voltus commands and their syntax, type the following command in the software console:
help
Using the man Command to View the Command Description
To see the complete set of information for a Voltus command, type the following command in the software console:
man command_name
For example, to see the complete set of information for the set_power_include_file command, type the following command:
man set_power_include_file
The software displays the following text:
Other Sources of Information

You can also get help on Cadence products by selecting 
Customer Support 
on the 
Help
 menu. The 
Customer Support
 submenu provides access to the following Cadence resources:

- Cadence Online Support 
 Opens Cadence Online Support in your browser.
- Web Collaboration 
 Opens SpaceCruiser in your browser.
For product, release, and installation information, see the README file at any of the following locations:

- downloads.cadence.com, where you can review the 
README
 before you download the software
- In the software installation, where it is also available when you are using or running the software
For information about Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution licenses, see "About Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution Licenses" in the Product and Licensing Information chapter.
Setting the Run-Time Environment
If install_dir is the location of your Voltus installation, you should set up your run-time environment like this:

- Add the install_dir/bin directory to your path. The bin directory has links to all the public executables in the install hierarchy.
- If you want the legacy Voltus man pages to be available from the Unix man command, you can add install_dir/share/voltus/man to your MANPATH envar.
- If you want the Common UI Voltus man pages to be available from the Unix man command, you can add install_dir/share/voltus/stylus/man to your MANPATH envar.
- If you want the Tcl man pages to be available from the Unix man command, you can add install_dir/share/tcltools/man to your MANPATH envar.
For example, you might add this to your startup shell script:
Supported and Compatible Platforms
The README file lists the supported and compatible platforms for this release.
Temporary File Locations
Each Voltus session creates its own temporary directory to store temporary files at the beginning of the run.
By default, tmp_dir is created in /tmp . If the Unix envar TMPDIR is set, then the tmp_dir directory is created inside TMPDIR .
The name of the tmp_dir directory will look like:
voltus_temp_[pid]_[hostname]_[user]_xxxxxx
Where _xxxxxx is a string added to make the directory unique. For example:
voltus_temp_10233_farm254_bob_nfp9ez
The temporary directory is automatically removed on exit or if the run terminated with a catchable signal (e.g. SIGSEGV).
Launching the Voltus Console

When you start Voltus in the non-GUI environment using the -no_gui parameter, the UNIX window (shell tool, xterm, and so on) where you start the Voltus session is called the Voltus console. This is where you enter all Voltus text commands and where the software displays messages. When a session is active, this console displays the following prompt:
voltus>
When you start Voltus in the GUI environment, the Voltus console is displayed within the main Voltus window.
If you use the console for other actions--for example, to use the vi editor--the session suspends until you finish the action.
If you suspend the session by typing Control-z , the voltus> prompt is no longer displayed. To return to the Voltus session, type fg , which brings the session to the foreground.
Tab Completing Command Names, Parameter Names, and Enum Values
You can use the Tab key within the software console to complete text command names.

After you type a partial text command name and press the Tab key, the software displays the exact command name that completes or matches the text you typed (if the string is unique to one text command) or all the commands that match the text you typed. For example, if you type set_power_ and press the Tab key, the software displays the following commands:
If you type set_power_p<Tab> , the software completes the command name as follows:
This function supports all Tcl commands, and the man first argument also supports it (for example, man set_power_p<Tab> ).
Tab Completing Parameter Names
The Tab completion capability is also available for parameter names starting with " - " for Voltus commands or for user commands registered with define_proc .
For example,
If you type set_pg_library_mode -enable_m<Tab> , the software completes the parameter name as follows:
To view all parameters of a command, type the command name followed by - and press Tab .
For example:
Tab Completing Enum Type Values for Parameters
Tab completion can be used to view or complete enum values.
For example, if you type set_pg_library_mode -cell_type followed by a space and press the Tab key, the software displays the following:
If you type set_pg_library_mode -cell_type t<Tab> , the software completes the value as follows:
Tab Completing Unix file and directory names
If you type a < Tab > in any other context, the Tcl shell will look for Unix file and directory names that match the string.
For example,
Command-Line Editing

The Voltus software provides a GNU Emacs-like editing interface. You can edit a line before it is sent to the calling program by typing control characters. A control character, shown below as a caret followed by a letter, is typed by holding down the 
Control
 key when typing the character.

Most editing commands can be given a repeat count, 
n
, where 
n
 is a number. To enter a repeat count, press the 
Esc
 key, the number, and then the command to execute. For example, 
Esc 4 ^f
 moves forward four characters. If a command can be given a repeat count, the text 
[
 
n
 
]
 is shown at the end of its description.

You can type an editing command anywhere on the line, not just at the beginning. You can press 
Return
 anywhere on the line, not just at the end.

Note: 
Editing commands are case sensitive: 
Esc F
 is not the same as 
Esc f
.
Control (^) Characters

data: Move to the beginning of the line
Kill to end of line (or column [n])
Search backward (forward if [n]) through history for text; must start line if text begins with an up arrow
Insert next character, even if it is an edit command
Move forward to next character c

lines: Setting Preferences

You set preferences at the beginning of a new design import. You can assign special characters for the design import parser for Verilog®, DEF, and PDEF files, and control the display of the Physical view window. You can also change the hierarchical delimiter character in the netlist before importing the design, and change the DEF hierarchical default character and the PDEF bus default delimiter before loading the file.
For information on setting design preferences, refer to the " Set Preferences " section in the View Menu chapter in Voltus Menu Reference .
The Voltus Power Integrity Solution software uses the following initialization files for setting preferences:

data: Used for setting Tcl parameters or adding user-defined Tcl commands. If different versions of this file exist in the installation, home, or working directories, the file in the working directory takes precedence.

Contains design preferences set using the Design and Console tabs in the Preferences form in the GUI. 
 
 
Note: 
By default, Voltus saves changes that you make to your preferences to the 
ssv.pref.tcl
 file in the working directory.
Contains design preferences set using the Console tab in the Preferences form in the GUI.

lines: The initialization files are read in the following sequence:

1. .ssvrc
 in the home directory
2. .ssvrc
 in the working directory
3. ssv.pref.tcl 
in the working directory
4. .ssv
 in the home directory

Note: 
If initialization files contain conflicting information, the last file read takes precedence.
Starting the Software

To start a Voltus session, type the following command with the appropriate parameters on the UNIX/Linux command line. If you type a command without parameters, the software starts in GUI mode and creates a log file and a command file. The system attempts to check out the license with the most functionality, then the license with the next most functionality, and so on.
voltus Starts one of the following products:

- Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution L
- Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution XL
For an overview of the products and product licensing, see Product and Licensing Information .

After you type a partial text command name and press the Tab key, the software displays the exact command name that completes or matches the text you typed (if the string is unique to one text command) or all the commands that match the text you typed. For example, if you type set_power_ and press the Tab key, the software displays the following commands:
If you type set_power_p<Tab> , the software completes the command name as follows:
This function supports all Tcl commands, and the man first argument also supports it (for example, man set_power_p<Tab> ).
Tab Completing Parameter Names
The Tab completion capability is also available for parameter names starting with " - " for Voltus commands or for user commands registered with define_proc .
For example,
If you type set_pg_library_mode -enable_m<Tab> , the software completes the parameter name as follows:
To view all parameters of a command, type the command name followed by - and press Tab .
For example:
Tab Completing Enum Type Values for Parameters
Tab completion can be used to view or complete enum values.
For example, if you type set_pg_library_mode -cell_type followed by a space and press the Tab key, the software displays the following:
If you type set_pg_library_mode -cell_type t<Tab> , the software completes the value as follows:
Tab Completing Unix file and directory names
If you type a < Tab > in any other context, the Tcl shell will look for Unix file and directory names that match the string.
For example,
Command-Line Editing

The Voltus software provides a GNU Emacs-like editing interface. You can edit a line before it is sent to the calling program by typing control characters. A control character, shown below as a caret followed by a letter, is typed by holding down the 
Control
 key when typing the character.

Most editing commands can be given a repeat count, 
n
, where 
n
 is a number. To enter a repeat count, press the 
Esc
 key, the number, and then the command to execute. For example, 
Esc 4 ^f
 moves forward four characters. If a command can be given a repeat count, the text 
[
 
n
 
]
 is shown at the end of its description.

You can type an editing command anywhere on the line, not just at the beginning. You can press 
Return
 anywhere on the line, not just at the end.

Note: 
Editing commands are case sensitive: 
Esc F
 is not the same as 
Esc f
.
Control (^) Characters

data: Move to the beginning of the line
Kill to end of line (or column [n])
Search backward (forward if [n]) through history for text; must start line if text begins with an up arrow
Insert next character, even if it is an edit command
Move forward to next character c

lines: Setting Preferences

You set preferences at the beginning of a new design import. You can assign special characters for the design import parser for Verilog®, DEF, and PDEF files, and control the display of the Physical view window. You can also change the hierarchical delimiter character in the netlist before importing the design, and change the DEF hierarchical default character and the PDEF bus default delimiter before loading the file.
For information on setting design preferences, refer to the " Set Preferences " section in the View Menu chapter in Voltus Menu Reference .
The Voltus Power Integrity Solution software uses the following initialization files for setting preferences:

data: Used for setting Tcl parameters or adding user-defined Tcl commands. If different versions of this file exist in the installation, home, or working directories, the file in the working directory takes precedence.

Contains design preferences set using the Design and Console tabs in the Preferences form in the GUI. 
 
 
Note: 
By default, Voltus saves changes that you make to your preferences to the 
ssv.pref.tcl
 file in the working directory.
Contains design preferences set using the Console tab in the Preferences form in the GUI.

lines: The initialization files are read in the following sequence:

1. .ssvrc
 in the home directory
2. .ssvrc
 in the working directory
3. ssv.pref.tcl 
in the working directory
4. .ssv
 in the home directory

Note: 
If initialization files contain conflicting information, the last file read takes precedence.
Starting the Software

To start a Voltus session, type the following command with the appropriate parameters on the UNIX/Linux command line. If you type a command without parameters, the software starts in GUI mode and creates a log file and a command file. The system attempts to check out the license with the most functionality, then the license with the next most functionality, and so on.
voltus Starts one of the following products:

- Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution L
- Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution XL
For an overview of the products and product licensing, see Product and Licensing Information .
Accessing Documentation and Help
You can access the Voltus documentation and help system by using the following methods:

- Launching Cadence Help From the Command Prompt
- Accessing Documentation and Help From the GUI
- Using the man and help Commands on the Text Command Line
- Other Sources of Information
Launching Cadence Help From the Command Prompt

1. Change to the following directory: 
 installation_dir/tools/bin
2. Enter the following command: 
 ./cdnshelp

After launching Cadence® Help, press 
F1
 or choose 
Help
 - 
Contents
 to display the help page for Cadence Help.
For more information see the Cadence Help manual.
Accessing Documentation and Help From the GUI
The software provides the following two methods to access documentation and help from the GUI:
Select Help on the Main Menu
Click Help on the main menu and then select Documentation Library to open the Cadence Help window. The Cadence Help window provides access to all the documentation shipped with the release.

Alternatively, select 
Help
, and then click on any of the following options to open a document directly:

- Text Command Reference

Opens the Table of Contents page of the text command reference.
- User Guide

Opens the Table of Contents page of the user guide.
- What’s New

Opens the Table of Contents page of the what’s new document
Select Help on a Voltus Form

Click the Help button in the bottom right corner of a form. Clicking the Help button opens the 
 
 Voltus Menu Reference entry for the form in the Cadence Help window.
Using the man and help Commands on the Text Command Line
Using the help Command to View the Command Syntax
To see syntax information for a command, type the following command in the software console:
help command_name

For example, to see syntax information for the 
 read_lib
 command, type the following command:
help set_power_include_file
The software displays the following text:
To see the entire list of Voltus commands and their syntax, type the following command in the software console:
help
Using the man Command to View the Command Description
To see the complete set of information for a Voltus command, type the following command in the software console:
man command_name
For example, to see the complete set of information for the set_power_include_file command, type the following command:
man set_power_include_file
The software displays the following text:

Customer Support 
on the 
Help
 menu. The 
Customer Support
 submenu provides access to the following Cadence resources:

- Cadence Online Support

 Opens SpaceCruiser in your browser.

title: Data Requirements for Power and IRDrop Analysis

lines: The following table shows the required and optional inputs for power and IRdrop analysis.

data:
Synopsys libraries (dot libs) and PVT corner for analysis. 
 
 Timing Window File (TWF) from STA for slews and vectorless dynamic analysis. If running within the Innovus platform, this file is optional as it will be generated on-the-fly. 
 
 Foundry, process node, and process name.
Technology LEF, standard cells, IOs, memories and IP LEF
Flattened DEF or Multiple DEFs for design top level and blocks
Flattened or multiple SPEFs for top level and blocks

Spice netlist for all cells in the design along with the Spice models. It is recommended that: 
 
 the Spice netlist for macros/memories and IO cells have device X/Y location file. 
 the Spice netlist is extracted.
Design components for IOs, memories, and IPs
For the GDS layer name, layer numbers for metal, vias, contact, poly and diffusion

Power and ground voltage source X Y location file or power pad cell names to use during analysis. 
 
 All Power Domains to be analyzed and their voltages.
Package RLC data to analyze package drop and impact of package inductance. Pin based R, L, and C values or spice subcircuit format is supported.
List of filler and decap cells

Full VCD file for vector-based analysis 
 
 Boundary TCF or VCD file to annotate activity at primary inputs of the design. If not available, default % activity is used at the primary inputs. 
 
 Global flop activity or partial TCF/VCD with activity on flops. 
 
 Clock gating instances and activity at the output.
Calibre, Hercules, or Assura for device recognition.

Expected static power. 
 
 Power consumption file for custom cells and IPs that do not have internal and leakage power defined in dot libs.
Current limits per unit area for each process layer. Usually available through foundry.

lines:
- Design DEF is optional if the Innovus database is available. You need to provide LEF, Verilog, SDC, and timing libraries associated with the config file in the Innovus database.

This section describes some of the basic features of the main window.
Menu Bar
The menu bar represents categories of commands. Each of the headings supports a pull-down menu of related commands. There are twelve pull-down menus:

- File
- View
- Power Rail
- Package
- ECO
- Clock
- Timing & SI
- Verify
- Tools
- Windows
- Flows
- Help 
 
The items in the ECO , Clock , and Timing & SI menu require Cadence® Tempus™ Timing Signoff Solution license. See the Tempus Timing Signoff Solution Menu Reference for details.

Click a menu bar category to display the group of represented commands. The menu names are enabled or disabled (grayed) depending on the stage of the design flow. With the drop-down menu visible, click on an enabled command to run it.

The drop-down menus support hotkey invocation for menu commands. The hotkey for each command name is shown with an underscore. For example, run the Power & Rail – Run Power Analysis command by clicking the Power & Rail menu and pressing Shift+w .

This section describes how to load an Innovus database.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- SPEF
Procedure
To load a design into Voltus, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : You can load the design database using the read_design command, as shown below:
Alternatively, you can read the design in fragments by loading libraries and netlists using commands, such as read_lib , read_verilog , and so on, as shown below:
 
 
 
 
 read_design -physical_data ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat super_filter 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 set lefs [list] 
 
 lappend lefs ../data/lef/gsclib090_tech.lef 
 
 lappend lefs ../data/lef/gsclib090_macro.lef 
 
 lappend lefs ../data/lef/pll.lef 
 
 lappend lefs ../data/lef/decap.lef 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Specify the CPF file for power domain information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Load/Commit CPF . 
 Tcl command : 
Alternatively, you can define each power/ground net’s voltage:
 
 
 
 
 read_power_domain -cpf ../design/super_filter.cpf 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Using the following options to define each power/ground net’s voltage: 
 
 set_dc_sources VDD_AO -power -voltage 0.9 -force 
 
 set_dc_sources VDD_external -power -voltage 0.9 -force 
 
 set_dc_sources VDD_ring -power -voltage 0.9 -force 
 
 set_dc_sources VDD_column -power -voltage 0.9 -force 
 
 set_dc_sources VSS -ground
3. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read SPEF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -decoupled\ ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
Importing Third Party Design

Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution allows you to load verilog, timing libraries, SDC, DEF(s), LEF(s) and SPEF(s). The design data can be loaded from the GUI using the 
Setup 
tab as shown in the following steps:

1. Select the Setup 
 
tab.
2. Fill out the fields or click Load to load a design setup file that has the appropriate Tcl commands.
3. Click Apply .
TCL Command:
The design data can be loaded using the following Tcl commands:

read_lib -lef tech.lef allcells.lef read_lib -min stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_lib -max stdcells_max.lib memories_max.lib read_verilog design.v set_top_module design read_sdc design.sdc read_def design.def.gz read_spef design.spef.gz read_power_domain -cpf design.cpf To improve the design import performance, the read_def command supports the -skip_signal parameter which skips signal nets when loading the DEF files.
read_def -skip_signal design.def
Working with OpenAcess Databases
Importing an OpenAccess Database
To load an OpenAccess database from the GUI, perform the following steps:

1. From the File menu, choose Read Design .
2. Choose the OA option.
3. Specify the following information:
  - Library - Specifies the OpenAccess database library.
  - Cell - Specifies the OpenAccess database cell.
  - View - Specifies the OpenAccess database view.
4. Choose the Read Physical Data option, and click OK .
You can use the following command to read physical information from the OpenAccess database:
read_design -cellview "designLib cell1 layout" -physical_data
You can use the following command parameter to read the LEF data directly from OpenAccess reference libraries:
read_lib -oaRef library_name
Saving an OpenAccess Database

Use the save_design -cellview {lib cell view} command to save an OpenAccess design. A Verilog based design can only be saved into OpenAccess if it uses OpenAccess reference libraries. It cannot be saved in OpenAccess if LEF files were used.
Restoring an OpenAccess Database
The OpenAccess database can be restored in Voltus by using the following Tcl command:
restore_oa_design designLib cell1 layout -physical_data
This command restores the OpenAccess design called cell1 . The library is called designLib and the view is layout .
Importing a Large Hierarchical Design
Hierarchy Flattener

Voltus includes a hierarchical netlist flattener that can read the hierarchical verilog, DEFs, and SPEFs to create a flattened Innovus database in memory. It flattens all levels of hierarchical DEFs automatically.
It is recommended that you specify the -skip_signal parameter when reading multiple DEFs to achieve better performance.

You must specify read_lib -lef to load the LEFs. This command should never include the LEF definition of the block-level partitions. It should only include the LEF definition for primitive cells like standard cells, IOs, and memories.
TCL Commands
#### Voltus Load Hierarchical Design Tcl Script

read_lib -lef tech.lef allcells.lef read_lib -min stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_lib -max stdcells_max.lib memories_max.lib read_verilog top.v block1.v block2.v set_top_module top read_sdc top.sdc read_def -skip_signal top.def block1.def block2.def subblock1.def read_spef top.spef block1.spef block2.spef
Saving a Database in Voltus and Innovus
The save_design command in Voltus ( saveDesign in Innovus) can be used to save timing graph, signal net parasitics, verilog, timing constraints, power constraints, and reference to design data.

The save_design command in Voltus ( saveDesign in Innovus) saves a power constraints file which saves all the power constraints and modes in the database into a file named <topcellname_power_constraints.tcl> . On restoring the database, this file be sourced to ensure that the same power constraint settings are used in the new session.
Sample Power Constraints File :
set_default_switching_activity -period 10.000000ns read_activity_file -format VCD -scope adder_tb_e.u1 /../adder_netlist.vcd
set_power_analysis_mode -create_binary_db false -handle_glitch false -handle_tri_state true -honor_negative_energy true -ignore_control_signals true
Optionally, you can use Innovus to create a flattened database.
TCL commands
#### Voltus Load and Save Design Tcl Script

read_lib -lef tech.lef allcells.lef read_lib -min stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_verilog dma.v set_top_module dma read_sdc top.sdc read_def -skip_signal dma.def read_spef dma.spef save_design -rc dma.enc
# In new Voltus session
read_design -physical_data dma.enc.dat top_cell_name
Running Analysis without Creating a Physical Database
Running the hierarchical flattener or restoring a physical database for very large designs (>10M components) can take a long time and significant resources.

Voltus allows you to pass the physical data of the design (DEFs) directly to the power and rail analysis engine without creating a physical database in the memory. The specify_def command allows you to pass DEF(s) directly to the power and rail analysis engines.
Once the analysis is complete, and you want to debug the result in the context of layout, a physical database can be read in the same session using the read_def command.
TCL Commands
#### Voltus Specify Hierarchical Design Tcl Script

read_lib -lef tech.lef allcells.lef read_lib stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_verilog top.v block1.v block2.v set_top_module top read_sdc top.sdc specify_def top.def block1.def block2.def read_spef top.spef block1.spef block2.spef read_power_domain -cpf design.cpf
# Perform analysis
set_power_analysis_mode -method static .. report_power set_rail_analysis_mode -method static .. analyze_rail -type domain ALL
# Read physical data
read_def -skip_signal top.def block1.def block2.def
Running Analysis without Design Loading

Voltus has the ability to run the dynamic power and rail analysis flow without loading a design, thereby, improving the the runtime and memory footprint for the whole flow. To run this flow, you must have an external TWF file as the timing engine will not be invoked from within Voltus. This flow is recommended for large designs (40M +) where front-end processing of SPEF/Verilog/timing is high.
Power Analysis
A sample script for running the dynamic vectorless power analysis flow is given below:

read_lib -min stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_lib -max stdcells_max.lib memories_max.lib read_twf ./test.twf specify_def./adder_routed.def specify_spef ./adder_routed.spef set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorless -disable_static false -power_grid_library … set_power ... set_default_switching_activity ... set_switching_activity ... read_activity_file ... set_power_output_dir ... set_power_include_file ... set_dynamic_power_simulation ... report_power
A sample script for running the dynamic vector-based power analysis flow is given below:

read_lib -min stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_lib -max stdcells_max.lib memories_max.lib read_twf ./test.twf specify_def./adder_routed.def specify_spef ./adder_routed.spef set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorbased -disable_static false -power_grid_library … set_power ... set_default_switching_activity ... set_switching_activity ... read_activity_file ... set_power_output_dir ... set_power_include_file ... set_dynamic_power_simulation ... report_power

For power analysis, it is recommended to provide either PGV or LEF for all the cells being analyzed. You can specify both LEF and PGV, but the software will only use one set of inputs. PGV has higher precedence over LEF due to the following reasons:

- Macro PGV with the dynamic/static current is required during power analysis so that the triggering event and/or current can be captured for rail analysis
- Powergate PGV contains information that allows power analysis to capture the switch net relationship as well as the powergate enable information for ramp-up analysis.

However, if there are other cells that are missing in PGV but available in LEF, you can specify both LEF and PGV along with the set_power_analysis_mode -use_lef_for_missing_cells true command parameter. This parameter specifies to use the LEF definition for cells that are missing in PGV. In case there are common cells between LEF and PGV, then PGV gets precedence.
The following commands and parameters are not supported in this flow:

- set_power and set_switching_activity will not accept a cell/instance/pin/net/portlist using the get* commands. You can use wild card support to give a list of design objects to these commands.
- The -view parameter of the report_power command.
- The -analysis_view parameter of the set_power_analysis_mode command are not supported.
- The write_tcf and dump_unannotated_nets commands are not supported in this flow.
Rail Analysis
Based on the static and dynamic current files generated in the power analysis flow, you can perform rail analysis as given below:

set_rail_analysis_mode -method dynamic/static -power_grid_library ... set_pg_nets -force ... set_rail_analysis_domain -name ...set_power_data -format ... set_power_pads -net ... set_package ... set_net_group ... set_advanced_rail_options ... set_dynamic_rail_simulation ... analyze_rail -type domain ...
The following parameter is not supported in this flow:
The -analysis_view parameter of the set_rail_analysis_ mode command is not supported.
Reading RC Parasitics for Power Calculation
There are multiple options to read RC parasitics for power calculation. These are:

- read_spef 
 This command reads RC parasitics in the memory and annotates it to the verilog and physical netlist. The SPEF must be read after DEF. Power calculation does not require coupling capacitance in the SPEF. Reading SPEF with coupling capacitance also takes a lot of memory. In such cases, use the read_spef -decoupled option. Multiple SPEFs can be read with this command.
- write_rcdb and read_rcdb 
 You can write RC parasitics database in the binary format using the write_rcdb command and later read this database directly using read_rcdb . The RC database has better runtime and memory performance.
- read_spef -rc_corner corner_namespef_file 
 When the CPF or MMMC views are loaded, SPEFs must be read after the set_analysis_view command. 
 set_analysis_view -setup view1 -hold view1
read_spef -rc_corner best design.spef
Working with Hierarchical Databases

Voltus provides a hierarchical database based GUI that allows you get both a flattened top view as well as a hierarchical view of a design. This feature allows you to view instance-level and design-level hierarchies. To create a hierarchical database (HDB), you need to specify a list of LEFs and hierarchical DEFs.
To create and load an HDB, you need to perform the following steps:

1. Start Voltus using the following command: 
 voltus –files input_file
2. Specify the read_lib -lef and init_hier_design commands to load the design (LEF files and hierarchical DEFs).

1. Specify the save_hier_design command to save a snapshot of the design data, including the binary timing data, timing constraints file, netlist, and the timing analysis state.
2. Specify the read_hier_design command to restore all logical design information from a previous hierarchical design session saved in the software.
3. Start the GUI using the start_gui command. 
 The top-level view is displayed, as shown below:
4. Click the icon on the toolbar to view the HDB menu, as shown blow: 
 
 
 The menu allows you to select all hierarchical instances at the same time and change the view level. The following snapshots shows level hierarchy for the complete design: 
 
 
 
 
 Push down to the bottom level 
 
 
 
 Pull up to the top level
5. Right-click on an instance to view the instance-level shortcut menu, as shown below: 
 
 
 The menu allows you to: 
 The following snapshots shows level hierarchy for an instance:
  - select an instance and push down level by level, or to directly push down to the bottom level
  - select an instance and pull up level by level, or to directly pull up to the top level
  - set different view levels to different instances

data: Push down to the bottom level
Pull up to the top level

lines: After loading the HDB database, you can load the rail directory and analyze the different rail plots.
TCL Commands :

The following are the Tcl commands for creating an HDB: read_lib -lef tech.lef allcells.lef init_hier_design -defs $designDir/Data/def/design1.def $designDir/Data/def/Cpu.def $designDir/Data/def/design2.def $designDir/Data/def/design3.def .. -skip_signal save_hier_design saveDB 
 The following are the Tcl commands for restoring a saved HDB and loading the state directory: read_hier_design saveDB Chip read_power_rail_results -rail_directory ./rail/pd_25C_dynamic_1 -instance_voltage_window {timing whole} -instance_voltage_method {avg worst}
Importing CPF
Use the read_power_domain command to import a Common Power Format (CPF) file.
read_power_domain file_name [-cpf file_name]
The option -cpf specifies the name of the CPF file, which contains the power domain definitions. This file uses a .cpf extension. The CPF file can be created in Innovus.
For additional information on CPF, see the CPF User Guide .
In the absence of CPF, you can define nets, power domains, cell power domain, and its operating voltage using the following commands:
set_pg_nets set_rail_analysis_domain set_cell_power_domain

A sample script for running the dynamic vector-based power analysis flow is given below:

read_lib -min stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_lib -max stdcells_max.lib memories_max.lib read_twf ./test.twf specify_def./adder_routed.def specify_spef ./adder_routed.spef set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorbased -disable_static false -power_grid_library … set_power ... set_default_switching_activity ... set_switching_activity ... read_activity_file ... set_power_output_dir ... set_power_include_file ... set_dynamic_power_simulation ... report_power

For power analysis, it is recommended to provide either PGV or LEF for all the cells being analyzed. You can specify both LEF and PGV, but the software will only use one set of inputs. PGV has higher precedence over LEF due to the following reasons:

- Macro PGV with the dynamic/static current is required during power analysis so that the triggering event and/or current can be captured for rail analysis
- Powergate PGV contains information that allows power analysis to capture the switch net relationship as well as the powergate enable information for ramp-up analysis.

However, if there are other cells that are missing in PGV but available in LEF, you can specify both LEF and PGV along with the set_power_analysis_mode -use_lef_for_missing_cells true command parameter. This parameter specifies to use the LEF definition for cells that are missing in PGV. In case there are common cells between LEF and PGV, then PGV gets precedence.
The following commands and parameters are not supported in this flow:

- set_power and set_switching_activity will not accept a cell/instance/pin/net/portlist using the get* commands. You can use wild card support to give a list of design objects to these commands.
- The -view parameter of the report_power command.
- The -analysis_view parameter of the set_power_analysis_mode command are not supported.
- The write_tcf and dump_unannotated_nets commands are not supported in this flow.
Rail Analysis
Based on the static and dynamic current files generated in the power analysis flow, you can perform rail analysis as given below:

set_rail_analysis_mode -method dynamic/static -power_grid_library ... set_pg_nets -force ... set_rail_analysis_domain -name ...set_power_data -format ... set_power_pads -net ... set_package ... set_net_group ... set_advanced_rail_options ... set_dynamic_rail_simulation ... analyze_rail -type domain ...
The following parameter is not supported in this flow:
The -analysis_view parameter of the set_rail_analysis_ mode command is not supported.
Reading RC Parasitics for Power Calculation
There are multiple options to read RC parasitics for power calculation. These are:

- read_spef 
 This command reads RC parasitics in the memory and annotates it to the verilog and physical netlist. The SPEF must be read after DEF. Power calculation does not require coupling capacitance in the SPEF. Reading SPEF with coupling capacitance also takes a lot of memory. In such cases, use the read_spef -decoupled option. Multiple SPEFs can be read with this command.
- write_rcdb and read_rcdb 
 You can write RC parasitics database in the binary format using the write_rcdb command and later read this database directly using read_rcdb . The RC database has better runtime and memory performance.
- read_spef -rc_corner corner_namespef_file 
 When the CPF or MMMC views are loaded, SPEFs must be read after the set_analysis_view command. 
 set_analysis_view -setup view1 -hold view1
read_spef -rc_corner best design.spef
Working with Hierarchical Databases

Voltus provides a hierarchical database based GUI that allows you get both a flattened top view as well as a hierarchical view of a design. This feature allows you to view instance-level and design-level hierarchies. To create a hierarchical database (HDB), you need to specify a list of LEFs and hierarchical DEFs.
To create and load an HDB, you need to perform the following steps:

1. Start Voltus using the following command: 
 voltus –files input_file
2. Specify the read_lib -lef and init_hier_design commands to load the design (LEF files and hierarchical DEFs).

1. Specify the save_hier_design command to save a snapshot of the design data, including the binary timing data, timing constraints file, netlist, and the timing analysis state.
2. Specify the read_hier_design command to restore all logical design information from a previous hierarchical design session saved in the software.
3. Start the GUI using the start_gui command. 
 The top-level view is displayed, as shown below:
4. Click the icon on the toolbar to view the HDB menu, as shown blow: 
 
 
 The menu allows you to select all hierarchical instances at the same time and change the view level. The following snapshots shows level hierarchy for the complete design: 
 
 
 
 
 Push down to the bottom level 
 
 
 
 Pull up to the top level
5. Right-click on an instance to view the instance-level shortcut menu, as shown below: 
 
 
 The menu allows you to: 
 The following snapshots shows level hierarchy for an instance:
  - select an instance and push down level by level, or to directly push down to the bottom level
  - select an instance and pull up level by level, or to directly pull up to the top level
  - set different view levels to different instances

data: Push down to the bottom level
Pull up to the top level

lines: After loading the HDB database, you can load the rail directory and analyze the different rail plots.
TCL Commands :

The following are the Tcl commands for creating an HDB: read_lib -lef tech.lef allcells.lef init_hier_design -defs $designDir/Data/def/design1.def $designDir/Data/def/Cpu.def $designDir/Data/def/design2.def $designDir/Data/def/design3.def .. -skip_signal save_hier_design saveDB 
 The following are the Tcl commands for restoring a saved HDB and loading the state directory: read_hier_design saveDB Chip read_power_rail_results -rail_directory ./rail/pd_25C_dynamic_1 -instance_voltage_window {timing whole} -instance_voltage_method {avg worst}
Importing CPF
Use the read_power_domain command to import a Common Power Format (CPF) file.
read_power_domain file_name [-cpf file_name]
The option -cpf specifies the name of the CPF file, which contains the power domain definitions. This file uses a .cpf extension. The CPF file can be created in Innovus.
For additional information on CPF, see the CPF User Guide .
In the absence of CPF, you can define nets, power domains, cell power domain, and its operating voltage using the following commands:
set_pg_nets set_rail_analysis_domain set_cell_power_domain
Importing an OpenAccess Database
To load an OpenAccess database from the GUI, perform the following steps:

1. From the File menu, choose Read Design .
2. Choose the OA option.
3. Specify the following information:
  - Library - Specifies the OpenAccess database library.
  - Cell - Specifies the OpenAccess database cell.
  - View - Specifies the OpenAccess database view.
4. Choose the Read Physical Data option, and click OK .
You can use the following command to read physical information from the OpenAccess database:
read_design -cellview "designLib cell1 layout" -physical_data
You can use the following command parameter to read the LEF data directly from OpenAccess reference libraries:
read_lib -oaRef library_name
Saving an OpenAccess Database

Use the save_design -cellview {lib cell view} command to save an OpenAccess design. A Verilog based design can only be saved into OpenAccess if it uses OpenAccess reference libraries. It cannot be saved in OpenAccess if LEF files were used.
Restoring an OpenAccess Database
The OpenAccess database can be restored in Voltus by using the following Tcl command:
restore_oa_design designLib cell1 layout -physical_data
This command restores the OpenAccess design called cell1 . The library is called designLib and the view is layout .
Importing a Large Hierarchical Design
Hierarchy Flattener

Voltus includes a hierarchical netlist flattener that can read the hierarchical verilog, DEFs, and SPEFs to create a flattened Innovus database in memory. It flattens all levels of hierarchical DEFs automatically.
It is recommended that you specify the -skip_signal parameter when reading multiple DEFs to achieve better performance.

You must specify read_lib -lef to load the LEFs. This command should never include the LEF definition of the block-level partitions. It should only include the LEF definition for primitive cells like standard cells, IOs, and memories.
TCL Commands
#### Voltus Load Hierarchical Design Tcl Script

read_lib -lef tech.lef allcells.lef read_lib -min stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_lib -max stdcells_max.lib memories_max.lib read_verilog top.v block1.v block2.v set_top_module top read_sdc top.sdc read_def -skip_signal top.def block1.def block2.def subblock1.def read_spef top.spef block1.spef block2.spef
Saving a Database in Voltus and Innovus
The save_design command in Voltus ( saveDesign in Innovus) can be used to save timing graph, signal net parasitics, verilog, timing constraints, power constraints, and reference to design data.

The save_design command in Voltus ( saveDesign in Innovus) saves a power constraints file which saves all the power constraints and modes in the database into a file named <topcellname_power_constraints.tcl> . On restoring the database, this file be sourced to ensure that the same power constraint settings are used in the new session.
Sample Power Constraints File :
set_default_switching_activity -period 10.000000ns read_activity_file -format VCD -scope adder_tb_e.u1 /../adder_netlist.vcd
set_power_analysis_mode -create_binary_db false -handle_glitch false -handle_tri_state true -honor_negative_energy true -ignore_control_signals true
Optionally, you can use Innovus to create a flattened database.
TCL commands
#### Voltus Load and Save Design Tcl Script

read_lib -lef tech.lef allcells.lef read_lib -min stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_verilog dma.v set_top_module dma read_sdc top.sdc read_def -skip_signal dma.def read_spef dma.spef save_design -rc dma.enc
# In new Voltus session
read_design -physical_data dma.enc.dat top_cell_name
Running Analysis without Creating a Physical Database
Running the hierarchical flattener or restoring a physical database for very large designs (>10M components) can take a long time and significant resources.

Voltus allows you to pass the physical data of the design (DEFs) directly to the power and rail analysis engine without creating a physical database in the memory. The specify_def command allows you to pass DEF(s) directly to the power and rail analysis engines.
Once the analysis is complete, and you want to debug the result in the context of layout, a physical database can be read in the same session using the read_def command.
TCL Commands
#### Voltus Specify Hierarchical Design Tcl Script

read_lib -lef tech.lef allcells.lef read_lib stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_verilog top.v block1.v block2.v set_top_module top read_sdc top.sdc specify_def top.def block1.def block2.def read_spef top.spef block1.spef block2.spef read_power_domain -cpf design.cpf
# Perform analysis
set_power_analysis_mode -method static .. report_power set_rail_analysis_mode -method static .. analyze_rail -type domain ALL
# Read physical data
read_def -skip_signal top.def block1.def block2.def
Running Analysis without Design Loading

Voltus has the ability to run the dynamic power and rail analysis flow without loading a design, thereby, improving the the runtime and memory footprint for the whole flow. To run this flow, you must have an external TWF file as the timing engine will not be invoked from within Voltus. This flow is recommended for large designs (40M +) where front-end processing of SPEF/Verilog/timing is high.
Power Analysis
A sample script for running the dynamic vectorless power analysis flow is given below:

read_lib -min stdcells_min.lib memories_min.lib read_lib -max stdcells_max.lib memories_max.lib read_twf ./test.twf specify_def./adder_routed.def specify_spef ./adder_routed.spef set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorless -disable_static false -power_grid_library … set_power ... set_default_switching_activity ... set_switching_activity ... read_activity_file ... set_power_output_dir ... set_power_include_file ... set_dynamic_power_simulation ... report_power

2. Specify the read_hier_design command to restore all logical design information from a previous hierarchical design session saved in the software.
3. Start the GUI using the start_gui command.

lines: After loading the HDB database, you can load the rail directory and analyze the different rail plots.
TCL Commands :

The following are the Tcl commands for creating an HDB: read_lib -lef tech.lef allcells.lef init_hier_design -defs $designDir/Data/def/design1.def $designDir/Data/def/Cpu.def $designDir/Data/def/design2.def $designDir/Data/def/design3.def .. -skip_signal save_hier_design saveDB 
 The following are the Tcl commands for restoring a saved HDB and loading the state directory: read_hier_design saveDB Chip read_power_rail_results -rail_directory ./rail/pd_25C_dynamic_1 -instance_voltage_window {timing whole} -instance_voltage_method {avg worst}
Importing CPF
Use the read_power_domain command to import a Common Power Format (CPF) file.
read_power_domain file_name [-cpf file_name]
The option -cpf specifies the name of the CPF file, which contains the power domain definitions. This file uses a .cpf extension. The CPF file can be created in Innovus.
For additional information on CPF, see the CPF User Guide .
In the absence of CPF, you can define nets, power domains, cell power domain, and its operating voltage using the following commands:
set_pg_nets set_rail_analysis_domain set_cell_power_domain

- Setting-up Distributed Processing
- Setting-up Power Analysis in Multi-CPU Mode
  - Standalone Distributed Power Analysis
  - Multi-size Heterogeneous Distributed Processing
- Setting-up Library Generation in Multi-CPU Mode
- Setting-up IR Drop Analysis in Multi-CPU Mode

Multiple CPUs can be used to improve the overall turn-around time of power and IR drop analysis. The run-time improvement may vary depending on multi-CPU configuration, design size, and type. Generally, performance improvement will start to diminish beyond 8 CPUs.
Licensing Scheme
Multi-CPU execution for standalone Voltus is licensed as follows:

- First VTS-L license enables 1 CPU 
 Parallel processing is not supported; that is, you cannot stack up two VTS-L licenses to run on 2 CPUs.
- First VTS-XL license enables 8 CPUs 
 Each additional VTS-XL license enables 8 additional CPUs, and each additional VTS-MP license enables 8 additional CPUs. VTS-MP takes precedence over VTS-XL in license acceleration if both are available.
The distributed processing is supported with two modes:

- Local Mode 
 In this mode, you can specify the number of CPUs to use on local machine. 
 set_distribute_host -local 
 set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu 4
- Distributed Mode 
 In this mode, you can specify one or more CPUs to use on network hosts for power-grid extraction. You can also specify the number of CPUs to use on local machine for power analysis and matrix solver. 
 set_distribute_host -rsh -add {pe-opt11 pe-opt12} 
 set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu 4 -remoteHost 2 -cpuPerRemoteHost 2
You must have rlogin access to remote host machines. You can specify LSF and SGE queue or custom job submission script for the multi-CPU mode.

title: Setting-up Power Analysis in Multi-CPU Mode

lines: Multi-CPU support is available in the static, dynamic vectorless, and vector-based power analysis. Both static and dynamic power analysis supports multi-CPU in the local and distributed modes.

Distributed processing allows faster runtime for hierarchical designs. In the distributed mode, you can use the set_power_analysis_mode -distributed_setup command parameter to customize the setup for running power analysis. The use model of this parameter is:
set_power_analysis_mode -distributed_setup file
The specified file has 4 columns:
INST/CELL <name> hier/block <power.inc>
where,

- INST/CELL : Keyword indicating cell or instance to be run in the distributed mode. Cell can be useful for repeated DEF blocks.
- name : Name of the hierarchical block or instance.
- hier/block : Keyword indicating hierarchy or block. hier implies that the full hierarchy will be preserved by including the DEFs from top to the block, and block implies that only the lower level DEF will be included
- power.inc : name of the power include file. This column is optional.
The unspecified instance/cell will be run with the top level as a single group. Each distributed block/instance should have corresponding TWF and SPEF to improve runtime.
Standalone Distributed Power Analysis

Voltus supports standalone distributed power analysis in both the vector-based and vectorless dynamic power analysis flows. This feature allows you to perform dynamic power analysis and vector profiling in the multi-CPU mode without running rail analysis. The following parameters can be specified to perform standalone distributed power analysis:

- the -distribute parameter of the report_power command to enable standalone distributed power analysis. 
 To run vectoring profiling in the distributed mode, you must specify the -vector_profile_mode event_based parameter of the set_power_analysis_mode command, as shown below: 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode event_based 
 report_power -distribute
- the -partition_twf parameter of the set_power_analysis_mode command to read the input TWF data (flat or hierarchical TWF files), and generate block-level TWFs. This parameter must be specified when the block/partition level TWF files are not available.

The standalone distributed power analysis flow works only on the DEF hierarchy. Before running this flow, you must set the partition (hierarchy or block) using the set_power_analysis_mode -distributed_setup <file> command parameter. Each partition is run like an independent block-level analysis on the specified distributed hosts.
TCL Script to Run Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis in the Local Mode
set_distribute_host -local set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu 4 read_lib -lef <LEF> read_lib <timing libraries> read_verilog <verilog netlist> read_sdc <timing constraints> read_def -skip_signal <DEFs>
# Use specify_def for better performance
read_spef <RC parasitic files>
# Option ( -disable_static true ) is available to turn off static power calculation during dynamic power calculation
set_power_analysis_mode -reset set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorless -disable_static true # Use 2 dominant cycles and ~50ps resolution
set_dynamic_power_simulation -period 8ns -resolution 50ps set_default_switching_activity -input_activity 0.2 -seq_activity 0.1 -period 10.0 report_power
TCL Script to Run Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis in the Distributed Mode
set_distribute_host –rsh –add “m1 m2”
##The following command specifies the number of remote hosts and number of CPUs per remote host set_multi_cpu_usage -remoteHost 8 - cpuPerRemoteHost 2 -localCpu 16
##The following command specifies that all the blocks included in the dp_setup.txt file will be run in parallel set_power_analysis_mode –method dynamic_vectorless -distributed_setup dp_setup.txt
The following is the content of the dp_setup.txt file:
INST top.block1 block INST top.block4 block CELL block2 block

In the above example, 4 power distributed jobs are created for: top.block1, top.block4, hierarchical cell block2, and any other hierarchical instances not specified in dp_setup.txt that are run with the top-level.
TCL Script to Run Standalone Distributed Power Analysis

set_distribute_host -lsf -queue lnx64 -timeout 500000 -args {-P resource:test -W 150:00 -o temp} -resource "lnx86_test" set_multi_cpu_usage -remoteHost 4 -cpuPerRemoteHost 8 -localCpu 16 ... ... set_power_analysis_mode \ -method dynamic_vectorless \ -disable_static false \ -power_grid_library "$DesignDir/../techonly.cl $DesignDir/../stdcells.cl -distributed_setup power_control.txt \ -create_binary_db true \ -partition_twf true \ set_db \ power_method dynamic_vectorless \ power_disable_static false \ power_grid_libraries "$DesignDir/../techonly.cl $DesignDir/../stdcells.cl power_distributed_setup power_control.txt \ power_write_db true \ power_partition_twf true \ 
report_power -distribute -report_prefix report -output static_power
Multi-size Heterogeneous Distributed Processing

Voltus supports multi-size heterogeneous distributed processing using the -custom_script_list parameter of the set_distribute_host command. This parameter allows you to specify multiple launch strings to specify machines with different memory size in the order of priority, and a maximum allowed job count for each string.
The use model of this parameter is: set_distribute_host -custom -custom_script_list {{{string1} count1} {{string2} count2} ... {{stringN} countN}}

If you request for N number of jobs, the command starts submitting jobs starting from string1, until the corresponding count is reached, at which point it starts submitting the next string in the list. Therefore, the command submits the first " count1 " jobs with string1 , the next " count2 " jobs with string2 , and so on. It stops when the total submitted jobs has reached the requested number (N).

When running distributed power analysis with heterogeneous machines, Voltus uses the order of jobs specified in the distributed configuration file ( set_power_analysis_mode -distributed_setup file ) to allocate machines. You need to specify the correct order for optimal allocation of machine resources.
TCL Script to Run Multi-size Heterogeneous Distributed Processing
##The following command requests for 32 machines wherein two machines require 512GB memory and 30 machines require 256GB memory: set_multi_cpu_usage -remoteHost 32
set_distribute_host -custom -custom_script_list {{{bsub -R "OSNAME==Linux && OSREL==EE50 && mem > 500000"} 2} {{bsub -R "OSNAME==Linux && OSREL==EE50 && mem > 256000"}} 30}
##The following command specifies that all the blocks included in the dp_setup.txt file will be run in parallel set_power_analysis_mode –method dynamic_vectorless -distributed_setup dp_setup.txt

The following is the content of the dp_setup.txt file: CELL block1 ./power.inc CELL block2 ./power.inc CELL graphics.block ./power.inc CELL block3 ./power.inc CELL block4 ./power.inc CELL block5 ./power.inc ...
In the above example, block1 will be scheduled on the first 512GB machine, block2 will be scheduled on the second 512GB machine, and the remaining blocks will be scheduled on the 256GB machines.

The following is the content of the dp_setup.txt file:

In the above example, 4 power distributed jobs are created for: top.block1, top.block4, hierarchical cell block2, and any other hierarchical instances not specified in dp_setup.txt that are run with the top-level.
TCL Script to Run Standalone Distributed Power Analysis

set_distribute_host -lsf -queue lnx64 -timeout 500000 -args {-P resource:test -W 150:00 -o temp} -resource "lnx86_test" set_multi_cpu_usage -remoteHost 4 -cpuPerRemoteHost 8 -localCpu 16 ... ... set_power_analysis_mode \ -method dynamic_vectorless \ -disable_static false \ -power_grid_library "$DesignDir/../techonly.cl $DesignDir/../stdcells.cl -distributed_setup power_control.txt \ -create_binary_db true \ -partition_twf true \ set_db \ power_method dynamic_vectorless \ power_disable_static false \ power_grid_libraries "$DesignDir/../techonly.cl $DesignDir/../stdcells.cl power_distributed_setup power_control.txt \ power_write_db true \ power_partition_twf true \ 
report_power -distribute -report_prefix report -output static_power
Multi-size Heterogeneous Distributed Processing
