title: Setting-up Library Generation in Multi-CPU Mode

lines: The Library Generation flow supports multi-CPU processing to improve the overall run time for large designs. You can enable this flow using the -enable_distributed_processing parameter of the set_pg_library_mode command.

The flow requires separate Tcl scripts for the macro and standard cells. For macros, you need to specify the -macros_config_file parameter of the set_pg_library_mode command to provide configuration details for each macro cell per Library Generation run. This is an additional input for macros cell type wherein you can provide macro specific Spice and GDS. The format of the file is:
CELL <cell1> GDS_FILE <gds_file1> SUBCKT_FILE <subckt_file1> TRIGGER_FILE <trigger_file_path> POWER_GATE_PARAMS <parameters>
CELL <cell2> GDS_FILE <gds_file1> SUBCKT_FILE <subckt_file1> ...

CELL is the keyword to specify a new cell that is to be characterized. This is a mandatory keyword. This keyword is followed by optional keywords, like GDS_FILE and SUBCKT_FILE , that are specific to the cell.

The use model for Library Generation in the multi-CPU mode is: set_distribute_host -local -timeout 240 set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu 4 set_pg_library_mode -enable_distributed_processing true -celltype macros|stdcells -macros_config_file config.file #applicable only for macros ..... generate_pg_library -output <directory_name>

title: Setting-up IR Drop Analysis in Multi-CPU Mode

lines: Multi-CPU support is available in power-grid extraction and matrix solver to perform IR drop analysis. You can use multiple CPUs for static and dynamic analysis when performing domain-based analysis. Dynamic analysis has better runtime as compared to static analysis.

Distributed processing in both local and distributed (rsh) modes is supported for extraction and matrix solver. By default, matrix solver uses multi-CPUs on the local machine, that is, it relies on local CPUs on the machine where the job is executed. The number of CPUs used on the local machine is controlled by the set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu command. For maximum performance, it is recommended that set_multi_cpu_usage - remoteHost is set to number of associated nets in the domain for better load balancing.

To enable distributed solver, you must specify the -enable_distributed_processing_in_solver parameter of the set_rail_analysis_ mode command. You can set up solver to use multiple network hosts using the following distributed processing commands:
set_distribute_host -rsh -add {host1 host2 host2 host2 host3 host3 host3}
set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu 4

Multiple specification of the same host using the set_distribute_host command will enable solver to use as many CPUs on that machine. You can enable distributed solver when peak memory of the analysis exceeds machine memory in the local multi-threaded mode. In the distributed mode, peak memory on the local machine will be reduced as child processes will be executed on network hosts. However, the master thread on the local machine will continue to consume majority of peak memory even in the distributed mode.

In domain based analysis, extraction jobs for associated nets are distributed across network hosts. The ideal setup for domain based analysis is to use network hosts with multi-CPU for parallel power-grid extraction and matrix solver. Using local mode for domain based analysis will increase memory requirement on the local machine as extraction for all nets will be performed in parallel, e.g. domain with a power and a ground net extracted in local machine in parallel will approximately take twice as much memory on local machine.
The net based analysis should always use local mode.
TCL Script for Domain Based IR Drop Analysis
# Parallel extraction for each net using two remote hosts and 2 CPUs on each host
# Matrix solver is run in parallel using 8 CPUs on local machine
set_distribute_host -rsh -add {pe-opt11 pe-opt12} set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu 8 -remoteHost 2 -cpuPerRemoteHost 2
read_lib -lef <LEF> read_lib <timing libraries> read_verilog <verilog netlist> read_sdc <timing constraints> read_def -skip_signal <DEFs>
# Use specify_def for better performance

set_rail_analysis_mode \ -method dynamic \ -accuracy hd \ -temperature 25 \ -report_msmv_format true \ -save_voltage_waveforms true \ -generate_movies true \ -power_grid_library { \ ../lab3_libraries/stdcells_accurate/accurate_stdcells.cl \ ../lab3_libraries/memories_accurate/TSDN65LPA1024X32M8F.cl \ ../lab3_libraries/memories_accurate/TSDN65LPA128X16M8F.cl}
# Use -reset option to reset previously set power data
set_power_data -reset set_power_data \ -format current \ -scale 1 \ {./dynamic_power_allOn/dynamic_VDD.ptiavg \ ./dynamic_power_allOn/dynamic_VSS.ptiavg }
set_pg_nets -net VDD -voltage 1.08 -threshold 0.972 -tolerance 0.3 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.108 -tolerance 0.3 set_rail_analysis_domain -name core -pwrnets VDD -gndnets VSS
# vsrc names must be unique for all nets in the domain,e.g. vdd1 vdd2,vss1 vss2
set_power_pads -net VDD -format xy -file ../DATA/dma_mac-VDD.ppl set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../DATA/dma_mac-VSS.ppl analyze_rail -type domain core

- Introduction to Voltus-XP
- Key Features and Concepts
- TCL Command Parameters for Performing Rail Analysis in the XP Mode
- Setting up and Running Dynamic Power and Rail Analysis
- Voltus-XP Output Directory Structure
- Distributed GUI

The Voltus-Extensively Parallel (XP) mode employs distributed processing technology for power-grid sign-off at advanced-node process technologies. This mode provides performance improvements of up to 5X, works on giga-scale designs , and enhances the Voltus solution’s massively parallel execution with more efficient near-linear performance scalability on thousands of CPUs and hundreds of machines.

When Voltus is enabled in the XP mode, it distributes processing over a large number of machines effectively and provides a high-performance power sign-off solution with reduced processing time and silicon-proven accuracy. The following diagram illustrates the scalable system architecture for massive distribution of work:

The XP mode supports exponential increase in the number of nodes per chip, which requires large machines to run the various flows or to load the GUI. The XP solution is intended for large designs that have demanding requirements for parallel execution.
The following key features of Voltus-XP make it scalable, easier to use, and faster than the traditional single-machine or distributed processing mode:

- Cloud-based Design Environment
- Partitioned Database and Fully Distributed Flow
- Sub-Flow Data Isolation
- Efficient Visualization of the Output Directory Structure
- Elastic Resource Usage
- Improved GUI Performance
- Enhanced Message Logging
Cloud-based Design Environment

Voltus-XP lets you utilize the cloud platform from Amazon Web Services (AWS) to support cloud-based design flows. The cloud-based design environment allows easy deployment and integration of the entire flow because it is server-less, that is, enables dynamic allocation of machine resources without requiring dedicated resources or persistent storage. The XP mode leverages scalability in cloud computing to support unlimited design size, to quickly increase or decrease machine resources based on demand, and to shrink flow run-times.
Partitioned Database and Fully Distributed Flow
The traditional single-machine or distributed processing runs are not practical for very large designs (up to 500 million instances) because of the following reasons:

- The processing runs require a large amount of memory (~Terabytes)
- The processing runs take days to complete
- GUI loading becomes extremely slow

The XP mode deploys partition-based database to enable fully scalable and massively distributed analysis in every stage of the flow. The partition-based database is stored in the compressed format, therefore taking up less disk space and providing a reduced disk latency. Voltus in the XP mode is able to perform parallel execution in chip-power calculation, grid parasitic extraction, IR drop analysis, EM analysis, and GUI loading by running them on many small machines, as shown below:
Simple Use Model

Voltus XP solution is a major technology evolution compared to the distributed processing solution, but has the same analysis algorithm, use model, and report structure. The XP mode uses the same Tcl scripts (setup and analysis commands) as the distributed processing mode, except for a few additional command parameters that are specific to the XP mode.
Sub-Flow Data Isolation

Voltus-XP allows data isolation for each stage or sub-flow of the main flow, such as extraction, electromigration, and simulation, in the output directory. Each sub-flow has its own data storage location containing reports, logs, and results. It provides a fault tolerant architecture that enables re-use of the output of a sub-flow at a later stage in the flow, helps in quickly debugging and restarting a failed step due to NFS glitches, and resumes from the failed step.
For example, a failed sub-flow step due to low disk space error can be quickly resumed by using the output of the previous sub-flow, without having to re-run all the previous sub-flow steps.
Each sub-flow step has its own log file, which records detailed events during each sub-flow run. Monitoring these log files will help you to easily determine and troubleshoot errors.
Efficient Visualization of the Output Directory Structure

Voltus XP improves usability by recording files generated at each flow step in its corresponding directory. The new output (state directory) structure enhances the way the files are stored and presented when analyzing reports and GIFs for each step. For example, all the rail-related reports are saved in the /Reports/rail folder and all the electromigration-related reports are saved in the /Reports/em folder within the state directory.
Elastic Resource Usage

The XP mode supports elastic resource usage or elastic scaling by automatically matching your resource requirements to the available resources, and allowing to start rail/EM analysis with partial resources.
Improved GUI Performance

Voltus-XP GUI has improved by enabling GUI launch in the distributed processing mode and generating GUI preparation data (image cache). This has led to smaller memory footprint, faster start-up time, and enhanced rendering of the analysis plots.
Enhanced Message Logging

The message logging method has been advanced to effectively troubleshoot issues at a sub-flow level. The messages logged by Voltus-XP are written to the console and the log files, as depicted in the following diagram:
The following log files are generated during the XP run:

- voltus.log - a consolidated log file that contains the complete log details for the entire run. This file is generated in the current working directory.
- voltus_xp.log - a log file that is specific to the XP mode. This file helps to track all messages related to each XP sub-flow step. The voltus_xp.log file is located in the / state_directory /logs sub-directory. 
 Each sub-flow step also has its own log file, which records detailed events during each sub-flow run. The sub-flow level log files are located in the corresponding folder (for example, design_report and simulation ) within the logs sub-directory.
- report_generation.log - a log file that includes the circuit profile (nodes, resistors, current sources, and voltage sources) and rail analysis plot reports for all nets. This file is generated in the / state_directory /reports/rail/domain sub-directory.

Voltus-XP lets you utilize the cloud platform from Amazon Web Services (AWS) to support cloud-based design flows. The cloud-based design environment allows easy deployment and integration of the entire flow because it is server-less, that is, enables dynamic allocation of machine resources without requiring dedicated resources or persistent storage. The XP mode leverages scalability in cloud computing to support unlimited design size, to quickly increase or decrease machine resources based on demand, and to shrink flow run-times.
Partitioned Database and Fully Distributed Flow
The traditional single-machine or distributed processing runs are not practical for very large designs (up to 500 million instances) because of the following reasons:

- The processing runs require a large amount of memory (~Terabytes)
- The processing runs take days to complete
- GUI loading becomes extremely slow

The XP mode deploys partition-based database to enable fully scalable and massively distributed analysis in every stage of the flow. The partition-based database is stored in the compressed format, therefore taking up less disk space and providing a reduced disk latency. Voltus in the XP mode is able to perform parallel execution in chip-power calculation, grid parasitic extraction, IR drop analysis, EM analysis, and GUI loading by running them on many small machines, as shown below:
Simple Use Model

Voltus XP solution is a major technology evolution compared to the distributed processing solution, but has the same analysis algorithm, use model, and report structure. The XP mode uses the same Tcl scripts (setup and analysis commands) as the distributed processing mode, except for a few additional command parameters that are specific to the XP mode.
Sub-Flow Data Isolation

Voltus-XP allows data isolation for each stage or sub-flow of the main flow, such as extraction, electromigration, and simulation, in the output directory. Each sub-flow has its own data storage location containing reports, logs, and results. It provides a fault tolerant architecture that enables re-use of the output of a sub-flow at a later stage in the flow, helps in quickly debugging and restarting a failed step due to NFS glitches, and resumes from the failed step.
For example, a failed sub-flow step due to low disk space error can be quickly resumed by using the output of the previous sub-flow, without having to re-run all the previous sub-flow steps.
Each sub-flow step has its own log file, which records detailed events during each sub-flow run. Monitoring these log files will help you to easily determine and troubleshoot errors.
Efficient Visualization of the Output Directory Structure

Voltus XP improves usability by recording files generated at each flow step in its corresponding directory. The new output (state directory) structure enhances the way the files are stored and presented when analyzing reports and GIFs for each step. For example, all the rail-related reports are saved in the /Reports/rail folder and all the electromigration-related reports are saved in the /Reports/em folder within the state directory.
Elastic Resource Usage

The XP mode supports elastic resource usage or elastic scaling by automatically matching your resource requirements to the available resources, and allowing to start rail/EM analysis with partial resources.
Improved GUI Performance

Voltus-XP GUI has improved by enabling GUI launch in the distributed processing mode and generating GUI preparation data (image cache). This has led to smaller memory footprint, faster start-up time, and enhanced rendering of the analysis plots.
Enhanced Message Logging

The message logging method has been advanced to effectively troubleshoot issues at a sub-flow level. The messages logged by Voltus-XP are written to the console and the log files, as depicted in the following diagram:
The following log files are generated during the XP run:

- voltus.log - a consolidated log file that contains the complete log details for the entire run. This file is generated in the current working directory.
- voltus_xp.log - a log file that is specific to the XP mode. This file helps to track all messages related to each XP sub-flow step. The voltus_xp.log file is located in the / state_directory /logs sub-directory. 
 Each sub-flow step also has its own log file, which records detailed events during each sub-flow run. The sub-flow level log files are located in the corresponding folder (for example, design_report and simulation ) within the logs sub-directory.
- report_generation.log - a log file that includes the circuit profile (nodes, resistors, current sources, and voltage sources) and rail analysis plot reports for all nets. This file is generated in the / state_directory /reports/rail/domain sub-directory.

title: Partitioned Database and Fully Distributed Flow

lines: The traditional single-machine or distributed processing runs are not practical for very large designs (up to 500 million instances) because of the following reasons:

- The processing runs require a large amount of memory (~Terabytes)
- The processing runs take days to complete
- GUI loading becomes extremely slow

title: TCL Command Parameters for Performing Rail Analysis in the XP Mode

lines: The following parameters of the set_rail_analysis_mode command are used for performing rail analysis in the XP mode:

- -enable_xp
- -xp_cpu_per_job_power
- -xp_cpu_per_job_simulation
- -xp_host_allocation_method
- -xp_purge
- -xp_resume
- -xp_reuse_extraction_directory
- -xp_reuse_power_directory
- -xp_simulation_cpu_timeout
- -xp_simulation_min_cpu
For detailed information on these parameters, refer to Voltus Text Command Reference .

title: Setting up and Running Dynamic Power and Rail Analysis

lines: This section describes how to set up the dynamic power and rail analysis mode and then run rail analysis in the XP mode.

- enable the XP mode ( set_rail_analysis_mode -enable_xp true )
  - specify the extraction technology file ( set_rail_analysis_mode -extraction_tech_file ) to remove dependency on the technology PGV.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- SPEF
- Power-grid libraries (.cl)
- Vectorless or vector-based files created during dynamic power calculation
- Quantus extraction technology file
Procedure
To perform dynamic power and rail analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Specify the distributed processing settings. This flow requires at least 8 CPUs. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu 8 -remoteHost 2 -cpuPerRemoteHost 8 
 
 set_distribute_host -custom -custom_script_list { {{bsub -m "sjf-dsgpe-07" -q hier -n 8 -K} 1} {{bsub -m "sjf-dsgpe-08" -q hier -n 8 -K} 1}} 
 
 
 
 By default, XP starts analysis with partial resources. For example, if you request for 16 hosts but the number of available hosts are only 8, the tool will start the run with 8 hosts.
2. Load the design data. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_twf ../design/super_filter.twf 
 
 specify_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
3. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 specify_spef ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
4. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVecLessPowerResults
5. Define the power analysis mode. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorlessPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl } \ 
 
 -enable_state_propagation true \ 
 
 -current_generation_method avg 
 
 -distributed_setup dp_setup.txt 
 
 
 
 
 
 It is recommended to specify the -distributed_setup parameter that allows you to give a user-defined file to control the number of partitions on which the power analysis run is performed.
6. Define the power of an instance or cell. For dynamic analysis, you can assign a piece-wise linear waveform to an instance. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power -instance u0 -dynamic_switch_pattern {READ 0 READ}
7. You can define default activities for the unassigned nets. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_default_switching_activity -input_activity 0.3 \ 
 
 -period 4.0 -clock_gates_output_ratio 2.0
8. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -period 20 -resolution 50 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
9. Define the rail analysis mode. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode\ 
 
 -method dynamic \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 -temperature 125 \ 
 
 -gif_resolution 0 \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } 
 
 -extraction_tech_file /design/super_filter/qrcTechFile 
 
 -report_power_in_parallel true 
 
 -enable_xp true 
 
 
 
 
You need to set the -report_power_in_parallel true parameter to run Voltus-XP power analysis in parallel with rail analysis to improve the overall turnaround time. 
 The additional options listed in the TCL Command Parameters for Performing Rail Analysis in the XP Mode section can be specified in the XP mode.
10. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. Then, use set_rail_analysis_domain to define the power domains. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_AO -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_external -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.05 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name core -pwrnets {VDD_AO VDD_external} -gndnets VSS
11. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_AO -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_AO.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_external -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_external.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp
12. Run rail analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_rail -type domain - output dynamic_rail core
The Voltus console will display rail analysis statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The rail output directory will contain the following information:

data:
For each run, the state directory is generated in the rail output directory. The directory has the default name < domainName >_< temperature >_dynamic_< X > for the dynamic analysis run, and < domainName >_< temperature >_avg_< X > for the static analysis run. 
 
 < domainName > will be replaced with ALL if all domains are to be analyzed ( analyze_rail -output dir -type domain ALL ), or with < netName > if a power net is to be analyzed. 
 
 The rail folder within < output directory >/< state_directory >/ reports contains folders for each net, such as VDD and VSS. It also contains a folder called domain for domain-based reports. For more information on the rail output directory structure, refer to Voltus-XP Output Directory Structure . 
 
 
The rail output directory ( analyze_rail -output dir ) contains a symbolic link called latest that points to the latest state directory.

lines: This section describes the rail analysis state directory structure and description of the individual high-level directories:

- gui - Stores GUI-related data files in the .json format. These files save information such as design name, layers, nets, partition number, layers mapping to each partition, nets, number of elements, number of nodes, number of instances, plots, and so on.
- logs - Stores detailed logs for various rail analysis steps, such as simulation, electromigration (em), and rail report generation (railrpt). It also includes a merged log file called voltus_xp.log that contains the complete log details of all the rail analysis steps.
- misc - Stores all the miscellaneous files generated by the various rail analysis steps. The miscellaneous files include temporary or application files generated by every step of the flow.
- pgdb - Stores all the electrical data used for analysis, such as nodes, elements, instances, tap nodes, voltage sources, tap caps, and what-if scales.
- reports - Stores all the report files generated by various steps of the design flow, such as design , em , and rail . 
 [user@machine reports]$ ls 
 design em rail 
 [user@machine reports]$ ls rail/ 
 domain VDD VSS package waveforms
  - The design folder contains design check reports, namely, snap layer information for each net, incomplete nets, power-grid view table for each net, shorts, instance floating pins, instance disconnected pins, physically disconnected instances, and missing library models.
  - The em folder contains the current density analysis report ( netname .rj.avg.rpt ) for each net.
  - The /reports/rail directory contains the following:
    - net name (for example, VDD, VDD_external, VSS) directories for net-based reports. The net name folder contains the GIFs for the rail plots, and various rail reports (layer-based IR report, power-integrity report, powergate report, RLRP report, and so on).
    - a directory called domain for domain-based reports. The domain folder contains the IVD GIFs, EIV GIFs, DIV GIFs, and IV reports. For 3DIC designs, another layer of directory with the die name will be created between reports and partition directories.
    - a directory called package for package-related information, such as the package terminal mapping file and .ptmf file.
    - a directory called waveforms for storing current waveform files (.ptiavg) for each net.
- results - Stores all the result files generated by various steps, such as simulation, em, and rail. Each folder contains directory for each partition and a top-level directory.
- voltus_data - Contains the run directory for each rail analysis step. This folder contains the binary files generated and used throughout the XP run. It includes detailed information, such as reports, logs, and results, that can be used for debugging purpose.

Voltus XP in the GUI mode is called Distributed GUI or DGUI. DGUI allows you to load very large designs with billions of instances. The DGUI operations are distributed on multiple hosts to reduce execution time, reduce memory consumption per host, and faster loading and analysis of large designs.
To start Voltus XP in the GUI mode, run the software in the distributed mode using the following commands:

- set_multi_cpu_usage
- set_distribute_host
After specifying the distributed processing commands, load the design and read the XP output rail directory.

DGUI is supported for both the flat and hierarchical database (HDB). You can load the HDB and read the rail directory using the read_power_rail_results command. This method is recommended for very large designs.
For information on generating and loading a hierarchical database, refer to the Working with Hierarchical Databases section in the “ Importing Design ” chapter of Voltus User Guide .
Example for Running DGUI for a Flat Database:
Run Voltus-XP in the non-GUI mode
... ##Run Voltus-XP in the DGUI mode
When the design is being loaded, the following “Caching images” message box appears indicating that GUI loading is in progress:

- Check Design
- Check Timing Library
- Verify Connectivity
  - Before you begin
  - Types of connectivity violations reported
- Verify Power Via
- Verify Power and Ground Shorts
  - Before you begin
  - Types of short violations reported
- Violation Browser
  - Violation Browser Features
  - Clearing Violations
- Design Browser
You can check the design at various stages of the design process and can check for missing or inconsistent library and design data.
You can check for the following data:

- Physical library
- Timing library
- Netlist
- I/Os
- Tie-high and tie-low pins
- Power and ground pins
Cadence recommends that you check libraries and data as follows:

- Perform I/O checking at any time. I/O problems might not impede any tool, but they might add to design problems.
- Perform netlist checking at any time after the design has been loaded.
- Perform physical library checking before floorplanning.
- Perform power and ground checking before routing and extraction, and verifying geometry and connectivity.
- Perform timing library checking before any timing-related operation (for example, timing-driven placement or routing, timing optimization, clock-tree synthesis, and static timing analysis).
- Perform tie-high and tie-low checking before routing and extraction.
You can check the design using the following steps:

1. Select File -> Check -> Check Design menu in GUI. 
 The following form appears:
2. Select the appropriate Check Design Options .
3. Select the appropriate Report Format .
4. Specify the output directory.
5. Select Apply or OK .
TCL Command:
The TCL command for checking the design is as follows:
check_design -out_file checkDesign -type {power_intent timing hierarchical pin_assign budget assign_statements place opt cts route signoff all}
Sample Output
The following steps are needed to view check design report.

1. Generate the report using the check_design command or GUI.
2. Select Tools -> Report .
3. Load the report using File - Open File from the check design output directory.
Alternatively, you can look at the text file if the report was saved as text.
The Check Timing Library program reports timing library cells with missing internal or leakage power data.
The following steps are for creating a timing library cell report:

1. Select File -> Check -> Timing Library . 
 The following form appears:
2. Specify the cells that you want to report on.
3. Specify Timing Library Report filename.
4. Click OK .
TCL Command:
The TCL command for checking a timing library is as follows:
check_library -outfile design.talib -checkpower -reportMissingPowerOnly

Verify the connectivity of your design to detect and report conditions such as opens, unconnected pins, dangling wires, loops, and partial routing. You can use the command to create violation markers in the design window and generate a violation report. There is no database impact from using this command unless you save the design, which saves the violation markers.

For regular wires, the software checks connectivity by using the center line of the wire segments and center of the vias. For special wires, the command checks the whole geometry. If a via or wire is slightly offset from where it should be, the software reports an error.
The software also detects connectivity loops based on the end points of a regular wire segment center line or the center of a via. It reports geometry loop violations.
Before you begin
Before you verify connectivity, the following conditions must be met:

- The design must be routed.
- The design must be loaded into the current Voltus session.
Types of connectivity violations reported

- Antennas - Unconnected wires (dangling wires). 
 To avoid antenna violations, wires and nets must meet the following conditions:
  - Regular wires 
 Must terminate on a pin or the center of a via. If a vertical wire intersects a horizontal wire along its axis, the end of the vertical wire must be covered by the horizontal wire. If the ends of a vertical wire and horizontal wire meet, they must meet at their end points.
  - Regular net vias 
 Must be covered by a pin. The center of the via must be the start or end point of a wire. The center point of stacked vias must be coincident.
  - Special wires 
 A point that is one-quarter of a wire width from the center of the ending edge of the special wire must be covered by a via, pin, or another wire on the same layer.
  - Special net vias 
 The metal rectangle of the special net via must overlap with a special wire or via of the same net.
- Opens 
 Parts of nets, such as wires or pins, that are connected to each other but are missing a connection to the net as a whole. Marks each part of a net that is missing a connection as an open and displays a violation marker between the parts.
Violation markers for opens are displayed as polygons that include all wires, pins, and via that connect to an island.
- Loops
- Unconnected pins 
 Pins that are not connected to any other objects
The following steps are needed to check verification connectivity:

1. Select Verify -> Connectivity in the GUI. 
 The following form appears:
2. Specify the type of nets
3. Specify which nets
4. Specify the types of connectivity checks you want to perform
5. Specify the report output file name
6. Specify the maximum number of errors and warnings you want to report.
7. Click Set Multiple CPU to specify multi-threading settings for verifying connectivity.
8. After verifying connectivity, you can use the Violation Browser for interactive viewing and highlighting of violation markers.
TCL Command:
The TCL command for doing verification of connectivity is as follows:
verify_connectivity -type special -error 1000 -warning 50 -report dma_mac.conn.rpt

Verify Power Via program does the following on the power vias:

- Checks for metal geometry overlap on PG nets
- Ignores metal fill geometries by default
- Supports stacked power via check
- Generates text report with location of missing via on PG net
- Highlights violations in the layout canvas
- Checks missing vias in a user-specified region
- Checks missing vias on non-orthogonal overlap
- Checks missing vias on wire-pin overlap
- Checks via utilization in metal overlap
Violations found by verify_power_via are highlighted in the layout canvas with cross-marks. They can be queried by selecting and pressing "Q" on the keyboard.
The Verify Power Via form can be executed in the GUI from the Verify -> Power Via menu.
For more information on the fields and descriptions, see Voltus Menu Reference .
TCL Command:
The TCL command examples are as follows:

- verify_power_via -report power_via.report 
 Checks missing vias on all power nets and all metal overlaps.
- verify_power_via -layer_range "M5 M8" -nets VDD 
 The -layer_range "M5 M8" option checks for the missing stacked vias between layer M5 and M8. Use the toolbar button called Clear DRC Violations in Layout tab to clear the violation markers.
- verify_power_via -via_util 0.7 -report via_utilization.rpt 
 Checks whether via utilization is 70% on metal overlap.

You can verify power and ground shorts in your design and report shorts between two geometries belonging to different nets. Support for power-ground short detection lets you perform power and ground short checks between the following:

- PG and PG nets
- PG and signal nets
- PG and other special nets

Use the verify_PG_short command to create violation markers in the design window and generate a violation report. Then, use the Violation Browser for interactive viewing and highlighting of violation markers.
Before you begin
Before you verify shorts, you must restore the physical design using the following command:
read_design -physical_data directory topcell or read_def filename
Types of short violations reported
The following figures illustrate short violations and their description:
Pin of Cell tt1/sram1 & Blockage
Special Wire of Net GND & Blockage
TCL Command:
The following TCL command checks short violations on the power net VDD:
verify_PG_short -net { VDD}

The Verify Power Ground Shorts feature supports multi-threading. To enable multi-threading, you must specify the set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu command before specifying verify _PG_short, as shown below:
set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu 4 verify_PG_short

Use Tools > Violation Browser form to view and highlight violation and lithography hotspot markers interactively.

The Violation Browser overwrites violation markers from the verify _connectivity, verify _power_via and verify _AC_limit commands if they are run more than once during a Voltus session. These commands are net-based, not area-based, so the browser does not make incremental updates for connectivity, process antennas, or AC limit. As a result of this behavior, the software does not keep the information from the first verification run.
For more information on the fields and descriptions, see the Voltus Menu Reference .
Violation Browser Features

- Click a violation on the violation list on the form to see a description of the violation. The description includes actual and target values for AC limit violations, process antenna violations, and geometry spacing violations.
  - An actual value is the current value
  - A target value is the value defined in the LEF file.
- Click the + or - sign to collapse or expand the listings of each violation type.
- Use the First , Previous , Next , Last , Up , and Down buttons to navigate through the list of violations.
- The browser displays the violations in the following hierarchical order: 
 + tool 
 + type 
 + subtype 
 Description 
 where the tool, type, and subtype value correspond to the value you specify using the createMarker command.
- Use cross probing between the design display area and the Violation Browser .
To display the details of a violation in the Violation Browser form, double-click the violation marker in the design display area.

- If there are violation markers for overlapped objects, select the top-most marker in the design display area and press the space bar on your keyboard to navigate through the other markers. The type and name of the selected violation is displayed in the lower-left corner of the window. Use the q keyboard shortcut key to select a violation and highlight it in the Violation Browser form.
- Use the Zoom buttons to change the magnification level of a violation.
- Use any of the following buttons to change the display for a selected violation:
  - Highlight Color
  - Highlight Violations
  - De-Highlight Violations
  - Delete Violations
  - Mark Violations as False
  - Mark Violations as True
- Generate a report file by clicking the Save button.
The report file includes information on the violations shown in the violation browser.

- Limit the number of violations to display by using the Show Types panel in the Settings page of the form.
- Limit the area to display by using the Show Area panel in the Settings page of the form.
- Filter the violations to display by using the Other Filters panel in the Settings page of the form.
Clearing Violations

- Overview
- Early Rail Analysis Key Features
- Setting up and Running Early Rail Analysis
- Viewing Early Rail Analysis Results
- TCL Command Examples

The Early Rail Analysis (ERA) feature provides rail analysis at the early stage of design with the same use model as Signoff Rail Analysis. Static ERA can also be run with the Innovus base license. ERA is not intended for sign-off; if you want to include custom power-grid views or do dynamic analysis, then a Voltus license is required. ERA is intended for the early stage analysis and can be run on a power-grid at the floorplan stage, partially placed, and fully placed and routed designs.

ERA has the ability to analyze power-grid integrity early in the floorplanning stage, after placement, as well as postrouting. It helps fix power-grid problems early in the flow, rather than waiting for when the layout is mostly done and the problems are much more difficult to correct. ERA will take whatever blocks, macros, standard cells, and routing that is available to help improve the accuracy of early rail analysis. The ERA flow allows you to specify various power constraints interactively during the power planning stage and quickly perform rail analysis to estimate whether the design meets these constraints. ERA does not need fully routed power-grid network. It has the ability to create virtual power connections to the placed instances based on logical connectivity. This allows you to refine the placement for the macros to achieve better power distribution and achieve the IR drop goals for the design.
The following diagram illustrates the ERA flow:
The following steps describe the ERA flow:

1. Grid Completion : The ERA engine checks if the follow pin routing has been done or not in the design; if not, it creates virtual follow pins and drops the required virtual vias. Missing virtual vias between stripes are also dropped. Refer to Additional Examples for Tcl command script.
2. Power Estimation and User-Defined Distribution : During ERA, you have the flexibility to specify power for the various modules. The three common methods to specify power for a design are: 
 The region-based power specification can be used in conjunction with the other methods. Refer to Example 1: Region-based Power Specification for Tcl command script. 
 Total Power and Text Power File - This method is used for a partially placed design. You can specify the total power consumption for the design, and optionally you can specify the estimated (or known) power consumption for the cell or macros: In addition, you can always specify the distribution of the total power using region based power specification. This method can be implemented using the following two options: 
 Option 1: Specify user-defined current region and user-defined cell power file. Refer to Example 2: User specified Current Regions and Instance Power File for Total Power for Tcl command script. 
 Option 2: Use tool-generated current region and tool-generated power file for placed instances. Refer to Example 3: Auto generated Current Regions and Instance Power File from Total Power for Tcl command script. 
 The following example illustrates the total power consumption for a design: 
 
 
 Here, power consumption for the known placed instances can be specified for better accuracy. In the unplaced cell region, current region is specified by interactively drawing regions in the GUI and specifying current on the desired layer. 
 Power Engine Calculated Power and Instance-based Power - If a design is fully placed, you can calculate power by running the report_power command, or using GUI ( Power & Rail -> Run Power Analysis menu). The power is computed using the synthesized verilog netlist so it will be computed for all the instances in the design. Refer to Example 4: Power Engine Calculated Power for Tcl command script.
  - Region Based Power Specification - This method is used for a fully unplaced design. You can specify power based on the region and layer interactively in the GUI or command file. The specified power is distributed evenly on the specified layer in the given region, as shown below:
3. Static IRdrop and EM Analysis : Run static IRdrop and electromigration analysis.
4. Dynamic Analysis : Run dynamic IRdrop analysis. For this, you need to specify explicit dynamic current region file or the dynamic instance current file.
ERA uses Voltus extractor and rail analysis engines. They can be used during power-grid prototyping to analyze power, IRdrop and power-grid integrity.

- Interactive current specification use-model to enable static and dynamic rail analysis at the floor-planning stage.
- Power and rail analysis during the placement stage using ERA driven virtual follow pin routing and virtual via for grid completion.
- Automatic current region generation accounting for unplaced instance in the design, and automatic area based distribution of power among placed instances in the design.
- PGV library is optional. If not provided, it is generated on the fly using the specified technology file.
- Static Power and Rail Analysis without PGV can also be run using the Innovus license.
- Power and rail analysis on a placed and routed database.
- Early power-switch analysis to refine power-switch placement.
- Support for multi-CPU in static/dynamic power and rail analysis, and static and dynamic power and rail analysis. For information on how to set up multi-CPU analysis, see the " Distributed Processing " chapter in Voltus User Guide .
- Support for the unplaced flow during static and dynamic analysis.
- What-if shape analysis to guide power-grid optimization.
- Support for native power-up analysis.

title: Prior to Running Early Rail Analysis

lines:
- The design must have power stripes for a net to be analyzed. If vias are not dropped among stripes, the ERA grid completion engine can create virtual connections.
- Instances must be logically connected to the PG net under consideration. Instances need not be placed.
- The design must be loaded into memory for virtual follow pin routing and virtual via completion.
- If the design has no instances, a single current region can be automatically created with all current attached to it. However, when assigning the single current region to a layer, the layer should have atleast a single tap/sink current for ERA to run the correct IR analysis.

title: Setting up and Running Early Rail Analysis

lines: This section describes how to set up and run the early rail analysis mode and then run rail analysis.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- SPEF File
- Power-grid libraries (.cl). See Power-Grid Library Generation for details on generating power-grid libraries.
- Extraction Technology File
Procedure
To perform early rail analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Specify the CPF file for power domain information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Load/Commit CPF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_domain -cpf ../design/super_filter.cpf
3. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read SPEF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -decoupled\ ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
4. Define the early rail analysis mode through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power & Rail - Set Rail Analysis Mode . For more information, refer to Setting Up the GUI to Run ERA . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method era_static \ 
 
 -accuracy xd \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -extraction_tech_file ../data/qrc/gpdk090_9l.tch \ 
 
 -temperature 125 \ 
 
 -era_current_region_file ../tcl/VSS_1.curRegion 
 
 
 Note: 
 
 For information on the ERA parameters, refer to the set_rail_analyis_mode command in the Voltus Text Command Reference .
  - The xd accuracy mode is used for the early implementation stage IR analysis.
  - You have the option to use the extraction (Quantus) technology file without power-grid library, or use the power-grid library without the extraction technology file.
  - The current region file specifies a list of regions and the amount of current (mA) to be distributed within a power-grid region. This parameter is to be used when a design is either fully unplaced or partially placed. Following is the format of the current region file: 
 #Format: 
 LABEL name NET netName AREA x1 y1 x2 y2 LAYER layername <CURRENT value | PWL (t1 i1 t2 i2 …)> INTRINSIC_CAP <value> LOADING_CAP <value> [ADD_OVERLAP | SUBTRACT_OVERLAP] 
 Static Example : 
 label test1 net VDD area 100 200 400 500 layer M1 current 10 
 Dynamic Example : 
 label test1 net VDD area 100 200 400 500 layer M1 pwl (0ns 0mA 1ns 0mA 1.9ns 0mA 2ns 10mA) intrinsic_cap 10 loading_cap 60
  - You can specify the following parameters for virtual follow pin insertion:
    - -era_insert_virtual_followpins
    - -era_insert_virtual_followpin_for_io
  - You can specify the following parameters for virtual via insertion:
    - -era_skip_virtual_via_by_type
    - -era_insert_virtual_via_on_layers
    - -era_skip_virtual_via_on_layers
5. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.05 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD -voltage 1.8 -threshold 1.62 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD1 -pwrnets VDD -gndnets VSS 
 
 
 
 The set_rail_analysis_domain command is required for domain-based analysis. It is not required for the net-based analysis.
6. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy \ -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp
7. Run early analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power & Rail - Run Rail Analysis . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_rail -output ./era_run -type net VSS
The Voltus console will display rail analysis statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The early rail output directory will contain the following information:

data:
For each run, an output directory is generated with the default name < domainName >_< temperature >_avg_< X >. < domainName > will be replaced with ALL if all domains are to be analyzed ( analyze _rail -output dir -type domain ALL ), or with < netName > if a power net is to be analyzed. The output directory contains state directories for each net, such as VDD and VSS. 
 
 Each state directory contains the following: 
 
 results – a file that gives a summary of the rail analysis results for various plots, such as tc (tap current), rc (resistor current), and so on. 
 Reports – a folder that contains a list of useful report files that contain all power-grid integrity data. 
 Auto-generated GIF files for the rail analysis plots

lines: Setting Up the GUI to Run ERA
To set up early rail analysis using GUI, perform the following steps.

1. Select Power & Rail - Set Rail Analysis Mode menu. 
 The Set Rail Analysis Mode form appears: 
 The content of the two tabs of this form change depending on the selection of the analysis method.
2. Set Analysis Stage as Early .
3. Set Analysis Method as Static or Dynamic . 
 By default, XD is selected as the Accuracy mode.
4. Specify Power-Grid Libraries or Extraction Tech File . See Power-Grid Library Generation for details on generating power-grid libraries.
5. Click the Advanced tab of the Set Rail Analysis Mode form.
6. Select the Specify Current Region check box to specify a file that includes a list of regions and the amount of current to be distributed within them for the power-grid. When you select this check box, the Create button gets enabled that lets you create current regions for Static and Dynamic analysis.
7. Click the Create button. 
 The Create Current Region form appears.
8. Specify the current region coordinates in the Create Current Region form. 
 The steps for obtaining the coordinates for domain-based current regions are: 
 The steps for obtaining the coordinates for net-based current regions are: 
 Select a net from the Net drop-down list. 
 Specify a label for the net in the Label field, and click Draw . 
 In the design layout, select an area. 
 This form will automatically display the coordinates of the selected area in the x1 , y1 , x2 , y2 fields. The form appears as:
  1. Select the Power Domain check box.
  2. Select a power domain from the drop-down list.
  3. Click Get Coordinates to pick the domain coordinates automatically. The form appears as:
9. Select a layer from the Layer drop-down list.
10. Enter the current for the region in the Static Current (mA) field.
11. Click the Add button to add the current region to Current Region List .
12. Click the Save button to save it to the current region file. For example, VSS_1.curRegion.
13. Repeat the steps 8-13 to create more current regions.
14. Click OK to close the Create Current Region form.
15. Click OK to save and apply the early rail analysis setup information.
The following fields in the Advanced tab are enabled only if you select Early analysis stage in the Set Rail Analysis Mode - Basic tab :

The following fields in the Advanced tab are enabled only if you select Early analysis stage in the Set Rail Analysis Mode - Basic tab :
To view the early rail analysis results:

1. Open the Power & Rail Setup form, accessed by clicking the DB Setup button on the Power & Rail Plots form.
2. Load the early rail database by clicking the Browse button next to the Rail Database field.
3. Click OK .
4. Plot the results using the rail analysis plot type ir ( set_power_rail_display –plot ir ). 
 You can use the Power & Rail Plots form to browse to other analysis runs and load them as well to view early analysis results and compare runs. You can specify the type of plot (Rail Analysis, Power Analysis, or Capacitance) and then select the specific plot type. 
 For Early Rail Analysis, the viewing of Power & Rail Plots is the same as that used for Sign-off Analysis. For additional information on viewing the plots, see " Viewing Static Rail Analysis Results " in the Voltus User Guide .

The Library Generation engine is used to create and modify a cell library database of power-grid views. In an integrated circuit design, it is common for many standard cells and megacells to be reused across multiple designs. Cadence flows require you to pre-process such cells, reducing the overall time required for extraction.

Library Generation takes LEF, GDSII, or a combination of LEF and GDSII as input and creates a binary power-grid view of the cell contents within the cell library database. Power-grid views contain the physical and electrical representation of all the cells and blocks in the design. They contain necessary information required for power and rail analysis:

- For rail analysis, it contains RC parasitics, tap currents, geometries
- For power analysis, it contains netlist and output capacitance loading
The following diagram illustrates the library generation flow:

Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution requires power-grid libraries for dynamic power calculation and static and dynamic IR drop analysis. The power-grid libraries are generated for standard cells, memories, IOs and custom macros in the design. Some of the library characterization features include the following:

- GUI driven and interactive TCL Command Interface
- Spectre-SIMI interface for accurate coupling capacitance characterization
- Automatic spawning of library generation jobs for macros (memories)
- Library Reports and Debugging
- Library viewer
Inputs
The inputs to Library Generation are:

- Technology LEF (header.LEF)
- Cell LEF with power pins
- Cell GDS (for macro blocks)
- Extraction technology file for Quantus (qrcTechFile)
- Layer map file for GDS to qrcTechFile technology file
- Layer map file for LEF to qrcTechFile technology file
- SPICE device models (Spectre or Hspice)
- Subcircuit SPICE-format netlist, preferably with device X,Y coordinates 
 This is required so that tap current is snapped to the nearest device and give most accurate load for the device. Without xy coordinates, it results in even distribution of tap current across the entire device.
Outputs
Library Generation outputs the following files:

- A cell library database that contains the following information:
  - LEF technology information, which includes the following:
    - Layer information for each layer: its name, its relationship to other layers, its routing width and direction
    - Via information for each via: its name, the layers that it connects, and a set of geometries accessible as polygons
  - Cell information, which may include the following:
    - Geometric views of the cell
    - Port information
    - Power-grid views
    - Process technology data
  - A bounding box for each cell
- A log file named *Libgen_*/library_name/*.log*
- Text report and summary files having detailed information about power-grid library
Types of Power-Grid Libraries
The two types of libraries are:

- Technology Library - The Technology Library contains the tech file, area capacitance specification, decap/filler/powergate cells, and Tech view for all the cells. A technology library is a minimum requirement to run rail analysis. 
 
The technology library is a mandatory requirement for rail analysis. However, to obtain accurate IR drop simulation, power-grid library should be characterized for standard cells and macro blocks. When specifying the list of PGVs to rail analysis using the set_rail_analysis_mode command, you must specify the technology library as the first PGV library in the list. If both technology and standard cell PGV libraries exist, the standard cell PGV is given a higher precedence.
- Power-Grid Library- The Power Grid Library contains three kind of power grid views (PGV), Early/IR/EM, for standard cell and macro/memory/io cells. The power-grid library does not contain the tech file, and the decoupling capacitance is derived from spice simulation. 
 Power Grid Libraries for standard cells are often generated by a third party and shipped to the customer with the corresponding process. In advanced process technologies (< 90nm) there may be multiple variants for the same process including different number of metal layers, different width and thickness combinations for top metal layers. Since standard cells usually only use the bottom couple of metal layers, these variants should not affect the standard cell library itself. A third party can ship technology libraries for each process variant to use with a single standard cell power grid library or can create a specific combination of corner and header.lef. Voltus will use the Quantus techfile embedded in the technology library to process all the Power Grid Libraries.
Power-Grid View Types
During rail analysis, different PGVs are used based on the accuracy mode selected. The PGV types correspond to the accuracy mode of rail analysis. The different power-grid view types are:

This view is the basic view generated in the technology library for every cell. It only contains the current distribution factor and decoupling capacitance at the power ports of the cell and does not include any information inside the cell. If any cells do not have a Power Grid Library defined, tech view will be used to run rail analysis, but it is recommended to generate Power Grid Library for all standard cells and macro blocks to obtain accurate rail analysis.

This view only contains the current distribution factor and spice simulated decoupling capacitance information at the power ports of the cell and does not include the parasitics associated with the interconnect, which prevents IR drop within the block or cell from being analyzed. Early power-grid views are default PGV view for rail analysis XD accuracy mode, which is recommended for early design phase.

IR View will perform reduction on extracted power-grid and merge small current value current taps together without significant impact on accuracy. The IR view was created for tool performance reasons. Current designs in many cases contain an embedded memory for boosting performance. The bit cells of the memory in general contribute to creation of a lot of current taps, however, they do not contribute to the IR much, but will affect the tool performance. This IR view suppresses those current taps which have a very small current value thus improving both memory usage and performance of the tool. There is limited visibility into the cell because the current taps for the bit-cells inside the memory could be collapsed. The IR view is the default for rail analysis HD mode, with the exception for static EM rail analysis where EM view will be applied.

The EM view contains the extracted power-grid from the GDSII data of a cell. It will create current taps on the bottom conducting layer or at the locations of the vias layer above it. GDSII and XY spice netlist is required to generate accurate current distribution and decoupling capacitance, but you can manually force current values and capacitance to be assigned to these taps. You can also set up a trigger file to manually define the dynamic current behavior or run Spice simulators to obtain current waveform.
The following table illustrates the definition of PGV:

When specifying the list of PGVs to rail analysis using the set_rail_analysis_mode command, you must specify the technology library as the first PGV library in the list. If both technology and standard cell PGV libraries exist, the standard cell PGV is given a higher precedence.
- Power-Grid Library- The Power Grid Library contains three kind of power grid views (PGV), Early/IR/EM, for standard cell and macro/memory/io cells. The power-grid library does not contain the tech file, and the decoupling capacitance is derived from spice simulation. 
 Power Grid Libraries for standard cells are often generated by a third party and shipped to the customer with the corresponding process. In advanced process technologies (< 90nm) there may be multiple variants for the same process including different number of metal layers, different width and thickness combinations for top metal layers. Since standard cells usually only use the bottom couple of metal layers, these variants should not affect the standard cell library itself. A third party can ship technology libraries for each process variant to use with a single standard cell power grid library or can create a specific combination of corner and header.lef. Voltus will use the Quantus techfile embedded in the technology library to process all the Power Grid Libraries.
Power-Grid View Types
During rail analysis, different PGVs are used based on the accuracy mode selected. The PGV types correspond to the accuracy mode of rail analysis. The different power-grid view types are:

This view is the basic view generated in the technology library for every cell. It only contains the current distribution factor and decoupling capacitance at the power ports of the cell and does not include any information inside the cell. If any cells do not have a Power Grid Library defined, tech view will be used to run rail analysis, but it is recommended to generate Power Grid Library for all standard cells and macro blocks to obtain accurate rail analysis.

This view only contains the current distribution factor and spice simulated decoupling capacitance information at the power ports of the cell and does not include the parasitics associated with the interconnect, which prevents IR drop within the block or cell from being analyzed. Early power-grid views are default PGV view for rail analysis XD accuracy mode, which is recommended for early design phase.

IR View will perform reduction on extracted power-grid and merge small current value current taps together without significant impact on accuracy. The IR view was created for tool performance reasons. Current designs in many cases contain an embedded memory for boosting performance. The bit cells of the memory in general contribute to creation of a lot of current taps, however, they do not contribute to the IR much, but will affect the tool performance. This IR view suppresses those current taps which have a very small current value thus improving both memory usage and performance of the tool. There is limited visibility into the cell because the current taps for the bit-cells inside the memory could be collapsed. The IR view is the default for rail analysis HD mode, with the exception for static EM rail analysis where EM view will be applied.

The EM view contains the extracted power-grid from the GDSII data of a cell. It will create current taps on the bottom conducting layer or at the locations of the vias layer above it. GDSII and XY spice netlist is required to generate accurate current distribution and decoupling capacitance, but you can manually force current values and capacitance to be assigned to these taps. You can also set up a trigger file to manually define the dynamic current behavior or run Spice simulators to obtain current waveform.
The following table illustrates the definition of PGV:
Library Generation outputs the following files:

- A cell library database that contains the following information:
  - LEF technology information, which includes the following:
    - Layer information for each layer: its name, its relationship to other layers, its routing width and direction
    - Via information for each via: its name, the layers that it connects, and a set of geometries accessible as polygons
  - Cell information, which may include the following:
    - Geometric views of the cell
    - Port information
    - Power-grid views
    - Process technology data
  - A bounding box for each cell
- A log file named *Libgen_*/library_name/*.log*
- Text report and summary files having detailed information about power-grid library
Types of Power-Grid Libraries
The two types of libraries are:

- Technology Library - The Technology Library contains the tech file, area capacitance specification, decap/filler/powergate cells, and Tech view for all the cells. A technology library is a minimum requirement to run rail analysis. 
 
The technology library is a mandatory requirement for rail analysis. However, to obtain accurate IR drop simulation, power-grid library should be characterized for standard cells and macro blocks. When specifying the list of PGVs to rail analysis using the set_rail_analysis_mode command, you must specify the technology library as the first PGV library in the list. If both technology and standard cell PGV libraries exist, the standard cell PGV is given a higher precedence.
