lines: The .summary file generated during the characterization of current waveforms for custom macros includes the functional modes or custom modes (if given by the user) and the area information for each mode.
Contents of Trigger File Based Memory/Macro Cell Library
Inside the accurate memory/macro power-grid view:

- Distributed RC for power net and intrinsic capacitance
- PWL current taps for each device (transistor) in the design
- Complete visibility inside the cell for power-grid and transistor
- Macro trigger information is used later by Power Analysis and Rail Analysis tools to automatically use current waveforms as per the trigger information. The Power Analysis software identifies the trigger condition saved in the EM power-grid view during vectorless or vector-based analysis and generates trigger files. The Rail Analysis software processes the trigger files generated by Power Analysis and the EM power library of the custom macro generated by the Library Characterization engine, and performs full-chip dynamic IR drop analysis with custom macros operating under different functional modes. 
 The following is a sample of the log information generated during Power Analysis: 
 ** INFO: (VOLTUS_POWR-2061): Writing ASCII trigger file DYNAMIC_POWER/trigger.txt 
 The trigger file contains instance names and when they will be triggered during simulation. The following is a sample auto-generated trigger file in the power analysis directory: 
 INST1 2.1995e-07 1 READ 
 The following is a sample of the log information generated during Rail Analysis: 
 INFO (VOLTUS_RAIL-5023): Found a dynamic tap current view for instance "INST1" whose waveform is triggered at time 1e-07s.
- Optimized collapsed views are incrementally generated by performing RC reduction and collapsing (merging) the devices in the region with relatively small current consumption, (e.g. bit cells region in the memory). This helps improve IR drop analysis performance without sacrificing accuracy of any significant order. The optimized views can be used during IR drop analysis by selecting Fast: Accurate accuracy mode.
Power-Grid View Generation Flow for Macros from PGDB/xDSPF

You can use PGDB and xDSPF as input to library characterization for generating power-grid libraries in advanced node designs. This feature supports third-party extractors that create an extracted view of the design in the xDSPF format. In addition, library characterization supports the PGDB format netlist generated using the Quantus extractor. When you specify xDSPF or PGDB as inputs, GDS files are not required.
Tcl command for PGDB input:
#Specify the -pgdb_layermap and -pgdb_list_file parameters to generate PGV from an external PGDB.
The syntax of the PGDB header file is:
CELL cell_name PGDB pgdb_directory
The PGDB directory contains the PGDB for all the power and ground nets of the cell. The PGDB directory has cellname_Netname.* files.
The syntax of the PGDB layermap file is:
Tcl command for xDSPF input:
#Specify the -import_xdspf_list_file and -xdspf_l ayermap parameters to generate PGV from an xDSPF netlist.
The syntax of the xDSPF layermap file is:

You must define layers in the stack from the top most layers to the bottom most layers. With reference to this layermap stack, the library characterizer will create the PGDB for the layers as defined in the xDSPF layermap file.
Multi-Mode Power-Grid View Generation

In the multi-mode PGV generation flow, the software allows you to save static or dynamic currents for multiple voltages and frequency per each functional mode in a single PGV. The software applies current from the PGV based on the specified mode, voltage, and frequency for different instances of the cell. This flow can be used in scenarios where multiple instances of the same cell are existing in a design, and each instance is operating at a different voltage and/or clock frequency.
To run this flow, specify the following keywords in the trigger file:

- MODE <mode_name> static | dynamic - the static keyword with the MODE keyword indicates static current behavior and the dynamic keyword with the MODE keyword indicates dynamic current behavior. 
 In the power analysis flow, you can specify the set_power -static_mode parameter to define the mode name or the custom label name associated with a cell or instance for computing instance current from PGV with static multi-voltage multi-frequency (MvMf) modes.
- VOLTAGE - specifies the voltage values at which simulation is done for each net.
- FREQ - specifies the frequency value at which simulation is done.
- CUSTOM_LABEL - specifies a label or tag to distinguish various voltage and frequency pairs across all modes.
- SIM_DIR - specifies the directory containing the simulation results corresponding to the specified voltage and frequency. The simulation directory ( SIM_DIR ) can point to the static or dynamic current files. If you specify the dynamic simulation data for the static mode, the software will derive the average current for each device and save it in the static PGV.
- RON_FILE - specifies the file containing the powergate RON values corresponding to the specified voltage and frequency. If RON_FILE is not specified, then the RON values are read from SIM_DIR .
- RON_METHOD – RON_FILE contains 3 possible RON values: min , max , avg . This keyword can be used to select one RON value. The default is max .
- RON_THRESHOLD – specifies the RON threshold value. The software compares the powergate RON value with the RON threshold to determine whether the powergate is ON or OFF.
- OFF_NETS - specifies the list of switched-nets inside the cell that are defined as off nets. If a net is specified as the off net, the net is dropped when the PGV is loaded for top-level rail analysis. This is referred to as the user-specified off nets. However, if the RON of all the powergates is greater than RON_THRESHOLD , the switched net is treated as off net. This is referred to as the derived off nets.
- CELL_DECAP_FILE <file> - specifies different cell decoupling capacitance values for multiple voltages in each mode. The format of the cell decap file is: 
 UNIT CAP <Cap unit> (default is fF)
UNIT RESISTANCE <Res unit> (default is Ohm)
CELL <cellname>
NET <netname1> <capacitance> <resistance>
- STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER {<NET_NAME> <VALUE> ….} - specifies to store user-defined net-based internal power in the static PGV. The value is in A/MHz. 
 If STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER is defined in the PGV, the power for macro is computed as below:
  - Leakage : obtained from PGV. If not defined in PGV, it is obtained from .lib.
  - Internal : STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER * frequency
  - Switching : sum of all output cap * transition density
Example 1: Dynamic PGV Generation
Following is an example of the trigger file for dynamic PGV generation:
CELL cell1 RON_THRESHOLD = 5 MODE READ dynamic CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !CEN & !WEN ) CONDITIONAL_PIN clk4 { RISE }
CUSTOM_LABEL One VOLTAGE { VDDG 0.6 } FREQ 100 RON_FILE ./ronfile1.txt RON_METHOD min OFF_NETS { VVDDG }
CUSTOM_LABEL two VOLTAGE { VDDG 0.9 } FREQ 100 RON_FILE ./ronfile1.txt END_MODE ..... END
The file specified with the RON_FILE keyword contains multiple on-resistance (Ron) values, that is minimum, maximum, and average, for power gates. The following is a snippet of the RON file:
D-Term-Net S-Term_Net Avg Max Min Gate
VDD VDDG 1 2 3 X2_unmatched
VDD VDDG 1 2 3 X3_unmatched
VDD VDDG 1 2 3 X5_unmatched
The following is a snippet of the additional information related to the multi-mode PGV generation that is added in the library report:
RON_THRESHOLD = <value> MODE = <mode/sub_mode> RON_METHOD = <min | avg | max> <Net> <#Powergates > <#Off Powergates> <Status>
The following is a snippet of the library report:

This trigger file information is used later by Power Analysis and Rail Analysis to automatically use current waveforms as per the trigger information. During power analysis, you can schedule the desired mode in the following two ways

- Specify the custom label corresponding to a voltage and frequency pair.
- Specify the mode name. In this case, the software selects the closest voltage and frequency pair for the instance based on the instance voltage and fastest frequency.

The Power Analysis engine supports multiple modes/custom labels in the switch-pattern if they are generated without RON files and off nets. However, if a switch-pattern has a mode generated with RON file or off nets, then the software ignores such modes and only uses the previous modes.
In the following example, the multi-voltage multi-frequency PGV has the following 3 modes, that is, two modes without a RON file and off nets, and the third mode with a RON file and off nets:
Read [ No off nets and RON files in the trigger file ] CUSTOM_LABEL R1 CUSTOM_LABEL R2
Write [ No off nets and RON files in the trigger file ] CUSTOM_LABEL W1 CUSTOM_LABEL W2
HV_IVD [ With off nets and RON files in the trigger file ] CUSTOM_LABEL H1 CUSTOM_LABEL H2
The following commands describe the use model for mixed multiple modes (with/without RON file/off nets):
The log information generated during Power Analysis reports the label and the corresponding voltage/frequency pair for the specific instance, as shown below: Instance 'I1' is using mode 'M1'
During Rail Analysis, the specified mode is triggered, and the off nets are removed from the power-grid.
Example 2: Static PGV Generation
Following is an example of the trigger file for static PGV generation:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL cell2 LEAKAGE_CURRENT { VSS 2p VDD 17n } 
MODE_NAME READ static CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } CONDITIONAL_PIN_REFERENCE_TIME 0.0

CUSTOM_LABEL A1 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.9 VDD_LP 0.9 VSS 0 } CAPACITANCE 10 USER_PWL_FILE /../pwl_1.txt TIME_FACTOR=2 CURRENT_FACTOR=2 STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER { VSS 1p VDD 17n VDD_LP 1 } CELL_DECAP_FILE cell_decap_read.txt SWITCHBIT_WINDOW {-3 4} VSS SWITCHBIT_WINDOW {4 8} VDD
CUSTOM_LABEL A2 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.7 VDD_LP 0.7 VSS 0 } CAPACITANCE 50 USER_PWL_FILE /../pwl_1.txt STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER { VSS 2p VDD 27n VDD_LP 1 } CELL_DECAP_FILE cell_decap_read.txt END_MODE
MODE_NAME WRITE static ... END_MODE END
Additional Reports
Tap Current Report

The macro PGV generation flow generates a report called as tapSnapReport.txt that gives detailed information about current assigned to taps/devices. The report allows you to verify the current and capacitance values assigned to each device, and the tap current distribution for each interface node.
The format of the file is:
Cell Name: Net Name: Layer Name: NODE DEVICE(X,Y) NODE(X,Y) CURRENT CAPVALUE TAP NAME SWITCH_NODE(X,Y) .... nodeId : NODE % of taps connected
Here,

- Cell Name is the macro cell name
- Net Name is the power/ground net that supplies power to the macro cell
- Layer Name is the via layer name. The current taps will be attached to the layer that is below the specified via or contact layer.
- NODE is the unique node ID
- DEVICE(X,Y) is the X,Y co-ordinate of the device specified in the netlist file
- NODE(X,Y) is the X,Y location of the node in GDS. This location is relative to the LEF coordinate space.
- CURRENT is the current value for the node
- CAPVALUE is the capacitance value for the node
- TAP NAME is the name of the device
- NODE % of taps connected is the % of taps connected to a node. This can be used to determine the list of nodes that are heavily connected.
- SWITCH_NODE(X,Y) is the X,Y co-ordinate of the switched net connected to the power/ground (PG) net. This column is displayed only if the PG net is connected to a switched net. The switched net will have a separate section for those taps where it is not connected to the PG net.

title: Trigger File Format - for simulation vector-based files

lines: The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD SIMULATION SIMULATOR APS CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) CONDITIONAL_STIMULUS_FILE <usim1.txt> END_MODE END
where,
"!" denotes low state of the signal
"&" denotes boolean AND operation
"|" denotes boolean OR operation

For each mode, you need to specify separate stimulus files (for example usim1.txt and usim2.txt). The stimulus files contain the PWL definitions that will be used by UltraSim for simulating the specific mode.
Example 1 - Simulation

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD SIMULATION SIMULATOR APS CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = (! ATPGM & EZ &! SCAN &! TEZ & TM &! TPAGE &! TWTZ &! TWZ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK {RISE} CONDITIONAL_STIMULUS_FILE usim.txt END_MODE END
Trigger File Format - for user-defined PWLs
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) USER_PWL_FILE pwl.txt PWL_START_TIME <time> PWL_END_TIME <time> END_MODE END
The two formats of the PWL file are:
Format 1: Single PWL for Each Net

UNIT CURRENT <current unit> (default is A, Supported values are A, mA, uA & nA) UNIT TIME <time unit> (default is s, Supported values are s, ms, us, ns, ps) NET <net_name1> REGION {t1 i1 t2 i2 t3 i3.. } | PWLFILE net1_pwl.txt NET <net_name2> REGION {t4 i4 t5 i5 t6 i6.. } | PWLFILE net2_pwl.txt

Here, the time and current units are as defined using UNIT CURRENT and UNIT TIME syntax. NET specifies that the REGION command following it applies to the specified net name. This stays in effect until another NET or CELL specification is encountered. REGION specifies the power up piecewise linear (PWL) stimulus in (time, current) pair format. Instead of the REGION keyword, you can use the PWLFILE keyword to include a supply net (power or ground) or a switched-net-specific PWL file.
The following is an example of the file specified with the PWLFILE keyword: 0, 3 1.0e-9, 5 1.2e-9, 3 ...
Here, each line contains the time and current pairs.

Both the REGION and PWLFILE keywords can be used, but the PWLFILE keyword is recommended for long waveforms so that you can easily edit the PWL values, insert some new points, and comment out certain points that are to be ignored.
Format 2: Multiple PWLs for Each Net (Specify Multiple PWLs for Different Regions)
UNIT CURRENT mA UNIT TIME ns NET VDD REGION { 0 1.0 0.100 1.0 ... } RECT {llx1 lly1 urx1 ury1} REGION { 0 0.5 0.100 2.5 ... } RECT {llx2 lly2 urx2 ury2}...
NET VSS REGION { 0 -1.0 0.100 -1.0 ... } RECT {llx1 lly1 urx1 ury1} REGION { 0 -0.5 0.100 -2.5 ... } RECT {llx2 lly2 urx2 ury2}

This format allows you to assign multiple PWLs to different regions inside a macro. This format has multiple occurrences of the REGION keyword in the NET section. Each occurrence of the REGION keyword specifies the entire PWL waveform in the time-current pair format as a single line. This is followed by the RECT keyword that specifies the coordinates of an area inside the macro. This PWL file format allows you to model the dynamic current waveform for the taps in different areas accurately.
Example 1: User-defined PWL

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } USER_PWL_FILE pwl.txt END_MODE END
The following is the sample content of the pwl.txt file:
The following is the sample content of the pwl.txt file that specifies multiple PWLs for different regions:
Example 2: User-defined PWL
Following is an example of the trigger file specifying time ranges for different modes using a single PWL input file:
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL MEM1
MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT !WE CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } USER_PWL_FILE pwl.txt PWL_START_TIME 0ns PWL_END_TIME 5ns END_MODE
MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT WE CONDITIONAL_PIN { RISE } USER_PWL_FILE pwl.txt PWL_START_TIME 5ns PWL_END_TIME 10ns END_MODE END
Example 3: User-defined PWL
Following is an example of the trigger file specifying different sub-modes (custom-labels):
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL MEM1
MODE_NAME READ dynamic CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE }
CUSTOM_LABEL A_1 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.9 VDD_LP 0.9 VSS 0 } USER_PWL_FILE pwl1.txt
CUSTOM_LABEL A_2 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.7 VDD_LP 0.7 VSS 0 } USER_PWL_FILE pwl1.txt
END_MODE ... END
Example 4: User-defined PWL
Following is an example for specifying time and current scale factor for each net in the trigger file:
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL cell2 LEAKAGE_CURRENT { VSS 2p VDD 17n }
MODE_NAME READ static CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } CONDITIONAL_PIN_REFERENCE_TIME 0.0

CUSTOM_LABEL A1 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.9 VDD_LP 0.9 VSS 0 } CAPACITANCE 10 USER_PWL_FILE /../pwl_1.txt TIME_FACTOR=2 CURRENT_FACTOR=2 NET=VDD TIME_FACTOR=1.400 CURRENT_FACTOR=1.200 NET=VSS STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER { VSS 1p VDD 17n VDD_LP 1 } CELL_DECAP_FILE cell_decap_read.txt SWITCHBIT_WINDOW {-3 4} VSS SWITCHBIT_WINDOW {4 8} VDD
END_MODE
END
Trigger File Format - FSDB
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) FDSB_START_TIME <time> FSDB_END_TIME <time> END_MODE FSDB_PATH <path> FSDB_NET_NAME <net> [<generic_name>] END
Example 1: FSDB

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FDSB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 10ns END_MODE FSDB_PATH “new.fsdb" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddpr)" "VDDPR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddar)" "VDDAR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddp)" "VDDP" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvss)" "VSS" END
Example 2: FSDB

You can read multiple FSDBs for different modes, and specify different start and end times for each mode. The following example shows that 2 FSDB files are read, wherein read.fsdb is read for the READ mode, and write.fsdb is read for the WRITE mode:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL BLULP00256032040 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FDSB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 10ns FSDB_PATH "read.fsdb" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddpr)" "VDDPR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddar)" "VDDAR" END MODE

MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FDSB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 15ns FSDB_PATH "write.fsdb" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddpr)" "VDDPR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddar)" "VDDAR" END MODE END
Example 3: FSDB

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL cellA MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { FALL } BIAS_VOLTAGE { A[1] 0.9 A[2] 0.9 } FSDB_START_TIME 19ns FSDB_END_TIME 33ns FSDB_PATH "./tran.fsdb" END_MODE
MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } BIAS_VOLTAGE { A[1] 0.9 A[2] 0.0 } FSDB_START_TIME 10ns FSDB_END_TIME 40ns FSDB_PATH "./tran.fsdb" END_MODE
Trigger File Format - Datasheet
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) DATASHEET_DELAY <time> DATASHEET_PARAMETER < {power_pin cap leakage_pwr} > PEAK_CURRENT {power_pin peak_current} END_MODE DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW <time> DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW <time> END
Example 1: Datasheet

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( WZ & EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 7.6913ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDDAR 0.1715pf 2.496e-05mW }{ VDDPR 18.8985pf 3.63e-06mW }{ VBBNW 0.1780pf 2.601e-06mW }} END_MODE

MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !WZ & !WTZ & EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 8.216ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDDAR 0.1715pf 1.355e-06mW }{ VDDPR 0.1715pf 1.81e-05mW } { VBBNW 0.1715pf 2.614e-06mW }} END_MODE

MODE_NAME STANDBY CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 0.6913ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDDAR 0.1715pf 2.902e-05mW }{ VDDPR 0.2024pf 1.81e-05mW } { VBBNW 0.1723pf 6.92e-07mW }} END_MODE
DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW 0.6ns DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW 0.3682ns END
Example 2: Datasheet

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( WZ & EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 7.6913ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDD 0.1715pf 2.5e-05W }{ VDDPR 18.8985pf 3.63e-06W }{ VBBNW 0.1780pf 2.601e-06W }} PEAK_CURRENT {{ VDD 0.15mA} { VDDPR 0.1mA} { VBBNW 0.25mA}} END_MODE
DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW 0.6ns DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW 0.8ns END
Example 3: Datasheet

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL COMPILER_MEM MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = (ME & !WE &!LS & !DS & !SD) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK {RISE} DATASHEET_DELAY 1.2ns DATASHEET_START_TIME 100e-12 DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{VDD 0.1193e-09 0.0025mW} {VDDM 0.00146e-09 0mW}} DATASHEET_PEAK_CURRENT {{VDD 68mA} {VDDM 0mA}} END_MODE

MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = (ME & !WE &!LS & !DS & !SD) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK {RISE} DATASHEET_DELAY 1.2ns DATASHEET_START_TIME 100e-12 DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{VDD 0.1813e-09 0.0025mW} {VDDM 0.00146e-09 0mW}} DATASHEET_PEAK_CURRENT {{VDD 82mA} {VDDM 0mA}} END_MODE
DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW 5.508481e-03ns DATASHEET_OUTPUT-SLEW 3.693163e-03ns END
Trigger File Format - for user-specified Spice Deck
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD SPICE_DECK_BASED CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) FDSB_START_TIME <time> FSDB_END_TIME <time> END_MODE DECK_FILE <filename> FSDB_NET_NAME <net> [<generic_name>] FSDB_FILE <filename> SIM_OPTIONS +aps END
Example 1: User-specified Spice Deck
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD SPICE_DECK_BASED CELL cellA MODE READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !CEN & !WEN ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FSDB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 0.5ns END_MODE
MODE WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !CEN & !WEN ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FSDB_START_TIME 0.6ns FSDB_END_TIME 2ns END_MODE
DECK_FILE deck.cir FSDB_NET_NAME i1(vvdd) VDD FSDB_NET_NAME i1(vvss) VSS FSDB_FILE usimCktFile.fsdb.gz SIM_OPTIONS +aps
END
Trigger File Format - Dotlib

For dotlib-based current characterization, the trigger and stimulus files are automatically generated from an existing dotlib. This flow is applicable only for memory cells. Voltus generates stimuli for the worst power groups (rise_power, fall_power or power) in the specified dotlib. It reads only the internal power groups having the "when" condition and constructs stimulus vectors. Each of the generated stimulus belongs to one of the following groups:

- pin power based (this group is associated with input pin)
- arc-based (this group is associated with output pin)
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DOTLIB DOTLIB_LIST {a list of memory dotlibs files} MODE_COUNT <specifies the maximum number of modes to be generated. Valid values are either a positive number or `all'. The default value is 4> MODE_CONFIG_FILE <file_path> SIM_STOP_TIME <time> SIM_STEP_SIZE <step_size>

If the dotlib contains arc-based power groups, you must specify MODE_CONFIG_FILE and include the memory_write condition in the memory description file. Otherwise, arc-based power groups will be skipped. The memory description file contains the following information:

- Signals slew : specify slew for signals. These slew values will be used in case rise or fall is to be applied on signal. The slew value specified using this construct takes priority over the slew value saved for the group. The default slew unit in the slews construct is ns. The syntax is: 
 slews {
<signal_1> <slew_value_1>
<signal_2> <slew_value_2>
....
}
- Signals : specify signals' active values irrespective of memory_write . The syntax is: 
 signals {
<sig_1_name> <active_value> 
<sig_2_name> <active_value> 
....
 }
 where <active value> can be H or L
- Memory write information : specify information required to write data to memory through memory_write block. 
 memory_write {
clock <clock_name> <type>, where <type> can be R, F, H or L
data_in <data_bus_name>
address <address bus name>
signal <sig_1 name> <active_value>, where <active value> can be H or L
signal <sig_2 name> <active_value>, where <active value> can be H or L
 ....
}
A sample memory description file is given below:
cell memory1 { memory_write { address A data_in D clock CLK R signal WEN L signal CEN L } slews
Example 1: Dotlib
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DOTLIB DOTLIB_LIST {} MODE_COUNT all SIM_STOP_TIME 10ns SIM_STEP_SIZE 100ps
Sample Library Generation Output Report
The following is a report generated from a power-grid library using a dotlib-based trigger file:

data:
================================================================== 
 
 Cell PowerNets Capacitance PowerGrid Views 
 
 ---------------------------------------------------------------| 
 
 Cell1 
 
 VSS(0.0000) 5.3e-12 EARLY(255 taps) EM(5279 devices) IR(4890 devices) 
 
 VDD(0.9000) 5.2e-12 EARLY(193 taps) EM(3371 devices) IR(2326 devices) 
 
 Cell_Status: PASS 
 
 INFO: Dynamic Tap Current views were created for this cell. MODE1 has Conditional input = "( !CEN & !WEN ) " and Conditional pin = "CLK RISE". 
 
 INFO: Dynamic Tap Current views were created for this cell. MODE2 has Conditional input = "( CEN & WEN ) " and Conditional pin = "CLK RISE". 
 
 -----------------------------------------------------------------|

lines: The .summary file generated during the characterization of current waveforms for custom macros includes the functional modes or custom modes (if given by the user) and the area information for each mode.
Contents of Trigger File Based Memory/Macro Cell Library
Inside the accurate memory/macro power-grid view:

- Distributed RC for power net and intrinsic capacitance
- PWL current taps for each device (transistor) in the design
- Complete visibility inside the cell for power-grid and transistor
- Macro trigger information is used later by Power Analysis and Rail Analysis tools to automatically use current waveforms as per the trigger information. The Power Analysis software identifies the trigger condition saved in the EM power-grid view during vectorless or vector-based analysis and generates trigger files. The Rail Analysis software processes the trigger files generated by Power Analysis and the EM power library of the custom macro generated by the Library Characterization engine, and performs full-chip dynamic IR drop analysis with custom macros operating under different functional modes. 
 The following is a sample of the log information generated during Power Analysis: 
 ** INFO: (VOLTUS_POWR-2061): Writing ASCII trigger file DYNAMIC_POWER/trigger.txt 
 The trigger file contains instance names and when they will be triggered during simulation. The following is a sample auto-generated trigger file in the power analysis directory: 
 INST1 2.1995e-07 1 READ 
 The following is a sample of the log information generated during Rail Analysis: 
 INFO (VOLTUS_RAIL-5023): Found a dynamic tap current view for instance "INST1" whose waveform is triggered at time 1e-07s.
- Optimized collapsed views are incrementally generated by performing RC reduction and collapsing (merging) the devices in the region with relatively small current consumption, (e.g. bit cells region in the memory). This helps improve IR drop analysis performance without sacrificing accuracy of any significant order. The optimized views can be used during IR drop analysis by selecting Fast: Accurate accuracy mode.
Power-Grid View Generation Flow for Macros from PGDB/xDSPF

You can use PGDB and xDSPF as input to library characterization for generating power-grid libraries in advanced node designs. This feature supports third-party extractors that create an extracted view of the design in the xDSPF format. In addition, library characterization supports the PGDB format netlist generated using the Quantus extractor. When you specify xDSPF or PGDB as inputs, GDS files are not required.
Tcl command for PGDB input:
#Specify the -pgdb_layermap and -pgdb_list_file parameters to generate PGV from an external PGDB.
The syntax of the PGDB header file is:
CELL cell_name PGDB pgdb_directory
The PGDB directory contains the PGDB for all the power and ground nets of the cell. The PGDB directory has cellname_Netname.* files.
The syntax of the PGDB layermap file is:
Tcl command for xDSPF input:
#Specify the -import_xdspf_list_file and -xdspf_l ayermap parameters to generate PGV from an xDSPF netlist.
The syntax of the xDSPF layermap file is:

You must define layers in the stack from the top most layers to the bottom most layers. With reference to this layermap stack, the library characterizer will create the PGDB for the layers as defined in the xDSPF layermap file.
Multi-Mode Power-Grid View Generation

In the multi-mode PGV generation flow, the software allows you to save static or dynamic currents for multiple voltages and frequency per each functional mode in a single PGV. The software applies current from the PGV based on the specified mode, voltage, and frequency for different instances of the cell. This flow can be used in scenarios where multiple instances of the same cell are existing in a design, and each instance is operating at a different voltage and/or clock frequency.
To run this flow, specify the following keywords in the trigger file:

- MODE <mode_name> static | dynamic - the static keyword with the MODE keyword indicates static current behavior and the dynamic keyword with the MODE keyword indicates dynamic current behavior. 
 In the power analysis flow, you can specify the set_power -static_mode parameter to define the mode name or the custom label name associated with a cell or instance for computing instance current from PGV with static multi-voltage multi-frequency (MvMf) modes.
- VOLTAGE - specifies the voltage values at which simulation is done for each net.
- FREQ - specifies the frequency value at which simulation is done.
- CUSTOM_LABEL - specifies a label or tag to distinguish various voltage and frequency pairs across all modes.
- SIM_DIR - specifies the directory containing the simulation results corresponding to the specified voltage and frequency. The simulation directory ( SIM_DIR ) can point to the static or dynamic current files. If you specify the dynamic simulation data for the static mode, the software will derive the average current for each device and save it in the static PGV.
- RON_FILE - specifies the file containing the powergate RON values corresponding to the specified voltage and frequency. If RON_FILE is not specified, then the RON values are read from SIM_DIR .
- RON_METHOD – RON_FILE contains 3 possible RON values: min , max , avg . This keyword can be used to select one RON value. The default is max .
- RON_THRESHOLD – specifies the RON threshold value. The software compares the powergate RON value with the RON threshold to determine whether the powergate is ON or OFF.
- OFF_NETS - specifies the list of switched-nets inside the cell that are defined as off nets. If a net is specified as the off net, the net is dropped when the PGV is loaded for top-level rail analysis. This is referred to as the user-specified off nets. However, if the RON of all the powergates is greater than RON_THRESHOLD , the switched net is treated as off net. This is referred to as the derived off nets.
- CELL_DECAP_FILE <file> - specifies different cell decoupling capacitance values for multiple voltages in each mode. The format of the cell decap file is: 
 UNIT CAP <Cap unit> (default is fF)
UNIT RESISTANCE <Res unit> (default is Ohm)
CELL <cellname>
NET <netname1> <capacitance> <resistance>
- STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER {<NET_NAME> <VALUE> ….} - specifies to store user-defined net-based internal power in the static PGV. The value is in A/MHz. 
 If STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER is defined in the PGV, the power for macro is computed as below:
  - Leakage : obtained from PGV. If not defined in PGV, it is obtained from .lib.
  - Internal : STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER * frequency
  - Switching : sum of all output cap * transition density
Example 1: Dynamic PGV Generation
Following is an example of the trigger file for dynamic PGV generation:
CELL cell1 RON_THRESHOLD = 5 MODE READ dynamic CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !CEN & !WEN ) CONDITIONAL_PIN clk4 { RISE }
CUSTOM_LABEL One VOLTAGE { VDDG 0.6 } FREQ 100 RON_FILE ./ronfile1.txt RON_METHOD min OFF_NETS { VVDDG }
CUSTOM_LABEL two VOLTAGE { VDDG 0.9 } FREQ 100 RON_FILE ./ronfile1.txt END_MODE ..... END
The file specified with the RON_FILE keyword contains multiple on-resistance (Ron) values, that is minimum, maximum, and average, for power gates. The following is a snippet of the RON file:
D-Term-Net S-Term_Net Avg Max Min Gate
VDD VDDG 1 2 3 X2_unmatched
VDD VDDG 1 2 3 X3_unmatched
VDD VDDG 1 2 3 X5_unmatched
The following is a snippet of the additional information related to the multi-mode PGV generation that is added in the library report:
RON_THRESHOLD = <value> MODE = <mode/sub_mode> RON_METHOD = <min | avg | max> <Net> <#Powergates > <#Off Powergates> <Status>
The following is a snippet of the library report:

This trigger file information is used later by Power Analysis and Rail Analysis to automatically use current waveforms as per the trigger information. During power analysis, you can schedule the desired mode in the following two ways

- Specify the custom label corresponding to a voltage and frequency pair.
- Specify the mode name. In this case, the software selects the closest voltage and frequency pair for the instance based on the instance voltage and fastest frequency.

The Power Analysis engine supports multiple modes/custom labels in the switch-pattern if they are generated without RON files and off nets. However, if a switch-pattern has a mode generated with RON file or off nets, then the software ignores such modes and only uses the previous modes.
In the following example, the multi-voltage multi-frequency PGV has the following 3 modes, that is, two modes without a RON file and off nets, and the third mode with a RON file and off nets:
Read [ No off nets and RON files in the trigger file ] CUSTOM_LABEL R1 CUSTOM_LABEL R2
Write [ No off nets and RON files in the trigger file ] CUSTOM_LABEL W1 CUSTOM_LABEL W2
HV_IVD [ With off nets and RON files in the trigger file ] CUSTOM_LABEL H1 CUSTOM_LABEL H2
The following commands describe the use model for mixed multiple modes (with/without RON file/off nets):
The log information generated during Power Analysis reports the label and the corresponding voltage/frequency pair for the specific instance, as shown below: Instance 'I1' is using mode 'M1'
During Rail Analysis, the specified mode is triggered, and the off nets are removed from the power-grid.
Example 2: Static PGV Generation
Following is an example of the trigger file for static PGV generation:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL cell2 LEAKAGE_CURRENT { VSS 2p VDD 17n } 
MODE_NAME READ static CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } CONDITIONAL_PIN_REFERENCE_TIME 0.0

CUSTOM_LABEL A1 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.9 VDD_LP 0.9 VSS 0 } CAPACITANCE 10 USER_PWL_FILE /../pwl_1.txt TIME_FACTOR=2 CURRENT_FACTOR=2 STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER { VSS 1p VDD 17n VDD_LP 1 } CELL_DECAP_FILE cell_decap_read.txt SWITCHBIT_WINDOW {-3 4} VSS SWITCHBIT_WINDOW {4 8} VDD
CUSTOM_LABEL A2 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.7 VDD_LP 0.7 VSS 0 } CAPACITANCE 50 USER_PWL_FILE /../pwl_1.txt STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER { VSS 2p VDD 27n VDD_LP 1 } CELL_DECAP_FILE cell_decap_read.txt END_MODE
MODE_NAME WRITE static ... END_MODE END
Additional Reports
Tap Current Report

The macro PGV generation flow generates a report called as tapSnapReport.txt that gives detailed information about current assigned to taps/devices. The report allows you to verify the current and capacitance values assigned to each device, and the tap current distribution for each interface node.
The format of the file is:
Cell Name: Net Name: Layer Name: NODE DEVICE(X,Y) NODE(X,Y) CURRENT CAPVALUE TAP NAME SWITCH_NODE(X,Y) .... nodeId : NODE % of taps connected
Here,

- Cell Name is the macro cell name
- Net Name is the power/ground net that supplies power to the macro cell
- Layer Name is the via layer name. The current taps will be attached to the layer that is below the specified via or contact layer.
- NODE is the unique node ID
- DEVICE(X,Y) is the X,Y co-ordinate of the device specified in the netlist file
- NODE(X,Y) is the X,Y location of the node in GDS. This location is relative to the LEF coordinate space.
- CURRENT is the current value for the node
- CAPVALUE is the capacitance value for the node
- TAP NAME is the name of the device
- NODE % of taps connected is the % of taps connected to a node. This can be used to determine the list of nodes that are heavily connected.
- SWITCH_NODE(X,Y) is the X,Y co-ordinate of the switched net connected to the power/ground (PG) net. This column is displayed only if the PG net is connected to a switched net. The switched net will have a separate section for those taps where it is not connected to the PG net.

title: Trigger File Format - for user-defined PWLs

lines: The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) USER_PWL_FILE pwl.txt PWL_START_TIME <time> PWL_END_TIME <time> END_MODE END
The two formats of the PWL file are:
Format 1: Single PWL for Each Net

UNIT CURRENT <current unit> (default is A, Supported values are A, mA, uA & nA) UNIT TIME <time unit> (default is s, Supported values are s, ms, us, ns, ps) NET <net_name1> REGION {t1 i1 t2 i2 t3 i3.. } | PWLFILE net1_pwl.txt NET <net_name2> REGION {t4 i4 t5 i5 t6 i6.. } | PWLFILE net2_pwl.txt

Here, the time and current units are as defined using UNIT CURRENT and UNIT TIME syntax. NET specifies that the REGION command following it applies to the specified net name. This stays in effect until another NET or CELL specification is encountered. REGION specifies the power up piecewise linear (PWL) stimulus in (time, current) pair format. Instead of the REGION keyword, you can use the PWLFILE keyword to include a supply net (power or ground) or a switched-net-specific PWL file.
The following is an example of the file specified with the PWLFILE keyword: 0, 3 1.0e-9, 5 1.2e-9, 3 ...
Here, each line contains the time and current pairs.

Both the REGION and PWLFILE keywords can be used, but the PWLFILE keyword is recommended for long waveforms so that you can easily edit the PWL values, insert some new points, and comment out certain points that are to be ignored.
Format 2: Multiple PWLs for Each Net (Specify Multiple PWLs for Different Regions)
UNIT CURRENT mA UNIT TIME ns NET VDD REGION { 0 1.0 0.100 1.0 ... } RECT {llx1 lly1 urx1 ury1} REGION { 0 0.5 0.100 2.5 ... } RECT {llx2 lly2 urx2 ury2}...
NET VSS REGION { 0 -1.0 0.100 -1.0 ... } RECT {llx1 lly1 urx1 ury1} REGION { 0 -0.5 0.100 -2.5 ... } RECT {llx2 lly2 urx2 ury2}

This format allows you to assign multiple PWLs to different regions inside a macro. This format has multiple occurrences of the REGION keyword in the NET section. Each occurrence of the REGION keyword specifies the entire PWL waveform in the time-current pair format as a single line. This is followed by the RECT keyword that specifies the coordinates of an area inside the macro. This PWL file format allows you to model the dynamic current waveform for the taps in different areas accurately.
Example 1: User-defined PWL

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } USER_PWL_FILE pwl.txt END_MODE END
The following is the sample content of the pwl.txt file:
The following is the sample content of the pwl.txt file that specifies multiple PWLs for different regions:
Example 2: User-defined PWL
Following is an example of the trigger file specifying time ranges for different modes using a single PWL input file:
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL MEM1
MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT !WE CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } USER_PWL_FILE pwl.txt PWL_START_TIME 0ns PWL_END_TIME 5ns END_MODE
MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT WE CONDITIONAL_PIN { RISE } USER_PWL_FILE pwl.txt PWL_START_TIME 5ns PWL_END_TIME 10ns END_MODE END
Example 3: User-defined PWL
Following is an example of the trigger file specifying different sub-modes (custom-labels):
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL MEM1
MODE_NAME READ dynamic CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE }
CUSTOM_LABEL A_1 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.9 VDD_LP 0.9 VSS 0 } USER_PWL_FILE pwl1.txt
CUSTOM_LABEL A_2 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.7 VDD_LP 0.7 VSS 0 } USER_PWL_FILE pwl1.txt
END_MODE ... END
Example 4: User-defined PWL
Following is an example for specifying time and current scale factor for each net in the trigger file:
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL cell2 LEAKAGE_CURRENT { VSS 2p VDD 17n }
MODE_NAME READ static CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } CONDITIONAL_PIN_REFERENCE_TIME 0.0

CUSTOM_LABEL A1 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.9 VDD_LP 0.9 VSS 0 } CAPACITANCE 10 USER_PWL_FILE /../pwl_1.txt TIME_FACTOR=2 CURRENT_FACTOR=2 NET=VDD TIME_FACTOR=1.400 CURRENT_FACTOR=1.200 NET=VSS STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER { VSS 1p VDD 17n VDD_LP 1 } CELL_DECAP_FILE cell_decap_read.txt SWITCHBIT_WINDOW {-3 4} VSS SWITCHBIT_WINDOW {4 8} VDD
END_MODE
END
Trigger File Format - FSDB
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) FDSB_START_TIME <time> FSDB_END_TIME <time> END_MODE FSDB_PATH <path> FSDB_NET_NAME <net> [<generic_name>] END
Example 1: FSDB

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FDSB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 10ns END_MODE FSDB_PATH “new.fsdb" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddpr)" "VDDPR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddar)" "VDDAR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddp)" "VDDP" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvss)" "VSS" END
Example 2: FSDB

You can read multiple FSDBs for different modes, and specify different start and end times for each mode. The following example shows that 2 FSDB files are read, wherein read.fsdb is read for the READ mode, and write.fsdb is read for the WRITE mode:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL BLULP00256032040 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FDSB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 10ns FSDB_PATH "read.fsdb" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddpr)" "VDDPR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddar)" "VDDAR" END MODE

MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FDSB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 15ns FSDB_PATH "write.fsdb" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddpr)" "VDDPR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddar)" "VDDAR" END MODE END
Example 3: FSDB

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL cellA MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { FALL } BIAS_VOLTAGE { A[1] 0.9 A[2] 0.9 } FSDB_START_TIME 19ns FSDB_END_TIME 33ns FSDB_PATH "./tran.fsdb" END_MODE
MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } BIAS_VOLTAGE { A[1] 0.9 A[2] 0.0 } FSDB_START_TIME 10ns FSDB_END_TIME 40ns FSDB_PATH "./tran.fsdb" END_MODE
Trigger File Format - Datasheet
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) DATASHEET_DELAY <time> DATASHEET_PARAMETER < {power_pin cap leakage_pwr} > PEAK_CURRENT {power_pin peak_current} END_MODE DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW <time> DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW <time> END
Example 1: Datasheet

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( WZ & EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 7.6913ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDDAR 0.1715pf 2.496e-05mW }{ VDDPR 18.8985pf 3.63e-06mW }{ VBBNW 0.1780pf 2.601e-06mW }} END_MODE

MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !WZ & !WTZ & EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 8.216ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDDAR 0.1715pf 1.355e-06mW }{ VDDPR 0.1715pf 1.81e-05mW } { VBBNW 0.1715pf 2.614e-06mW }} END_MODE

MODE_NAME STANDBY CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 0.6913ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDDAR 0.1715pf 2.902e-05mW }{ VDDPR 0.2024pf 1.81e-05mW } { VBBNW 0.1723pf 6.92e-07mW }} END_MODE
DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW 0.6ns DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW 0.3682ns END
Example 2: Datasheet

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( WZ & EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 7.6913ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDD 0.1715pf 2.5e-05W }{ VDDPR 18.8985pf 3.63e-06W }{ VBBNW 0.1780pf 2.601e-06W }} PEAK_CURRENT {{ VDD 0.15mA} { VDDPR 0.1mA} { VBBNW 0.25mA}} END_MODE
DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW 0.6ns DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW 0.8ns END
Example 3: Datasheet

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL COMPILER_MEM MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = (ME & !WE &!LS & !DS & !SD) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK {RISE} DATASHEET_DELAY 1.2ns DATASHEET_START_TIME 100e-12 DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{VDD 0.1193e-09 0.0025mW} {VDDM 0.00146e-09 0mW}} DATASHEET_PEAK_CURRENT {{VDD 68mA} {VDDM 0mA}} END_MODE

MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = (ME & !WE &!LS & !DS & !SD) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK {RISE} DATASHEET_DELAY 1.2ns DATASHEET_START_TIME 100e-12 DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{VDD 0.1813e-09 0.0025mW} {VDDM 0.00146e-09 0mW}} DATASHEET_PEAK_CURRENT {{VDD 82mA} {VDDM 0mA}} END_MODE
DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW 5.508481e-03ns DATASHEET_OUTPUT-SLEW 3.693163e-03ns END
Trigger File Format - for user-specified Spice Deck
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD SPICE_DECK_BASED CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) FDSB_START_TIME <time> FSDB_END_TIME <time> END_MODE DECK_FILE <filename> FSDB_NET_NAME <net> [<generic_name>] FSDB_FILE <filename> SIM_OPTIONS +aps END
Example 1: User-specified Spice Deck
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD SPICE_DECK_BASED CELL cellA MODE READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !CEN & !WEN ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FSDB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 0.5ns END_MODE
MODE WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !CEN & !WEN ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FSDB_START_TIME 0.6ns FSDB_END_TIME 2ns END_MODE
DECK_FILE deck.cir FSDB_NET_NAME i1(vvdd) VDD FSDB_NET_NAME i1(vvss) VSS FSDB_FILE usimCktFile.fsdb.gz SIM_OPTIONS +aps
END
Trigger File Format - Dotlib

For dotlib-based current characterization, the trigger and stimulus files are automatically generated from an existing dotlib. This flow is applicable only for memory cells. Voltus generates stimuli for the worst power groups (rise_power, fall_power or power) in the specified dotlib. It reads only the internal power groups having the "when" condition and constructs stimulus vectors. Each of the generated stimulus belongs to one of the following groups:

- pin power based (this group is associated with input pin)
- arc-based (this group is associated with output pin)
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DOTLIB DOTLIB_LIST {a list of memory dotlibs files} MODE_COUNT <specifies the maximum number of modes to be generated. Valid values are either a positive number or `all'. The default value is 4> MODE_CONFIG_FILE <file_path> SIM_STOP_TIME <time> SIM_STEP_SIZE <step_size>

If the dotlib contains arc-based power groups, you must specify MODE_CONFIG_FILE and include the memory_write condition in the memory description file. Otherwise, arc-based power groups will be skipped. The memory description file contains the following information:

- Signals slew : specify slew for signals. These slew values will be used in case rise or fall is to be applied on signal. The slew value specified using this construct takes priority over the slew value saved for the group. The default slew unit in the slews construct is ns. The syntax is: 
 slews {
<signal_1> <slew_value_1>
<signal_2> <slew_value_2>
....
}
- Signals : specify signals' active values irrespective of memory_write . The syntax is: 
 signals {
<sig_1_name> <active_value> 
<sig_2_name> <active_value> 
....
 }
 where <active value> can be H or L
- Memory write information : specify information required to write data to memory through memory_write block. 
 memory_write {
clock <clock_name> <type>, where <type> can be R, F, H or L
data_in <data_bus_name>
address <address bus name>
signal <sig_1 name> <active_value>, where <active value> can be H or L
signal <sig_2 name> <active_value>, where <active value> can be H or L
 ....
}
A sample memory description file is given below:
cell memory1 { memory_write { address A data_in D clock CLK R signal WEN L signal CEN L } slews
Example 1: Dotlib
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DOTLIB DOTLIB_LIST {} MODE_COUNT all SIM_STOP_TIME 10ns SIM_STEP_SIZE 100ps
Sample Library Generation Output Report
The following is a report generated from a power-grid library using a dotlib-based trigger file:

data:
==================================================================

lines: The .summary file generated during the characterization of current waveforms for custom macros includes the functional modes or custom modes (if given by the user) and the area information for each mode.
Contents of Trigger File Based Memory/Macro Cell Library
Inside the accurate memory/macro power-grid view:

- Distributed RC for power net and intrinsic capacitance
- PWL current taps for each device (transistor) in the design
- Complete visibility inside the cell for power-grid and transistor
- Macro trigger information is used later by Power Analysis and Rail Analysis tools to automatically use current waveforms as per the trigger information. The Power Analysis software identifies the trigger condition saved in the EM power-grid view during vectorless or vector-based analysis and generates trigger files. The Rail Analysis software processes the trigger files generated by Power Analysis and the EM power library of the custom macro generated by the Library Characterization engine, and performs full-chip dynamic IR drop analysis with custom macros operating under different functional modes. 
 The following is a sample of the log information generated during Power Analysis: 
 ** INFO: (VOLTUS_POWR-2061): Writing ASCII trigger file DYNAMIC_POWER/trigger.txt 
 The trigger file contains instance names and when they will be triggered during simulation. The following is a sample auto-generated trigger file in the power analysis directory: 
 INST1 2.1995e-07 1 READ 
 The following is a sample of the log information generated during Rail Analysis: 
 INFO (VOLTUS_RAIL-5023): Found a dynamic tap current view for instance "INST1" whose waveform is triggered at time 1e-07s.
- Optimized collapsed views are incrementally generated by performing RC reduction and collapsing (merging) the devices in the region with relatively small current consumption, (e.g. bit cells region in the memory). This helps improve IR drop analysis performance without sacrificing accuracy of any significant order. The optimized views can be used during IR drop analysis by selecting Fast: Accurate accuracy mode.
Power-Grid View Generation Flow for Macros from PGDB/xDSPF

You can use PGDB and xDSPF as input to library characterization for generating power-grid libraries in advanced node designs. This feature supports third-party extractors that create an extracted view of the design in the xDSPF format. In addition, library characterization supports the PGDB format netlist generated using the Quantus extractor. When you specify xDSPF or PGDB as inputs, GDS files are not required.
Tcl command for PGDB input:
#Specify the -pgdb_layermap and -pgdb_list_file parameters to generate PGV from an external PGDB.
The syntax of the PGDB header file is:
CELL cell_name PGDB pgdb_directory
The PGDB directory contains the PGDB for all the power and ground nets of the cell. The PGDB directory has cellname_Netname.* files.
The syntax of the PGDB layermap file is:
Tcl command for xDSPF input:
#Specify the -import_xdspf_list_file and -xdspf_l ayermap parameters to generate PGV from an xDSPF netlist.
The syntax of the xDSPF layermap file is:

You must define layers in the stack from the top most layers to the bottom most layers. With reference to this layermap stack, the library characterizer will create the PGDB for the layers as defined in the xDSPF layermap file.
Multi-Mode Power-Grid View Generation

In the multi-mode PGV generation flow, the software allows you to save static or dynamic currents for multiple voltages and frequency per each functional mode in a single PGV. The software applies current from the PGV based on the specified mode, voltage, and frequency for different instances of the cell. This flow can be used in scenarios where multiple instances of the same cell are existing in a design, and each instance is operating at a different voltage and/or clock frequency.
To run this flow, specify the following keywords in the trigger file:

- MODE <mode_name> static | dynamic - the static keyword with the MODE keyword indicates static current behavior and the dynamic keyword with the MODE keyword indicates dynamic current behavior. 
 In the power analysis flow, you can specify the set_power -static_mode parameter to define the mode name or the custom label name associated with a cell or instance for computing instance current from PGV with static multi-voltage multi-frequency (MvMf) modes.
- VOLTAGE - specifies the voltage values at which simulation is done for each net.
- FREQ - specifies the frequency value at which simulation is done.
- CUSTOM_LABEL - specifies a label or tag to distinguish various voltage and frequency pairs across all modes.
- SIM_DIR - specifies the directory containing the simulation results corresponding to the specified voltage and frequency. The simulation directory ( SIM_DIR ) can point to the static or dynamic current files. If you specify the dynamic simulation data for the static mode, the software will derive the average current for each device and save it in the static PGV.
- RON_FILE - specifies the file containing the powergate RON values corresponding to the specified voltage and frequency. If RON_FILE is not specified, then the RON values are read from SIM_DIR .
- RON_METHOD – RON_FILE contains 3 possible RON values: min , max , avg . This keyword can be used to select one RON value. The default is max .
- RON_THRESHOLD – specifies the RON threshold value. The software compares the powergate RON value with the RON threshold to determine whether the powergate is ON or OFF.
- OFF_NETS - specifies the list of switched-nets inside the cell that are defined as off nets. If a net is specified as the off net, the net is dropped when the PGV is loaded for top-level rail analysis. This is referred to as the user-specified off nets. However, if the RON of all the powergates is greater than RON_THRESHOLD , the switched net is treated as off net. This is referred to as the derived off nets.
- CELL_DECAP_FILE <file> - specifies different cell decoupling capacitance values for multiple voltages in each mode. The format of the cell decap file is: 
 UNIT CAP <Cap unit> (default is fF)
UNIT RESISTANCE <Res unit> (default is Ohm)
CELL <cellname>
NET <netname1> <capacitance> <resistance>
- STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER {<NET_NAME> <VALUE> ….} - specifies to store user-defined net-based internal power in the static PGV. The value is in A/MHz. 
 If STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER is defined in the PGV, the power for macro is computed as below:
  - Leakage : obtained from PGV. If not defined in PGV, it is obtained from .lib.
  - Internal : STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER * frequency
  - Switching : sum of all output cap * transition density
Example 1: Dynamic PGV Generation
Following is an example of the trigger file for dynamic PGV generation:
CELL cell1 RON_THRESHOLD = 5 MODE READ dynamic CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !CEN & !WEN ) CONDITIONAL_PIN clk4 { RISE }
CUSTOM_LABEL One VOLTAGE { VDDG 0.6 } FREQ 100 RON_FILE ./ronfile1.txt RON_METHOD min OFF_NETS { VVDDG }
CUSTOM_LABEL two VOLTAGE { VDDG 0.9 } FREQ 100 RON_FILE ./ronfile1.txt END_MODE ..... END
The file specified with the RON_FILE keyword contains multiple on-resistance (Ron) values, that is minimum, maximum, and average, for power gates. The following is a snippet of the RON file:
D-Term-Net S-Term_Net Avg Max Min Gate
VDD VDDG 1 2 3 X2_unmatched
VDD VDDG 1 2 3 X3_unmatched
VDD VDDG 1 2 3 X5_unmatched
The following is a snippet of the additional information related to the multi-mode PGV generation that is added in the library report:
RON_THRESHOLD = <value> MODE = <mode/sub_mode> RON_METHOD = <min | avg | max> <Net> <#Powergates > <#Off Powergates> <Status>
The following is a snippet of the library report:

This trigger file information is used later by Power Analysis and Rail Analysis to automatically use current waveforms as per the trigger information. During power analysis, you can schedule the desired mode in the following two ways

- Specify the custom label corresponding to a voltage and frequency pair.
- Specify the mode name. In this case, the software selects the closest voltage and frequency pair for the instance based on the instance voltage and fastest frequency.

The Power Analysis engine supports multiple modes/custom labels in the switch-pattern if they are generated without RON files and off nets. However, if a switch-pattern has a mode generated with RON file or off nets, then the software ignores such modes and only uses the previous modes.
In the following example, the multi-voltage multi-frequency PGV has the following 3 modes, that is, two modes without a RON file and off nets, and the third mode with a RON file and off nets:
Read [ No off nets and RON files in the trigger file ] CUSTOM_LABEL R1 CUSTOM_LABEL R2
Write [ No off nets and RON files in the trigger file ] CUSTOM_LABEL W1 CUSTOM_LABEL W2
HV_IVD [ With off nets and RON files in the trigger file ] CUSTOM_LABEL H1 CUSTOM_LABEL H2
The following commands describe the use model for mixed multiple modes (with/without RON file/off nets):
The log information generated during Power Analysis reports the label and the corresponding voltage/frequency pair for the specific instance, as shown below: Instance 'I1' is using mode 'M1'
During Rail Analysis, the specified mode is triggered, and the off nets are removed from the power-grid.
Example 2: Static PGV Generation
Following is an example of the trigger file for static PGV generation:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD PWL CELL cell2 LEAKAGE_CURRENT { VSS 2p VDD 17n } 
MODE_NAME READ static CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } CONDITIONAL_PIN_REFERENCE_TIME 0.0

CUSTOM_LABEL A1 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.9 VDD_LP 0.9 VSS 0 } CAPACITANCE 10 USER_PWL_FILE /../pwl_1.txt TIME_FACTOR=2 CURRENT_FACTOR=2 STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER { VSS 1p VDD 17n VDD_LP 1 } CELL_DECAP_FILE cell_decap_read.txt SWITCHBIT_WINDOW {-3 4} VSS SWITCHBIT_WINDOW {4 8} VDD
CUSTOM_LABEL A2 VOLTAGE { VDD 0.7 VDD_LP 0.7 VSS 0 } CAPACITANCE 50 USER_PWL_FILE /../pwl_1.txt STATIC_INTERNAL_POWER { VSS 2p VDD 27n VDD_LP 1 } CELL_DECAP_FILE cell_decap_read.txt END_MODE
MODE_NAME WRITE static ... END_MODE END
Additional Reports
Tap Current Report

The macro PGV generation flow generates a report called as tapSnapReport.txt that gives detailed information about current assigned to taps/devices. The report allows you to verify the current and capacitance values assigned to each device, and the tap current distribution for each interface node.
The format of the file is:
Cell Name: Net Name: Layer Name: NODE DEVICE(X,Y) NODE(X,Y) CURRENT CAPVALUE TAP NAME SWITCH_NODE(X,Y) .... nodeId : NODE % of taps connected
Here,

- Cell Name is the macro cell name
- Net Name is the power/ground net that supplies power to the macro cell
- Layer Name is the via layer name. The current taps will be attached to the layer that is below the specified via or contact layer.
- NODE is the unique node ID
- DEVICE(X,Y) is the X,Y co-ordinate of the device specified in the netlist file
- NODE(X,Y) is the X,Y location of the node in GDS. This location is relative to the LEF coordinate space.
- CURRENT is the current value for the node
- CAPVALUE is the capacitance value for the node
- TAP NAME is the name of the device
- NODE % of taps connected is the % of taps connected to a node. This can be used to determine the list of nodes that are heavily connected.
- SWITCH_NODE(X,Y) is the X,Y co-ordinate of the switched net connected to the power/ground (PG) net. This column is displayed only if the PG net is connected to a switched net. The switched net will have a separate section for those taps where it is not connected to the PG net.
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) FDSB_START_TIME <time> FSDB_END_TIME <time> END_MODE FSDB_PATH <path> FSDB_NET_NAME <net> [<generic_name>] END
Example 1: FSDB

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FDSB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 10ns END_MODE FSDB_PATH “new.fsdb" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddpr)" "VDDPR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddar)" "VDDAR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddp)" "VDDP" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvss)" "VSS" END
Example 2: FSDB

You can read multiple FSDBs for different modes, and specify different start and end times for each mode. The following example shows that 2 FSDB files are read, wherein read.fsdb is read for the READ mode, and write.fsdb is read for the WRITE mode:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL BLULP00256032040 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FDSB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 10ns FSDB_PATH "read.fsdb" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddpr)" "VDDPR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddar)" "VDDAR" END MODE

MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !ATPGM & EZ & !SCAN & !TEZ & TM & !TPAGE & !TWTZ & !TWZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FDSB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 15ns FSDB_PATH "write.fsdb" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddpr)" "VDDPR" FSDB_NET_NAME "i1(vvddar)" "VDDAR" END MODE END
Example 3: FSDB

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD FSDB CELL cellA MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { FALL } BIAS_VOLTAGE { A[1] 0.9 A[2] 0.9 } FSDB_START_TIME 19ns FSDB_END_TIME 33ns FSDB_PATH "./tran.fsdb" END_MODE
MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = !CEN CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } BIAS_VOLTAGE { A[1] 0.9 A[2] 0.0 } FSDB_START_TIME 10ns FSDB_END_TIME 40ns FSDB_PATH "./tran.fsdb" END_MODE
Trigger File Format - Datasheet
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) DATASHEET_DELAY <time> DATASHEET_PARAMETER < {power_pin cap leakage_pwr} > PEAK_CURRENT {power_pin peak_current} END_MODE DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW <time> DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW <time> END
Example 1: Datasheet

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( WZ & EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 7.6913ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDDAR 0.1715pf 2.496e-05mW }{ VDDPR 18.8985pf 3.63e-06mW }{ VBBNW 0.1780pf 2.601e-06mW }} END_MODE

MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !WZ & !WTZ & EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 8.216ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDDAR 0.1715pf 1.355e-06mW }{ VDDPR 0.1715pf 1.81e-05mW } { VBBNW 0.1715pf 2.614e-06mW }} END_MODE

MODE_NAME STANDBY CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 0.6913ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDDAR 0.1715pf 2.902e-05mW }{ VDDPR 0.2024pf 1.81e-05mW } { VBBNW 0.1723pf 6.92e-07mW }} END_MODE
DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW 0.6ns DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW 0.3682ns END
Example 2: Datasheet

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL mem1 MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( WZ & EZ ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } DATASHEET_DELAY 7.6913ns DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{ VDD 0.1715pf 2.5e-05W }{ VDDPR 18.8985pf 3.63e-06W }{ VBBNW 0.1780pf 2.601e-06W }} PEAK_CURRENT {{ VDD 0.15mA} { VDDPR 0.1mA} { VBBNW 0.25mA}} END_MODE
DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW 0.6ns DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW 0.8ns END
Example 3: Datasheet

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DATASHEET CELL COMPILER_MEM MODE_NAME READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = (ME & !WE &!LS & !DS & !SD) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK {RISE} DATASHEET_DELAY 1.2ns DATASHEET_START_TIME 100e-12 DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{VDD 0.1193e-09 0.0025mW} {VDDM 0.00146e-09 0mW}} DATASHEET_PEAK_CURRENT {{VDD 68mA} {VDDM 0mA}} END_MODE

MODE_NAME WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = (ME & !WE &!LS & !DS & !SD) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK {RISE} DATASHEET_DELAY 1.2ns DATASHEET_START_TIME 100e-12 DATASHEET_PARAMETER {{VDD 0.1813e-09 0.0025mW} {VDDM 0.00146e-09 0mW}} DATASHEET_PEAK_CURRENT {{VDD 82mA} {VDDM 0mA}} END_MODE
DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW 5.508481e-03ns DATASHEET_OUTPUT-SLEW 3.693163e-03ns END
Trigger File Format - for user-specified Spice Deck
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD SPICE_DECK_BASED CELL <cellname1> MODE_NAME <name1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT <conditional input statement for cellname1> CONDITIONAL_PIN <pin_name> (rise|fall|both) FDSB_START_TIME <time> FSDB_END_TIME <time> END_MODE DECK_FILE <filename> FSDB_NET_NAME <net> [<generic_name>] FSDB_FILE <filename> SIM_OPTIONS +aps END
Example 1: User-specified Spice Deck
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD SPICE_DECK_BASED CELL cellA MODE READ CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !CEN & !WEN ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FSDB_START_TIME 0ns FSDB_END_TIME 0.5ns END_MODE
MODE WRITE CONDITIONAL_INPUT = ( !CEN & !WEN ) CONDITIONAL_PIN CLK { RISE } FSDB_START_TIME 0.6ns FSDB_END_TIME 2ns END_MODE
DECK_FILE deck.cir FSDB_NET_NAME i1(vvdd) VDD FSDB_NET_NAME i1(vvss) VSS FSDB_FILE usimCktFile.fsdb.gz SIM_OPTIONS +aps
END
Trigger File Format - Dotlib

For dotlib-based current characterization, the trigger and stimulus files are automatically generated from an existing dotlib. This flow is applicable only for memory cells. Voltus generates stimuli for the worst power groups (rise_power, fall_power or power) in the specified dotlib. It reads only the internal power groups having the "when" condition and constructs stimulus vectors. Each of the generated stimulus belongs to one of the following groups:

- pin power based (this group is associated with input pin)
- arc-based (this group is associated with output pin)
The file format of the trigger file is:

CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DOTLIB DOTLIB_LIST {a list of memory dotlibs files} MODE_COUNT <specifies the maximum number of modes to be generated. Valid values are either a positive number or `all'. The default value is 4> MODE_CONFIG_FILE <file_path> SIM_STOP_TIME <time> SIM_STEP_SIZE <step_size>

If the dotlib contains arc-based power groups, you must specify MODE_CONFIG_FILE and include the memory_write condition in the memory description file. Otherwise, arc-based power groups will be skipped. The memory description file contains the following information:

- Signals slew : specify slew for signals. These slew values will be used in case rise or fall is to be applied on signal. The slew value specified using this construct takes priority over the slew value saved for the group. The default slew unit in the slews construct is ns. The syntax is: 
 slews {
<signal_1> <slew_value_1>
<signal_2> <slew_value_2>
....
}
- Signals : specify signals' active values irrespective of memory_write . The syntax is: 
 signals {
<sig_1_name> <active_value> 
<sig_2_name> <active_value> 
....
 }
 where <active value> can be H or L
- Memory write information : specify information required to write data to memory through memory_write block. 
 memory_write {
clock <clock_name> <type>, where <type> can be R, F, H or L
data_in <data_bus_name>
address <address bus name>
signal <sig_1 name> <active_value>, where <active value> can be H or L
signal <sig_2 name> <active_value>, where <active value> can be H or L
 ....
}
A sample memory description file is given below:
cell memory1 { memory_write { address A data_in D clock CLK R signal WEN L signal CEN L } slews
Example 1: Dotlib
CURRENT_CHARACTERIZATION_METHOD DOTLIB DOTLIB_LIST {} MODE_COUNT all SIM_STOP_TIME 10ns SIM_STEP_SIZE 100ps
