Viewing Static Power Analysis Results

The calculated static power can be debugged in the context of physical layout using interactive power analysis plots. Voltus allows you to visualize power consumption in the design using the following methods:

- Power Results Viewer
- Power Debugger
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Power database generated during static power calculation
Procedure (Power Results Viewer)
To visualize power results using power results viewer, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Load the power data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_rail_results -power_db staticPowerResults/staticPower.db
  1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots . 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the power analysis results.
  2. Click the DB Setup button. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
  3. You can enter the power database or click Browse next to the Power Database field, and select a location from the Binary DB File browser window.
  4. Click OK .
3. In the Power & Rail Plots form, select the Power radio button and select a power analysis type from the drop-down list. 
 The selected plot will be overlaid on the design. 
 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 
 #Displays the specified plot 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot ip -filter_max 0.0038 \ -filter_min 1.00001e-05 
#Displays the specified plot and generates a report (power_rail_< plot_type >.rpt) in the current working directory report_power_rail_results -plot freq 
 
 #Clear the display 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot none
4. Click the Range Max/Min icon to specify the minimum and maximum value to include in the report/display. Use the scissor icon to further filter out the data within the range specified. “Filter Max” will hide any data point greater than the filtered value, and vice-versa for “Filter Min”.
  - You can select the Auto Apply for Color Scale checkbox to automatically redraw the layout after modifying the range values. If you do not select this checkbox, you can click the Apply button to manually redraw the layout.
  - You can click the Basic / Advanced toggle button to alternate between the color gradient ( Basic ) and color range ( Advanced ) mode. By default, the Basic button and the color gradient based filter are displayed. When you click the Basic button, the button text changes to Advanced , and the form shows the color range based filter. The color gradient based filter allows you to modify the minimum and maximum values, and the color range based filter allows you to modify 8 equal filters between the minimum and maximum values.
5. Select the Result Browser option to display the Results Browser form. This form allows you to check the power distribution of each instance and identify areas of the design that have high current. The Result Browser form has the following two tabs: 
 
 
 Detail - provides details of the individual data points of the plots displayed. By default, the first 1000 data points are displayed. It lists the instances from the highest total instance power to the lowest instance power.
  - Summary - provides summary information about the plot displayed. The data points are divided into buckets based on the range specified. The summary information provides the statistic of each bucket, and displays the power analysis results in the histogram form.
6. Select the Auto Select check box to automatically zoom in to the selected instance, as shown below:
7. Click Close to close the Result Browser form.
8. Experiment with the remaining power plots, as listed in the following table:

data: Total power plots show power distribution of all the instances in the design. It can be used to debug regions of high IRdrop in the design.

Internal power is a component of total power and displayed for all instances in the design. It can be used to debug instances that consume unexpectedly a large total power. The internal power is derived from the.lib file.

Switching power is a component of total power and displayed for all instances in the design. The switching power plot can be used in conjunction with transition density and loading capacitance plots to understand the switching profile for the design (i.e. high activity and loading capacitance for the instance translates to high switching power).
Leakage power is a component of total power and displayed for all instances in the design. The leakage power plot can be used to debug high leakage power instances.
Displays power of clock instances in the design.
Displays instance-based total power density to determine total power consumed by area.
Displays instance-based internal power density to determine internal power consumed by area.
Displays instance-based switching power density to determine switching power consumed by area.
Displays instance-based leakage power density to determine leakage power consumed by area.

The frequency domain plot displays the operating frequency of all instances in the design. The instances operating with multiple clock domains are displayed using the fastest clock frequency for the instance. The power is directly proportional to the frequency, so this plot can be used to debug instances with high power consumption.

Slack for the instance is derived by Common Timing Engine or external Timing Window File. This plot is primarily used to identify time critical instances and can be useful when performing timing aware decap optimization and IRdrop aware timing analysis.

Transition density is the product of frequency and activity. It is plotted for all instances in the design. This plot can be used to debug instances with high power consumption and regions of high activity. Transition density is plotted for the off domains in power-gated design. However, switching power plot correctly displays that no power is calculated for the off domains.
Displays power density map with user-specified grid size.
Displays the worst activity for the output and inout pins of every instance.

Displays toggle rates for all instances. The toggle rate for an instance is calculated as the average of toggle rates of all outputs of the instance. 
 
 This plot allows you to determine the design area that toggles more than the others.

lines: Procedure (Power Debugger)
To visualize power results using power debugger, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI.
2. Load the power data through the command line or through the GUI.
3. Select Power & Rail - Histograms - Power Analysis . 
 The Power Debug form appears that describes how power is distributed between the levels of hierarchy:
4. Double-click on any part of the pie chart to descend the hierarchy. Click the Up Level button to go up one level. 
 The power pie chart provides an easy way to access any part of the design hierarchy and examine the power results. The Power Histogram section of the form displays how power is distributed between the internal, leakage, and switching power for the current level of hierarchy.
5. Click the Histograms button to bring up the various histograms. 
 The Power Details form appears:
6. You can select the various tabs that help visualize how the power is distributed.

data: Shows how power is distributed among sequential, standard cells, IOs, and macros.
Shows power distribution by clock domain
Shows power distribution by power domain
Shows power distribution by power net
Shows power distribution by nets with highest power
Shows power distribution by nets with highest toggle rate
Shows power distribution by nets with highest toggle probability

lines: You can search for net(s) in the Net , Net Toggle , and Net Probability tabs using wildcard entries (*) and filter any net(s) to display in the histogram when debugging static power. This displays 50 nets at a time, starting with the first 50 nets.
The net that you select in the histogram gets highlighted in the table, and similarly, the net that you select in the table gets highlighted in the histogram.
Information about the selected net is displayed across all the three tabs - By Net , Net Toggle , and Net Probability .
This section describes how to set up the static power analysis mode and then run power analysis.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- SPEF
Procedure
To perform static power analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Specify the CPF file for power domain information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Load/Commit CPF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_domain -cpf ../design/super_filter.cpf
3. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read SPEF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -decoupled\ ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
4. Define the power analysis mode through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power & Rail - Set Power Analysis Mode . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name staticPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method static
5. Optional. Define the power of an instance or cell, if you do not specify .lib. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power -reset 
 
 set_power -cell pll -pin VDD 0.5 
 
 set_power -instance u0 -pin VDD 0.5 
 
 
 
 You can also specify power-grid views using the set_power_analysis_mode -power_grid_library parameter, instead of specifying .lib or defining a power value with set_power .
6. Specify the toggle rates on specific instances, nets, and primary inputs, if they are not defined through user attributes, TCF, VCD, or clock constraints. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_switching_activity -reset 
 
 set_switching_activity -input_port rst -activity 0.25 -duty 0.30 set_switching_activity \ 
 
 -pin [ get_pins -of_objects [ get_cells RC_CGIC* -hierarchical] \ 
 
 -filter "@direction == out" ] \ 
 
 -activity 0.1 
 
 propagate_activity 
 
 
 
 You can specify the propagate_activity command to propagate activities in the design and check for activity annotation before running power calculation. This is an optional step because the report_power command will also propagate activities, and then do power calculation based on the propagated activities.
7. You can check whether the activities were applied properly. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 get_activity -port rst 
 
 get_activity -pin [ get_pins -of_objects [ get_cells RC_CGIC* -hierarchical] \ 
 
 -filter "@direction == out" ]
8. You can define default activities for the unassigned nets. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_default_switching_activity -input_activity 0.3 \ 
 
 -period 4.0 -clock_gates_output_ratio 0.5
9. Specify the directory where the power results will be stored. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir staticPowerResults
10. Run power analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 report_power -outfile static.rpt 
 
 #Generate additional report formats 
 
 report_power -instances {u0} -outfile u0.rpt 
 
 report_power -instances {*} -outfile all.rpt 
 
 report_power -instances {ring} -outfile ring.rpt
  - Select Power & Rail - Run Power Analysis to access the Run Power Analysis form that can be used to specify cell/instance power ( set_power ), toggle rates ( set_switching_activity and set_default_switching_activity ), power result directory ( set_power_output_dir ), and then run power analysis ( report_power ).
  - Select Power & Rail - Textual Reports - Power Analysis to access the Report Power form that can be used to generate incremental power reports.
The Voltus console will display power statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The power output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the binary database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -binary_db_name parameter. The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.
This TCL file stores the commands used to set up power analysis.

The name of the power report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. The power report summarizes power consumption, by groups (sequential cells, macros, clocks), power net, and clock domain.
This is the current file for each net. The set_power_analysis_mode -write_static_currents parameter will generate the current files that are required for rail analysis.

lines: You can use the set_dc_sources command to specify the operating voltages of the power/ground nets. This command is used to specify a global voltage or voltages for specific power nets in the design.

- auto_wire_load_selection
- enable_wire_load_model_support
- report_wire_load
- reset_wire_load_mode
- reset_wire_load_model
- reset_wire_load_selection_group
- set_wire_load_mode
- set_wire_load_model
- set_wire_load_selection_group
For more information about these commands, see the Tempus Text Command Reference .
MMMC Mode Default Views
If set to the MMMC mode, it is recommended to specify the view by one of the below commands,
set_power_analysis_mode -analysis_view viewname
or
report_power -view viewname
If you do not, then the default power view is the first setup or hold view depending on the checkType of the set_analysis_mode command.

Power analysis runs on only one view at a time, and therefore, the argument to the -view parameter must specify only one view. If you give multiple view names to the -view parameter of report_power or you do not activate the view using the set_analysis_mode command, a warning message will be displayed.
For multiple views, you need to have multiple runs, one for each view. Also, you need to make the view active in order to do power analysis.
If specified as shown below,
then the power view in this case is the first setup view which is view1 .The default for -checkType is setup . See set_analysis_mode in the Tempus Text Command Reference for details of this command.
If specified as shown in the next example,
then the power view in this case is the first hold view which is view3 .
If multiple command types specify the view, the priority is given as follows (from highest to lowest): report_power , set_power_analysis_mode , set_analysis_mode
For additional MMMC details, see Performing Multi-Mode Multi-Corner Timing Analysis in the Tempus User Guide .

Power Map File for Thermal Analysis
Voltus supports generation of a tile-based power map file to perform accurate thermal analysis. This power map file is later used by PowerDC, the thermal analysis tool for packages and boards.
To generate the power mapping file, you need to specify the following parameters of the report_power command:

- -thermal_power_map_file file_name - generates the power map file with the specified name in the output directory.
- -thermal_power_map_tile {Xint Yint} - defines the tile number in the X and Y direction. By default 10*10 will be used.
- -thermal_leakage_temp {temp1 temp2 temp3 …} - defines the temperature to calculate leakage for the power map file in a simple format.
- -thermal_power_map_format {simple | stack} - generates a tile-based power map file in the simple or stack format to perform accurate thermal analysis. The simple format generates a 2D power map file and the stack format generates a 3D power map file. You must use the stack format to support multiple dies in a package for thermal analysis. The default format is simple .
- -thermal_conductivity_inputs file_name - specifies a file containing the thermal conductivity value for layers. You can use this parameter to include the thermal conductivity value of the following layers in the power map file:
  - Silicon
  - Metal
  - Dielectric
  - Micro Bump (optional for 3DIC design)
  - Fill material in micro bump layer (optional for 3DIC design)
The format of the thermal conductivity input file is:
Simple Format
The format of the power map file is divided into two parts, TRANSIENT POWER MAP and LEAKAGE POWER MAP , as shown below:

data:
Power Unit : mW 
 
 Time Unit : ms

- nx and ny is the tile number in the power map file
- P(0,0) …. is the power value assigned to a tile at a specific coordinate

The temperature value in the LEAKAGE POWER MAP part come from the values defined using the -thermal_leakage_temp parameter. If the -thermal_leakage_temp parameter is not used; the temperature values defined in the timing library is used for the LEAKAGE POWER MAP part. If the LEAKAGE POWER MAP part is included in the power map file, the leakage power is not included in the TRANSIENT POWER MAP part. The power value in the TRANSIENT POWER MAP part is the sum of the internal power and switching power calculated using the temperature defined by the set_power_calc_temperature command.

For the non tri-lib flow , the LEAKAGE POWER MAP is not included, and only the TRANSIENT POWER MAP part is included in the power map file. The power value in the TRANSIENT POWER MAP part is the sum of the internal power, switching power, and leakage power.

  - You can select the Auto Apply for Color Scale checkbox to automatically redraw the layout after modifying the range values. If you do not select this checkbox, you can click the Apply button to manually redraw the layout.
  - You can click the Basic / Advanced toggle button to alternate between the color gradient ( Basic ) and color range ( Advanced ) mode. By default, the Basic button and the color gradient based filter are displayed. When you click the Basic button, the button text changes to Advanced , and the form shows the color range based filter. The color gradient based filter allows you to modify the minimum and maximum values, and the color range based filter allows you to modify 8 equal filters between the minimum and maximum values.
5. Select the Result Browser option to display the Results Browser form. This form allows you to check the power distribution of each instance and identify areas of the design that have high current. The Result Browser form has the following two tabs: 
 
 
 Detail - provides details of the individual data points of the plots displayed. By default, the first 1000 data points are displayed. It lists the instances from the highest total instance power to the lowest instance power.
  - Summary - provides summary information about the plot displayed. The data points are divided into buckets based on the range specified. The summary information provides the statistic of each bucket, and displays the power analysis results in the histogram form.
6. Select the Auto Select check box to automatically zoom in to the selected instance, as shown below:
7. Click Close to close the Result Browser form.
8. Experiment with the remaining power plots, as listed in the following table:

data: Total power plots show power distribution of all the instances in the design. It can be used to debug regions of high IRdrop in the design.

Internal power is a component of total power and displayed for all instances in the design. It can be used to debug instances that consume unexpectedly a large total power. The internal power is derived from the.lib file.

Switching power is a component of total power and displayed for all instances in the design. The switching power plot can be used in conjunction with transition density and loading capacitance plots to understand the switching profile for the design (i.e. high activity and loading capacitance for the instance translates to high switching power).
Leakage power is a component of total power and displayed for all instances in the design. The leakage power plot can be used to debug high leakage power instances.
Displays power of clock instances in the design.
Displays instance-based total power density to determine total power consumed by area.
Displays instance-based internal power density to determine internal power consumed by area.
Displays instance-based switching power density to determine switching power consumed by area.
Displays instance-based leakage power density to determine leakage power consumed by area.

The frequency domain plot displays the operating frequency of all instances in the design. The instances operating with multiple clock domains are displayed using the fastest clock frequency for the instance. The power is directly proportional to the frequency, so this plot can be used to debug instances with high power consumption.

Slack for the instance is derived by Common Timing Engine or external Timing Window File. This plot is primarily used to identify time critical instances and can be useful when performing timing aware decap optimization and IRdrop aware timing analysis.

Transition density is the product of frequency and activity. It is plotted for all instances in the design. This plot can be used to debug instances with high power consumption and regions of high activity. Transition density is plotted for the off domains in power-gated design. However, switching power plot correctly displays that no power is calculated for the off domains.
Displays power density map with user-specified grid size.
Displays the worst activity for the output and inout pins of every instance.

Displays toggle rates for all instances. The toggle rate for an instance is calculated as the average of toggle rates of all outputs of the instance. 
 
 This plot allows you to determine the design area that toggles more than the others.

title: Power Estimation of a Pre-CTS Netlist

lines: You can run the set_virtual_clock_network_parameters command to analyze and provide fast power estimation of the clock tree power of a pre-CTS netlist. When this command is specified, Voltus constructs a virtual clock tree and estimates power of the clock network. You can estimate power selectively for specific clocks in the design.

Power Map File for Thermal Analysis
Voltus supports generation of a tile-based power map file to perform accurate thermal analysis. This power map file is later used by PowerDC, the thermal analysis tool for packages and boards.
To generate the power mapping file, you need to specify the following parameters of the report_power command:

- -thermal_power_map_file file_name - generates the power map file with the specified name in the output directory.
- -thermal_power_map_tile {Xint Yint} - defines the tile number in the X and Y direction. By default 10*10 will be used.
- -thermal_leakage_temp {temp1 temp2 temp3 …} - defines the temperature to calculate leakage for the power map file in a simple format.
- -thermal_power_map_format {simple | stack} - generates a tile-based power map file in the simple or stack format to perform accurate thermal analysis. The simple format generates a 2D power map file and the stack format generates a 3D power map file. You must use the stack format to support multiple dies in a package for thermal analysis. The default format is simple .
- -thermal_conductivity_inputs file_name - specifies a file containing the thermal conductivity value for layers. You can use this parameter to include the thermal conductivity value of the following layers in the power map file:
  - Silicon
  - Metal
  - Dielectric
  - Micro Bump (optional for 3DIC design)
  - Fill material in micro bump layer (optional for 3DIC design)
The format of the thermal conductivity input file is:
Simple Format
The format of the power map file is divided into two parts, TRANSIENT POWER MAP and LEAKAGE POWER MAP , as shown below:

data:
Power Unit : mW 
 
 Time Unit : ms

lines: where,

- nx and ny is the tile number in the power map file
- P(0,0) …. is the power value assigned to a tile at a specific coordinate
The following diagram illustrates the location of each tile:

The power value of each tile is the total power of instances located in the tile. If one instance overlaps with more than one tile, its power is distributed to each of the tile according to the area overlaps. The package thermal analysis tool uses tile names in the power map file to get the coordinates of each tile.

For the tri-lib flow , Voltus writes out the LEAKAGE POWER MAP part to the power map file to include the temperature dependent leakage power of each tile. The temperature value in the LEAKAGE POWER MAP part come from the values defined using the -thermal_leakage_temp parameter. If the -thermal_leakage_temp parameter is not used; the temperature values defined in the timing library is used for the LEAKAGE POWER MAP part. If the LEAKAGE POWER MAP part is included in the power map file, the leakage power is not included in the TRANSIENT POWER MAP part. The power value in the TRANSIENT POWER MAP part is the sum of the internal power and switching power calculated using the temperature defined by the set_power_calc_temperature command.

For the non tri-lib flow , the LEAKAGE POWER MAP is not included, and only the TRANSIENT POWER MAP part is included in the power map file. The power value in the TRANSIENT POWER MAP part is the sum of the internal power, switching power, and leakage power.
Stack Format
The stack format of the power map file is divided into two parts, DIE STACK POWER MAP and LEAKAGE POWER MAP , as shown below:

data:
Length Unit : um 
 
 Power Unit : mW 
 
 Thermal_conductivity Unit : ((mW/(um * C))) 
 
 Time Unit : ms
xmin(um) ymin(um) xmax(um) ymax(um) nx(int) ny(int)

layer_name0(string) layer_thickness0(um) 
 
 metal_thermal_conductivity(mW/(um * C)) silicon_thermal_conductivity(mW/(um * C)) 
 
 XCOOR x0(0) x0(1) x0(2)... ...x0(nx-1) YCOOR y0(0) y0(1) y0(2)... ...y0(ny-1)

lines: where,

- DIE_NAME is the name is the die instance
- DIE_AREA specifies the coordinates of the die area
- NUMBER_OF_LAYERS is the number of layers in each die instance
- LAYER specifies the LEF layer name, layer thickness, and coordinates of each layer
- BEGIN_POWER_SEQUENCE section reports the transient power for all tiles at each time step.
- nx and ny is the tile number in the power map file. This is specified using the -thermal_power_map_tile {Xint Yint} parameter to divide the power map in tiles.
- P(0,0) …. is the power value assigned to a tile at a specific coordinate
- P(x,y) is total power value of all the instances falling in this tile.
- the thermal conductivity value for layers are included in the power map file as:
  - metal_thermal_conductivity(mW/(um * C))
  - silicon_thermal_conductivity(mW/(um * C))
  - bump_thermal_conductivity(mW/(um * C))
  - fill_material_thermal_conductivity(mW/(um * C))
  - dielectric_thermal_conductivity(mW/(um * C)
The following diagram illustrates that the die is always facing up, and also shows the location of each tile:

- If the stack format is chosen, and no power grid library is provided using the set_power_analysis_mode -power_grid_library parameter, only one layer called SUBSTRATE will be generated in the power map file with distributed power information. As there is no thickness information, NULL will be used for thickness of SUBSTRATE layer for you to manually edit it in PowerDC.
- If the stack format is chosen, and power grid library is provided using the set_power_analysis_mode -power_grid_library parameter, the thickness of each layer is obtained from the QRC technology file stored in the techonly power grid library. For most of the die with no definition for the substrate thickness, NULL will be the thickness of the SUBSTRATE layer for you to manually edit it in PowerDC. If there is thickness definition in the QRC technology file, write the value defined in the QRC technology file to the power map file.

Voltus Thermal Model Generation for Thermal Analysis

Voltus supports generation of a thermal model that divides the design into equal sized tiles, and includes data on the power and thermal material properties of each tile. The flow uses the specify_def command instead of the read_def command, therefore allows you to handle very large designs without loading design data into memory.
The flow is illustrated in the following diagram:
The flow has the following main steps:

1. Extracts metal density information using the set_metal_density_options and extract_metal_density commands. This step generates the Voltus Thermal Model (VTM) metal density file for Celsius Thermal Solver (thermal analysis tool) without performing power calculation.
2. Generates the power database using the set_power_analysis_mode -save_bbox parameter and report_power command. This command saves the power data with the instance boundary box information.
3. Combines the metal density file and power database using the create_thermal_model command for VTM generation. 
 Optionally, you can use the merge_vtm command to import the top-level and block-level VTMs to generate a merged uniform single VTM.
TCL Script for VTM Generation
The TCL script for running the VTM flow are as follows:
Viewing Static Power Analysis Results

The calculated static power can be debugged in the context of physical layout using interactive power analysis plots. Voltus allows you to visualize power consumption in the design using the following methods:

- Power Results Viewer
- Power Debugger
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Power database generated during static power calculation
Procedure (Power Results Viewer)
To visualize power results using power results viewer, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Load the power data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_rail_results -power_db staticPowerResults/staticPower.db
  1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots . 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the power analysis results.
  2. Click the DB Setup button. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
  3. You can enter the power database or click Browse next to the Power Database field, and select a location from the Binary DB File browser window.
  4. Click OK .
3. In the Power & Rail Plots form, select the Power radio button and select a power analysis type from the drop-down list. 
 The selected plot will be overlaid on the design. 
 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 
 #Displays the specified plot 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot ip -filter_max 0.0038 \ -filter_min 1.00001e-05 
#Displays the specified plot and generates a report (power_rail_< plot_type >.rpt) in the current working directory report_power_rail_results -plot freq 
 
 #Clear the display 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot none
4. Click the Range Max/Min icon to specify the minimum and maximum value to include in the report/display. Use the scissor icon to further filter out the data within the range specified. “Filter Max” will hide any data point greater than the filtered value, and vice-versa for “Filter Min”.
  - You can select the Auto Apply for Color Scale checkbox to automatically redraw the layout after modifying the range values. If you do not select this checkbox, you can click the Apply button to manually redraw the layout.
  - You can click the Basic / Advanced toggle button to alternate between the color gradient ( Basic ) and color range ( Advanced ) mode. By default, the Basic button and the color gradient based filter are displayed. When you click the Basic button, the button text changes to Advanced , and the form shows the color range based filter. The color gradient based filter allows you to modify the minimum and maximum values, and the color range based filter allows you to modify 8 equal filters between the minimum and maximum values.
5. Select the Result Browser option to display the Results Browser form. This form allows you to check the power distribution of each instance and identify areas of the design that have high current. The Result Browser form has the following two tabs: 
 
 
 Detail - provides details of the individual data points of the plots displayed. By default, the first 1000 data points are displayed. It lists the instances from the highest total instance power to the lowest instance power.
  - Summary - provides summary information about the plot displayed. The data points are divided into buckets based on the range specified. The summary information provides the statistic of each bucket, and displays the power analysis results in the histogram form.
6. Select the Auto Select check box to automatically zoom in to the selected instance, as shown below:
7. Click Close to close the Result Browser form.
8. Experiment with the remaining power plots, as listed in the following table:

Internal power is a component of total power and displayed for all instances in the design. It can be used to debug instances that consume unexpectedly a large total power. The internal power is derived from the.lib file.

Switching power is a component of total power and displayed for all instances in the design. The switching power plot can be used in conjunction with transition density and loading capacitance plots to understand the switching profile for the design (i.e. high activity and loading capacitance for the instance translates to high switching power).
Leakage power is a component of total power and displayed for all instances in the design. The leakage power plot can be used to debug high leakage power instances.
Displays power of clock instances in the design.
Displays instance-based total power density to determine total power consumed by area.
Displays instance-based internal power density to determine internal power consumed by area.
Displays instance-based switching power density to determine switching power consumed by area.
Displays instance-based leakage power density to determine leakage power consumed by area.

The frequency domain plot displays the operating frequency of all instances in the design. The instances operating with multiple clock domains are displayed using the fastest clock frequency for the instance. The power is directly proportional to the frequency, so this plot can be used to debug instances with high power consumption.

Slack for the instance is derived by Common Timing Engine or external Timing Window File. This plot is primarily used to identify time critical instances and can be useful when performing timing aware decap optimization and IRdrop aware timing analysis.

Transition density is the product of frequency and activity. It is plotted for all instances in the design. This plot can be used to debug instances with high power consumption and regions of high activity. Transition density is plotted for the off domains in power-gated design. However, switching power plot correctly displays that no power is calculated for the off domains.
Displays power density map with user-specified grid size.
Displays the worst activity for the output and inout pins of every instance.

Displays toggle rates for all instances. The toggle rate for an instance is calculated as the average of toggle rates of all outputs of the instance. 
 
 This plot allows you to determine the design area that toggles more than the others.

title: Voltus Thermal Model Generation for Thermal Analysis

lines: Voltus supports generation of a thermal model that divides the design into equal sized tiles, and includes data on the power and thermal material properties of each tile. The flow uses the specify_def command instead of the read_def command, therefore allows you to handle very large designs without loading design data into memory.
The flow is illustrated in the following diagram:
The flow has the following main steps:

1. Extracts metal density information using the set_metal_density_options and extract_metal_density commands. This step generates the Voltus Thermal Model (VTM) metal density file for Celsius Thermal Solver (thermal analysis tool) without performing power calculation.
2. Generates the power database using the set_power_analysis_mode -save_bbox parameter and report_power command. This command saves the power data with the instance boundary box information.
3. Combines the metal density file and power database using the create_thermal_model command for VTM generation. 
 Optionally, you can use the merge_vtm command to import the top-level and block-level VTMs to generate a merged uniform single VTM.
TCL Script for VTM Generation
The TCL script for running the VTM flow are as follows:
Viewing Static Power Analysis Results

The calculated static power can be debugged in the context of physical layout using interactive power analysis plots. Voltus allows you to visualize power consumption in the design using the following methods:

- Power Results Viewer
- Power Debugger
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Power database generated during static power calculation
Procedure (Power Results Viewer)
To visualize power results using power results viewer, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Load the power data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_rail_results -power_db staticPowerResults/staticPower.db
  1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots . 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the power analysis results.
  2. Click the DB Setup button. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
  3. You can enter the power database or click Browse next to the Power Database field, and select a location from the Binary DB File browser window.
  4. Click OK .
3. In the Power & Rail Plots form, select the Power radio button and select a power analysis type from the drop-down list. 
 The selected plot will be overlaid on the design. 
 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 
 #Displays the specified plot 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot ip -filter_max 0.0038 \ -filter_min 1.00001e-05 
#Displays the specified plot and generates a report (power_rail_< plot_type >.rpt) in the current working directory report_power_rail_results -plot freq 
 
 #Clear the display 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot none
4. Click the Range Max/Min icon to specify the minimum and maximum value to include in the report/display. Use the scissor icon to further filter out the data within the range specified. “Filter Max” will hide any data point greater than the filtered value, and vice-versa for “Filter Min”.
  - You can select the Auto Apply for Color Scale checkbox to automatically redraw the layout after modifying the range values. If you do not select this checkbox, you can click the Apply button to manually redraw the layout.
  - You can click the Basic / Advanced toggle button to alternate between the color gradient ( Basic ) and color range ( Advanced ) mode. By default, the Basic button and the color gradient based filter are displayed. When you click the Basic button, the button text changes to Advanced , and the form shows the color range based filter. The color gradient based filter allows you to modify the minimum and maximum values, and the color range based filter allows you to modify 8 equal filters between the minimum and maximum values.
5. Select the Result Browser option to display the Results Browser form. This form allows you to check the power distribution of each instance and identify areas of the design that have high current. The Result Browser form has the following two tabs: 
 
 
 Detail - provides details of the individual data points of the plots displayed. By default, the first 1000 data points are displayed. It lists the instances from the highest total instance power to the lowest instance power.
  - Summary - provides summary information about the plot displayed. The data points are divided into buckets based on the range specified. The summary information provides the statistic of each bucket, and displays the power analysis results in the histogram form.
6. Select the Auto Select check box to automatically zoom in to the selected instance, as shown below:
7. Click Close to close the Result Browser form.
8. Experiment with the remaining power plots, as listed in the following table:

The power data can be queried interactively in GUI by selecting an instance and pressing "q" on keyboard. The Attribute Viewer displays total, internal, switching and leakage power for the instance. In addition, it lists frequency domain, transition density and associated power domains of the instance.

You can also query the power data using the get_metric Tcl command that allows to query predefined metrics directly after the report_power command is run, without any need for log parsing. The report_power command generates the following metrics:

data:
power.total 11.8963 
 
 Total power consumption is 11.8963 mW.

power.total.leakage 0.0411615 
 
 Total leakage power is .0411615 mW.

power.total.internal 11.4083 
 
 Total internal power consumption is 11.4083 mW.

power.total.switching 0.446901 
 
 Total switching power is .446901 mW.
Returns total power consumed by clocks

power.total.clock 0.0272253 
 
 Total power consumed by clocks is .0272253 mW.

lines: The following set of commands returns metrics for the report_power command:
The final output is as follows:
Debugging Instance Power

The calculated instance power can be debugged using the report_instance_power command. This command generates a text based report for the specified instance. The command enables you to determine how the different components of static power are calculated. You can use the report to debug after performing power analysis ( report_power ).

This command is available through both the static and dynamic power engines. This command is supported in the dynamic power engine by including this command in the power include file through the command set_power_include_file .
The format of the report is given below:
#This section gives a breakup of the total Internal, Switching, and Leakage power of the instance:
#This section shows the calculation of switching power of each net associated with the instance.
#It includes:

data: power for the output pin (calculated using 1/2CV2*TD)

lines: #This section shows the calculation of Leakage power for state dependent leakage. For different states, the power value is multiplied with the duty cycle. Finally, all the state dependent power values are added to get the Total Leakage power reported in the first section.
#This section shows how the internal power is calculated for each arc.

For example, the first row signifies to calculate power for 1st arc when pin A is rising and Y is rising and B is high. Activity from either the activity file or calculated by power engine is 0.0390625/1e-09 . Energy number from the .lib is 3.84665e-15 . Power for the arc is calculated as activity * energy 0.0390625/1e-09*3.84665e-15 = 0.00015026mw . Similarly, internal power is calculated for each arc.
The total internal power is calculated as shown below:
step 1) calculate power: A -> Y
step 2) calculate power: B -> Y
then, output power at Y is:
See (#1)
Saving and Restoring Power Database

You can save power databases for incremental power report generation (including TCF output), view power histograms and graphical power plots, without having to re-run power analysis during both the static and dynamic runs. This is applicable for flat or hierarchical designs.
TCL Script for Generating Power Database
# Use specify_def for better performance
#You can choose the method to be dynamic vector-based or static
#The power database will not be generated if set_power_analysis_mode -disable_static is set to true when running either of the dynamic power analyses
#Power database name is optional. The default name is power.db
TCL Script for Restoring Power Database
# You will be able to view the power histograms once the power database has been restored

# Incremental report generation can be done without re-doing power analysis. The power db can be restored in the same session also. It is important that you should not change any power mode settings because that will re-trigger power analysis.
Generating Standalone Power Reports

Voltus supports incremental and interactive standalone report generation from an existing binary power database stored in the power output directory. This feature allows you to quickly and periodically generate power reports for specific cells/instances from a previous power analysis run.
The use model for standalone power report generation is:
report_power -power_db_directory < database_dir > -outfile < report_name >
The following parameters of the set_power_analysis_mode command are required to perform standalone report generation in the Extensively Parallel (XP) mode:

- -enable_xp {true | false}
- -extraction_tech_file filename

The power database is located in the power output directory (for example, dynamic_power/power.db ) for single-machine multi-threading (local) mode. In case of the distributed and XP modes, the power database is located in the distributed host/partition sub-directory within the power output directory (for example, dynamic_power/dist_X/power.db or dynamic_power/part_X/power.db ).
TCL Script for Generating Standalone Power Reports in the XP Mode

TCL Script for Generating Standalone Power Reports in the XP Mode

The Effective Resistance Analysis feature allows you to perform effective resistance calculation for all instances in a design in both the net-based and domain-based analysis modes. When you perform domain-based resistance analysis, Voltus gives the sum effective resistance values (Rvdd + Rvss) for all nets in the domain.

You can use the analyze_resistance command to perform effective resistance analysis. Use the analyze_resistance -net parameter for net-based analysis, and analyze_resistance -domain parameter for domain-based analysis.
The following resistance analysis methods enable you to determine the type of analysis to be performed:

- Full-Chip Analysis - calculates the effective resistance between the specified nodes or instances to all voltage sources/bumps in a design. This type of analysis can be done using one of the followings inputs:
  - specifying a list of instances ( -instance_list / -instance_list_file ) - Reports effective resistance between the specified user-defined instances and all voltage sources in the design. By default, the tool will use the instance pin associated with the net being analyzed. You can also specify a pin when two different pins of an instance are connected to the same net. When an instance has multiple pins, the effective resistance at all pins are computed.
  - specifying a master cell name ( -cell ) - Reports effective resistance between all instances of the specified master cell name and all voltage sources in the design. This method allows you to perform effective resistance calculation for all instances of a particular cell. You can specify multiple cells for performing effective resistance analysis of multiple cells simultaneously.
  - specifying a list of nodes ( -node_list / -node_list_file ) - Reports effective resistance between the specified user-defined nodes and all voltage sources in the design. You can add a list of nodes in a region on a given layer.
- Point-to-Point Analysis - calculate the effective resistance between the selected node pairs or instance pairs. The point-to-point resistance analysis flow ignores all voltage sources. This type of analysis can be done using one of the following inputs:
  - specifying instance pairs ( -instance_pair_list / -instance_pair_list_file ) - Reports effective resistance between two instances in a design. You can specify multiple instance pairs at a time. 
 If the pin name of an instance is specified, the tool will short all nodes only of the specified pin. If an instance pair is specified without their pin names, all nodes of all pins of the instance pair are shorted.
  - specifying node pairs ( -node_pair_list / -node_pair_list_file ) - Reports effective resistance between two nodes on the power-grid. You can specify multiple node pairs at a time.

The analyze_resistance command can be run without performing power calculation and IR drop analysis. The resistance analysis flow requires you to load the design, set the rail analysis mode, set power nets, set power pads, and run resistance analysis.
Setting up and Running Resistance Analysis
This section describes how to set up and run the following resistance analysis flows:

- domain-based resistance analysis
- net-based node to pad resistance analysis
- net-based node to node resistance analysis
Required Input Files
- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
Procedure - Domain-based Resistance Analysis
To perform domain-based resistance analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Define the rail analysis mode through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power & Rail - Set Rail Analysis Mode . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method static \ 
 
 -accuracy xd \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } \ 
 
 -verbosity true \ 
 
 -temperature 125
3. Set up domain-based effective resistance analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power & Rail - Run Resistance Analysis . 
 Tcl command: 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -reset 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy \ -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_external -format xy \ 
 
 -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_external.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_AO -format xy \ 
 
 -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_AO.pp 
 
 analyze_resistance -domain ALL -output_dir Reff_domain
  1. Select the Domain Based option.
  2. Select a domain name from the Domain Name drop-down list. Domain Name is populated automatically if using CPF.
  3. Specify the power and ground nets that are in the domain for which resistance analysis will be performed on, in the Power Net(s) and Ground Net(s) fields. For each net, specify the voltage and threshold values in the Voltage(s) and Threshold fields.
  4. Select the method to generate voltage sources by selecting Power Pads and XY File . 
 You can use any one of the following methods to generate voltage sources: Def Pins , Pad File , XY File , Boundary File . You can click the Create button to fetch all voltage sources of a power or ground net and place it in a text file.
  5. Click the File Name browse button to specify the power pad file containing the voltage source information.
  6. Specify the name of the power net for which the power pad file has been added in the Net Name field, and click the Add button to add the net to the list. The Remove button removes the net from the list. 
 You must specify power pad information for every PG net that is a part of the domain.
  7. Specify the directory name where the resistance analysis results will be stored in the Output Directory field.
  8. Select the Effr to Power Pads option.
  9. Click OK . 
 The filled up form appears as shown below:
Procedure - Net-based Node to Power Pad Resistance Analysis (Node to Voltage Source)
To perform net-based resistance analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Set up the net-based effective resistance analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power & Rail - Run Resistance Analysis .
  1. Select the Net Based option.
  2. Select a net name from the Net Based drop-down list.
  3. Specify the power or ground net for which resistance analysis will be performed on, in the Power Net(s) or Ground Net(s) field. For the net, specify the voltage and threshold values in the Voltage(s) and Threshold fields.
  4. Select the method to generate voltage sources by selecting Power Pads and XY File. 
 You can use any one of the following methods to generate voltage sources: Def Pins , Pad File , XY File , Boundary File . You can click the Create button to fetch all voltage sources of a power or ground net and place it in a text file.
  5. Click the File Name browse button to specify the power pad file containing the voltage source information.
  6. Specify the name of the power net for which the power pad file has been added in the Net Name field, and click the Add button.
  7. Specify the directory name where the resistance analysis results will be stored in the Output Directory field.
  8. Select the Effr to Power Pads option.
  9. Add the list of nodes using the following fields:
    - In the Node1 section, click the Get Coordinate button and select a region from the Layout tab to get the x y coordinates of any node in the design. 
 The selected nodes will be displayed in the x1 and y1 fields.
    - Select the layer name from the Layer1 field.
    - Specify a node name in the Label field.
    - Click the Add button to add the node to the node list.
    - Continue adding nodes to calculate effective resistance between the specified user-defined nodes and all voltage sources.
2. Click OK . 
 The filled up form appears as shown below: 
 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0 -threshold 0.5 
 
 set_power_pads -reset 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy \ 
 
 -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp 
 
 analyze_resistance -net VSS -output_dir Reff_VSS_node \ 
 
 -node_list {{ 15.968 287.7695 Metal1 n1 } \ 
 
 { 137.117 214.088 Metal5 n2 } \ 
 
 { 99.568 182.207 Metal5 n3 }}
Procedure - Net-based Node to Node Resistance Analysis
To perform net-based node to node resistance analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Repeat the steps described in the " Procedure - Net-based Node to Power Pad Resistance Analysis (Node to Voltage Source) " section to specify the net details, power pad file, and output directory.
2. Select the Point-To-Point Effr option.
3. In the Node1 section, click the Get Coordinate button and select a region from the Layout tab to get the x y coordinates of the first node. 
 The selected nodes will be displayed in the x1 and y1 fields.
4. In the Node2 section, click the Get Coordinate button and select a region from the Layout tab to get the x y coordinates of the second node. 
 The selected nodes will be displayed in the x2 and y2 fields.
5. Select the layer name from the Layer1 and Layer2 fields.
6. Specify a node name in the Label field.
7. Click the Add button to add the two nodes.
8. Click OK . 
 The filled up form appears as shown below: 
 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0 -threshold 0.5 
 
 set_power_pads -reset 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy \ 
 
 -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp 
 
 analyze_resistance -net VSS -output_dir Reff_VSS_node_to_node \ 
 
 -node_pair_list {{ 75.4795 318.9425 Metal1 61.31 272.183 Metal4 n1 }}
You can perform effective resistance calculation at the node or instance level using the following fields:

- for a list of nodes using Node1 field or Node File .
- for a list of instances using Instance1 field or Instance File .
- for node pairs between which effective resistance needs to be calculated using Node1 and Node2 , or Node-Pair File .
- for instance pairs between which effective resistance needs to be calculated using Instance1 and Instance2 , or Instance-Pair File .
Output
The output directory will contain the following information:

data:
effr.rpt for net-based analysis and domain_effr.rpt for domain-based analysis. 
 
 The file reports the total effective resistance values (Rvdd + Rvss) for all the nets in the domain, and the percentage of each net. These files are located in the state directory under the specified resistance analysis directory. For example, the report file for all nets in the domain is located at: 
 
 domain-based report: 
 Reff_domain/ALL_25C_reff_1/domain_effr.rpt 
 
 You can also view the effective resistance reports for each net. For example, the report file for VSS is located at: 
 
 net-based report: 
 Reff_domain/ALL_25C_reff_1/VSS/REFF/effr.rpt
This file is generated only for net-based analysis.

Viewing Resistance Analysis Results
To view the resistance analysis results:

1. Open the Power & Rail Setup form, accessed by clicking the DB Setup button on the Power & Rail Plots form.
2. Load the rail database and effective resistance report ( effr.rpt ) by clicking the Browse button next to the Rail Database and Effective Res fields.
3. Click OK .
4. Plot the results using the rail analysis plot type effr ( set_power_rail_display –plot effr ). 
 The following is a snapshot of an effective resistance plot: 
 
 
 You can analyze the effr and node_reff (node-based effective resistance) plots in the GUI by specifying the effr.rpt for each net in the Power & Rail Setup form.
