The following are example TCL commands for running different types of resistance analysis:

- The following command performs effective resistance calculation for the list of nodes and instances: 
 analyze _resistance -net VSS -node_list { 231 1061.6 M1 } -instance_list_file instance.list
- The following command specifying a list of user-defined nodes and a node list file for effective resistance calculation: 
 analyze _resistance -net VDD -node_list {{ 213.556 133.469 M1 I123 }} -node_list_file /../node_list.txt.1 -output reff_rpt
- The following is an example of specifying a list of instances for effective resistance calculation: 
 analyze _resistance -instance_list {{INV11 VDD} {INV21 VDD}} -net VDD
- The following is an example of specifying a list of user-defined nodes for effective resistance calculation: 
 analyze _resistance -net VDD -node_list {{800 11.88 M4} {999.768 11.88 M4} {1000.296 11.880 M4}} -output out.txt
- The following is an example of specifying node pair list for effective resistance calculation: 
 analyze _resistance -net VDD -node_pair_list {195.728 133.369 M1 213.556 133.469 M1} -output out_nodelist.txt
- The following is an example of specifying instance pair list for effective resistance calculation: 
 analyze _resistance -net GND -instance_pair_list {inst1 inst2 GND GND} -output outfile -threshold 100
- The following command performs effective resistance calculation for full-chip analysis: 
 analyze _resistance -threshold 13.0 -domain_threshold 25.0 -domain ALL
- The following command performs effective resistance calculation for instance list analysis: 
 analyze _resistance -domain ALL -threshold 13.0 -domain_threshold 25.0 -instance_list_file inst.list
- The following command reports reff for all instances of the cell type CKBD8: 
 analyze _resistance -net VDD -cell CKBD8
Static IRDrop and EM Analysis Overview
IR Drop and Ground Bounce
IR drop and ground bounce refer to the variations in voltage caused by current flowing through a resistive power network.
IR Drop
IR drop is a reduction in voltage that occurs on power supply networks (VDD) in integrated circuits.

Integrated-circuit design usually assumes the availability of an ideal power supply that can instantly deliver any amount of current to maintain the specified voltage throughout the chip. In reality, however, the combination of increasing current per-unit area on the die and narrower metal line widths (which causes an increase in the power-grid resistance) causes localized voltage drops within the power grid, leading to decreased power supply voltage at cells and transistors.
These localized drops in the power supply voltage decrease the operating voltage of the chip, potentially causing timing problems and functional failures.
Ground Bounce
Ground bounce is an increase in voltage that occurs on ground networks (VSS or GND) in integrated circuits.
The current that is sourced onto the ground network combined with a finite resistance of the ground network leads to localized increases in the ground voltages around the chip.
As with IR drop, these increases in the ground voltage also decrease the operating voltage of the chip, resulting in the same potential timing problems and functional failures.
Concepts in IR Drop and Ground Bounce

The following figure illustrates a power-grid network that helps to explain the concept of IR drop and ground bounce. It shows a chip power supply connected to the chip pads. The power-grid network is illustrated by the R11-R14 resistors for VDD and R21-R24 resistors for VSS. These resistors represent the resistance of the interconnect from the pads to the cells or transistors. G1-G4 are cells connected to VDD and VSS.
Example Power-Grid Network

When you perform transistor- or gate-level simulation, the voltages (V1- V4) are typically assumed to be equal. It is further assumed that all power-grid resistances are zero ohms and that all chip components receive ideal power supply voltages.

In reality, the power-grid resistances of a chip cannot be ignored. For example, cell G4 never has an ideal VDD voltage at its VDD pin when it is active; it has a lower voltage because the current flowing from the VDD pad to G4 must flow through resistances R11-R14. Ohm's law states that a current, I, flowing through an effective resistance, R, introduces a voltage drop, as given by the equation V = IR.

Similarly, cell G4 never has an ideal VSS at its ground pin, because the current flowing from G4 back to the VSS pad must also travel through the ground network resistances R21-R24. In this case, cell G4 has an increased voltage at its ground pin.

The power-grid network diagram also illustrates the complexity of power grids and IR drop. Assume that gate G4 consumes I4 mA and that no other cell is drawing current. This current flows from the power supply through the resistors of the power grid to G4. The IR drop at cell G4 is given by the following equation:
In addition, cell G2 experiences an IR drop of I4(R11+R12) because of the current drawn by cell G4.
Clearly, the current associated with each cell or transistor in a design can influence the IR drop or ground bounce for all other devices in the design.
Average and Peak Current

If the cells on a single metal VDD line switch together, the instantaneous IR drop can be large. Given simultaneous currents I1-I4 for the G1-G4 cells (as shown in Example Power-Grid Network), the IR drop at G4 would be given by the following equation:
If this peak current occurred over one fourth of the clock cycle, the average IR drop at cell G4 over the entire clock cycle would be one quarter of that value.

The peak IR drop at any location in a chip can therefore be much larger than the IR drop averaged over a clock cycle. As the size of a block increases, the ratio of the peak IR drop to the average decreases towards 1.0, because the probability of many devices switching at once decreases significantly.
Static IRdrop

For 130-nanometer designs and above, static power-rail analysis is still widely used to validate that the basic power network is sufficiently robust. Static analysis accurately highlights IR drop and electromigration problems caused by issues such as open circuits in the power rails, missing vias or via arrays, high current and high current densities, lack of power straps, and insufficient power routing widths. Static analysis is the preferred approach for power electromigration verification because it emulates the average impact of a design operating over a long period of time. It therefore calculates the average voltage drop. It is a good approach to use when high decoupling capacitance is available. As noted earlier, decoupling capacitors are added to mitigate voltage drops in all available locations.

Static analysis is best used early in the flow and validation process to detect and correct major supply grid problems. Cadence recommends that you first perform static analysis to verify that the power rails are sufficiently robust before you perform dynamic analysis. You should immediately address issues with excessive static IR drop until the IR drop is within user-specified limits (each design team will have their own limits, but a typical goal is 2 to 5 percent). You can establish a baseline for IR drop from the static analysis, then use dynamic analysis to show additional transient IR drop. If you add sufficient decoupling capacitance to these areas, the results from dynamic analysis should be close to the results from static analysis because the transient IR drops will be effectively smoothed.
The following diagram shows the static analysis approach:
Static Analysis Approach
Global and Local IR Drop and Ground Bounce
IR drop and ground bounce can be both local and global phenomena.

- They can be local phenomena when a number of cells in close proximity switch simultaneously, causing IR drop or ground bounce in that localized area. A higher power-grid resistance to a specific portion of the chip can also cause localized IR drop or ground bounce.
- They can be global phenomena when activity in one region of a chip causes effects in other regions. For example, block B2 in the following diagram suffers from IR drop because of the current drawn by block B1.
IR Drop as a Global Phenomenon

In an ideal power grid with equally distributed current and peripheral power pads, the power grid has a set of equapotential surfaces that form concentric circles centered in the middle of the chip, as shown in the following diagram:
Equally distributed current resulting in concentric rings of IR Drop or Ground Bounce

The center of these concentric circles has the largest IR drop or ground bounce, and the amount of the effect decreases toward the pads. Discontinuities in these circles typically identify regions containing discontinuities in the power grid or unequally distributed current flow.
Simultaneous Switching

Large amounts of IR drop or ground bounce can be caused by simultaneous switching of cells and transistors. If all cells and transistors of a design switched at once, the local or global IR drop or ground bounce on a chip would be extremely large. Luckily, the logic itself typically prevents this from happening.
Potential causes of simultaneous switching are the following:

- Synchronized clock networks
- Bus drivers
- Memory word line drivers
- Signal I/O circuitry
- Any groups of large drivers designed to switch together
Symptoms of IR Drop and Ground Bounce
Because the number of failures resulting from poor power-grid networks has become significant only recently, many designers do not look at power distribution as a potential source of chip failure.
Symptoms of IR drop and ground bounce problems include the following:

- Non-functional chips 
 If the global IR drop or ground bounce is too high when a chip operates, logic gates malfunction. The failure resembles a logical functional failure or manufacturing problem, although simulation indicates that the design is logically correct. One way of diagnosing this symptom is to increase the power supply voltage and see if the chip works.
- Intermittent or data-dependent functional failures 
 Local power-grid problems are sensitized, or forced to occur, by specific operations in close proximity, such as all bits of a bus switching at once. In normal operation, the specific sensitization might not occur. However, a specific data input activates the problem. The symptom appears as a logic functional failure of that portion of the chip.
- Intermittent or data-dependent timing failures 
 Like intermittent functional failures, specific data inputs can cause IR drop or ground bounce that appears to be a timing failure. An intermittent timing failure of this type is also symptomatic of a signal net cross-coupling capacitance problem. One way of diagnosing this symptom is to decrease the clock frequency and see if the chip works.
- Hard timing failures 
 When the power-grid problems are not high enough to cause complete logic failure, the symptom can be a timing failure of the chip. In this case, the IR drop or ground bounce slows down the speed of the circuitry, causing a hard timing failure.
- Clock signal jitter 
 Experiments have shown that a 5 percent IR drop on a clock buffer can slow down its speed by up to 15 percent. The reduction in speed is due to two mechanisms, as shown in the following diagram:
Impact of IR Drop on speed

First, the IR drop on the power input of inverter1 slows the charging rate of the output. Explained simply, the output voltage of inverter1 rises only up to VDD minus the IR drop. In turn, this drop reduces the logical high input voltage (VIH) to inverter2, reducing its switching speed as well.
Two problems have resulted from the traditional approach to working around potential IR drop problems:

- Timing libraries are constructed on the basis of assumptions about a certain amount of IR drop, such as 10 percent. These assumptions add to the timing margins applied to the design. The added margins are becoming quite significant and impede timing closure. As a result, the final chip timing is increasingly unpredictable. Chips can fail because the IR drop was actually higher, or they can operate much faster than the specified frequency.
- Power grids are heavily over designed. These grids remove a significant amount of chip area from use for signal routing and may result in larger chips. In addition, designers frequently over design the power grid but by an unknown amount.
Manufacturing Effects in Power-grid Extraction

The extractor embedded in Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution can include the effects of manufacturing on resistance, capacitance, or both in the extraction results. In copper processes and processes at or below 130 nanometers, the manufacturing steps of resist, lithography, etching, and chemical mechanical polishing (CMP) result in differences between the layout drawn by designers and the manufactured design. Earlier processes exhibited minor effective changes in a wire's characteristics, such as a change of wire width resulting from a trapezoidal wire shape. Copper processes also exhibit a trapezoidal effect, although more pronounced. In addition, copper processes also exhibit more significant variations in wire width and thickness with respect to the drawn layout.

Accurate prediction of circuit behavior in deep submicron silicon requires the modeling of these manufacturing effects. In the past, it was difficult to obtain measurements and specifications of these manufacturing effects, so they were generally represented by process variations. As consistent sources of these variations have been identified, extraction and analysis tools have been developed that include not only accuracy, capacity, and performance, but models for these deep submicron effects. With each new process generation, the number of such models is expected to increase.
The manufacturing effects on a wire that cause variation from the drawn wire are wire-edge enlargement, resistivity as a function of wire width and erosion.

- Wire-edge enlargement refers to the effective change to a wire's width as a function of its width and spacing to its neighbors, because of its optical proximity to neighboring wires.
- Resistivity as a function of a wire's width refers to the effective variations in a wire's resistivity because of dishing, slotting, and cladding thickness.
- Erosion refers to the change in a wire's thickness as a function of metal density in a portion of a chip. The Fire & Ice ZX extractor computes the manufactured dimensions of a wire (width and thickness) and applies parasitic models to the resulting wire.
These effects are described in detail in the following sections.
Wire-Edge Enlargement

Before manufacturing, reticle enhancement technologies (RET), such as optical proximity correction (OPC) and phase shift masks (PSM), are employed to help create the correct dimensions on silicon from the drawn layout. Even with the use of these advanced techniques, the manufacturing process contains optical effects that result in systematic wire width variations that must be accounted for in nanometer designs.

Basically, the coherent light source that is used to expose a mask onto the wafer creates diffraction patterns around the edges of the mask geometries. These patterns cause a variation in the exposure, which eventually results in a variation of the spacing, and therefore the width, of the wires.

Foundries usually focus the lithographic equipment to allow an increase in the width of wires only to avoid a reduction in wire width that may cause fatal open circuits. The size of the width increase is influenced by the width of the wire in question, its proximity to neighboring wires, and the size and spacing of the neighboring wires. The following diagram illustrates the results of this wire-edge enlargement:
Wire Edge Enlargement
The spacing and width of the neighboring wire may enlarge each wire edge by a different amount.
Resistivity as a Function of Width (R s = f(W s ) )

Variation in resistivity is caused by a number of phenomena. Copper is a softer material than the dielectric in which it is embedded. As a result, the polishing of the wafer during the CMP process has a tendency to remove a little extra copper from the top of the wire. This effect is called dishing because of the shape of the resulting wire top. The effect becomes more pronounced as the wire width increases. This effect is shown in exaggerated form in (A) in the following figure:

To reduce the effect of dishing on wide wires, small holes, or slots, can be inserted at regular intervals in wide wires. These slots insert a form of hard "posts" in the wire so that the CMP process removes less copper. This technique reduces the dishing, as well as the effective resistivity.

Another contributor to resistivity variation is the cladding in copper wires. Cladding is the material grown around the sides and bottom of copper wires to protect them from chemical reactions with the dielectric material. This cladding is illustrated in gray in the above diagram. The thickness of the cladding on the sides and bottoms of wires also varies with the width of a wire. Because cladding has a much higher resistance than copper, it impacts the effective resistivity of copper wires. This effect is more pronounced in the narrowest wires.

The combination of the effects of dishing, slotting, and cladding thickness is modeled by the wire resistivity as a function of the wire width in silicon. The drawn width of a wire is not representative of the silicon width of a wire after wire-edge enlargement effects are considered.
Erosion

Erosion is the wearing away of the top of the metal by the CMP process. Dishing is limited to a single wire, but erosion impacts a large area of the wafer. It is caused by the varying density of metal across the die or wafer.

Variations in metal density cause the CMP process to over-polish areas of high metal density compared to areas of low metal density, because the amount of harder dielectric varies with the metal density. High metal density is associated with low dielectric density, and vice-versa, and this affects the general hardness of an area. The impact of metal density on erosion is illustrated in an exaggerated form in Dishing with Erosion and Without Erosion . (A) shows dishing without erosion, and (B) shows dishing with erosion.
The impact of erosion on a design can be quite significant. Measurements have shown that high metal-density areas can suffer up to a 30 percent reduction in metal thickness.

Dishing with Erosion and Without Erosion also illustrates an important difference between dishing and erosion. Erosion has a greater impact on capacitance values than dishing. The sides of a wire with dishing effects are of the desired thickness. But erosion affects the thickness of the wire edges, causing a decrease in lateral coupling capacitance.

In order to minimize erosion, most manufacturers have established metal density rules that force a more consistent density of metal (and therefore of dielectric) throughout a design. Although these rules help to minimize the impact of erosion, extraction tools must model erosion for nanometer designs.
Metal Fill

As noted previously, the thickness of wires after CMP varies as a function of the density of metal in an area. In the ideal case, all layers of the entire chip would have a uniform density of about 50 percent (effectively, minimum width and spaced wires), but this is not possible in practice. Extreme ranges of density may have highly non-linear wire thickness. Therefore, thickness variation is managed to some extent through design rules that require all areas of a chip to have densities greater than a specified amount, such as 20 percent, and less than another amount, such as 80 percent. It is common for portions of a design on a specific layer to have a very low density of metal in wires. To increase the overall density of metal in these areas, pieces of metal are inserted in the layout that have no logical function in the circuit but serve only to increase the density of metal. These pieces of metal are called metal fill. The use of metal fill can vary in terms of the size, shape, spacing of the pieces, and whether they are connected to electrical nets in the design, such as VDD or VSS.

In the past, metal fill was inserted at the manufacturing stage of a tapeout. However, since inserted metal fill can influence the resistance and capacitance parasitics of wires in the design, it is now necessary to generate metal fill in design tools and to model the impact of metal fill in parasitic extraction tools. In a typical densely routed design, metal fill has little impact on wire capacitance. In the worst case, however, metal fill can vary wire capacitance by more than 100 percent. Floating metal fill, which refers to those pieces not connected to electrical nets of the design, contributes less to overall net capacitance but can increase the magnitude of coupled capacitance to other nets. The implementation style of the metal fill can therefore have an impact on timing and signal integrity, so it must be modeled in extraction.
You can choose two types of metal fill:

- Grounded, which has a fixed electrical charge. Typically, grounded metal has a higher capacitance.
- Floating, which has no fixed electrical charge

Metal fill is usually specified in the input DEF file. If the metal fill in the DEF file is incomplete or otherwise unsatisfactory, you can import the metal fill from an external GDSII file, frequently obtained from a foundry. When you use this GDSII file, Fire & Ice ZX ignores the metal fill polygons in the input DEF file.
Electromigration (EM)

Electromigration is used as a general term to describe failure mechanisms in the metal wires of a computer chip caused by the movement of metal atoms in a wire because of high current stress. As electrons move through a metal wire, they collide with the atoms in that wire. These collisions cause wires to become heated, and if enough electrons collide with a metal atom over a period of time, the metal atom may move in the direction of the electron flow, causing two failure mechanisms:

- First, if enough atoms are moved, the wire effectively breaks and becomes an open circuit.
- Second, if enough atoms move to the same location, a short to an adjacent metal wire can be created. This phenomenon is commonly known as fusing.
Either of these mechanisms changes the functionality of the chip.
Two distinct physical phenomena cause electromigration: wearout and Joule heating.
Wearout
Electromigration in a specific sense is a long-term wearout mechanism of chip interconnect wires.
As metal atoms are moved and the wire becomes narrower (in the location from which the atoms are moving), the resistance of the wire increases. If atom movement continues, the wire eventually breaks.
Because wearout can take a significant amount of time to be observed, failure due to wearout is measured as a statistical process and is characterized by a wire's mean time to failure (MTTF).

To reduce the impact of electromigration, chip interconnect wires on a given metal layer are built from several metal materials in a sandwich fashion. The extra metal layers in the wire, generally the top and the bottom layers in the sandwich structure, are more resistant to electromigration, so they help prevent total wire failure because of breaks. Electromigration failures are therefore defined as an unacceptable increase in the resistance of the wire rather than an outright break in the wire. Because electromigration is the result of long-term wearout, the risk to a wire is measured in terms of the long-term average current flowing through the wire.

There remains some debate in the reliability community about the proper model for wires exposed to alternating current flow. One approach indicates that when the average current is 0 A, no electromigration occurs. Another approach says that the recovery from the reversed current is not full, so the average should be the peak in one direction minus a recovery factor times the reverse current. A recovery factor of zero indicates no recovery, and a factor of 1.0 indicates full recovery.
Joule Heating

Joule heating refers to the excessive heating of a specific segment of a wire because of high alternating currents. Wires are heated as alternating current moves through them. This heating occurs regardless of the direction of the current. If the heating of the wire becomes significant, thermal expansion and temperature-induced electromigration occurs. Since the risk of Joule heating increases with temperature, and increasing frequency increases heating effects, electromigration due to Joule heating is associated with high-frequency wires.
Because the root cause of Joule heating failure is different from electromigration, different measures are applied in verification.
Fusing

Fusing is a specific failure mechanism related to electromigration. It refers to the instant failure of a wire segment because of excessive instantaneous current. To address this failure mechanism, different measures are applied in verification. Some reliability researchers classify this failure mechanism as a subset of Joule heating.
Symptoms of Electromigration Problems

Because electromigration is a wearout phenomenon, its symptoms are primarily a change in either the timing or functionality of a chip over time. Wires that provide unique connectivity in the circuit cause total functional failure if they fail because of electromigration. Wires that are inherently redundant, such as a meshed power grid, exhibit symptoms of higher IR drop or ground bounce after electromigration failure results in disconnected sections of the power grid. If electromigration causes a short between wires on a chip, total functional failure occurs.
Advanced Processing Techniques
Using metals such as titanium nitride and copper in chips can also impact electromigration.
Titanium Nitride
To reduce the impact of electromigration, chip interconnect wires on a given metal layer are built from several metal materials in a sandwich fashion, as shown in the following diagram:
Multiple Materials Used to Build a Single Wire

Generally the top and the bottom layers in the sandwich structure are constructed from titanium nitride (TiN), which is more resistant to electromigration. This structure helps to prevent total open circuit failures; however, because the resistivity of the TiN layer is greater than aluminum, electromigration failures are defined as an unacceptable increase in the resistance of the wire rather than an outright break in the wire.
Copper Interconnect

State-of-the-art process technologies use copper as chip interconnect material because copper offers not only lower resistivity but also higher resistance to electromigration wearout. However, copper interconnect structures are not as deep as aluminium structures, so the cross-section area is smaller and therefore the current densities are higher. As a result, electromigration-based failures still occur in copper structures.
In contrast to aluminum-based processes, vias built with copper increase the likelihood of electromigration wearout and require more detailed verification.
Evaluating Electromigration Risk

Electromigration occurs in metal wires that conduct high current densities. Conducting electrons collide with diffusing metal atoms and impel the atoms in the direction of electron flow. This collision produces a mass flux opposite to that of the current. Divergences in this mass flux can result in damage to the conductor in the form of voids or hillocks. You can minimize this problem by limiting the current density in metal conductor lines, vias, and contacts.

Electromigration resistance is primarily a function of processing parameters. Many of the variations observed in the lifetime behavior of conductors because of electromigration cannot be attributed to a specific cause. In addition, material contains significant lot-to-lot variations. To fully understand electromigration within the chip, you must be familiar with the specific properties of your own material and process.

In some cases, complete data is not available. The following suggestions may help you in making an evaluation. The figures quoted here are from relevant literature over the past 25 years; you should consider them non-definitive values and not necessarily representative of your material or process. Cadence provides these figures only as background information.

In recent evaluations of electromigration performance in the industry, it has become commonplace to observe that failures in lines connected to bond pads obey 1/j2 kinetics and that lines connected to vias and contacts usually obey 1/j kinetics. Therefore, these are the default values used in this simulation. If you have evidence that your process provides other kinetics, you can make suitable changes in the modeling.

The following table shows the pre-exponential and activation energy for line and via electromigration failure for wide and narrow lines for a variety of alloys. The values chosen are conservative and represent the latest data available from leading industrial laboratories. Al/Si figures are estimated from the expectation that lifetimes without copper alloying are about 10 percent of those with copper. No recent data on this assumption is available, and earlier data is questionable in view of recent advances in electromigration testing procedures.
Table 8-1 Pre-exponential and activation energy for line and Via electromigration failure

data: Al/Si narrow line or Al/Cu wide line, with or without barrier shunt layer
Al/Cu narrow line without barrier shunt layer
Al/Cu narrow line with barrier shunt layer

lines:
- n is either 2 for nucleation-dominated failure or 1 for growth-dominated failure.
- A is the technology- and structure-dependent pre-factor. The units of A are the following:
  - Amp-hours per cm2 degree for n =1
  - Amp2-hours per cm4 degrees for n =2
- Ea is the activation energy for the electromigration mechanism.

For vias, the current density is defined as the current density in the wire immediately contacting the via or the contact to silicon. This density is assumed to be for a line with a width equal to the via. Al and Al/Si are assumed to be equivalent, and all Al/Cu alloys are assumed to be created equal.
Modeling electromigration risk

Electromigration risk analysis requires the use of failure models. The choice of failure model depends on the particular manufacturing process and design rules. The failure kinetics vary according to the metal line width. They must be determined from the test structures. Model parameters such as A_n , A_w , and n (the current density coefficient) are used to describe the predominant failure kinetics.
On the basis of the calculated average current density, Cadence analysis tools use Black’s Equation to determine the mean time to failure (MTTF):

- n is either 2 for nucleation-dominated failure or 1 for growth-dominated failure.
- A is the technology- and structure-dependent pre-factor.
- T is the temperature.
- m is the temperature coefficient.
- j is the average current density.
- Ea is the activation energy for the electromigration mechanism.
- k is Boltzmann’s constant.
The MTTF is then used to calculate the probability of failure for a line segment:

- t is the expected lifetime of the design, which you can specify by using the Lifetime environment variable.
- σ is the lognormal standard deviation. It is process-dependent and must be determined by using test structures. It is a model parameter that you can specify layer by layer.
After Cadence analysis tools calculate the failure probability for each wire segment, they use the following calculation to determine the failure probability for the full chip:
This approach assumes a lognormal failure distribution, which is analogous to the weakest link in a chain.
Cadence analysis tools report the overall probability of failure after they finish the analysis. They report the inverse of the failure probability for each wire segment as electromigration risk.
You must specify several processing parameters for each layer to obtain good results from electromigration risk analysis.

- The following is an example of using the via_range option.

 Where, via_range=2 refers to number of vias from 1 to 2.

 Where, via_range=8 refers to number of vias from 5 to 8, since 1 to 4 was covered by the prior two commands. For any cases that have more than 8 vias, this model will also be applied. 
 The via_range rules on metal layers are only applied to resistors that belong to a segment with a via connection to the upper layer. If there is no via connection (i.e top metal layer), then the default rule (without via_range , like command shown below) will be used.

 If wire width and number of vias rules are both specified, the number of vias rule will be applied first. 
 The " info r ”, " which r ", and " print rj " commands will list the number of via cuts to be used in EM modeling. All of these commands require a previous EM analysis with via_range models performed, since only then will it run the connectivity retracing to account for via connections. 
 Temperature effects on resistance are available. The model parameters for each layer include fields for TC1, TC2, and TREF. The resistance of a resistor is calculated by using the following formula: 
 Reffective = R*(1 + TC1 * delta_t + TC2 * delta_t2) 
 This syntax contains the following parameters:
  - delta_t - Specifies the default temperature minus the nominal model temperature ( TREF ).
  - TC1 - Specifies the linear temperature coefficient. The default is 0.0 in 1/degrees.
  - TC2 - Specifies the quadratic temperature coefficient. The default is 0.0 in 1/degrees2.
  - TREF - Specifies the nominal model temperature, in degrees Celsius. The default is 25 degrees Celsius.
  - TC1 and TC2 come from the foundry. TREF refers to the temperature at which the technology file was generated. The temperature variable sets the operating temperature at which you want to run the analysis. The tool then recalculates the resistance from the extracted TREF at the analysis temperature that you want.

EM Modeling using QRC Technology File

In addition to the set_rail_analysis_mode -em_models file command parameter, you can use the QRC Techfile for EM model specification. The set_rail_analysis_ mode -process_techgen_em_rules true parameter specifies to process the EM models in the ICT section of the qrcTechFile.

If you are defining an em_model to be used for limit based analysis, specify the em_jmax_ac_avg parameter that specifies the maximum AC average current density which can either be defined as a single value, a PWL table, or an equation (EQU). The following examples illustrate the limits supported:

- Single value - use a single number limit for current density. 
 em_jmax_dc_avg 12.15
- PWL table - use piecewise linear (PWL) interpolation in the em_model to accurately determine current density using interpolation of specified points on a graph. It allows you to specify Jmax value for each width/area of metal/via in the design to support width dependency on limits. 
 em_jmax_dc_avg PWL 0.1672 0.002025 0.3344 0.005265
  - Conductor: em_jmax_dc_avg = "jmax_value_1 width_1 … jmax_value_n width_n"
  - Via: em_jmax_dc_avg = "jmax_value_1 area_1 … jmax_value_n area_n"
- Equation - use an equation to calculate current density. The EQU keyword denotes the definition of an equation for current density. It supports width dependencies. 
 em_jmax_dc_avg EQU 1 * 0.996 * ( w - 0.003 )

Permitted current on the metal routing layer will change depending on the number of vias. When many vias are placed, the permitted current of metal layer on EM rule will be large. When a small number of vias are placed, the permitted current of metal layer on EM rule will be small.

The permitted current (Jmax) value can be defined based on the metal width and the number of vias. via_range is an optional parameter which can be used with the via layers. The value of via_range denotes number of vias for which the calculated limits are valid.The following is an example of using the via_range option:
via_range means the number of vias in the array. It could be 3x3 or 1x9, 2x2 or 1x4, respectively. It allows you to handle current density limit on a via based on number of vias.
Temperature Dependent Current Density Limits
You can handle temperature dependent current density limits by adjusting the Jmax value by a user defined co-efficient value which considers the temperature and life-time effect.

- jmax_factor is the optional scaling factor to use at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature. 
 jmax_factor <temp1> <scale1> [<temp2> <scale2>...] 
 em_jmax_dc_avg 12.15 jmax_factor 105 1.434 110 1.000 115 0.704 120 0.500 125 0.358
- jmax_lifetime provides the ability to set the scaling factor that applies to the current density limits for different lifetimes. 
 jmax_lifetime <lifetime1> <scale1> [<lifetime2> <scale2>...] 
 em_jmax_dc_avg EQU 3 * (w - 0.00485) jmax_lifetime 40000 2.05 50000 1.73 75000 1.26 100000 1.0 110000 0.924 150000 0.711 200000 0.556
Length and Width Dependency

The L and W parameters are used to specify the length and width-based jmax values in microns for both metal and vias. These parameters are used when the dimensions to be measured are the same for the metal above and below a via.

For long metal interconnect segments, current density limits are given as a constant. As wires become shorter (i.e. below 20um), there is a point where electromigration becomes less and less of a problem. There is also a lower limit for the length of the interconnect known as "Blech length", and any wire that has a length below this will not fail by electromigration. The "Blech length" is modeled using higher current density limits for shorter wires.

where, W is the wire width and L is the Blech length (or the path length in the current density -rj report). Blech length (Path length/ Short length) is the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer. After the Blech length is determined, resistors on these connected wire shapes on the same metal layer use the same Blech length to get the EM limit.

When certain constraints (“ L>3W<0.4 ”) are met, its EM limit can be 2X, 3X, or multiple X of the base EM limit. These multiples are called Benefit Factor (red-highlighted in the above example - “1”, "2").

Voltus supports metal length/width rules above and below vias wherein the dimensions to be checked for the rules are different for the metal above and the metal below. In this case, Lu/Wu refers to dimensions of the metal above the via and Lb/Wb refers to dimensions of the metal below the via.
