EM Modeling using QRC Technology File

In addition to the set_rail_analysis_mode -em_models file command parameter, you can use the QRC Techfile for EM model specification. The set_rail_analysis_ mode -process_techgen_em_rules true parameter specifies to process the EM models in the ICT section of the qrcTechFile.

If you are defining an em_model to be used for limit based analysis, specify the em_jmax_ac_avg parameter that specifies the maximum AC average current density which can either be defined as a single value, a PWL table, or an equation (EQU). The following examples illustrate the limits supported:

- Single value - use a single number limit for current density. 
 em_jmax_dc_avg 12.15
- PWL table - use piecewise linear (PWL) interpolation in the em_model to accurately determine current density using interpolation of specified points on a graph. It allows you to specify Jmax value for each width/area of metal/via in the design to support width dependency on limits. 
 em_jmax_dc_avg PWL 0.1672 0.002025 0.3344 0.005265
  - Conductor: em_jmax_dc_avg = "jmax_value_1 width_1 … jmax_value_n width_n"
  - Via: em_jmax_dc_avg = "jmax_value_1 area_1 … jmax_value_n area_n"
- Equation - use an equation to calculate current density. The EQU keyword denotes the definition of an equation for current density. It supports width dependencies. 
 em_jmax_dc_avg EQU 1 * 0.996 * ( w - 0.003 )
Topology-Dependent EM Rule Check

Permitted current on the metal routing layer will change depending on the number of vias. When many vias are placed, the permitted current of metal layer on EM rule will be large. When a small number of vias are placed, the permitted current of metal layer on EM rule will be small.

The permitted current (Jmax) value can be defined based on the metal width and the number of vias. via_range is an optional parameter which can be used with the via layers. The value of via_range denotes number of vias for which the calculated limits are valid.The following is an example of using the via_range option:
via_range means the number of vias in the array. It could be 3x3 or 1x9, 2x2 or 1x4, respectively. It allows you to handle current density limit on a via based on number of vias.
Temperature Dependent Current Density Limits
You can handle temperature dependent current density limits by adjusting the Jmax value by a user defined co-efficient value which considers the temperature and life-time effect.

- jmax_factor is the optional scaling factor to use at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature. 
 jmax_factor <temp1> <scale1> [<temp2> <scale2>...] 
 em_jmax_dc_avg 12.15 jmax_factor 105 1.434 110 1.000 115 0.704 120 0.500 125 0.358
- jmax_lifetime provides the ability to set the scaling factor that applies to the current density limits for different lifetimes. 
 jmax_lifetime <lifetime1> <scale1> [<lifetime2> <scale2>...] 
 em_jmax_dc_avg EQU 3 * (w - 0.00485) jmax_lifetime 40000 2.05 50000 1.73 75000 1.26 100000 1.0 110000 0.924 150000 0.711 200000 0.556
Length and Width Dependency

The L and W parameters are used to specify the length and width-based jmax values in microns for both metal and vias. These parameters are used when the dimensions to be measured are the same for the metal above and below a via.

For long metal interconnect segments, current density limits are given as a constant. As wires become shorter (i.e. below 20um), there is a point where electromigration becomes less and less of a problem. There is also a lower limit for the length of the interconnect known as "Blech length", and any wire that has a length below this will not fail by electromigration. The "Blech length" is modeled using higher current density limits for shorter wires.

where, W is the wire width and L is the Blech length (or the path length in the current density -rj report). Blech length (Path length/ Short length) is the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer. After the Blech length is determined, resistors on these connected wire shapes on the same metal layer use the same Blech length to get the EM limit.

When certain constraints (“ L>3W<0.4 ”) are met, its EM limit can be 2X, 3X, or multiple X of the base EM limit. These multiples are called Benefit Factor (red-highlighted in the above example - “1”, "2").

Voltus supports metal length/width rules above and below vias wherein the dimensions to be checked for the rules are different for the metal above and the metal below. In this case, Lu/Wu refers to dimensions of the metal above the via and Lb/Wb refers to dimensions of the metal below the via.
The following diagram illustrates a scenario where dimensions are different for the metal above and below a via (Lu > 5, Wu >= 0.8, Lb > 5, and Wb >= 0.4):
The following example illustrates the EM model syntax for different rules above and below:
The following length and width dependent EM rules are supported:

- Differentiate between single square cut via and square via-array using the keyword single . The following example shows how to use the single keyword: 
 em_jmax_dc_avg PWL 0.0276 0.001024 jmax_factor 105 1.434 110 1.000 115 
 0.704 120 0.500 125 0.358 130 0.258 140 0.138 150 0.076 current_direction up Lb > 4 Wb >= 0.064 current_direction up single
- Modes to calculate length as centerline or outer edge of a wire segment. The process definition parameter em_segment_length is used to determine how to calculate the length of a wire segment. This parameter determines whether the segment length is of the type perimeter or centerline . The difference between the perimeter and centerline segment length types is as follows: 
 The following example shows how to use the em_segment_length parameter: 
 em_segment_length centerline
  - perimeter - specifies that the length calculation is along the wire outer edge boundary. This value is used for 28-nm process nodes.
  - centerline - specifies that the length is extended to the edge of the segment along the centerline path. This value is used for 20-nm process nodes.
- Via current direction dependency rule. The syntax of current direction is: 
 current_direction [ up | down | both ]
  - up - means the current is from down to top and specifies to use the length and width of the metal below Lb/Wb for the VIA Jmax factor
  - down - means the current is from top to down and specifies to use the length and width of the metal above Lu/Wu for VIA Jmax factor
  - both - This rule is only applicable when current direction is uncertain. This rule is unlikely to be applied since the software calculates the current direction.
Static Rail Analysis Setup and Run
Voltus rail analysis has two accuracy modes, XD and HD.

- XD (accelerated definition) – use for early implementation stage IR/EM analysis
- HD (high definition) – use for final verification stage IR/EM analysis
The following table illustrates the default PGV selected for static and dynamic analysis based on the accuracy mode (XD/HD):

The EM power-grid view is enabled for static HD accuracy mode when -em_models or -process_techgen_em_rules true is specified. By default, the DFM effects and fillers are disabled. To change the PGV selection or enable DFM effects, use the following parameters of the set_rail_analysis_mode command:

- -use_early_view_list filename
- -use_ir_view_list filename
- -use_em_view_list filename
- -enable_manufacturing_effects {true|false}
This section describes how to set up the static rail analysis mode and then run rail analysis.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- Power-grid libraries (.cl). See Power-Grid Library Generation for details on generating power-grid libraries.
- Power database generated during static power calculation
- Binary current files created during static power calculation
Procedure
To perform static rail analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell
2. Specify the CPF file for power domain information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Load/Commit CPF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_domain -cpf ../design/super_filter.cpf
3. Define the rail analysis mode through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power & Rail - Set Rail Analysis Mode . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method static \ 
 
 -accuracy xd \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } \ 
 
 -use_em_view_list ../data/voltus/em_view.list \ 
 
 -enable_rlrp_analysis true \ 
 
 -verbosity true \ 
 
 -temperature 125 
 
 
 Note:
  - XD uses LEF based views, and HD uses GDS based accurate views.
  - The technology library is a mandatory requirement for rail analysis. However, to obtain accurate IR drop simulation, power-grid library should be characterized for standard cells and macro blocks. When specifying the list of PGVs to rail analysis using the set_rail_analysis_mode command or the Set Rail Analysis Mode form, you must specify the technology library as the first PGV library in the list. If both technology and standard cell PGV libraries exist, the standard cell PGV is given a higher precedence.
  - You need to specify the EM analysis models if analyzing Electromigration. If you do not specify an Electromigration model file, the software will select Electromigration models from the Quantus technology file. You can use the command set_rail_analysis_mode -em_models file to override these settings.
4. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. Then, use set_rail_analysis_domain to define the power domains. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_AO -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_ring -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.05 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD_ring -pwrnets VDD_AO -gndnets VSS -threshold 0.10 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD_AO -pwrnets VDD_AO -gndnets VSS -threshold 0.10
5. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_AO -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_AO.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp
6. Specify the location of the binary current files generated through power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_data -format current { staticPowerResults/static_VDD_AO.ptiavg staticPowerResults/static_VDD_ring.ptiavg staticPowerResults/static_VSS.ptiavg}
7. Run rail analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power & Rail - Run Rail Analysis . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 #Analyze all domains 
 
 analyze_rail - output ./staticRailResults -type domain ALL 
 
 #Analyze only one domain 
 
 analyze_rail -output ./staticRailResults -type domain PD_AO 
 
 #Analyze a power net if the design did not have CPF and power domains 
 
 analyze_rail -output ./staticRailResults -type net VDD_AO
The Voltus console will display rail analysis statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The rail output directory will contain the following information:

data:
For each run, an output directory is generated with the default name < domainName >_< temperature >_avg_< X >. < domainName > will be replaced with ALL if all domains are to be analyzed ( analyze _rail -output dir -type domain ALL ), or with < netName > if a power net is to be analyzed. The output directory contains state directories for each net, such as VDD and VSS. 
 
 Each state directory contains the following: 
 
 results – a file that gives a summary of the rail analysis results for various plots, such as tc (tap current), rc (resistor current), and so on. 
 Reports – a folder that contains a list of useful report files that contain all power-grid integrity data. 
 You can specify the set_rail_analysis_mode -def_based_hierarchical_reports true parameter to generate separate rail reports (EIV, RLRP, and EFFR) for each DEF instance. Reports will be generated for every net in each DEF hierarchy using the following directory structure: 
 <output_dir>/<state_dir>/Reports/HIER/hier_<HIER_ID>/<domain_name | net_name>.<report_type> 
 Auto-generated GIF files for the rail analysis plots

lines: Viewing Static Rail Analysis Results

The rail analysis results can be debugged in the context of physical layout using interactive rail analysis plots. Voltus allows you to visualize rail analysis results in the design using the Rail Results Viewer.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Power database generated during static power calculation
- Rail analysis output directory
Procedure (Rail Results Viewer)
To visualize rail results using rail results viewer, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell
2. Load the rail data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_rail_results -power_db staticPowerResults/staticPower.db -rail_directory staticRailResults/ALL_125C_avg_1
  1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots . 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the rail analysis results.
  2. Click the DB Setup button. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
  3. You can enter the power database or click Browse next to the Power Database field, and select a location from the Binary DB File browser window.
  4. You can enter the rail output directory or click Browse next to the Rail Database field, and select a location from the Rail State Directory browser window.
  5. In the Selective Loading section, you can select the following fields to reduce the amount of the rail analysis data being loaded into the GUI:
    - Nets - Specify whether all nets ( All ) or selected nets ( Partial ) are to be loaded into the GUI. If you select Partial , click the Select button to select the required power and ground nets. 
The selected nets will appear in the Nets tab of the Power & Rail Layers/Nets form.
    - IV Window - Load the instance voltage data for the selected EIV window.
    - IV Method Type - Load the instance voltage data for the specified EIV computing method.
  6. Click OK .
3. Optional. You can select the nets or layers to be viewed through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_rail_layers_nets -disable_visible_layers {Metal7 Via5 Via4 Via3} -disable_report_layers {Metal7 Via5 Via4 Via3} -disable_visible_sem_layers {} -disable_nets VDD_AO
  1. Click the Layers/Nets button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 The Power & Rail Layers/Nets form appears.
  2. In the Layers tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the specific layers whose visibility you want to turn on or off in the Visible column, and to control the processing of individual layers in the Report column. Then, use the slider control to control the transparency of the rail analysis output and the design database.
  3. In the Nets tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the power or ground nets to modify the display of nets. The Nets tab allows you to control the display of the switch and always-on nets in the GUI. When you load an always-on net state directory, the Power Nets section shows both the always-on and switch nets as a sub-tree under each net, as shown below:
  4. Click OK .
4. In the Power & Rail Plots form, select the Rail radio button and select a rail analysis type from the drop-down list. 
 The selected plot will be overlaid on the design. 
 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 
 #Displays the specified plot 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot ir -filter_max 0.0038 \ -filter_min 1.00001e-05 
#Displays the specified plot and generates a report (power_rail_< plot_type >.rpt) in the current working directory report_power_rail_results -plot tc 
 
 #Clear the display 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot none
5. Set the filter options, as required. When a plot type is selected, the filter values automatically get filled in. 
 You can select the Auto Apply for Color Scale checkbox to automatically redraw the layout after modifying the range values. If you do not select this checkbox, you can click the Apply button to manually redraw the layout.
6. Select the Result Browser option to display the Results Browser form. Based on the plot selected, this form allows you to check the resistor or instance information.
7. Select the Voltage Source check box to view nets with their voltage sources. If you want to view voltage sources of specific nets, you can clear the check boxes adjacent to the remaining power/ground nets in the Nets tab of the Power & Rail Layers/Nets form.
8. Click Measure and draw an area in the main window to view the tap current and capacitance of the specified region. The following is an example of the information that will be displayed in the Voltus console: 
 Net: VDD_AO 
 Region: (20.842, 100.715) (47.0065, 115.105) 
 Tap Current: 1.16205e-05 A 
 Tap Current On Unconnected Grids: 0 A 
 Worst IR: 0.884208 V 
 Grid Capacitance: 0 F 
 Intrinsic Capacitance(C1): 0 F 
 Load Capacitance(C2): 0 F 
 Decap Required: 0 F
9. For the rlrp rail plot, if you select an object (instance or resistor) and click the Trace button, Voltus highlights the least effective resistance path from the object to the nearest voltage source, and will open the Resistance Path form. This form will list the break down of all resistor segments in the rlrp path. You can select a resistor in the Resistance Path form, single click to select/zoom, and double click to query. Click the Clear button to clear the RLRP path. The RLRP path will be retained until you click the Clear button. If you select/deselect a resistor on the RLRP path to view its attributes, the RLRP path will continue to be displayed. 
 The Resistance Path form has the following fields, in addition to information on the layer/via name, and node coordinates (location for each segment in the least resistance path): 
 The form shows multiple objects/resistors. You can click the Prev/Next button, or select a resistor from the drop-down list, to view the details of each resistor. 
 The rlrp report at the command prompt contains 2 parts: a detailed report showing each rlrp segment information, and a summary report showing information for each layer. 
 The rail analysis must include RLRP analysis ( set_rail_analysis_mode –enable_rlrp_analysis true ) in order to have the required data for this functionality.
  - Power Gate - specifies if a resistor is identified as a power-gate Ron resistor for easy identification.
  - Resistance (Cumulative) - displays each resistance and cumulative resistance (in bracket) from a voltage source to the specific resistor.
  - Voltage Drop (Cumulative) - displays voltage drop of each resistance (based on the worst IR drop that occurred between two connected nodes), and the cumulative voltage drop (in bracket) from a voltage source to the specific resistor.
  - Auto Zoom Level - enables you to specify the appropriate zoom level and improve visibility. The zoom levels are “+2, +1, 0, 1, 2”. 0 is selected by default. The prefix “+” means zoom-in whereas “-” means zoom-out.
  - Auto Select - allows you to highlight any resistor on the list and the resistor will be auto-zoomed.
  - Show Single Path - allows you to select and view a single RLRP path. For an instance with multiple RLRP paths, the single path display feature allows you to debug the desired path easily.
  - Enable Multi Segments Selection - allows you to select multiple resistors in the RLRP path. When you select the Enable Multi Segments Selection check box and double-click on a row in the Resistance Path form, the Attribute Viewer form appears, as shown below:
10. Click the Apply button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 Selecting Apply will accept the changes, and Reset will set it to default values. By default, the range does not auto update. You can click the Reset button to auto-scale.
11. Experiment with the remaining rail plots, as listed in the following table:

data: The voltage drop (IRdrop) plot is displayed on all the power-grid segments of the analyzed net. The IRdrop data is saved inside the state directory of the net. The worst case voltage can be displayed by selecting the Result Browser option. The segments with high IRdrop can be debugged using the rail analysis plots in conjunction with power plots.

The grid resistance plot displays the resistance of each power-grid segment. This plot does not consider the power distribution when computing resistance thus highlighting power-grid weakness and resistance hot spots in the design irrespective of activity vector coverage. 
 
 This analysis plot allows designers to check power-grid robustness during early stages of the power-grid design when worst case vectors that produce high power and IRdrop are unknown. The plot can be used during decap optimization (dynamic) as well because adding decaps along the path of high resistance has no effect on localized dynamic IRdrop.

The instance effective resistance plot is similar to grid resistance plot except it is plotted for the instances connected to the power-grid. 
 
 The resistance for the instance pin is calculated as total resistance along the least resistance path. If an instance has multiple power pins connected to the power-grid, instance based rlrp plot uses the pin with the worst (highest) resistance value to plot the instance based data. Like grid resistance plot, instance based resistance plot is also independent of power vectors. This allows you to identify weakly connected instances in the design during early stages of power planning. It can be used during the decap optimization as well because adding decaps along the path of high resistance has no effect on localized dynamic IRdrop.

After observing IR drop plots, the next question is how the current flows in the design to create the IR drop plot generated. It is important to examine these plots because they also exhibit behaviors that are not obvious from examining the IR drop plots. These plots show current-flow trends that you might not have expected or current from several power pins reconverging in the middle of the chip to create high current through some wires.

The tap current plot shows the current distribution inside the cell. The power-grid library generation program characterizes the current distribution inside the cell and replaces devices connected to the power-grid with current taps (sink). The relative current distribution inside the cell power-grid view library is based on device width and length. The instance based power calculated during power analysis is fed to rail analysis during IRdrop analysis. The supplied instance power is then distributed inside the cell and can be viewed using tap current plot. 
 
 If accurate power-grid view is used during rail analysis for the memories, the tap current plot clearly highlights the low power (current) distribution in bit cells region and high power distribution in sense amplifier and decoder circuit of the memory. The tap current plot can be used to debug high IR drop caused by high power density in the region.

Once you understand how current flows through your design, you may want to know where the high current densities occur. Use the Current Density analysis type to examine them. 
 
 Current density reporting does not plot the current density of the segment but it is based on the ratio of wire current density over the current density limit that is allowed by the process - (J/Jmax). You therefore want all wires to have a ratio less than 1.0 and debug or fix the current density violations that have greater that 1.0 value. This ratio is easier to work with because its value is more consistent across designs.
The IRdrop plot displays the net-based instance voltage drop. ivdn shows the worst instance voltage drop for each net. To see only one net, you can disable all the nets except one.

ivdd will show the worst instance voltage drop for each power-ground pair. When at least one power and one ground net is selected, the plot will show the worst instance drop value. 
 
 For dynamic analysis, you can also select the type of IV method (best, worst, avg, worst avg) and/or the IV window (switching/timing window, whole window) in the Layers tab of the Power and Rail Plots - Layers/Nets form.

This plot is only applicable to the power-gated design and analysis. 
 
 The power-gate current (I/Idsat) plot displays the ratio of current through the power-gate and the saturation current (Idsat).This plot can be used to analyze and debug whether the current through power-gates exceeds the saturation current. The saturation current and on-resistance of the power-switches are characterized and stored inside the power-grid library of the power-gate cell. A ratio of greater than 1 means that the current requirement of the power-gated block can not be met with placed power-switch instance in that region. Either the power-switch needs to be upsized or more power-gate instances need to be placed. 
 
 In addition to the plot, rail analysis creates the text report (pi.report) and binary ECO file ( vs2fe_pg.rpt ) for Innovus Implementation System (Innovus) inside the state directory. The ECO file can be read inside Innovus using " optPowerSwitch -vsdgInFile " command to optimize power-gates.

The power-gate voltage plot displays the IRdrop across power-switch instances. This plot can be used to analyze and debug the IRdrop inside the power-gated block (e.g. if the IRdrop across power gate is already high, the IRdrop inside the power-gated block will be much higher. In this case, the power-gate placement will have to be refined or more power-gates will have to be added in order to resolve the IRdrop problem).

Resistor sensitivity is calculated by the software as: 
 
 S = delta (Worst Voltage)/ delta (Resistance) in V/Ohm 
 
 Resistor sensitivity analysis is supported only in static rail analysis and can be enabled using the " set_rail_analysis_mode -enable_sensitivity_analysis true " option. By default it is false . The resistor sensitivity for power-grid can be viewed by plotting "rs" analysis type in GUI. The sensitivity analysis can be used as guidance for power-grid optimization. Sensitivity is calculated for all power-grid segments and the resistors with higher sensitivity when optimized have better chance to improve overall IRdrop profile of the power-grid. 
 
 The optimization in this context means reducing overall resistance of the power-grid, e.g. wire widening. Although Voltus stand-alone product does not allow design and netlist changes, it can be used inside the Innovus platform which has wide range of power-grid editing and routing features. Optionally, you can perform what-if analysis ( What-If Rail Analysis ) inside the Voltus environment which allows you to scale resistance of power-grid segments on-the-fly to analyze and estimate the optimization efforts to improve IRdrop profile of the power-grid. 
 
 There are two additional rs plots called rs (wire) and rs (wire group) to display resistor sensitivity on a wire or wire-group basis. Instead of displaying sensitivity of an individual resistor in a wire, these plots display highest sensitivity of a collection of resistors that form a wire or a wire group. These plots are only available when you invoke Voltus or ERA analysis in Innovus where the original power planning was done. The power planning commands define the wire or wire-groups that form the power-grid of the design, and Voltus or ERA is used to analyze sensitivity of these wires or wire-groups and optimize the power-grid using a power-planning ECO file.

lines: The following table lists the static analysis plot types and the corresponding controls to enable them:

data: power gate current I/Idsat (pi) and power gate voltage (pv)
enabled automatically when power-gate(s) are connected to the analyzed net.
enabled automatically when package data is provided (spice model, in power pin location file, or through default_package_* commands)
enabled if EM models are present with set_rail_analysis_mode –process_techgen_em_rules true or –em_models

lines: Generating Text Reports

In addition to auto-generated HTML reports, Voltus can incrementally generate text reports for all analysis types, post analysis. These reports can be generated using the report_power_rail_results command and report option. You are required to load state directory which has rail analysis results. The power reports can be generated after restoring the power database.
Example TCL Flow
# Import design
# Load rail analysis state directory
# IRdrop report
# Resistance analysis report
# Current density (EM) violation report
# Sensitivity analysis report
# Instance-based least resistance path report without violation report
There are two ways to generate static power reports in Voltus:

- Using report_power after restoring power database
- Using report_power_rail_results after restoring power database

Both methods generate report in different format. See Viewing Static Power Analysis Results to view report_power options. In general, report_power has more detailed options to generate power distribution reports, e.g. for design hierarchy, cell type, instance, clock domain, power domain etc. In contrast, report_power_rail_results can only generate global power, activity and frequency reports to highlight worst-case values.
Example TCL flow
OR
Example IR Drop Report
Example Effective Instance Voltage Report
Example Current Density Report

In addition to text reports, you can measure the worst IR drop, total current and capacitance values in the region interactively using " Draw " and " Measure " buttons in Power & Rail Results form. This feature is also supported through TCL command as follows,
read_power_rail_results -rail_directory ALL_25C_dynamic_1/VDD report_power_rail_results -region {0 0 100 100}
Power-Grid Integrity Reports

Rail Analysis generates a single consolidated report containing all power-grid integrity data in the .rpt and .html formats ( net_name.pg_integrity.rpt and net_name.pg_integrity.html ). This report lists all power-grid integrity related problems, such as instances without voltage-source connectivity in PGDB, disconnected PGVs in PGDB, missing cells, physically disconnected cells, and weakly connected power segments.
The format of the PG integrity report in the DP and XP modes is given below.
Distributed Processing Mode
The consolidated report is generated for each net in the state directory at: ../state_dir/<net>/Reports.
The report format is:
* Missing Cell Power-Grid Views - these leaf-level cells are found to be connected to the power net being analyzed but cell power-grid views for these cells are missing.
* Physically Disconnected Instances - all instances listed below are disconnected from the power-grid.
* Instances with Floating Power Pins - instances in this list are physically connected to at least one power pin but one or more power pins are disconnected from the power net.

* Disconnected Wire Segments - lists wire segments are disconnected on top-level and inside the cell which are not connected to voltage source. View unconnected plot and gif file in state directory for graphical representation.
* Weakly Connected Metal Segments - lists wire segments that have no connections to the upper metal layers through via.
* Dropped Voltage Sources - lists voltage sources that could not be inserted on to the power-grid either due to incorrect location or missing power-grid geometry.
Extensively Parallel (XP) Mode
The consolidated report is generated for each net in the state directory at: ../state_dir/reports/rail/<net> .
The report format is:
* Missing Cell Power-Grid Views - these leaf-level cells are found to be connected to the power net being analyzed but cell power-grid views for these cells are missing.
* Unconnected Instance Pins - all instance pins listed below are disconnected from the power-grid.
* Unconnected Nodes and Resistors - all nodes and resistors listed below are not connected to a voltage source.
* Dropped Voltage Sources - lists voltage sources that could not be inserted on to the power-grid either due to incorrect location or missing power-grid geometry.
* Unmapped Package Terminals - lists package terminals that are not mapped to the grid locations or the top-level pins.
* Unconnected/Weakly Connected Pin Nodes - lists pin nodes that are physically connected to instances, but are disconnected from the power net.
Layer-Based IR Drop Report

By default, Voltus generates a layer-based IR drop report for each net in the static and dynamic rail analysis flows. The name of the report is < net_name >. layerbased_ir.rpt , and it is located within the < output directory >/< state_directory >/ Reports /< net_name >/ directory. This report prints the highest and the lowest IR drop on each metal layer. The IR drop report can be used to debug the IR drop contribution from each layer.
The following is the format of the report:
Here,

- Layer is the metal layer name.
- IR drop (volt) is the difference between the maximum IR drop and the minimum IR drop.
- IR drop range is the node voltage range, that is, max(node voltage) –> min(node voltage).
- Elements is the number of nodes.
The following is a snippet from the report:
HTML Reports

HTML reports are automatically generated and can be viewed by selecting the Report menu item from the Tools menu. Select File - Open File to load an html file. The main HTML file that includes links to other reports will be in a directory call Reports in the state directory (i.e. .../PDcore_25C_avg_1/Reports/dma_mac.main.html ).
Once the main html file is loaded, you can scroll down through the various reports. Some of these have additional links that will bring up other sub-reports, as shown below:
In the following form, you can see there is an IR Drop Analysis Report, which has a link to a Layer Based IR Drop Report:
This link will display the layer-based IR drop report, as shown below:

title: Static Rail Analysis Setup and Run

lines: Voltus rail analysis has two accuracy modes, XD and HD.

- XD (accelerated definition) – use for early implementation stage IR/EM analysis
- HD (high definition) – use for final verification stage IR/EM analysis
The following table illustrates the default PGV selected for static and dynamic analysis based on the accuracy mode (XD/HD):

The EM power-grid view is enabled for static HD accuracy mode when -em_models or -process_techgen_em_rules true is specified. By default, the DFM effects and fillers are disabled. To change the PGV selection or enable DFM effects, use the following parameters of the set_rail_analysis_mode command:

- -use_early_view_list filename
- -use_ir_view_list filename
- -use_em_view_list filename
- -enable_manufacturing_effects {true|false}
This section describes how to set up the static rail analysis mode and then run rail analysis.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- Power-grid libraries (.cl). See Power-Grid Library Generation for details on generating power-grid libraries.
- Power database generated during static power calculation
- Binary current files created during static power calculation
Procedure
To perform static rail analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell
2. Specify the CPF file for power domain information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Load/Commit CPF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_domain -cpf ../design/super_filter.cpf
3. Define the rail analysis mode through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power & Rail - Set Rail Analysis Mode . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method static \ 
 
 -accuracy xd \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } \ 
 
 -use_em_view_list ../data/voltus/em_view.list \ 
 
 -enable_rlrp_analysis true \ 
 
 -verbosity true \ 
 
 -temperature 125 
 
 
 Note:
  - XD uses LEF based views, and HD uses GDS based accurate views.
  - The technology library is a mandatory requirement for rail analysis. However, to obtain accurate IR drop simulation, power-grid library should be characterized for standard cells and macro blocks. When specifying the list of PGVs to rail analysis using the set_rail_analysis_mode command or the Set Rail Analysis Mode form, you must specify the technology library as the first PGV library in the list. If both technology and standard cell PGV libraries exist, the standard cell PGV is given a higher precedence.
  - You need to specify the EM analysis models if analyzing Electromigration. If you do not specify an Electromigration model file, the software will select Electromigration models from the Quantus technology file. You can use the command set_rail_analysis_mode -em_models file to override these settings.
4. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. Then, use set_rail_analysis_domain to define the power domains. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_AO -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_ring -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.05 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD_ring -pwrnets VDD_AO -gndnets VSS -threshold 0.10 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD_AO -pwrnets VDD_AO -gndnets VSS -threshold 0.10
5. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_AO -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_AO.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp
6. Specify the location of the binary current files generated through power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_data -format current { staticPowerResults/static_VDD_AO.ptiavg staticPowerResults/static_VDD_ring.ptiavg staticPowerResults/static_VSS.ptiavg}
7. Run rail analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power & Rail - Run Rail Analysis . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 #Analyze all domains 
 
 analyze_rail - output ./staticRailResults -type domain ALL 
 
 #Analyze only one domain 
 
 analyze_rail -output ./staticRailResults -type domain PD_AO 
 
 #Analyze a power net if the design did not have CPF and power domains 
 
 analyze_rail -output ./staticRailResults -type net VDD_AO
The Voltus console will display rail analysis statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The rail output directory will contain the following information:

data:
For each run, an output directory is generated with the default name < domainName >_< temperature >_avg_< X >. < domainName > will be replaced with ALL if all domains are to be analyzed ( analyze _rail -output dir -type domain ALL ), or with < netName > if a power net is to be analyzed. The output directory contains state directories for each net, such as VDD and VSS. 
 
 Each state directory contains the following: 
 
 results – a file that gives a summary of the rail analysis results for various plots, such as tc (tap current), rc (resistor current), and so on. 
 Reports – a folder that contains a list of useful report files that contain all power-grid integrity data. 
 You can specify the set_rail_analysis_mode -def_based_hierarchical_reports true parameter to generate separate rail reports (EIV, RLRP, and EFFR) for each DEF instance. Reports will be generated for every net in each DEF hierarchy using the following directory structure: 
 <output_dir>/<state_dir>/Reports/HIER/hier_<HIER_ID>/<domain_name | net_name>.<report_type> 
 Auto-generated GIF files for the rail analysis plots

lines: Viewing Static Rail Analysis Results

The rail analysis results can be debugged in the context of physical layout using interactive rail analysis plots. Voltus allows you to visualize rail analysis results in the design using the Rail Results Viewer.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Power database generated during static power calculation
- Rail analysis output directory
Procedure (Rail Results Viewer)
To visualize rail results using rail results viewer, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell
2. Load the rail data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_rail_results -power_db staticPowerResults/staticPower.db -rail_directory staticRailResults/ALL_125C_avg_1
  1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots . 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the rail analysis results.
  2. Click the DB Setup button. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
  3. You can enter the power database or click Browse next to the Power Database field, and select a location from the Binary DB File browser window.
  4. You can enter the rail output directory or click Browse next to the Rail Database field, and select a location from the Rail State Directory browser window.
  5. In the Selective Loading section, you can select the following fields to reduce the amount of the rail analysis data being loaded into the GUI:
    - Nets - Specify whether all nets ( All ) or selected nets ( Partial ) are to be loaded into the GUI. If you select Partial , click the Select button to select the required power and ground nets. 
The selected nets will appear in the Nets tab of the Power & Rail Layers/Nets form.
    - IV Window - Load the instance voltage data for the selected EIV window.
    - IV Method Type - Load the instance voltage data for the specified EIV computing method.
  6. Click OK .
3. Optional. You can select the nets or layers to be viewed through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_rail_layers_nets -disable_visible_layers {Metal7 Via5 Via4 Via3} -disable_report_layers {Metal7 Via5 Via4 Via3} -disable_visible_sem_layers {} -disable_nets VDD_AO
  1. Click the Layers/Nets button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 The Power & Rail Layers/Nets form appears.
  2. In the Layers tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the specific layers whose visibility you want to turn on or off in the Visible column, and to control the processing of individual layers in the Report column. Then, use the slider control to control the transparency of the rail analysis output and the design database.
  3. In the Nets tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the power or ground nets to modify the display of nets. The Nets tab allows you to control the display of the switch and always-on nets in the GUI. When you load an always-on net state directory, the Power Nets section shows both the always-on and switch nets as a sub-tree under each net, as shown below:
  4. Click OK .
4. In the Power & Rail Plots form, select the Rail radio button and select a rail analysis type from the drop-down list. 
 The selected plot will be overlaid on the design. 
 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 
 #Displays the specified plot 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot ir -filter_max 0.0038 \ -filter_min 1.00001e-05 
#Displays the specified plot and generates a report (power_rail_< plot_type >.rpt) in the current working directory report_power_rail_results -plot tc 
 
 #Clear the display 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot none
5. Set the filter options, as required. When a plot type is selected, the filter values automatically get filled in. 
 You can select the Auto Apply for Color Scale checkbox to automatically redraw the layout after modifying the range values. If you do not select this checkbox, you can click the Apply button to manually redraw the layout.
6. Select the Result Browser option to display the Results Browser form. Based on the plot selected, this form allows you to check the resistor or instance information.
7. Select the Voltage Source check box to view nets with their voltage sources. If you want to view voltage sources of specific nets, you can clear the check boxes adjacent to the remaining power/ground nets in the Nets tab of the Power & Rail Layers/Nets form.
8. Click Measure and draw an area in the main window to view the tap current and capacitance of the specified region. The following is an example of the information that will be displayed in the Voltus console: 
 Net: VDD_AO 
 Region: (20.842, 100.715) (47.0065, 115.105) 
 Tap Current: 1.16205e-05 A 
 Tap Current On Unconnected Grids: 0 A 
 Worst IR: 0.884208 V 
 Grid Capacitance: 0 F 
 Intrinsic Capacitance(C1): 0 F 
 Load Capacitance(C2): 0 F 
 Decap Required: 0 F
9. For the rlrp rail plot, if you select an object (instance or resistor) and click the Trace button, Voltus highlights the least effective resistance path from the object to the nearest voltage source, and will open the Resistance Path form. This form will list the break down of all resistor segments in the rlrp path. You can select a resistor in the Resistance Path form, single click to select/zoom, and double click to query. Click the Clear button to clear the RLRP path. The RLRP path will be retained until you click the Clear button. If you select/deselect a resistor on the RLRP path to view its attributes, the RLRP path will continue to be displayed. 
 The Resistance Path form has the following fields, in addition to information on the layer/via name, and node coordinates (location for each segment in the least resistance path): 
 The form shows multiple objects/resistors. You can click the Prev/Next button, or select a resistor from the drop-down list, to view the details of each resistor. 
 The rlrp report at the command prompt contains 2 parts: a detailed report showing each rlrp segment information, and a summary report showing information for each layer. 
 The rail analysis must include RLRP analysis ( set_rail_analysis_mode –enable_rlrp_analysis true ) in order to have the required data for this functionality.
  - Power Gate - specifies if a resistor is identified as a power-gate Ron resistor for easy identification.
  - Resistance (Cumulative) - displays each resistance and cumulative resistance (in bracket) from a voltage source to the specific resistor.
  - Voltage Drop (Cumulative) - displays voltage drop of each resistance (based on the worst IR drop that occurred between two connected nodes), and the cumulative voltage drop (in bracket) from a voltage source to the specific resistor.
  - Auto Zoom Level - enables you to specify the appropriate zoom level and improve visibility. The zoom levels are “+2, +1, 0, 1, 2”. 0 is selected by default. The prefix “+” means zoom-in whereas “-” means zoom-out.
  - Auto Select - allows you to highlight any resistor on the list and the resistor will be auto-zoomed.
  - Show Single Path - allows you to select and view a single RLRP path. For an instance with multiple RLRP paths, the single path display feature allows you to debug the desired path easily.
  - Enable Multi Segments Selection - allows you to select multiple resistors in the RLRP path. When you select the Enable Multi Segments Selection check box and double-click on a row in the Resistance Path form, the Attribute Viewer form appears, as shown below:
10. Click the Apply button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 Selecting Apply will accept the changes, and Reset will set it to default values. By default, the range does not auto update. You can click the Reset button to auto-scale.
11. Experiment with the remaining rail plots, as listed in the following table:

data: The voltage drop (IRdrop) plot is displayed on all the power-grid segments of the analyzed net. The IRdrop data is saved inside the state directory of the net. The worst case voltage can be displayed by selecting the Result Browser option. The segments with high IRdrop can be debugged using the rail analysis plots in conjunction with power plots.

The grid resistance plot displays the resistance of each power-grid segment. This plot does not consider the power distribution when computing resistance thus highlighting power-grid weakness and resistance hot spots in the design irrespective of activity vector coverage. 
 
 This analysis plot allows designers to check power-grid robustness during early stages of the power-grid design when worst case vectors that produce high power and IRdrop are unknown. The plot can be used during decap optimization (dynamic) as well because adding decaps along the path of high resistance has no effect on localized dynamic IRdrop.

The instance effective resistance plot is similar to grid resistance plot except it is plotted for the instances connected to the power-grid. 
 
 The resistance for the instance pin is calculated as total resistance along the least resistance path. If an instance has multiple power pins connected to the power-grid, instance based rlrp plot uses the pin with the worst (highest) resistance value to plot the instance based data. Like grid resistance plot, instance based resistance plot is also independent of power vectors. This allows you to identify weakly connected instances in the design during early stages of power planning. It can be used during the decap optimization as well because adding decaps along the path of high resistance has no effect on localized dynamic IRdrop.

After observing IR drop plots, the next question is how the current flows in the design to create the IR drop plot generated. It is important to examine these plots because they also exhibit behaviors that are not obvious from examining the IR drop plots. These plots show current-flow trends that you might not have expected or current from several power pins reconverging in the middle of the chip to create high current through some wires.

The tap current plot shows the current distribution inside the cell. The power-grid library generation program characterizes the current distribution inside the cell and replaces devices connected to the power-grid with current taps (sink). The relative current distribution inside the cell power-grid view library is based on device width and length. The instance based power calculated during power analysis is fed to rail analysis during IRdrop analysis. The supplied instance power is then distributed inside the cell and can be viewed using tap current plot. 
 
 If accurate power-grid view is used during rail analysis for the memories, the tap current plot clearly highlights the low power (current) distribution in bit cells region and high power distribution in sense amplifier and decoder circuit of the memory. The tap current plot can be used to debug high IR drop caused by high power density in the region.

Once you understand how current flows through your design, you may want to know where the high current densities occur. Use the Current Density analysis type to examine them. 
 
 Current density reporting does not plot the current density of the segment but it is based on the ratio of wire current density over the current density limit that is allowed by the process - (J/Jmax). You therefore want all wires to have a ratio less than 1.0 and debug or fix the current density violations that have greater that 1.0 value. This ratio is easier to work with because its value is more consistent across designs.
The IRdrop plot displays the net-based instance voltage drop. ivdn shows the worst instance voltage drop for each net. To see only one net, you can disable all the nets except one.

ivdd will show the worst instance voltage drop for each power-ground pair. When at least one power and one ground net is selected, the plot will show the worst instance drop value. 
 
 For dynamic analysis, you can also select the type of IV method (best, worst, avg, worst avg) and/or the IV window (switching/timing window, whole window) in the Layers tab of the Power and Rail Plots - Layers/Nets form.

This plot is only applicable to the power-gated design and analysis. 
 
 The power-gate current (I/Idsat) plot displays the ratio of current through the power-gate and the saturation current (Idsat).This plot can be used to analyze and debug whether the current through power-gates exceeds the saturation current. The saturation current and on-resistance of the power-switches are characterized and stored inside the power-grid library of the power-gate cell. A ratio of greater than 1 means that the current requirement of the power-gated block can not be met with placed power-switch instance in that region. Either the power-switch needs to be upsized or more power-gate instances need to be placed. 
 
 In addition to the plot, rail analysis creates the text report (pi.report) and binary ECO file ( vs2fe_pg.rpt ) for Innovus Implementation System (Innovus) inside the state directory. The ECO file can be read inside Innovus using " optPowerSwitch -vsdgInFile " command to optimize power-gates.

The power-gate voltage plot displays the IRdrop across power-switch instances. This plot can be used to analyze and debug the IRdrop inside the power-gated block (e.g. if the IRdrop across power gate is already high, the IRdrop inside the power-gated block will be much higher. In this case, the power-gate placement will have to be refined or more power-gates will have to be added in order to resolve the IRdrop problem).

Resistor sensitivity is calculated by the software as: 
 
 S = delta (Worst Voltage)/ delta (Resistance) in V/Ohm 
 
 Resistor sensitivity analysis is supported only in static rail analysis and can be enabled using the " set_rail_analysis_mode -enable_sensitivity_analysis true " option. By default it is false . The resistor sensitivity for power-grid can be viewed by plotting "rs" analysis type in GUI. The sensitivity analysis can be used as guidance for power-grid optimization. Sensitivity is calculated for all power-grid segments and the resistors with higher sensitivity when optimized have better chance to improve overall IRdrop profile of the power-grid. 
 
 The optimization in this context means reducing overall resistance of the power-grid, e.g. wire widening. Although Voltus stand-alone product does not allow design and netlist changes, it can be used inside the Innovus platform which has wide range of power-grid editing and routing features. Optionally, you can perform what-if analysis ( What-If Rail Analysis ) inside the Voltus environment which allows you to scale resistance of power-grid segments on-the-fly to analyze and estimate the optimization efforts to improve IRdrop profile of the power-grid. 
 
 There are two additional rs plots called rs (wire) and rs (wire group) to display resistor sensitivity on a wire or wire-group basis. Instead of displaying sensitivity of an individual resistor in a wire, these plots display highest sensitivity of a collection of resistors that form a wire or a wire group. These plots are only available when you invoke Voltus or ERA analysis in Innovus where the original power planning was done. The power planning commands define the wire or wire-groups that form the power-grid of the design, and Voltus or ERA is used to analyze sensitivity of these wires or wire-groups and optimize the power-grid using a power-planning ECO file.

lines: The following table lists the static analysis plot types and the corresponding controls to enable them:

data: power gate current I/Idsat (pi) and power gate voltage (pv)
enabled automatically when power-gate(s) are connected to the analyzed net.
enabled automatically when package data is provided (spice model, in power pin location file, or through default_package_* commands)
enabled if EM models are present with set_rail_analysis_mode –process_techgen_em_rules true or –em_models

lines: Generating Text Reports

In addition to auto-generated HTML reports, Voltus can incrementally generate text reports for all analysis types, post analysis. These reports can be generated using the report_power_rail_results command and report option. You are required to load state directory which has rail analysis results. The power reports can be generated after restoring the power database.
Example TCL Flow
# Import design
# Load rail analysis state directory
# IRdrop report
# Resistance analysis report
# Current density (EM) violation report
# Sensitivity analysis report
# Instance-based least resistance path report without violation report
There are two ways to generate static power reports in Voltus:

- Using report_power after restoring power database
- Using report_power_rail_results after restoring power database

Both methods generate report in different format. See Viewing Static Power Analysis Results to view report_power options. In general, report_power has more detailed options to generate power distribution reports, e.g. for design hierarchy, cell type, instance, clock domain, power domain etc. In contrast, report_power_rail_results can only generate global power, activity and frequency reports to highlight worst-case values.
Example TCL flow
OR
Example IR Drop Report
Example Effective Instance Voltage Report
Example Current Density Report

In addition to text reports, you can measure the worst IR drop, total current and capacitance values in the region interactively using " Draw " and " Measure " buttons in Power & Rail Results form. This feature is also supported through TCL command as follows,
read_power_rail_results -rail_directory ALL_25C_dynamic_1/VDD report_power_rail_results -region {0 0 100 100}
Power-Grid Integrity Reports

Rail Analysis generates a single consolidated report containing all power-grid integrity data in the .rpt and .html formats ( net_name.pg_integrity.rpt and net_name.pg_integrity.html ). This report lists all power-grid integrity related problems, such as instances without voltage-source connectivity in PGDB, disconnected PGVs in PGDB, missing cells, physically disconnected cells, and weakly connected power segments.
The format of the PG integrity report in the DP and XP modes is given below.
Distributed Processing Mode
The consolidated report is generated for each net in the state directory at: ../state_dir/<net>/Reports.
The report format is:
* Missing Cell Power-Grid Views - these leaf-level cells are found to be connected to the power net being analyzed but cell power-grid views for these cells are missing.
* Physically Disconnected Instances - all instances listed below are disconnected from the power-grid.
* Instances with Floating Power Pins - instances in this list are physically connected to at least one power pin but one or more power pins are disconnected from the power net.

* Disconnected Wire Segments - lists wire segments are disconnected on top-level and inside the cell which are not connected to voltage source. View unconnected plot and gif file in state directory for graphical representation.
* Weakly Connected Metal Segments - lists wire segments that have no connections to the upper metal layers through via.
* Dropped Voltage Sources - lists voltage sources that could not be inserted on to the power-grid either due to incorrect location or missing power-grid geometry.
Extensively Parallel (XP) Mode
The consolidated report is generated for each net in the state directory at: ../state_dir/reports/rail/<net> .
The report format is:
* Missing Cell Power-Grid Views - these leaf-level cells are found to be connected to the power net being analyzed but cell power-grid views for these cells are missing.
* Unconnected Instance Pins - all instance pins listed below are disconnected from the power-grid.
* Unconnected Nodes and Resistors - all nodes and resistors listed below are not connected to a voltage source.
* Dropped Voltage Sources - lists voltage sources that could not be inserted on to the power-grid either due to incorrect location or missing power-grid geometry.
* Unmapped Package Terminals - lists package terminals that are not mapped to the grid locations or the top-level pins.
* Unconnected/Weakly Connected Pin Nodes - lists pin nodes that are physically connected to instances, but are disconnected from the power net.
Layer-Based IR Drop Report

By default, Voltus generates a layer-based IR drop report for each net in the static and dynamic rail analysis flows. The name of the report is < net_name >. layerbased_ir.rpt , and it is located within the < output directory >/< state_directory >/ Reports /< net_name >/ directory. This report prints the highest and the lowest IR drop on each metal layer. The IR drop report can be used to debug the IR drop contribution from each layer.
The following is the format of the report:
Here,

- Layer is the metal layer name.
- IR drop (volt) is the difference between the maximum IR drop and the minimum IR drop.
- IR drop range is the node voltage range, that is, max(node voltage) –> min(node voltage).
- Elements is the number of nodes.
The following is a snippet from the report:
HTML Reports

HTML reports are automatically generated and can be viewed by selecting the Report menu item from the Tools menu. Select File - Open File to load an html file. The main HTML file that includes links to other reports will be in a directory call Reports in the state directory (i.e. .../PDcore_25C_avg_1/Reports/dma_mac.main.html ).
Once the main html file is loaded, you can scroll down through the various reports. Some of these have additional links that will bring up other sub-reports, as shown below:
In the following form, you can see there is an IR Drop Analysis Report, which has a link to a Layer Based IR Drop Report:
This link will display the layer-based IR drop report, as shown below:

 The rlrp report at the command prompt contains 2 parts: a detailed report showing each rlrp segment information, and a summary report showing information for each layer. 
 The rail analysis must include RLRP analysis ( set_rail_analysis_mode –enable_rlrp_analysis true ) in order to have the required data for this functionality.
  - Power Gate - specifies if a resistor is identified as a power-gate Ron resistor for easy identification.
  - Resistance (Cumulative) - displays each resistance and cumulative resistance (in bracket) from a voltage source to the specific resistor.
  - Voltage Drop (Cumulative) - displays voltage drop of each resistance (based on the worst IR drop that occurred between two connected nodes), and the cumulative voltage drop (in bracket) from a voltage source to the specific resistor.
  - Auto Zoom Level - enables you to specify the appropriate zoom level and improve visibility. The zoom levels are “+2, +1, 0, 1, 2”. 0 is selected by default. The prefix “+” means zoom-in whereas “-” means zoom-out.
  - Auto Select - allows you to highlight any resistor on the list and the resistor will be auto-zoomed.
  - Show Single Path - allows you to select and view a single RLRP path. For an instance with multiple RLRP paths, the single path display feature allows you to debug the desired path easily.
  - Enable Multi Segments Selection - allows you to select multiple resistors in the RLRP path. When you select the Enable Multi Segments Selection check box and double-click on a row in the Resistance Path form, the Attribute Viewer form appears, as shown below:
10. Click the Apply button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 Selecting Apply will accept the changes, and Reset will set it to default values. By default, the range does not auto update. You can click the Reset button to auto-scale.
11. Experiment with the remaining rail plots, as listed in the following table:

data: The voltage drop (IRdrop) plot is displayed on all the power-grid segments of the analyzed net. The IRdrop data is saved inside the state directory of the net. The worst case voltage can be displayed by selecting the Result Browser option. The segments with high IRdrop can be debugged using the rail analysis plots in conjunction with power plots.

The grid resistance plot displays the resistance of each power-grid segment. This plot does not consider the power distribution when computing resistance thus highlighting power-grid weakness and resistance hot spots in the design irrespective of activity vector coverage. 
 
 This analysis plot allows designers to check power-grid robustness during early stages of the power-grid design when worst case vectors that produce high power and IRdrop are unknown. The plot can be used during decap optimization (dynamic) as well because adding decaps along the path of high resistance has no effect on localized dynamic IRdrop.

The instance effective resistance plot is similar to grid resistance plot except it is plotted for the instances connected to the power-grid. 
 
 The resistance for the instance pin is calculated as total resistance along the least resistance path. If an instance has multiple power pins connected to the power-grid, instance based rlrp plot uses the pin with the worst (highest) resistance value to plot the instance based data. Like grid resistance plot, instance based resistance plot is also independent of power vectors. This allows you to identify weakly connected instances in the design during early stages of power planning. It can be used during the decap optimization as well because adding decaps along the path of high resistance has no effect on localized dynamic IRdrop.

After observing IR drop plots, the next question is how the current flows in the design to create the IR drop plot generated. It is important to examine these plots because they also exhibit behaviors that are not obvious from examining the IR drop plots. These plots show current-flow trends that you might not have expected or current from several power pins reconverging in the middle of the chip to create high current through some wires.

The tap current plot shows the current distribution inside the cell. The power-grid library generation program characterizes the current distribution inside the cell and replaces devices connected to the power-grid with current taps (sink). The relative current distribution inside the cell power-grid view library is based on device width and length. The instance based power calculated during power analysis is fed to rail analysis during IRdrop analysis. The supplied instance power is then distributed inside the cell and can be viewed using tap current plot. 
 
 If accurate power-grid view is used during rail analysis for the memories, the tap current plot clearly highlights the low power (current) distribution in bit cells region and high power distribution in sense amplifier and decoder circuit of the memory. The tap current plot can be used to debug high IR drop caused by high power density in the region.

Once you understand how current flows through your design, you may want to know where the high current densities occur. Use the Current Density analysis type to examine them. 
 
 Current density reporting does not plot the current density of the segment but it is based on the ratio of wire current density over the current density limit that is allowed by the process - (J/Jmax). You therefore want all wires to have a ratio less than 1.0 and debug or fix the current density violations that have greater that 1.0 value. This ratio is easier to work with because its value is more consistent across designs.
The IRdrop plot displays the net-based instance voltage drop. ivdn shows the worst instance voltage drop for each net. To see only one net, you can disable all the nets except one.

ivdd will show the worst instance voltage drop for each power-ground pair. When at least one power and one ground net is selected, the plot will show the worst instance drop value. 
 
 For dynamic analysis, you can also select the type of IV method (best, worst, avg, worst avg) and/or the IV window (switching/timing window, whole window) in the Layers tab of the Power and Rail Plots - Layers/Nets form.

This plot is only applicable to the power-gated design and analysis. 
 
 The power-gate current (I/Idsat) plot displays the ratio of current through the power-gate and the saturation current (Idsat).This plot can be used to analyze and debug whether the current through power-gates exceeds the saturation current. The saturation current and on-resistance of the power-switches are characterized and stored inside the power-grid library of the power-gate cell. A ratio of greater than 1 means that the current requirement of the power-gated block can not be met with placed power-switch instance in that region. Either the power-switch needs to be upsized or more power-gate instances need to be placed. 
 
 In addition to the plot, rail analysis creates the text report (pi.report) and binary ECO file ( vs2fe_pg.rpt ) for Innovus Implementation System (Innovus) inside the state directory. The ECO file can be read inside Innovus using " optPowerSwitch -vsdgInFile " command to optimize power-gates.

The power-gate voltage plot displays the IRdrop across power-switch instances. This plot can be used to analyze and debug the IRdrop inside the power-gated block (e.g. if the IRdrop across power gate is already high, the IRdrop inside the power-gated block will be much higher. In this case, the power-gate placement will have to be refined or more power-gates will have to be added in order to resolve the IRdrop problem).

Resistor sensitivity is calculated by the software as: 
 
 S = delta (Worst Voltage)/ delta (Resistance) in V/Ohm 
 
 Resistor sensitivity analysis is supported only in static rail analysis and can be enabled using the " set_rail_analysis_mode -enable_sensitivity_analysis true " option. By default it is false . The resistor sensitivity for power-grid can be viewed by plotting "rs" analysis type in GUI. The sensitivity analysis can be used as guidance for power-grid optimization. Sensitivity is calculated for all power-grid segments and the resistors with higher sensitivity when optimized have better chance to improve overall IRdrop profile of the power-grid. 
 
 The optimization in this context means reducing overall resistance of the power-grid, e.g. wire widening. Although Voltus stand-alone product does not allow design and netlist changes, it can be used inside the Innovus platform which has wide range of power-grid editing and routing features. Optionally, you can perform what-if analysis ( What-If Rail Analysis ) inside the Voltus environment which allows you to scale resistance of power-grid segments on-the-fly to analyze and estimate the optimization efforts to improve IRdrop profile of the power-grid. 
 
 There are two additional rs plots called rs (wire) and rs (wire group) to display resistor sensitivity on a wire or wire-group basis. Instead of displaying sensitivity of an individual resistor in a wire, these plots display highest sensitivity of a collection of resistors that form a wire or a wire group. These plots are only available when you invoke Voltus or ERA analysis in Innovus where the original power planning was done. The power planning commands define the wire or wire-groups that form the power-grid of the design, and Voltus or ERA is used to analyze sensitivity of these wires or wire-groups and optimize the power-grid using a power-planning ECO file.

lines: Generating Text Reports

In addition to auto-generated HTML reports, Voltus can incrementally generate text reports for all analysis types, post analysis. These reports can be generated using the report_power_rail_results command and report option. You are required to load state directory which has rail analysis results. The power reports can be generated after restoring the power database.
Example TCL Flow
# Import design
# Load rail analysis state directory
# IRdrop report
# Resistance analysis report
# Current density (EM) violation report
# Sensitivity analysis report
# Instance-based least resistance path report without violation report
There are two ways to generate static power reports in Voltus:

- Using report_power after restoring power database
- Using report_power_rail_results after restoring power database

Both methods generate report in different format. See Viewing Static Power Analysis Results to view report_power options. In general, report_power has more detailed options to generate power distribution reports, e.g. for design hierarchy, cell type, instance, clock domain, power domain etc. In contrast, report_power_rail_results can only generate global power, activity and frequency reports to highlight worst-case values.
Example TCL flow
OR
Example IR Drop Report
Example Effective Instance Voltage Report
Example Current Density Report

In addition to text reports, you can measure the worst IR drop, total current and capacitance values in the region interactively using " Draw " and " Measure " buttons in Power & Rail Results form. This feature is also supported through TCL command as follows,
read_power_rail_results -rail_directory ALL_25C_dynamic_1/VDD report_power_rail_results -region {0 0 100 100}
Power-Grid Integrity Reports

Rail Analysis generates a single consolidated report containing all power-grid integrity data in the .rpt and .html formats ( net_name.pg_integrity.rpt and net_name.pg_integrity.html ). This report lists all power-grid integrity related problems, such as instances without voltage-source connectivity in PGDB, disconnected PGVs in PGDB, missing cells, physically disconnected cells, and weakly connected power segments.
The format of the PG integrity report in the DP and XP modes is given below.
Distributed Processing Mode
The consolidated report is generated for each net in the state directory at: ../state_dir/<net>/Reports.
The report format is:
* Missing Cell Power-Grid Views - these leaf-level cells are found to be connected to the power net being analyzed but cell power-grid views for these cells are missing.
* Physically Disconnected Instances - all instances listed below are disconnected from the power-grid.
* Instances with Floating Power Pins - instances in this list are physically connected to at least one power pin but one or more power pins are disconnected from the power net.

* Disconnected Wire Segments - lists wire segments are disconnected on top-level and inside the cell which are not connected to voltage source. View unconnected plot and gif file in state directory for graphical representation.
* Weakly Connected Metal Segments - lists wire segments that have no connections to the upper metal layers through via.
* Dropped Voltage Sources - lists voltage sources that could not be inserted on to the power-grid either due to incorrect location or missing power-grid geometry.
Extensively Parallel (XP) Mode
The consolidated report is generated for each net in the state directory at: ../state_dir/reports/rail/<net> .
The report format is:
* Missing Cell Power-Grid Views - these leaf-level cells are found to be connected to the power net being analyzed but cell power-grid views for these cells are missing.
* Unconnected Instance Pins - all instance pins listed below are disconnected from the power-grid.
* Unconnected Nodes and Resistors - all nodes and resistors listed below are not connected to a voltage source.
* Dropped Voltage Sources - lists voltage sources that could not be inserted on to the power-grid either due to incorrect location or missing power-grid geometry.
* Unmapped Package Terminals - lists package terminals that are not mapped to the grid locations or the top-level pins.
* Unconnected/Weakly Connected Pin Nodes - lists pin nodes that are physically connected to instances, but are disconnected from the power net.
Layer-Based IR Drop Report

By default, Voltus generates a layer-based IR drop report for each net in the static and dynamic rail analysis flows. The name of the report is < net_name >. layerbased_ir.rpt , and it is located within the < output directory >/< state_directory >/ Reports /< net_name >/ directory. This report prints the highest and the lowest IR drop on each metal layer. The IR drop report can be used to debug the IR drop contribution from each layer.
The following is the format of the report:
Here,

- Layer is the metal layer name.
- IR drop (volt) is the difference between the maximum IR drop and the minimum IR drop.
- IR drop range is the node voltage range, that is, max(node voltage) –> min(node voltage).
- Elements is the number of nodes.
The following is a snippet from the report:
