title: Constant Current Source Based IR Analysis

lines: Voltus allows you to perform constant current source based IR drop analysis, where power pads are connected with current sources instead of voltage sources. Using this flow, you can perform accurate analysis in design scenarios where voltage regulators are used to ensure constant current supply at power pads. In this mode, there could be higher voltages for some instances, and the hotspot would impact a larger portion of the design.
To run this flow, you must specify:

- a power pad file in the xy format ( set_power_pads -format xy ) to define all the pad locations (voltage/current source locations).
- a power pad file in the xyiv format using the set_power_pads -format parameter. The xyiv format indicates whether the voltage sources specified in the xy power pad location file are current source or reference voltage source. There must be at least one reference voltage source defined in the xyiv file. The format is the same as the xy file, except the additional first and last columns to indicate the current/voltage source and current/voltage value. The name specified in the xyiv file must match the xy file. 
 The format of the xyiv file is: 
 <Notation - I/V> <Current/Voltage Source Name> <X Y Coordinates in micron> <Layer Name> <Value (I in milliampere and V in volts)> 
 The following is an example of power pad file: 
 I ISR1 12.5 0 M3 100
V VSRC1 16.401 18.432 M1 0.8 
 where, I is the current source and V is the reference voltage
- the -enable_ccs_analysis parameter of the set_rail_analysis_ mode command to enable constant current source analysis. The default value is false .
The following is a sample Tcl script to run this flow:

Hotspot Debugger enables you to identify and fix the hotspots in a design. Hotspot Debugger can automatically identify the region with worst IRdrops and EM violations, and provide necessary information to determine and fix the root cause. Using this feature, you can identify whether the hotspot is caused by one or more of the following:

- Data error
- Weak power-grids or missing vias
- High average power density, simultaneous switching
- Low decap density
- Lack of power-gates
- Lack of power bumps
- Package effects
Hotspot Debugger will identify the top violating instances in each region and report the following information:

- Hotspot Region Coordinate
- Timing Library Analysis
- Power-Grid View Analysis
- Power-Grid Integrity Analysis
- IR-Drop Composition
- Root Cause Analysis

Use the debug_irdrop command to identify hotspots. You must specify this command after running the analyze_rail command. For more information on the command and Hot-Spot Debugger report, refer to debug_irdrop in Voltus Text Command Reference .

Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution offers dynamic Power and Rail Analysis on power-grid network. Some of the features include,

- Support for RDL and full-chip GDS for power-grid extraction
- XD and HD accuracy modes for analysis
- Various interactive power-grid analysis plots like IRdrop, current across resistors, device tap currents, IRdrop across package, instance based voltage, grid capacitance, etc.
- CPF Support
  - Power domain definition
  - Power domain operating conditions
  - Off power-switch nets
- Package analysis and die-model generation to take into account the on-chip dynamic switching effects during package design
- Dynamic IRdrop analysis on power-switch design during
  - Steady-state on and off mode
  - Impact of rush current on global power-grid during power-up mode
  - Power-switch ECO for Innovus (optPowerSwitch)
- Dynamic IRdrop waveform with SimVision waveform viewer interface
- Dynamic IRdrop movies
- Dynamic IRdrop waveform for critical path analysis in Tempus Timing Signoff
- Violation browser
- Interactive query
- HTML reports
- GUI and TCL command interface
Voltus offers sign-off quality dynamic power and IRdrop analysis.

Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution requires the use of dynamic power-consumption data for each instance and is output by the power calculator when you perform dynamic power-grid analysis. The current is required to calculate the IR drop. Dynamic, or transient, analysis at the gate level is a way to detect power integrity problems, determine the optimal number and placement of decoupling capacitors to minimize power leakage, provide insight into the effect of simultaneously switching outputs, and assess the impact of resistance, capacitance, and inductance due to packaging, bond wire, and C4 bumps on transient IR drop.

Dynamic power-consumption calculation is a method of analyzing a circuit to obtain operating currents and voltages. It is time-based, analyzing the circuit netlist over a specified period, such as a clock cycle.
The power calculator uses two methods to calculate the dynamic power consumption:

- The vector-driven approach uses the full VCD output of a logic simulator to identify the instances that are switching and when they switch. This method is described in detail in Vector-Driven Methodology .
- The vectorless approach uses timing arrival window information from a static timing analysis tool to determine when instances switch. A timing arrival window describes when a signal can change within a clock cycle. An additional algorithm then determines the instances that switch. This method is called the vectorless, timing-windows-based, or pseudo-dynamic approach. This method is described in detail in Vectorless Methodology .
The following table summarizes where the power calculator obtains signal switching information for both vector-driven and vectorless power-consumption calculation.
Table 9-1 Where the Power Calculator Obtains Signal Switching Information

data: User specified activity and activity propagation

lines: The vectorless methodology can be classified into the following two types:

- Probability-Based - Annotates switching probability to all the cells in the datapath.
- State-Propagation-Based - Annotates logic events (rise/fall) to the cells on the data path by propagating the rise/fall logic events from the flip-flops’ outputs to the downstream logic cones.
The following diagram illustrates probability and state propagation based vectorless methodology:
Probability-Based Vectorless Methodology

In the vectorless, probability-based approach to dynamic power-consumption calculation, the power calculator generates a virtual worst-case power vector using design information and activity and switching constraints that you supply. The challenge posed by vectorless methods of dynamic power-consumption calculation is to obtain information on what instances to consider for switching and when to schedule them to produce a worst-case vector without using actual simulation. The power calculator's vectorless approach creates a realistic, but not too pessimistic, dynamic profile for the design so that you can analyze worst-case dynamic IRdrop in the design.

To determine the time of switching, the power calculator uses the timing database or timing windows file (TWF) generated by a static timing analysis program like Tempus Timing Signoff. In addition to clock domains, slews, slack and constant, it also includes arrival times for each instance pin. The window of arrival times is not an exact time of transition but it is a union of several arrival times combined into one clock cycle, possibly through different combinational paths, at different clock periods. The power calculator uses Monte Carlo algorithm to schedule (instance's exact switching time) inside this timing window.

To determine how often an instance is switching, the power calculator uses activity propagation along with any user specified activity. The known activity data on primary inputs, sequential output, and clock gates further helps generation of accurate and realistic worst-case dynamic profile. See Activity propagation through clock network and clock gates to see how having some partial VCD information can help with power calculation accuracy.

Once the activity and time of switching is determined, the program uses output load and slew to create a weighted average current profile for the instance based on power arcs defined in .lib for the cell. The calculated dynamic currents are saved for all power nets associated with the instance. These current waveforms can be viewed using SimVision waveform viewer interface. The calculated current waveforms are then fed to dynamic rail analysis engine to perform dynamic IRdrop analysis.

Using a TWF file is less accurate than using a VCD file because it contains ranges of information rather than precise arrival times, however, constructing a worst-case dynamic profile offers great improvement in run time over a vector-based solution which is not scaleable for full-chip. Using a TWF file is also faster than using a VCD file, because it requires less preparation. With a VCD file, you must understand how to prepare stimulus to prepare worst case vector that could produce worst-case power and IRdrop.
State-Propagation-Based Vectorless Methodology
The vectorless, state-propagation-based approach can be done using the following two methods:

- User-defined activity - the software will schedule the clock sources, primary inputs, sequential outputs, and macro outputs based on the user-defined activity settings, along with the cells in the fanout cone of such instances. This flow requires the following optional inputs: flip-flop activity, clock network activity (clock gating factor), primary input activity, and macro activity. 
 Each clock cycle simulates a different set of cells to switch based on the following configurable parameters: 
 This flow is enabled by the following command:
 set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorless -enable_state_propagation true 
 The state-propagation-based dynamic analysis generates a switching scenario file ( voltus_power.stateprop.switchsrc ) that contains the switching information of flip-flops. 
 The following is a snippet of the report:
0.0973 DFF11/DFF Q r
0.0987 DFF31/DFF Q f
0.1064 DFF11/DFF QN f
0.1067 DFF31/DFF QN r
.... 
 This report contains the following columns: 
 first column: time values when the flip-flops toggle 
 second column: flip-flop name 
 third column: output pin of the flip-flop 
 fourth column: present logic of the pin, that is, r indicates "rise" and f indicates "fall" 
 For every flip-flop, toggles at both Q and QN outputs are reported in this file. For information on the TCL commands for this flow, see State-Propagation-Based Vectorless Flow with User-Defined Activity .
  - clock domain frequency: Prioritizes scheduling cells associated with faster clocks
  - fanout: Prioritizes scheduling cells with higher fanout
  - user-defined activity: Prioritizes scheduling cells with higher activity
- User-defined power target - the software will utilize the user-defined power targets for full-chip power analysis in the XP mode. You can specify the power targets for full-chip, physical block, logical block, and power/ground net. When these targets are specified, Voltus will automatically scale the toggle rates of clock gate ratio, input, macro, and sequential activities to meet the specified power targets. The tool will run multiple iterations of state propagation with a different activity value (pre-defined upper and lower bounds) for each iteration till the power targets are achieved. 
 This flow is enabled by the following command:
 set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorless -enable_state_propagation true –enable_power_target_flow true In addition, the flow has the following steps:
  - Specify the power target values using the set_power value [–instance/-cell blockname ] [–pg_net netname ] command.
  - Specify whether to keep the activities fixed or automatically adjust the activity values for each iteration. This can be specified using the following parameters: 
 When values are set to fixed for either CGO, input or macro activity, the user-defined values for these parameters (specified with the set_default_switching_activity command) will be honored. For information on the TCL commands for this flow, see State-Propagation-Based Vectorless Flow with User-Defined Power Targets .
    - -keep_clock_gate_ratio_in_iterations true | false
    - -adjust_input_activity_in_iterations true|false
    - -adjust_macro_activity_in_iterations true|false

The VCD file is the output from a gate-level simulation of many vectors and contains all the net switching activity information for the design. The VCD simulation approach to dynamic power-consumption calculation uses the same VCD file as that used for static power-consumption calculation, but unlike the static VCD approach, it uses the information on the switching instances and when they switch. Using output load and slew, it creates current profile for the instance based on power arcs defined in .lib for the cell. The calculated dynamic currents are saved for all power nets associated with the instance. These current waveforms can be viewed using SimVision waveform viewer interface. The accuracy of the current waveform depends on the power tables in the liberty (.lib) file.

Generally the VCD file can be very large in terms of total simulation duration. While static power calculation can process such full VCD file and derive average activity to calculate static power, running dynamic simulation on full VCD is unrealistic due to performance and resource limitation. Hence it is recommended that you identify the power hungry cycles or cycles with high switching activity up-front to perform dynamic power calculation. For information on the VCD profiling feature, see Vector Profiling .
It is recommended that VCD window (start and stop time) for dynamic power calculation does not exceed more than five cycles of dominant clock in the design.
The vector-driven approach for dynamic power-consumption calculation offers several advantages:

- The data that it yields is exact for the given vectors. For a given vector, you know exactly what is switching and when.
- You can use it to validate the vectorless, timing-windows based simulation.
- It is a well-understood methodology, if power-hungry cycles are known and is therefore easy to develop.
Vector Profiling

The Vector profiling feature enables you to identify windows with maximum activity and power which can then be used to drive dynamic vector-based power analysis. Voltus supports event-based vector profiling, wherein the vector profiler computes power profile of every event on each net. Event-based peak power profiling enables you to accurately capture vectors that could produce peak power using a very small resolution. The recommended step size for event-based profiling is two times the fastest clock, and 100 steps are created by default.
The following example illustrates how internal power is calculated in event-based power profiling:

In the 1st period, pin A has a fall transition and pin B is stable at 2.5ns, pin B has a fall transition and pin A is stable at 5ns, and pin A has a rise transition at 7.5ns. Vector profiling calculates power as and when an event happens. Here,

- For the 1st period, power will be calculated as summation of events happening at 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, and 10ns.
- For the 2nd period, power will be calculated as summation of events happening at 12.5 and 17.5ns.
In this example, internal power using event-based power profiling is calculated as:
The Rise and Fall Energy values are derived from the Liberty file.

To perform Vector profiling, you must provide basic data such as the verilog netlist, SDC file, Liberty files (.libs), SPEF file, DEF file, and the vector files (VCD, TCF, FSDB, SAIF, PHY, or SHM). Use the read_activity_file -format command to specify the file format.

The Power Analysis engine generates a text based profiling report and profiling database, which can be viewed using the SimVision interface, based on the options defined with the report_power and set_power_analysis_mode commands.
The Vector profiling flow can be described as follows:
Combining Vector Profiling and Power Analysis

You can combine Vector Profiling and Power Analysis in a single run by first running Vector Profiling, and then running Static and Dynamic power analysis on the worst case window identified by Vector Profiler.

This is done by storing the worst case window from the last profiler run as variables $worst_power_window_start and $worst_power_window_end. You can specify these variables to set the -start and -end parameters of the read_activity_file command. When specified, the software takes the worst case start and end times from the vector profiling run.
The use model of this flow is:

You can perform dynamic power analysis and vector profiling for multiple windows by using the set_power_analysis_mode - worst_window_count parameter. This parameter allows you to specify the number of worst windows from the last vector profile run to be used for dynamic power analysis. If this parameter is not specified, power analysis is performed on the single worst window.
Power Density Aware Vector Profiling

The Vector Profiler flow identifies worst activity and worst average power cycles in the VCD/FSDB that are then used for detailed dynamic power and IRdrop analysis. However, if you need to take into account core regions of high power density that could produce high dynamic switching current and IRdrop, use tile dimension for power density calculation. In this flow, you can specify the number of physical tiles, and the tool will:

- Compute power density in each tile at every time step
- Track which tiles have worst power density during VCD profile
- Sort vectors based on worst-case power density
To run this flow, you must specify the following parameters:
The use model of this flow is:
The format of the vector profile report is:
The following is a sample report:

Detailed Tile Profile For Each Step Step(ns) Instance Power(mW) Internal Power(mW) Switching Power(mW) Leakage Power(mW) Activity ------------------------------------------------------------------------- 0.000 to 1.000 Top 2.408 2.384 0.02222 0.001835 7 ...
Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis Flow
This section describes how to set up the dynamic vectorless power analysis mode and then run power analysis.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- Timing/Power Libraries (.lib)
- SDC or TWF
- SPEF 
 
Static Power Analysis should be run first and all static problems should be resolved prior to running dynamic analysis.
Procedure
To perform dynamic vectorless power analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Specify the CPF file for power domain information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Load/Commit CPF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_domain -cpf ../design/super_filter.cpf
3. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read SPEF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -decoupled\ ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
4. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVecLessPowerResults
5. Define the power analysis mode through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power Rail - Set Power Analysis Mode . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorlessPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless 
 
 
 
 
 
 This Tcl command is the same as for the static power analysis, except that -method must be dynamic_vectorless .
6. Define the power of an instance or cell. For dynamic analysis, you can assign a piece-wise linear waveform to an instance. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power -reset 
 
 set_power -pg_net VDD_AO -pwl -instance instA { 0ns 0mA 0.075ns 0mA 0.175ns 45mA 0.225ns 15mA 0.425ns 0mA } -sticky
  - You can also specify power-grid views using the set_power_analysis_mode -power_grid_library parameter, instead of specifying .lib or defining a power value with set_power .
  - Time and current units must always be in ns and mA.
  - -sticky will apply the waveform as specified in the simulation. If -sticky is not specified for -pwl , the waveform will be applied each time the output toggles.
  - If you are using a VCD file and start the simulation at some point other than t=0 , the simulator will consider that start time t=0 .
7. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
8. Run power analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 report_power -outfile dyn.rpt 
 
 #Generate additional report formats 
 
 report_power -instances {u0} -outfile u0.rpt 
 
 report_power -instances {*} -outfile all.rpt 
 
 report_power -instances {ring} -outfile ring.rpt 
 
 
 
 The Voltus console will display power statistics, as shown in the following snippet: 
 Total Power 
 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Total Internal Power: 2.30917507 64.6507% 
 Total Switching Power: 1.03379946 28.9436% 
 Total Leakage Power: 0.22879451 6.4056% 
 Total Power: 3.57176907 
 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Ended Static Power Report Generation: (cpu=0:00:00, real=0:00:00, 
 mem(process/total/peak)=750.07MB/1877.45MB/750.07MB) 
 
 Begin Creating Binary Database 
 Ended Creating Binary Database: (cpu=0:00:00, real=0:00:00, 
 mem(process/total/peak)=750.34MB/1877.45MB/750.34MB)
  - Select Power Rail - Run Power Analysis to access the Run Power Analysis form that can be used to specify cell/instance power ( set_power ), toggle rates ( set_switching_activity and set_default_switching_activity ), power result directory ( set_power_output_dir ), and then run power analysis ( report_power ).
  - Select Power Rail - Textual Reports - Power Analysis to access the Report Power form that can be used to generate incremental power reports.
9. View the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy or the whole design. For more information, refer to Viewing Dynamic Current Waveforms .
Output
The power output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the binary database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -binary_db_name parameter.. The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.

The name of the power report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. The power report summarizes power consumption, by groups (sequential cells, macros, clocks), power net, and clock domain.
This is the static current file for each net. The set_power_analysis_mode -write_static_currents parameterwill generate the current files that are required for rail analysis.
This is the dynamic current file for each net. This file allows you to view the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy or the whole design.

lines: Viewing Dynamic Current Waveforms

Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution has embedded SimVision waveform viewer to analyze the generated dynamic current waveforms. You can view the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy, or the whole design.
Required Input Files

- Waveform files
- Power database generated during power calculation
Procedure
To view dynamic power (current) waveforms in GUI, the following steps are needed:

1. Select Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms . 
 The following form appears:
2. Select the Power (Current) Waveforms option.
3. To plot an instance current waveform:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window. 
 You can query the waveforms of all the instances of the selected power net.
  2. Perform one of the following steps to select instances:
    - Select an instance from the list box, as shown below:
    - Filter instances using the filter pattern. To filter instances, enter a pattern ( ref_clk* ) in the Pattern field, and click Instances . The required instance ( ref_clk__L1_I0 ) appears in the list box, as shown below: 
 
 
 You can click the " X " button in the Pattern field to clear the text in the field.
    - Select instances from the Layout GUI, and click the Select From GUI button, as shown below:
  3. Click the Plot button. 
 The SimVision plot appears.
4. To plot current for the entire design:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Select Total Current from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  3. Click the Plot button.
5. To plot a composite dynamic current profile for a clock domain:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Click the browse button next to the Power Database field, and select a database file from the Open Power DB File browser window.
  3. Select Clock from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  4. Select a clock net to produce composite dynamic waveform. Optionally, select the " all " option to produce total composite dynamic waveform for all clock domains.
  5. Click the Plot button.
6. To plot composite dynamic waveform for a hierarchical module:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Select Hierarchy from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  3. Specify a hierarchy name to generate its total dynamic current profile in the text field.
  4. Click the Plot button. 
 
The View Waveforms form supports multi-threading to enable the search of instances in parallel, and thereby improve the search time in large designs. Multi-threading is supported for instance search using both the Instances and the Select From GUI buttons.For this feature, you must set up multi-CPU usage before accessing the View Waveforms form, as shown below:
 set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu number_of_cpus
TCL Commands for Viewing Waveforms

data:
##TCL command for viewing instance current waveform: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -instance inst1 
 
 
 
 ##TCL command for viewing composite total current waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -composite_waveform_type total_current 
 
 
 
 ##TCL Command for viewing composite clock waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -power_db power.db \ -composite_waveform_type clock \ -composite_waveform_name clk 
 
 
 
 ##TCL Command for viewing hierarchical waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -composite_waveform_type hierarchy \ -composite_waveform_name ethernet_mac_2

lines: Dynamic Vector-based Power Analysis Flow
This section describes how to combine vector profiling and power analysis for the worst activity windows.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- SPEF
- Activity file
Procedure
To perform vector profiling, complete the following steps:

1. Repeat the steps 1-3 given in the Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis Flow section to load the design data, CPF, and the SPEF file.
2. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVectorbasedPowerResults
3. Specify the activity file. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -report_missing_nets true 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format VCD -scope FIR_TB.Unit -start {} -end {} -block {} ../design/ncsim.vcd
  - The -report_missing_nets parameter gives a list of nets in the design that are not annotated by VCD. This is useful when pipe-cleaning the flow. The default value is false .
  - Voltus can read activity files in the following formats: VCD, TCF, SAIF, FSDB, PHY, and SHM.
4. Run vector profiling by defining the vector profile power analysis mode to identify where the maximum activity is taking place. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 Event-based Vector Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorbasedPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method vector_profile \ 
 
 -worst_step_size 2ns \ 
 
 -write_profiling_db true \ 
 
 -worst_window_count 2 
 
 report_power -nworst 5 -outfile eventbased_profiler.rpt -time_based_report 
 
 The following are options required for the different types of profiling: 
 
 Activity Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {activity} 
 
 report_power - outfile activity_profiler.rpt 
 
 Power Density Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {power_density} 
 
 report_power - outfile tiling -nworst 5 -time_based_report -pg_net {VDD VSS} -power_density_tiles_row_col { 5 5 } 
 
 
 Note:
  - This Tcl command is the same as for the static power analysis or dynamic vectorless flow, except that -method must be vector_profile .
  - Use the -worst_window_count parameter to perform dynamic power analysis and vector profiling for multiple windows.
5. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
6. Run dynamic power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorbased 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format SHM ADDER.trn -scope adder_tb_e/u1 -start ${worst_power_window_start} -end ${worst_power_window_end} 
 
 report_power 
 
 
 
 The worst start/end activity windows are stored in memory through the Tcl variables $worst_power_window_start and $worst_power_window_end specified using the read_activity_file command.
The Voltus console will display vector profiling statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The power output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the binary database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -binary_db_name parameter. The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.

The name of the vector profile report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. 
 
 Average Vector Profiling : The report details the average power of all events in the user-specified window. This report includes a summary based on the power components, cell type, power rail, and clock network. The worst window report is captured in a file specified with the - outfile parameter, and the report is suffixed by ‘ .avgpower ’. When - outfile is not specified, the vectroprofile.report.avgpower report file is created. 
 Activity Vector Profiling : The report generates only the worst activity report, and does not generate the average power report. The name of the file is as specified using the - outfile parameter. 
 Power Density Profiling : The report generates power for each tile of the design, where design can be considered to be physically divided into equal area tiles. By default, a design is divided in 10x10 tiles.

The name of the profiling database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -write_profiling_db parameter. The profiling database is a binary database of the profile data and can be viewed with SimVision. It allows you to visualize the activity of the design.

lines: Viewing Profiling Database
Voltus uses the SimVision waveform viewer to visualize the activity of the design.
Required Input Files
- Binary database of the profile data (.dsn and .trn files)
Procedure
To view the activity of the design in the GUI, the following steps are needed.

1. Select Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms . 
 The View Waveforms form appears.
2. Select the Profiling Histograms option.
3. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a database file ( file .trn ) from the Select a File browser window.
4. Click the Plot button. 
 The SimVision window appears.
5. In the Design Browser part of the window, click on the database folder in the upper section. 
 The folder content will appear in the lower section, as shown below:
6. Click on a node in the lower section, and the waveform will appear in the waveform window.
7. Select View -> Zoom -> Full X and Full Y to see the full waveform, as shown below:
8. You can zoom in and view the activity over time.
TCL Commands for Viewing Profiling Database

data:
##TCL command for viewing profiling database: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform -type profile \ -waveform_files {vectorprofile.report.trn}

lines: Additional Tcl Command Examples
Vector-Based Flow - Multiple VCD/FSDB Files
The TCL commands for dynamic vector-based power analysis are shown below.

You can specify multiple VCD/FSDB files with different windows for the vector-based dynamic analysis/vector profiling flow and write out a single dynamic current file/profiling report. The TCL commands for dynamic vectorbased power analysis using multiple VCD files are shown below:
By default, the software aligns all signals at t=0 (or min start time). You can disable the start time alignment by setting set_power_analysis_mode -start_time_alignment to false .
#Simulation period is max(endtime - starttime), that is, 11ns.
#Start times for all VCDs will be aligned at t=0

You can specify the read_activity_file command multiple times to read multiple VCD files. When you specify the -start_time_shift parameter of this command, the software first aligns all the signals of the specified activity files at time zero, and then shifts the start time of the activity file specified with the -start_time_shift parameter. A sample Tcl command to illustrate this is given below:
#Simulation period is max(endtime - starttime), that is, 11ns.
#Start times for the top + block2 VCD will be aligned at t=0 whereas for block1.vcd it will start at 2ns.
Probability-Based Vectorless Flow
The TCL commands for dynamic probability-based vectorless power analysis are shown below.
You can use the set_dc_sources command to specify the operating voltages of the power/ground nets. This command is used to specify a global voltage or voltages for specific power nets in the design.
# To enable multi-threading in dynamic vectorless flow
State-Propagation-Based Vectorless Flow with User-Defined Activity
The TCL commands for dynamic state-propagation-based vectorless power analysis are shown below.

Voltus generates a time varying switching profile report ( voltus_power.togglestats ) in the output directory for both the vector-based and state-propagation-based vectorless dynamic power analysis flows. The report includes the number of output toggles with the corresponding simulation time.
The following is a snippet of the report:
Following is a sample of a detailed vector profile report:
State-Propagation-Based Vectorless Flow with User-Defined Power Targets
The TCL commands for state-propagation-based vectorless power analysis with user-defined power targets are shown below.
Example1: Enable the power target flow with fixed CGO
In this case, the clock gate output is fixed, while the values for input, macro, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the power target of 3mW.
Example2: Enable the power target flow with a single power target of 3mW
In this case, the values for input, macro, clock gate output, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the power target of 3mW.
Example3: Enable the power target flow with two power targets
In this case, the values for input, macro, clock gate output, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the two power targets.

The Vector profiling feature enables you to identify windows with maximum activity and power which can then be used to drive dynamic vector-based power analysis. Voltus supports event-based vector profiling, wherein the vector profiler computes power profile of every event on each net. Event-based peak power profiling enables you to accurately capture vectors that could produce peak power using a very small resolution. The recommended step size for event-based profiling is two times the fastest clock, and 100 steps are created by default.
The following example illustrates how internal power is calculated in event-based power profiling:

In the 1st period, pin A has a fall transition and pin B is stable at 2.5ns, pin B has a fall transition and pin A is stable at 5ns, and pin A has a rise transition at 7.5ns. Vector profiling calculates power as and when an event happens. Here,

- For the 1st period, power will be calculated as summation of events happening at 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, and 10ns.
- For the 2nd period, power will be calculated as summation of events happening at 12.5 and 17.5ns.
In this example, internal power using event-based power profiling is calculated as:
The Rise and Fall Energy values are derived from the Liberty file.

To perform Vector profiling, you must provide basic data such as the verilog netlist, SDC file, Liberty files (.libs), SPEF file, DEF file, and the vector files (VCD, TCF, FSDB, SAIF, PHY, or SHM). Use the read_activity_file -format command to specify the file format.

The Power Analysis engine generates a text based profiling report and profiling database, which can be viewed using the SimVision interface, based on the options defined with the report_power and set_power_analysis_mode commands.
The Vector profiling flow can be described as follows:
Combining Vector Profiling and Power Analysis

You can combine Vector Profiling and Power Analysis in a single run by first running Vector Profiling, and then running Static and Dynamic power analysis on the worst case window identified by Vector Profiler.

This is done by storing the worst case window from the last profiler run as variables $worst_power_window_start and $worst_power_window_end. You can specify these variables to set the -start and -end parameters of the read_activity_file command. When specified, the software takes the worst case start and end times from the vector profiling run.
The use model of this flow is:

You can perform dynamic power analysis and vector profiling for multiple windows by using the set_power_analysis_mode - worst_window_count parameter. This parameter allows you to specify the number of worst windows from the last vector profile run to be used for dynamic power analysis. If this parameter is not specified, power analysis is performed on the single worst window.
Power Density Aware Vector Profiling

The Vector Profiler flow identifies worst activity and worst average power cycles in the VCD/FSDB that are then used for detailed dynamic power and IRdrop analysis. However, if you need to take into account core regions of high power density that could produce high dynamic switching current and IRdrop, use tile dimension for power density calculation. In this flow, you can specify the number of physical tiles, and the tool will:

- Compute power density in each tile at every time step
- Track which tiles have worst power density during VCD profile
- Sort vectors based on worst-case power density
To run this flow, you must specify the following parameters:
The use model of this flow is:
The format of the vector profile report is:
The following is a sample report:

Detailed Tile Profile For Each Step Step(ns) Instance Power(mW) Internal Power(mW) Switching Power(mW) Leakage Power(mW) Activity ------------------------------------------------------------------------- 0.000 to 1.000 Top 2.408 2.384 0.02222 0.001835 7 ...
Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis Flow
This section describes how to set up the dynamic vectorless power analysis mode and then run power analysis.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- Timing/Power Libraries (.lib)
- SDC or TWF
- SPEF 
 
Static Power Analysis should be run first and all static problems should be resolved prior to running dynamic analysis.
Procedure
To perform dynamic vectorless power analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Specify the CPF file for power domain information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Load/Commit CPF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_domain -cpf ../design/super_filter.cpf
3. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read SPEF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -decoupled\ ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
4. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVecLessPowerResults
5. Define the power analysis mode through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power Rail - Set Power Analysis Mode . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorlessPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless 
 
 
 
 
 
 This Tcl command is the same as for the static power analysis, except that -method must be dynamic_vectorless .
6. Define the power of an instance or cell. For dynamic analysis, you can assign a piece-wise linear waveform to an instance. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power -reset 
 
 set_power -pg_net VDD_AO -pwl -instance instA { 0ns 0mA 0.075ns 0mA 0.175ns 45mA 0.225ns 15mA 0.425ns 0mA } -sticky
  - You can also specify power-grid views using the set_power_analysis_mode -power_grid_library parameter, instead of specifying .lib or defining a power value with set_power .
  - Time and current units must always be in ns and mA.
  - -sticky will apply the waveform as specified in the simulation. If -sticky is not specified for -pwl , the waveform will be applied each time the output toggles.
  - If you are using a VCD file and start the simulation at some point other than t=0 , the simulator will consider that start time t=0 .
7. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
8. Run power analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 report_power -outfile dyn.rpt 
 
 #Generate additional report formats 
 
 report_power -instances {u0} -outfile u0.rpt 
 
 report_power -instances {*} -outfile all.rpt 
 
 report_power -instances {ring} -outfile ring.rpt 
 
 
 
 The Voltus console will display power statistics, as shown in the following snippet: 
 Total Power 
 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Total Internal Power: 2.30917507 64.6507% 
 Total Switching Power: 1.03379946 28.9436% 
 Total Leakage Power: 0.22879451 6.4056% 
 Total Power: 3.57176907 
 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Ended Static Power Report Generation: (cpu=0:00:00, real=0:00:00, 
 mem(process/total/peak)=750.07MB/1877.45MB/750.07MB) 
 
 Begin Creating Binary Database 
 Ended Creating Binary Database: (cpu=0:00:00, real=0:00:00, 
 mem(process/total/peak)=750.34MB/1877.45MB/750.34MB)
  - Select Power Rail - Run Power Analysis to access the Run Power Analysis form that can be used to specify cell/instance power ( set_power ), toggle rates ( set_switching_activity and set_default_switching_activity ), power result directory ( set_power_output_dir ), and then run power analysis ( report_power ).
  - Select Power Rail - Textual Reports - Power Analysis to access the Report Power form that can be used to generate incremental power reports.
9. View the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy or the whole design. For more information, refer to Viewing Dynamic Current Waveforms .
Output
The power output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the binary database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -binary_db_name parameter.. The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.

The name of the power report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. The power report summarizes power consumption, by groups (sequential cells, macros, clocks), power net, and clock domain.
This is the static current file for each net. The set_power_analysis_mode -write_static_currents parameterwill generate the current files that are required for rail analysis.
This is the dynamic current file for each net. This file allows you to view the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy or the whole design.

lines: Viewing Dynamic Current Waveforms

Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution has embedded SimVision waveform viewer to analyze the generated dynamic current waveforms. You can view the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy, or the whole design.
Required Input Files

- Waveform files
- Power database generated during power calculation
Procedure
To view dynamic power (current) waveforms in GUI, the following steps are needed:

1. Select Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms . 
 The following form appears:
2. Select the Power (Current) Waveforms option.
3. To plot an instance current waveform:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window. 
 You can query the waveforms of all the instances of the selected power net.
  2. Perform one of the following steps to select instances:
    - Select an instance from the list box, as shown below:
    - Filter instances using the filter pattern. To filter instances, enter a pattern ( ref_clk* ) in the Pattern field, and click Instances . The required instance ( ref_clk__L1_I0 ) appears in the list box, as shown below: 
 
 
 You can click the " X " button in the Pattern field to clear the text in the field.
    - Select instances from the Layout GUI, and click the Select From GUI button, as shown below:
  3. Click the Plot button. 
 The SimVision plot appears.
4. To plot current for the entire design:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Select Total Current from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  3. Click the Plot button.
5. To plot a composite dynamic current profile for a clock domain:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Click the browse button next to the Power Database field, and select a database file from the Open Power DB File browser window.
  3. Select Clock from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  4. Select a clock net to produce composite dynamic waveform. Optionally, select the " all " option to produce total composite dynamic waveform for all clock domains.
  5. Click the Plot button.
6. To plot composite dynamic waveform for a hierarchical module:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Select Hierarchy from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  3. Specify a hierarchy name to generate its total dynamic current profile in the text field.
  4. Click the Plot button. 
 
The View Waveforms form supports multi-threading to enable the search of instances in parallel, and thereby improve the search time in large designs. Multi-threading is supported for instance search using both the Instances and the Select From GUI buttons.For this feature, you must set up multi-CPU usage before accessing the View Waveforms form, as shown below:
 set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu number_of_cpus
TCL Commands for Viewing Waveforms

data:
##TCL command for viewing instance current waveform: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -instance inst1 
 
 
 
 ##TCL command for viewing composite total current waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -composite_waveform_type total_current 
 
 
 
 ##TCL Command for viewing composite clock waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -power_db power.db \ -composite_waveform_type clock \ -composite_waveform_name clk 
 
 
 
 ##TCL Command for viewing hierarchical waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -composite_waveform_type hierarchy \ -composite_waveform_name ethernet_mac_2

lines: Dynamic Vector-based Power Analysis Flow
This section describes how to combine vector profiling and power analysis for the worst activity windows.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- SPEF
- Activity file
Procedure
To perform vector profiling, complete the following steps:

1. Repeat the steps 1-3 given in the Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis Flow section to load the design data, CPF, and the SPEF file.
2. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVectorbasedPowerResults
3. Specify the activity file. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -report_missing_nets true 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format VCD -scope FIR_TB.Unit -start {} -end {} -block {} ../design/ncsim.vcd
  - The -report_missing_nets parameter gives a list of nets in the design that are not annotated by VCD. This is useful when pipe-cleaning the flow. The default value is false .
  - Voltus can read activity files in the following formats: VCD, TCF, SAIF, FSDB, PHY, and SHM.
4. Run vector profiling by defining the vector profile power analysis mode to identify where the maximum activity is taking place. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 Event-based Vector Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorbasedPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method vector_profile \ 
 
 -worst_step_size 2ns \ 
 
 -write_profiling_db true \ 
 
 -worst_window_count 2 
 
 report_power -nworst 5 -outfile eventbased_profiler.rpt -time_based_report 
 
 The following are options required for the different types of profiling: 
 
 Activity Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {activity} 
 
 report_power - outfile activity_profiler.rpt 
 
 Power Density Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {power_density} 
 
 report_power - outfile tiling -nworst 5 -time_based_report -pg_net {VDD VSS} -power_density_tiles_row_col { 5 5 } 
 
 
 Note:
  - This Tcl command is the same as for the static power analysis or dynamic vectorless flow, except that -method must be vector_profile .
  - Use the -worst_window_count parameter to perform dynamic power analysis and vector profiling for multiple windows.
5. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
6. Run dynamic power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorbased 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format SHM ADDER.trn -scope adder_tb_e/u1 -start ${worst_power_window_start} -end ${worst_power_window_end} 
 
 report_power 
 
 
 
 The worst start/end activity windows are stored in memory through the Tcl variables $worst_power_window_start and $worst_power_window_end specified using the read_activity_file command.
The Voltus console will display vector profiling statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The power output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the binary database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -binary_db_name parameter. The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.

The name of the vector profile report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. 
 
 Average Vector Profiling : The report details the average power of all events in the user-specified window. This report includes a summary based on the power components, cell type, power rail, and clock network. The worst window report is captured in a file specified with the - outfile parameter, and the report is suffixed by ‘ .avgpower ’. When - outfile is not specified, the vectroprofile.report.avgpower report file is created. 
 Activity Vector Profiling : The report generates only the worst activity report, and does not generate the average power report. The name of the file is as specified using the - outfile parameter. 
 Power Density Profiling : The report generates power for each tile of the design, where design can be considered to be physically divided into equal area tiles. By default, a design is divided in 10x10 tiles.

The name of the profiling database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -write_profiling_db parameter. The profiling database is a binary database of the profile data and can be viewed with SimVision. It allows you to visualize the activity of the design.

- Track which tiles have worst power density during VCD profile
- Sort vectors based on worst-case power density
To run this flow, you must specify the following parameters:
The use model of this flow is:
The format of the vector profile report is:
The following is a sample report:

Detailed Tile Profile For Each Step Step(ns) Instance Power(mW) Internal Power(mW) Switching Power(mW) Leakage Power(mW) Activity ------------------------------------------------------------------------- 0.000 to 1.000 Top 2.408 2.384 0.02222 0.001835 7 ...
Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis Flow
This section describes how to set up the dynamic vectorless power analysis mode and then run power analysis.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- Timing/Power Libraries (.lib)
- SDC or TWF
- SPEF 
 
Static Power Analysis should be run first and all static problems should be resolved prior to running dynamic analysis.
Procedure
To perform dynamic vectorless power analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Specify the CPF file for power domain information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Load/Commit CPF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_domain -cpf ../design/super_filter.cpf
3. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read SPEF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -decoupled\ ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
4. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVecLessPowerResults
5. Define the power analysis mode through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power Rail - Set Power Analysis Mode . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorlessPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless 
 
 
 
 
 
 This Tcl command is the same as for the static power analysis, except that -method must be dynamic_vectorless .
6. Define the power of an instance or cell. For dynamic analysis, you can assign a piece-wise linear waveform to an instance. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power -reset 
 
 set_power -pg_net VDD_AO -pwl -instance instA { 0ns 0mA 0.075ns 0mA 0.175ns 45mA 0.225ns 15mA 0.425ns 0mA } -sticky
  - You can also specify power-grid views using the set_power_analysis_mode -power_grid_library parameter, instead of specifying .lib or defining a power value with set_power .
  - Time and current units must always be in ns and mA.
  - -sticky will apply the waveform as specified in the simulation. If -sticky is not specified for -pwl , the waveform will be applied each time the output toggles.
  - If you are using a VCD file and start the simulation at some point other than t=0 , the simulator will consider that start time t=0 .
7. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
8. Run power analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 report_power -outfile dyn.rpt 
 
 #Generate additional report formats 
 
 report_power -instances {u0} -outfile u0.rpt 
 
 report_power -instances {*} -outfile all.rpt 
 
 report_power -instances {ring} -outfile ring.rpt 
 
 
 
 The Voltus console will display power statistics, as shown in the following snippet: 
 Total Power 
 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Total Internal Power: 2.30917507 64.6507% 
 Total Switching Power: 1.03379946 28.9436% 
 Total Leakage Power: 0.22879451 6.4056% 
 Total Power: 3.57176907 
 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Ended Static Power Report Generation: (cpu=0:00:00, real=0:00:00, 
 mem(process/total/peak)=750.07MB/1877.45MB/750.07MB) 
 
 Begin Creating Binary Database 
 Ended Creating Binary Database: (cpu=0:00:00, real=0:00:00, 
 mem(process/total/peak)=750.34MB/1877.45MB/750.34MB)
  - Select Power Rail - Run Power Analysis to access the Run Power Analysis form that can be used to specify cell/instance power ( set_power ), toggle rates ( set_switching_activity and set_default_switching_activity ), power result directory ( set_power_output_dir ), and then run power analysis ( report_power ).
  - Select Power Rail - Textual Reports - Power Analysis to access the Report Power form that can be used to generate incremental power reports.
9. View the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy or the whole design. For more information, refer to Viewing Dynamic Current Waveforms .
Output
The power output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the binary database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -binary_db_name parameter.. The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.

The name of the power report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. The power report summarizes power consumption, by groups (sequential cells, macros, clocks), power net, and clock domain.
This is the static current file for each net. The set_power_analysis_mode -write_static_currents parameterwill generate the current files that are required for rail analysis.
This is the dynamic current file for each net. This file allows you to view the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy or the whole design.

lines: Viewing Dynamic Current Waveforms

Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution has embedded SimVision waveform viewer to analyze the generated dynamic current waveforms. You can view the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy, or the whole design.
Required Input Files

- Waveform files
- Power database generated during power calculation
Procedure
To view dynamic power (current) waveforms in GUI, the following steps are needed:

1. Select Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms . 
 The following form appears:
2. Select the Power (Current) Waveforms option.
3. To plot an instance current waveform:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window. 
 You can query the waveforms of all the instances of the selected power net.
  2. Perform one of the following steps to select instances:
    - Select an instance from the list box, as shown below:
    - Filter instances using the filter pattern. To filter instances, enter a pattern ( ref_clk* ) in the Pattern field, and click Instances . The required instance ( ref_clk__L1_I0 ) appears in the list box, as shown below: 
 
 
 You can click the " X " button in the Pattern field to clear the text in the field.
    - Select instances from the Layout GUI, and click the Select From GUI button, as shown below:
  3. Click the Plot button. 
 The SimVision plot appears.
4. To plot current for the entire design:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Select Total Current from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  3. Click the Plot button.
5. To plot a composite dynamic current profile for a clock domain:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Click the browse button next to the Power Database field, and select a database file from the Open Power DB File browser window.
  3. Select Clock from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  4. Select a clock net to produce composite dynamic waveform. Optionally, select the " all " option to produce total composite dynamic waveform for all clock domains.
  5. Click the Plot button.
6. To plot composite dynamic waveform for a hierarchical module:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Select Hierarchy from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  3. Specify a hierarchy name to generate its total dynamic current profile in the text field.
  4. Click the Plot button. 
 
The View Waveforms form supports multi-threading to enable the search of instances in parallel, and thereby improve the search time in large designs. Multi-threading is supported for instance search using both the Instances and the Select From GUI buttons.For this feature, you must set up multi-CPU usage before accessing the View Waveforms form, as shown below:
 set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu number_of_cpus
TCL Commands for Viewing Waveforms

data:
##TCL command for viewing instance current waveform: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -instance inst1 
 
 
 
 ##TCL command for viewing composite total current waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -composite_waveform_type total_current 
 
 
 
 ##TCL Command for viewing composite clock waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -power_db power.db \ -composite_waveform_type clock \ -composite_waveform_name clk 
 
 
 
 ##TCL Command for viewing hierarchical waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -composite_waveform_type hierarchy \ -composite_waveform_name ethernet_mac_2

lines: Dynamic Vector-based Power Analysis Flow
This section describes how to combine vector profiling and power analysis for the worst activity windows.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- SPEF
- Activity file
Procedure
To perform vector profiling, complete the following steps:

1. Repeat the steps 1-3 given in the Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis Flow section to load the design data, CPF, and the SPEF file.
2. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVectorbasedPowerResults
3. Specify the activity file. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -report_missing_nets true 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format VCD -scope FIR_TB.Unit -start {} -end {} -block {} ../design/ncsim.vcd
  - The -report_missing_nets parameter gives a list of nets in the design that are not annotated by VCD. This is useful when pipe-cleaning the flow. The default value is false .
  - Voltus can read activity files in the following formats: VCD, TCF, SAIF, FSDB, PHY, and SHM.
4. Run vector profiling by defining the vector profile power analysis mode to identify where the maximum activity is taking place. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 Event-based Vector Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorbasedPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method vector_profile \ 
 
 -worst_step_size 2ns \ 
 
 -write_profiling_db true \ 
 
 -worst_window_count 2 
 
 report_power -nworst 5 -outfile eventbased_profiler.rpt -time_based_report 
 
 The following are options required for the different types of profiling: 
 
 Activity Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {activity} 
 
 report_power - outfile activity_profiler.rpt 
 
 Power Density Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {power_density} 
 
 report_power - outfile tiling -nworst 5 -time_based_report -pg_net {VDD VSS} -power_density_tiles_row_col { 5 5 } 
 
 
 Note:
  - This Tcl command is the same as for the static power analysis or dynamic vectorless flow, except that -method must be vector_profile .
  - Use the -worst_window_count parameter to perform dynamic power analysis and vector profiling for multiple windows.
5. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
6. Run dynamic power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorbased 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format SHM ADDER.trn -scope adder_tb_e/u1 -start ${worst_power_window_start} -end ${worst_power_window_end} 
 
 report_power 
 
 
 
 The worst start/end activity windows are stored in memory through the Tcl variables $worst_power_window_start and $worst_power_window_end specified using the read_activity_file command.
The Voltus console will display vector profiling statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The power output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the binary database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -binary_db_name parameter. The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.

The name of the vector profile report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. 
 
 Average Vector Profiling : The report details the average power of all events in the user-specified window. This report includes a summary based on the power components, cell type, power rail, and clock network. The worst window report is captured in a file specified with the - outfile parameter, and the report is suffixed by ‘ .avgpower ’. When - outfile is not specified, the vectroprofile.report.avgpower report file is created. 
 Activity Vector Profiling : The report generates only the worst activity report, and does not generate the average power report. The name of the file is as specified using the - outfile parameter. 
 Power Density Profiling : The report generates power for each tile of the design, where design can be considered to be physically divided into equal area tiles. By default, a design is divided in 10x10 tiles.

The name of the profiling database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -write_profiling_db parameter. The profiling database is a binary database of the profile data and can be viewed with SimVision. It allows you to visualize the activity of the design.

lines: Viewing Profiling Database
Voltus uses the SimVision waveform viewer to visualize the activity of the design.
Required Input Files
- Binary database of the profile data (.dsn and .trn files)
Procedure
To view the activity of the design in the GUI, the following steps are needed.

1. Select Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms . 
 The View Waveforms form appears.
2. Select the Profiling Histograms option.
3. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a database file ( file .trn ) from the Select a File browser window.
4. Click the Plot button. 
 The SimVision window appears.
5. In the Design Browser part of the window, click on the database folder in the upper section. 
 The folder content will appear in the lower section, as shown below:
6. Click on a node in the lower section, and the waveform will appear in the waveform window.
7. Select View -> Zoom -> Full X and Full Y to see the full waveform, as shown below:
8. You can zoom in and view the activity over time.
TCL Commands for Viewing Profiling Database

data:
##TCL command for viewing profiling database: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform -type profile \ -waveform_files {vectorprofile.report.trn}

lines: Additional Tcl Command Examples
Vector-Based Flow - Multiple VCD/FSDB Files
The TCL commands for dynamic vector-based power analysis are shown below.

You can specify multiple VCD/FSDB files with different windows for the vector-based dynamic analysis/vector profiling flow and write out a single dynamic current file/profiling report. The TCL commands for dynamic vectorbased power analysis using multiple VCD files are shown below:
By default, the software aligns all signals at t=0 (or min start time). You can disable the start time alignment by setting set_power_analysis_mode -start_time_alignment to false .
#Simulation period is max(endtime - starttime), that is, 11ns.
#Start times for all VCDs will be aligned at t=0

You can specify the read_activity_file command multiple times to read multiple VCD files. When you specify the -start_time_shift parameter of this command, the software first aligns all the signals of the specified activity files at time zero, and then shifts the start time of the activity file specified with the -start_time_shift parameter. A sample Tcl command to illustrate this is given below:
#Simulation period is max(endtime - starttime), that is, 11ns.
#Start times for the top + block2 VCD will be aligned at t=0 whereas for block1.vcd it will start at 2ns.
Probability-Based Vectorless Flow
The TCL commands for dynamic probability-based vectorless power analysis are shown below.
You can use the set_dc_sources command to specify the operating voltages of the power/ground nets. This command is used to specify a global voltage or voltages for specific power nets in the design.
# To enable multi-threading in dynamic vectorless flow
State-Propagation-Based Vectorless Flow with User-Defined Activity
The TCL commands for dynamic state-propagation-based vectorless power analysis are shown below.

Voltus generates a time varying switching profile report ( voltus_power.togglestats ) in the output directory for both the vector-based and state-propagation-based vectorless dynamic power analysis flows. The report includes the number of output toggles with the corresponding simulation time.
The following is a snippet of the report:
Following is a sample of a detailed vector profile report:
State-Propagation-Based Vectorless Flow with User-Defined Power Targets
The TCL commands for state-propagation-based vectorless power analysis with user-defined power targets are shown below.
Example1: Enable the power target flow with fixed CGO
In this case, the clock gate output is fixed, while the values for input, macro, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the power target of 3mW.
Example2: Enable the power target flow with a single power target of 3mW
In this case, the values for input, macro, clock gate output, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the power target of 3mW.
Example3: Enable the power target flow with two power targets
In this case, the values for input, macro, clock gate output, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the two power targets.

The Vector Profiler flow identifies worst activity and worst average power cycles in the VCD/FSDB that are then used for detailed dynamic power and IRdrop analysis. However, if you need to take into account core regions of high power density that could produce high dynamic switching current and IRdrop, use tile dimension for power density calculation. In this flow, you can specify the number of physical tiles, and the tool will:

- Compute power density in each tile at every time step
- Track which tiles have worst power density during VCD profile
- Sort vectors based on worst-case power density

This section describes how to combine vector profiling and power analysis for the worst activity windows.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- SPEF
