Procedure
To perform vector profiling, complete the following steps:

1. Repeat the steps 1-3 given in the Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis Flow section to load the design data, CPF, and the SPEF file.
2. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVectorbasedPowerResults
3. Specify the activity file. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -report_missing_nets true 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format VCD -scope FIR_TB.Unit -start {} -end {} -block {} ../design/ncsim.vcd
  - The -report_missing_nets parameter gives a list of nets in the design that are not annotated by VCD. This is useful when pipe-cleaning the flow. The default value is false .
  - Voltus can read activity files in the following formats: VCD, TCF, SAIF, FSDB, PHY, and SHM.
4. Run vector profiling by defining the vector profile power analysis mode to identify where the maximum activity is taking place. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 Event-based Vector Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorbasedPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method vector_profile \ 
 
 -worst_step_size 2ns \ 
 
 -write_profiling_db true \ 
 
 -worst_window_count 2 
 
 report_power -nworst 5 -outfile eventbased_profiler.rpt -time_based_report 
 
 The following are options required for the different types of profiling: 
 
 Activity Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {activity} 
 
 report_power - outfile activity_profiler.rpt 
 
 Power Density Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {power_density} 
 
 report_power - outfile tiling -nworst 5 -time_based_report -pg_net {VDD VSS} -power_density_tiles_row_col { 5 5 } 
 
 
 Note:
  - This Tcl command is the same as for the static power analysis or dynamic vectorless flow, except that -method must be vector_profile .
  - Use the -worst_window_count parameter to perform dynamic power analysis and vector profiling for multiple windows.
5. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
6. Run dynamic power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorbased 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format SHM ADDER.trn -scope adder_tb_e/u1 -start ${worst_power_window_start} -end ${worst_power_window_end} 
 
 report_power 
 
 
 
 The worst start/end activity windows are stored in memory through the Tcl variables $worst_power_window_start and $worst_power_window_end specified using the read_activity_file command.
The Voltus console will display vector profiling statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The power output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the binary database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -binary_db_name parameter. The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.

The name of the vector profile report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. 
 
 Average Vector Profiling : The report details the average power of all events in the user-specified window. This report includes a summary based on the power components, cell type, power rail, and clock network. The worst window report is captured in a file specified with the - outfile parameter, and the report is suffixed by ‘ .avgpower ’. When - outfile is not specified, the vectroprofile.report.avgpower report file is created. 
 Activity Vector Profiling : The report generates only the worst activity report, and does not generate the average power report. The name of the file is as specified using the - outfile parameter. 
 Power Density Profiling : The report generates power for each tile of the design, where design can be considered to be physically divided into equal area tiles. By default, a design is divided in 10x10 tiles.

The name of the profiling database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -write_profiling_db parameter. The profiling database is a binary database of the profile data and can be viewed with SimVision. It allows you to visualize the activity of the design.

lines: Viewing Profiling Database
Voltus uses the SimVision waveform viewer to visualize the activity of the design.
Required Input Files
- Binary database of the profile data (.dsn and .trn files)
Procedure
To view the activity of the design in the GUI, the following steps are needed.

1. Select Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms . 
 The View Waveforms form appears.
2. Select the Profiling Histograms option.
3. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a database file ( file .trn ) from the Select a File browser window.
4. Click the Plot button. 
 The SimVision window appears.
5. In the Design Browser part of the window, click on the database folder in the upper section. 
 The folder content will appear in the lower section, as shown below:
6. Click on a node in the lower section, and the waveform will appear in the waveform window.
7. Select View -> Zoom -> Full X and Full Y to see the full waveform, as shown below:
8. You can zoom in and view the activity over time.
TCL Commands for Viewing Profiling Database

data:
##TCL command for viewing profiling database: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform -type profile \ -waveform_files {vectorprofile.report.trn}

lines: Additional Tcl Command Examples
Vector-Based Flow - Multiple VCD/FSDB Files
The TCL commands for dynamic vector-based power analysis are shown below.

You can specify multiple VCD/FSDB files with different windows for the vector-based dynamic analysis/vector profiling flow and write out a single dynamic current file/profiling report. The TCL commands for dynamic vectorbased power analysis using multiple VCD files are shown below:
By default, the software aligns all signals at t=0 (or min start time). You can disable the start time alignment by setting set_power_analysis_mode -start_time_alignment to false .
#Simulation period is max(endtime - starttime), that is, 11ns.
#Start times for all VCDs will be aligned at t=0

You can specify the read_activity_file command multiple times to read multiple VCD files. When you specify the -start_time_shift parameter of this command, the software first aligns all the signals of the specified activity files at time zero, and then shifts the start time of the activity file specified with the -start_time_shift parameter. A sample Tcl command to illustrate this is given below:
#Simulation period is max(endtime - starttime), that is, 11ns.
#Start times for the top + block2 VCD will be aligned at t=0 whereas for block1.vcd it will start at 2ns.
Probability-Based Vectorless Flow
The TCL commands for dynamic probability-based vectorless power analysis are shown below.
You can use the set_dc_sources command to specify the operating voltages of the power/ground nets. This command is used to specify a global voltage or voltages for specific power nets in the design.
# To enable multi-threading in dynamic vectorless flow
State-Propagation-Based Vectorless Flow with User-Defined Activity
The TCL commands for dynamic state-propagation-based vectorless power analysis are shown below.

Voltus generates a time varying switching profile report ( voltus_power.togglestats ) in the output directory for both the vector-based and state-propagation-based vectorless dynamic power analysis flows. The report includes the number of output toggles with the corresponding simulation time.
The following is a snippet of the report:
Following is a sample of a detailed vector profile report:
State-Propagation-Based Vectorless Flow with User-Defined Power Targets
The TCL commands for state-propagation-based vectorless power analysis with user-defined power targets are shown below.
Example1: Enable the power target flow with fixed CGO
In this case, the clock gate output is fixed, while the values for input, macro, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the power target of 3mW.
Example2: Enable the power target flow with a single power target of 3mW
In this case, the values for input, macro, clock gate output, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the power target of 3mW.
Example3: Enable the power target flow with two power targets
In this case, the values for input, macro, clock gate output, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the two power targets.
This section describes how to set up the dynamic vectorless power analysis mode and then run power analysis.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- Timing/Power Libraries (.lib)
- SDC or TWF
- SPEF 
 
Static Power Analysis should be run first and all static problems should be resolved prior to running dynamic analysis.
Procedure
To perform dynamic vectorless power analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Specify the CPF file for power domain information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Load/Commit CPF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_domain -cpf ../design/super_filter.cpf
3. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select File - Read SPEF . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -decoupled\ ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
4. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVecLessPowerResults
5. Define the power analysis mode through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power Rail - Set Power Analysis Mode . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorlessPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless 
 
 
 
 
 
 This Tcl command is the same as for the static power analysis, except that -method must be dynamic_vectorless .
6. Define the power of an instance or cell. For dynamic analysis, you can assign a piece-wise linear waveform to an instance. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power -reset 
 
 set_power -pg_net VDD_AO -pwl -instance instA { 0ns 0mA 0.075ns 0mA 0.175ns 45mA 0.225ns 15mA 0.425ns 0mA } -sticky
  - You can also specify power-grid views using the set_power_analysis_mode -power_grid_library parameter, instead of specifying .lib or defining a power value with set_power .
  - Time and current units must always be in ns and mA.
  - -sticky will apply the waveform as specified in the simulation. If -sticky is not specified for -pwl , the waveform will be applied each time the output toggles.
  - If you are using a VCD file and start the simulation at some point other than t=0 , the simulator will consider that start time t=0 .
7. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
8. Run power analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 report_power -outfile dyn.rpt 
 
 #Generate additional report formats 
 
 report_power -instances {u0} -outfile u0.rpt 
 
 report_power -instances {*} -outfile all.rpt 
 
 report_power -instances {ring} -outfile ring.rpt 
 
 
 
 The Voltus console will display power statistics, as shown in the following snippet: 
 Total Power 
 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Total Internal Power: 2.30917507 64.6507% 
 Total Switching Power: 1.03379946 28.9436% 
 Total Leakage Power: 0.22879451 6.4056% 
 Total Power: 3.57176907 
 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Ended Static Power Report Generation: (cpu=0:00:00, real=0:00:00, 
 mem(process/total/peak)=750.07MB/1877.45MB/750.07MB) 
 
 Begin Creating Binary Database 
 Ended Creating Binary Database: (cpu=0:00:00, real=0:00:00, 
 mem(process/total/peak)=750.34MB/1877.45MB/750.34MB)
  - Select Power Rail - Run Power Analysis to access the Run Power Analysis form that can be used to specify cell/instance power ( set_power ), toggle rates ( set_switching_activity and set_default_switching_activity ), power result directory ( set_power_output_dir ), and then run power analysis ( report_power ).
  - Select Power Rail - Textual Reports - Power Analysis to access the Report Power form that can be used to generate incremental power reports.
9. View the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy or the whole design. For more information, refer to Viewing Dynamic Current Waveforms .
Output
The power output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the binary database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -binary_db_name parameter.. The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.

The name of the power report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. The power report summarizes power consumption, by groups (sequential cells, macros, clocks), power net, and clock domain.
This is the static current file for each net. The set_power_analysis_mode -write_static_currents parameterwill generate the current files that are required for rail analysis.
This is the dynamic current file for each net. This file allows you to view the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy or the whole design.

lines: Viewing Dynamic Current Waveforms

Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution has embedded SimVision waveform viewer to analyze the generated dynamic current waveforms. You can view the peak current for a particular instance, at the level of hierarchy, or the whole design.
Required Input Files

- Waveform files
- Power database generated during power calculation
Procedure
To view dynamic power (current) waveforms in GUI, the following steps are needed:

1. Select Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms . 
 The following form appears:
2. Select the Power (Current) Waveforms option.
3. To plot an instance current waveform:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window. 
 You can query the waveforms of all the instances of the selected power net.
  2. Perform one of the following steps to select instances:
    - Select an instance from the list box, as shown below:
    - Filter instances using the filter pattern. To filter instances, enter a pattern ( ref_clk* ) in the Pattern field, and click Instances . The required instance ( ref_clk__L1_I0 ) appears in the list box, as shown below: 
 
 
 You can click the " X " button in the Pattern field to clear the text in the field.
    - Select instances from the Layout GUI, and click the Select From GUI button, as shown below:
  3. Click the Plot button. 
 The SimVision plot appears.
4. To plot current for the entire design:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Select Total Current from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  3. Click the Plot button.
5. To plot a composite dynamic current profile for a clock domain:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Click the browse button next to the Power Database field, and select a database file from the Open Power DB File browser window.
  3. Select Clock from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  4. Select a clock net to produce composite dynamic waveform. Optionally, select the " all " option to produce total composite dynamic waveform for all clock domains.
  5. Click the Plot button.
6. To plot composite dynamic waveform for a hierarchical module:
  1. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a dynamic current waveform ( dynamic_ netname .ptiavg ) file from the Select a File browser window.
  2. Select Hierarchy from the Composite Waveform drop-down list.
  3. Specify a hierarchy name to generate its total dynamic current profile in the text field.
  4. Click the Plot button. 
 
The View Waveforms form supports multi-threading to enable the search of instances in parallel, and thereby improve the search time in large designs. Multi-threading is supported for instance search using both the Instances and the Select From GUI buttons.For this feature, you must set up multi-CPU usage before accessing the View Waveforms form, as shown below:
 set_multi_cpu_usage -localCpu number_of_cpus
TCL Commands for Viewing Waveforms

data:
##TCL command for viewing instance current waveform: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -instance inst1 
 
 
 
 ##TCL command for viewing composite total current waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -composite_waveform_type total_current 
 
 
 
 ##TCL Command for viewing composite clock waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -power_db power.db \ -composite_waveform_type clock \ -composite_waveform_name clk 
 
 
 
 ##TCL Command for viewing hierarchical waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -composite_waveform_type hierarchy \ -composite_waveform_name ethernet_mac_2

lines: Dynamic Vector-based Power Analysis Flow
This section describes how to combine vector profiling and power analysis for the worst activity windows.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)

Procedure

1. Repeat the steps 1-3 given in the Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis Flow section to load the design data, CPF, and the SPEF file.
2. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command :

3. Specify the activity file. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -report_missing_nets true 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format VCD -scope FIR_TB.Unit -start {} -end {} -block {} ../design/ncsim.vcd
  - The -report_missing_nets parameter gives a list of nets in the design that are not annotated by VCD. This is useful when pipe-cleaning the flow. The default value is false .
  - Voltus can read activity files in the following formats: VCD, TCF, SAIF, FSDB, PHY, and SHM.
4. Run vector profiling by defining the vector profile power analysis mode to identify where the maximum activity is taking place. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 Event-based Vector Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorbasedPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method vector_profile \ 
 
 -worst_step_size 2ns \ 
 
 -write_profiling_db true \ 
 
 -worst_window_count 2 
 
 report_power -nworst 5 -outfile eventbased_profiler.rpt -time_based_report 
 
 The following are options required for the different types of profiling: 
 
 Activity Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {activity} 
 
 report_power - outfile activity_profiler.rpt

 report_power - outfile tiling -nworst 5 -time_based_report -pg_net {VDD VSS} -power_density_tiles_row_col { 5 5 } 
 
 
 Note:
  - This Tcl command is the same as for the static power analysis or dynamic vectorless flow, except that -method must be vector_profile .
  - Use the -worst_window_count parameter to perform dynamic power analysis and vector profiling for multiple windows.
5. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
6. Run dynamic power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorbased 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format SHM ADDER.trn -scope adder_tb_e/u1 -start ${worst_power_window_start} -end ${worst_power_window_end}

The Voltus console will display vector profiling statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output

The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.

The name of the vector profile report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. 
 
 Average Vector Profiling : The report details the average power of all events in the user-specified window. This report includes a summary based on the power components, cell type, power rail, and clock network. The worst window report is captured in a file specified with the - outfile parameter, and the report is suffixed by ‘ .avgpower ’. When - outfile is not specified, the vectroprofile.report.avgpower report file is created.

 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -instance inst1 
 
 
 
 ##TCL command for viewing composite total current waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -composite_waveform_type total_current 
 
 
 
 ##TCL Command for viewing composite clock waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -power_db power.db \ -composite_waveform_type clock \ -composite_waveform_name clk 
 
 
 
 ##TCL Command for viewing hierarchical waveform is as follows: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform \ -waveform_type current \ -waveform_files dynamic_vss.ptiavg \ -composite_waveform_type hierarchy \ -composite_waveform_name ethernet_mac_2

lines: Dynamic Vector-based Power Analysis Flow
This section describes how to combine vector profiling and power analysis for the worst activity windows.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- SPEF
- Activity file
Procedure
To perform vector profiling, complete the following steps:

1. Repeat the steps 1-3 given in the Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis Flow section to load the design data, CPF, and the SPEF file.
2. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVectorbasedPowerResults
3. Specify the activity file. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -report_missing_nets true 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format VCD -scope FIR_TB.Unit -start {} -end {} -block {} ../design/ncsim.vcd
  - The -report_missing_nets parameter gives a list of nets in the design that are not annotated by VCD. This is useful when pipe-cleaning the flow. The default value is false .
  - Voltus can read activity files in the following formats: VCD, TCF, SAIF, FSDB, PHY, and SHM.
4. Run vector profiling by defining the vector profile power analysis mode to identify where the maximum activity is taking place. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 Event-based Vector Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorbasedPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method vector_profile \ 
 
 -worst_step_size 2ns \ 
 
 -write_profiling_db true \ 
 
 -worst_window_count 2 
 
 report_power -nworst 5 -outfile eventbased_profiler.rpt -time_based_report 
 
 The following are options required for the different types of profiling: 
 
 Activity Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {activity} 
 
 report_power - outfile activity_profiler.rpt 
 
 Power Density Profiling 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -vector_profile_mode {power_density} 
 
 report_power - outfile tiling -nworst 5 -time_based_report -pg_net {VDD VSS} -power_density_tiles_row_col { 5 5 } 
 
 
 Note:
  - This Tcl command is the same as for the static power analysis or dynamic vectorless flow, except that -method must be vector_profile .
  - Use the -worst_window_count parameter to perform dynamic power analysis and vector profiling for multiple windows.
5. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 
 
 If the resolution is high, the current peaks may be truncated, affecting the accuracy of the analysis. Alternatively, a small resolution may lead to increase in runtime.
6. Run dynamic power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorbased 
 
 read_activity_file -reset 
 
 read_activity_file -format SHM ADDER.trn -scope adder_tb_e/u1 -start ${worst_power_window_start} -end ${worst_power_window_end} 
 
 report_power 
 
 
 
 The worst start/end activity windows are stored in memory through the Tcl variables $worst_power_window_start and $worst_power_window_end specified using the read_activity_file command.
The Voltus console will display vector profiling statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The power output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the binary database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -binary_db_name parameter. The database can be loaded in the Voltus GUI for viewing and analyzing different types of power plots, and GUI interactive debugging. It cross references the instance names and locations with the power results.

The name of the vector profile report is specified using the report_power -outfile parameter. 
 
 Average Vector Profiling : The report details the average power of all events in the user-specified window. This report includes a summary based on the power components, cell type, power rail, and clock network. The worst window report is captured in a file specified with the - outfile parameter, and the report is suffixed by ‘ .avgpower ’. When - outfile is not specified, the vectroprofile.report.avgpower report file is created. 
 Activity Vector Profiling : The report generates only the worst activity report, and does not generate the average power report. The name of the file is as specified using the - outfile parameter. 
 Power Density Profiling : The report generates power for each tile of the design, where design can be considered to be physically divided into equal area tiles. By default, a design is divided in 10x10 tiles.

The name of the profiling database is specified using the set_power_analysis_mode -write_profiling_db parameter. The profiling database is a binary database of the profile data and can be viewed with SimVision. It allows you to visualize the activity of the design.

lines: Viewing Profiling Database
Voltus uses the SimVision waveform viewer to visualize the activity of the design.
Required Input Files
- Binary database of the profile data (.dsn and .trn files)
Procedure
To view the activity of the design in the GUI, the following steps are needed.

1. Select Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms . 
 The View Waveforms form appears.
2. Select the Profiling Histograms option.
3. In the Waveform Files section, click Add and select a database file ( file .trn ) from the Select a File browser window.
4. Click the Plot button. 
 The SimVision window appears.
5. In the Design Browser part of the window, click on the database folder in the upper section. 
 The folder content will appear in the lower section, as shown below:
6. Click on a node in the lower section, and the waveform will appear in the waveform window.
7. Select View -> Zoom -> Full X and Full Y to see the full waveform, as shown below:
8. You can zoom in and view the activity over time.
TCL Commands for Viewing Profiling Database

data:
##TCL command for viewing profiling database: 
 
 view_dynamic_waveform -type profile \ -waveform_files {vectorprofile.report.trn}

In this case, the clock gate output is fixed, while the values for input, macro, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the power target of 3mW.
Example2: Enable the power target flow with a single power target of 3mW
In this case, the values for input, macro, clock gate output, and sequential activities will be adjusted to meet the power target of 3mW.

Scan Mode Analysis is a method used in design for testing (DFT) to build scan-chains that are used to detect stuck-at faults in a circuit. In scan-design, the daisy chained scan-flops are connected in series to form a scan-chain which are populated (shifted) with test vectors at scan clock frequency when the scan_enable pin is high. The daisy-chained scan flops are bigger flops with a multiplexer.The following diagram illustrates scan chain configuration:

“ Scan In ” pin is connected to the input of the first flop in the chain to send a series of patterns (1’s and 0’s) into the chain. " Scan Out " is the output pin where the shifted pattern comes out. “Scan Enable” pin is the input pin used to enable the scan mode. " Scan Clock " is the input pin used to apply the clock signal to do the shifting of patterns. Scan mode analysis supports the following two modes:

- Scan shift mode - You need to provide the initial state of all scan flops and scan-chain order. In this mode, Voltus will:
  1. “shift” the state across scan-chain at scan clock freq (SDC) depending upon number of cycles specified for simulation
  2. make “state” assumption for combinational logic which is driven from PI
  3. perform “state/logic” propagation of flop states to the logic cone.
  4. capture dynamic current profile, and run Irdrop analysis w/ package.
- Vector-less mode - You need to provide scan-chain order and % activity [global | each scan-chain]. In this mode, Voltus will:
  1. generate scan pattern based on activity and internal vector-less heuristic to decide which flops will toggle in each cycle
  2. use “state/logic” propagation or “vector-less” propagation for combination logic cone
  3. capture dynamic current profile, and run Irdrop analysis w/ package.
To run this flow, you must specify the -scan_control_file parameter of the set_power_analysis_mode command. The use model for this flow is:
set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_vectorbased -scan_control_file filename The scan control file contains details about the scan analysis mode used for testing.
Scan Control File Format
The format of the scan control file is:

Note that <initial_state> is optional in the vector-less mode, <master_cell_name> , <scale> , <mode> , <scan_in_pin> , <scan_out_pin> , <scan_en_pin> are optional and only needed for the first internal scan flop inside one macro.
Where,

- Mode specifies the mode used for scan design analysis. The two modes are: SHIFT and VECTORLESS .
- VECTORLESS_GLOBAL_ACTIVITY specifies the activity specification used in the VECTORLESS mode.
- SCAN_PATTERN specifies the digital 0/1 pattern to be used in the scan chain repeatedly, and this pattern will be shifted afterwards through the scan chain. You can specify the scan pattern only in the SHIFT mode. The initial state of the scan chain follows the SCAN_PATTERN from the start of the scan chain to the end of the scan chain, as shown in the following diagram: 
 
 
 You must specify the scan chain name in the SCAN_PATTERN line for each scan chain to assign a unique digital pattern to the specified scan chain. In the following scan control file example, scan pattern has been specified for two scan chains: 
 MODE = SHIFT
SCAN_PATTERN = 10101 AutoChain_1
 AutoChain_1 inst_i0
AutoChain_1 inst_i1
... 
 MODE = SHIFT
SCAN_PATTERN = 11111 AutoChain_2
 AutoChain_2 inst_i5
AutoChain_2 inst_i6
... 
 You can also assign the same digital pattern to all the chains present in the design (global specification) by not specifying the scan chain name in the SCAN_PATTERN line, as shown in the following example: 
 MODE = SHIFT
SCAN_PATTERN = 1000
SC1 SC1/DFF1
SC1 SC1/DFF2
SC1 SC1/DFF3
SC1 SC1/DFF4 
 SC2 SC2/DFF1
SC2 SC2/DFF2
SC2 SC2/DFF3
SC2 SC2/DFF4 
 here,
  - SC1 chain and SC2 chain will have the same digital pattern, that is, the pattern 1000 will be passed through its flop instances.
  - chain_name is the name of the scan chain.
  - initial_state is the initial state of all scan flops.
  - master_cell_name is the name of the macro.
  - scale is the scaling factor applied to the current.
  - mode is mode name of the scan mode in the PGV view.
  - scan_in_pin is the macro’s scan_in pin name.
  - scan_out_pin is the macro’s scan_out pin name.
  - scan_en_pin is the macro’s scan_enable pin name.
The following is a sample scan control file for the vectorless mode:
The following is a sample scan control file for the shift mode:

You can use the set_power_analysis_mode -report_scan_chain_stats parameter to generate a report file called voltus_power.scanchain.stat during scan mode analysis. The report gives the statistics for the scan chains in the design.

title: Generating User-defined Mode Based Current Waveform with Automatic PWL Scaling

lines: Given one master cell PWL waveform, Voltus can automatically generate scaled PWL waveforms of the other companion cells defined in a trigger file proportional to the master cell internal power. It keeps the waveform shape and scales the waveform magnitude based on the mode and net-dependent relative ratio of the NLPM internal power energy table value to the master cell model. This feature decouples the mode-based current waveform from PGV, and allows power analysis to directly take the PWL format from the user-defined trigger file. You do not need a dynamic PGV for Voltus power and rail analysis.
Use the -custom_macro_pwl parameter of the set_power command to specify a custom trigger file that allows you to apply instance or cell PWL waveforms.

- In the vector-based analysis, the user-defined PWL model overrides the .lib model. When instance input pins' state matches with the CONDITONAL_INPUT and CONDITIONAL_PIN description, the PWL waveform is applied.
- In the vectorless mode, the -custom_macro_pwl parameter must be used with set_power –dynamic_switch_pattern in the vectorless flow.
The use model of the -custom_macro_pwl parameter is:
Example of a Custom Macro Power Trigger File (cell.trigger)
For information on the trigger file format, see the Trigger File Format section in the set_power section of Voltus Text Command Reference .
The following information is displayed in the log file:

Voltus power saves the waveform scaling factor of each companion cell in an individual text file in the output directory. The file name is scale_factor_report_<master_cell_name>.txt . The following is an example of the text file:

In addition to static analysis, Voltus offers you the option of running dynamic gate-level analysis. You can use the dynamic approach to optimize the power network, minimize IR drop transients, and reduce leakage associated with added decoupling capacitance. Specifically, dynamic power-rail analysis can:

- Detect power integrity problems.
- Provide insight into the effect of local simultaneously switching high-power drivers.
- Quantify instantaneous IR drop.
- Assess the impact of resistance, capacitance, and inductance due to packaging, bond wire, and C4 bumps on transient IR drop. See Package Analysis .

Rather than averaging currents as static analysis does, dynamic analysis uses peak currents as the basis for the analysis, enabling you to see the finer time variation of currents over a clock cycle. Voltus's dynamic, or transient, approach to power-rail analysis uses a dynamic power or current waveform that you can generate either by performing logic simulation or by using timing window information. The analysis also includes the impact of package inductance.
Power integrity
Power is consumed in three basic ways in integrated circuits:

- Switching power, which is the power consumed in the charging and discharging of interconnect capacitances. In most cases, this type of power consumption dominates because of large drivers having to drive large capacitive loads.
- Internal power, which is the power consumed in the charging and discharging of the internal capacitances within gates and the capacitances in driving the load. It also includes feedthrough current from VDD to VSS, which occurs for a short time when both NMOS and PMOS devices are on. Feedthrough current is also known as crossbar, crowbar, or short-circuit current. The faster the slew rate, the less feedthrough current there is.
- Leakage power, which is the power consumed by devices when they are not switching. It includes state-dependent leakage, which is leakage that depends on the state of the gate, that is, whether a transistor is on or off.
For additional details, see Type of power (internal, leakage, switching) .
Source of Decoupling Capacitance
There are five kinds of decoupling capacitance that can impact dynamic power rail analysis:

- Parasitic capacitance between power and ground
- Device decoupling (diffusion and gate capacitance)
- Discrete decoupling, added to the power rails
- Natural decoupling capacitance from signal net capacitances through gates
- Well capacitance

The well capacitance component is not currently modeled by Voltus, because the impact of this component is less than 10 percent for common process and design styles, when compared to the other four components.All other components of decoupling capacitance are accurately extracted using the embedded three-dimensional parasitic extraction engine, either during the processing of the main design input files or when generating the power grid views. A design can include all five forms. Decoupling capacitors operate as charge reservoirs to stabilize the power and ground grid and to minimize transients. Before the advent of 90-nanometer designs, design teams would typically add a significant number of decoupling capacitors between the power and ground rails to help smooth any voltage transients on the rails and to minimize transient voltage spikes caused by the switching of large buffers. As a result, the noise on the power grid was minimized.For 90-nanometer designs and below, however, the increased leakage from decoupling capacitors begins to add to the power consumption, so you can no longer liberally add them to minimize transients. For low-power and 90-nanometer designs, analyzing where to add and remove decoupling capacitors is of critical importance. Dynamic power-rail analysis gives you the necessary information to understand how to maximize the efficiency of the added decoupling capacitors to minimize the power consumption. Dynamic power-rail analysis helps you understand the optimal size of local decoupling capacitance, the optimal location in which to place the decoupling capacitors, and the impact of inductance on power rail transients. For example, consider the situation shown in the following figure:
Dynamic IR Drop
In the area shown in the circle, simultaneously switching outputs have caused more power to be used, resulting in voltage dropping below 1.15 volts.
You could do one of three things to correct this problem:

- Re-position the existing decoupling capacitors to be more effective.
- Add local decoupling capacitors to reduce the IR drop transients.
- Import transient IR drop data into the timing flow to understand the impact on delay.

Voltus's dynamic analysis reports regions with too few decoupling capacitors, enabling you to add more decoupling capacitors to limit IR drop transients, and regions with too many decoupling capacitors, enabling you to save power and space. It also includes the impact of package inductance.Dynamic analysis reports IR drop transients on the power networks, typically caused by localized simultaneous switching of devices, which can be smoothed by the addition of decoupling capacitance. Voltus also reports both the density and effectiveness of the decoupling capacitors, which can be used to direct where decoupling capacitance can be optimized to either reduce IR drop transients or lessen leakage.The following figure shows how adding decoupled capacitance smooths the transient drops on the VDD line.
Effect of added Decoupling Capacitance

Adding decoupling capacitance is performed by replacing filler cells with filler cells containing decoupling capacitance (decaps). Filler cells are cells that do not have signal pins and do not consume any power. Decoupling capacitance cells are cells that do not have signal pins, but have an internal capacitance (mostly in the form of MOS gate capacitance) implemented. Because of the internal capacitance, they cause leakage current.
Simultaneous switching

Groups of high-power drivers located in the same power region that switch synchronously drain power from the power rail and cause local transient IR drop in places, which must be included in the timing flow. Static analysis provides only limited insight into this problem. When your design has several simultaneously switching events and little decoupling capacitance, dynamic analysis enables you to identify optimal placement of decoupling capacitors to minimize transients.IR drop due to simultaneous switching is known as transient IR drop.The following figure shows a group of local high-power drivers that switch synchronously:
High-power drivers in local area switching synchronously
Instantaneous IR Drop

IR drop is an instantaneous phenomenon that is time-dependent and therefore only measurable by dynamic transient techniques. In addition, obtaining just the measurement of the IR drop is often insufficient. It is the shape and the magnitude of the IR drop that has the highest impact on timing. A large, very fast spike is typically recoverable and may have minimal impact on timing, but a spike of lesser magnitude but with a much longer duration can disrupt timing much more.Static analysis only gives a qualitative assessment of the design while dynamic analysis gives quantitative assessment. Static analysis give visibility into the quality of the grid, but not in absolute numbers over time as is done in dynamic analysis.
Packaging parasitics

For designs 130 nanometers and smaller, parasitic resistance, capacitance, and inductance due to packaging, bond wire, and C4 bumps can have a significant impact on transient IR drop at modest frequencies. At higher frequencies, the resonance can interfere with the on-chip clocks. Inductance in particular becomes a problem outside the chip because of the effects of the bond wire and the packaging routing.Voltus accepts package RLC modeling in the form of SPICE subcircuits generated from different packaging modeling products such as Allegro. You can specify the name of a file containing a SPICE subcircuit describing a package model.See Package Analysis for additional details.
Setting up and running Dynamic Rail Analysis
This section describes how to set up the dynamic rail analysis mode and then run rail analysis.
Required Input Files

- Power-grid libraries (.cl). See Power-Grid Library Generation for details on generating power-grid libraries.
- Vectorless or vector-based files created during dynamic power calculation
Procedure
To perform dynamic rail analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Define the rail analysis mode through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power Rail - Set Rail Analysis Mode . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method dynamic \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -generate_movies true \ 
 
 -save_voltage_waveforms true \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 -temperature 125 \ 
 
 -gif_resolution 0 \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } 
 
 
 Note:
  - XD uses LEF based views, and HD uses GDS based accurate views.
  - The technology library is a mandatory requirement for rail analysis. However, to obtain accurate IR drop simulation, power-grid library should be characterized for standard cells and macro blocks. When specifying the list of PGVs to rail analysis using the set_rail_analyis_mode command or the Set Rail Analysis Mode form, you must specify the technology library as the first PGV library in the list.
  - You need to specify the EM analysis models if analyzing Electromigration. If you do not specify an Electromigration model file, the software will select Electromigration models from the Quantus technology file. You can use the command set_rail_analyis_mode -em_models file to override these settings.
2. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. Then, use set_rail_analysis_domain to define the power domains. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_AO -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_ring -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.05 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD_ring -pwrnets VDD_AO -gndnets VSS -threshold 0.10 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD_AO -pwrnets VDD_AO -gndnets VSS -threshold 0.10
3. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_AO -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_AO.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp
4. Specify the location of the current files generated during dynamic power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_data -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_rail_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_rail_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 set_power_data \ 
 
 -format current \ 
 
 { \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_AO.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_column.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_ring.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_external.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VSS.ptiavg \ 
 
 }
5. Run rail analysis through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : Select Power Rail - Run Rail Analysis . 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_rail \ 
 
 -output ./dynVecLessRailResults \ 
 
 -type domain \
The Voltus console will display rail analysis statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The rail output directory will contain the following information:

data:
For each run, an output directory is generated with the default name < domainName >_< temperature >_dynamic_< X >. < domainName > will be replaced with ALL if all domains are to be analyzed ( analyze _rail -output dir -type domain ALL ), or with < netName > if a power net is to be analyzed. The output directory contains state directories for each net, such as VDD and VSS. 
 
 Each state directory contains the following: 
 
 results – a file that gives a summary of the rail analysis results for various plots, such as tc (tap current), rc (resistor current), and so on. 
 Reports – a folder that contains a list of useful report files that contain all power-grid integrity data. 
 Auto-generated GIF files for the rail analysis plots

Run Dynamic Power Analysis and Rail Analysis in Parallel
You can run dynamic power analysis in parallel with rail analysis to improve the overall turnaround time. To run this flow, you must specify the following parameter:

In this usage model, you do not need to modify the power analysis setup. Remove the report_power command and set the –report_power_in_parallel parameter of set_rail_analyis_mode to true . The analyze_rail command will run both dynamic power and rail analysis in parallel. The usage model is given below:
#Power Analysis Setup
#Rail Analysis Setup
#Run Dynamic Power and Rail Analysis
Dynamic Rail Analysis Plots, Movies and Waveforms

Dynamic rail analysis plots interface is available through Power Rail - Power Rail Results form. The interface is same as static rail analysis plots but in addition dynamic rail analysis offers various capacitance plots like grid capacitance, available capacitance, filler cell density, etc. Refer to Viewing Static Rail Analysis Results for more information on GUI and TCL interface.
The following table lists the dynamic analysis plot types and the corresponding controls to enable them:

data: power gate current I/Idsat (pi), power gate voltage (pv)
enabled automatically when power-gate(s) are connected to the analyzed net.
enabled automatically when package data is provided (spice model, in power pin location file, or through default_package_* commands)

lines: In addition to dynamic plots, dynamic IRdrop and Tap Current movies are generated that can be viewed using Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms form. The movies are also available using the TCL command: view_dynamic_movie -type ir -movies_directory VDD/movies_directory . The dynamic IRdrop waveforms are created for all instances in state directory as net.ptiavg file. The current waveforms through voltage sources are saved inside voltus_rail_pad.tran.ptiavg file to analyze impact of on-chip capacitance. The dynamic IRdrop waveforms can be plotted using Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms form.
To plot rail current, voltage, and power current waveforms:

1. Select Power Rail -> Dynamic Results -> Waveforms . 
 The following form appears:
2. In the Waveform Files section, click Add to add the current and voltage waveform (.ptiavg) and profiling (.trn) files. 
 The list of nets/instances for the selected waveform files are displayed.
3. Select Rail Current , Voltage , and/or Power Current to filter the instances based on the waveform type.
  - Rail Current displays waveform instance names with prefix TC_ .
  - Voltage displays waveform instance names with suffix :ir .
  - Power Current displays waveform instance names without a prefix or suffix.
4. Filter instances using the filter pattern. To filter instances, enter a pattern in the Pattern field, and click Instances .
5. Select the required instances and click the Plot button. 
 The following form appears:
TCL Commands:
TCL Command for viewing dynamic voltage waveforms
Dynamic Rail Analysis for On-Chip Voltage Regulators

System-on-a-chip (SoC) voltage regulators (Vreg) have been widely used in designs to improve device power efficiency as well as device stability. Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution supports analysis of on-chip Vreg in SoC to stabilize voltage supplies and to handle Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS). This analysis has been included in the IR Drop analysis flow to understand how Vreg affects the on-chip voltage drop, chip package co-design, and how it interacts with the IC/package/board system. This feature allows you to accurately model Vreg modules for rail analysis.In the following diagram, the chip configuration has a Vreg that is connected to an input and output domain along with a common ground:

During Vreg analysis, the tool captures the noise at the output of Vreg caused due to loading current and RC effects, and uses this time varying voltage waveform for rail analysis. When you perform Vreg analysis, a reduced grid is created for both input and output, and reduced ground resulting in a much concise RC netlist to do simulations of vectors with these loadings. The noise at the output of Vreg is captured and used for rail analysis.

Use the set_voltage_regulator_module command to enable the Voltage Regulator analysis flow. You must specify this command multiple times for multiple Vreg instances. This command is used to specify all required information for defining a Vreg module, such as Vreg module name, SPICE netlist, power pin mapping for input/output/ground domains, component list (DEF instances working as a voltage regulator), and top-level Vreg subcircuit. The Spice netlist of the Vreg cell should encapsulate the testbench required for the Vreg module to work.When Voltus loads this netlist, it traces the connectivity inside the netlist and maps the top-level pin to instance pin, if the VRM is constructed by multiple sub-instances. After identifying all physical pin locations for Vreg input and output, the physical pins for Vreg output is used as voltage source. The location and placement of voltage sources on VRM cells is done by the tool based on the LEF port definitions of the power pin of the VRM cells.
Example TCL script for Vreg analysis
# Load the design# Perform Power Analysis# Rail Analysis

After performing rail analysis, you can view the time varying waveform created at the output domain of the voltage regulator. The current waveforms through voltage sources are saved inside the voltus_rail.tran.ptiavg file to analyze voltage variation. The dynamic IRdrop waveforms can be plotted using Power Rail - Report - Dynamic Waveforms form. The following is a sample waveform that illustrates the voltage variation at the output domain:

The noise at the output of Vreg is captured and used for rail analysis.

Use the set_voltage_regulator_module command to enable the Voltage Regulator analysis flow. You must specify this command multiple times for multiple Vreg instances. This command is used to specify all required information for defining a Vreg module, such as Vreg module name, SPICE netlist, power pin mapping for input/output/ground domains, component list (DEF instances working as a voltage regulator), and top-level Vreg subcircuit. The Spice netlist of the Vreg cell should encapsulate the testbench required for the Vreg module to work.When Voltus loads this netlist, it traces the connectivity inside the netlist and maps the top-level pin to instance pin, if the VRM is constructed by multiple sub-instances. After identifying all physical pin locations for Vreg input and output, the physical pins for Vreg output is used as voltage source. The location and placement of voltage sources on VRM cells is done by the tool based on the LEF port definitions of the power pin of the VRM cells.
Example TCL script for Vreg analysis
# Load the design# Perform Power Analysis# Rail Analysis

After performing rail analysis, you can view the time varying waveform created at the output domain of the voltage regulator. The current waveforms through voltage sources are saved inside the voltus_rail.tran.ptiavg file to analyze voltage variation. The dynamic IRdrop waveforms can be plotted using Power Rail - Report - Dynamic Waveforms form. The following is a sample waveform that illustrates the voltage variation at the output domain:

title: Dynamic Rail Analysis Plots, Movies and Waveforms

lines: Dynamic rail analysis plots interface is available through Power Rail - Power Rail Results form. The interface is same as static rail analysis plots but in addition dynamic rail analysis offers various capacitance plots like grid capacitance, available capacitance, filler cell density, etc. Refer to Viewing Static Rail Analysis Results for more information on GUI and TCL interface.
The following table lists the dynamic analysis plot types and the corresponding controls to enable them:

data: power gate current I/Idsat (pi), power gate voltage (pv)
enabled automatically when power-gate(s) are connected to the analyzed net.
enabled automatically when package data is provided (spice model, in power pin location file, or through default_package_* commands)

lines: In addition to dynamic plots, dynamic IRdrop and Tap Current movies are generated that can be viewed using Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms form. The movies are also available using the TCL command: view_dynamic_movie -type ir -movies_directory VDD/movies_directory . The dynamic IRdrop waveforms are created for all instances in state directory as net.ptiavg file. The current waveforms through voltage sources are saved inside voltus_rail_pad.tran.ptiavg file to analyze impact of on-chip capacitance. The dynamic IRdrop waveforms can be plotted using Power Rail - Dynamic Results - Waveforms form.
To plot rail current, voltage, and power current waveforms:

1. Select Power Rail -> Dynamic Results -> Waveforms . 
 The following form appears:
2. In the Waveform Files section, click Add to add the current and voltage waveform (.ptiavg) and profiling (.trn) files. 
 The list of nets/instances for the selected waveform files are displayed.
3. Select Rail Current , Voltage , and/or Power Current to filter the instances based on the waveform type.
  - Rail Current displays waveform instance names with prefix TC_ .
  - Voltage displays waveform instance names with suffix :ir .
  - Power Current displays waveform instance names without a prefix or suffix.
4. Filter instances using the filter pattern. To filter instances, enter a pattern in the Pattern field, and click Instances .
5. Select the required instances and click the Plot button. 
 The following form appears:
TCL Commands:
TCL Command for viewing dynamic voltage waveforms
Dynamic Rail Analysis for On-Chip Voltage Regulators

System-on-a-chip (SoC) voltage regulators (Vreg) have been widely used in designs to improve device power efficiency as well as device stability. Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution supports analysis of on-chip Vreg in SoC to stabilize voltage supplies and to handle Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS). This analysis has been included in the IR Drop analysis flow to understand how Vreg affects the on-chip voltage drop, chip package co-design, and how it interacts with the IC/package/board system. This feature allows you to accurately model Vreg modules for rail analysis.In the following diagram, the chip configuration has a Vreg that is connected to an input and output domain along with a common ground:

During Vreg analysis, the tool captures the noise at the output of Vreg caused due to loading current and RC effects, and uses this time varying voltage waveform for rail analysis. When you perform Vreg analysis, a reduced grid is created for both input and output, and reduced ground resulting in a much concise RC netlist to do simulations of vectors with these loadings. The noise at the output of Vreg is captured and used for rail analysis.

Use the set_voltage_regulator_module command to enable the Voltage Regulator analysis flow. You must specify this command multiple times for multiple Vreg instances. This command is used to specify all required information for defining a Vreg module, such as Vreg module name, SPICE netlist, power pin mapping for input/output/ground domains, component list (DEF instances working as a voltage regulator), and top-level Vreg subcircuit. The Spice netlist of the Vreg cell should encapsulate the testbench required for the Vreg module to work.When Voltus loads this netlist, it traces the connectivity inside the netlist and maps the top-level pin to instance pin, if the VRM is constructed by multiple sub-instances. After identifying all physical pin locations for Vreg input and output, the physical pins for Vreg output is used as voltage source. The location and placement of voltage sources on VRM cells is done by the tool based on the LEF port definitions of the power pin of the VRM cells.

The dynamic IRdrop waveforms can be plotted using Power Rail - Report - Dynamic Waveforms form. The following is a sample waveform that illustrates the voltage variation at the output domain:

title: Using Characterized Current PGV for Dynamic Power and Rail Analysis

lines: Power-grid view library with dynamic data is supported in both the vector-based and vectorless Voltus power calculation. By default, Voltus computes dynamic power from .lib files. When there are pre-defined currents stored in PGV, Voltus dynamic power calculation automatically identifies the dynamic data and trigger information inside the power-grid view library.

- In the vector-based analysis, Voltus looks at the trigger condition in VCD and schedules the current modes with the matching condition.
- In the vectorless mode, you can control the trigger scenario by using the set_power –dynamic_switching_pattern parameter. This can be performed in both the instance-based and cell-based switching.
Example of instance-based switching :
This command specifies to trigger MODE1 @ first clock edge and MODE2 @ 3rd clock edge.
Example of cell-based switching :
This command specifies to trigger MODE_0 @ 1st, 2nd, and 3rd clock edges.
Following is an example of a trigger.txt file generated in the output directory at the end of the run that shows the various modes and trigger times:
mem1 1.95e-09 1 MODE1 mem1 3.195e-08 1 MODE1 mem1 4.195e-08 1 MODE1 mem1 2.205e-08 1 MODE2 mem1 3.205e-08 1 MODE2

Voltus dynamic rail analysis checks availability of the detailed dynamic data in the power-grid views from a given list of cell libraries. Rail analysis triggers the waveform stored inside a power-grid view based on the trigger time from the power analysis run. The following is a snapshot of the log file:

If the power-grid view library does not have any associated trigger time, then the dynamic current stored inside this library starts at t = 0. To shift this waveform, you can use the set_rail_analyis_mode -dynamic_trigger_file < filename > parameter. The syntax of dynamic trigger file is as follows:
You can capture the current/voltage waveforms using the set_rail_analyis_mode -probe_waveform_list parameter.
Example TCL script for Dynamic Switch Pattern in the Vectorless Mode

title: Dynamic Mixed Mode (Vector-based and Vectorless) Analysis

lines: In addition to the regular methods ( static , dynamic_vectorless , dynamic_vectorbased ) of power analysis, you can use the dynamic_mixed_mode method to combine the vector-based and state-propagation-based vectorless dynamic analysis. This flow can be used in the following cases:

data: Case 1: vectors are not available for every block of a full-chip design
The software uses the state-propagation-based vectorless methodology for blocks with incomplete vectors
Case 2: blocks with vectors having unannotated combinational logic

The software performs state propagation for the missing combinational logic 
 The software uses the vectorless methodology for a logic cone that does not have the primary inputs or flops annotated from the vector, and then performs state propagation

lines: The two cases are illustrated in the following diagram:
To enable the dynamic mixed mode analysis flow, you must specify the following commands:
In this flow:

- the specified global activity will be used for selection of unannotated flops and primary inputs in each physical tile.
- if there is a mismatch between the TWF and vector files in terms of clock frequency for the unannotated flops/primary inputs, the TWF frequency will take precedence and will be used for scheduling.
- when multiple vectors are provided for different blocks, they are required to be of the same vector type (post-simulation/zero-delay/rtl) and the same format (VCD, FSDB, PHY, SHM). Mixing of the vector types and formats of the different blocks is not supported.
- the simulation period for different windows in the vector-based analysis is derived from the max(endtime – starttime) specified using the read_activity_file -start and -end command parameters.
Refer to the Dynamic Vectorless Power Analysis Flow and Dynamic Vector-based Power Analysis Flow section for the required input files and the detailed steps to perform dynamic analysis.
In addition to the dynamic current files (dynamic_< net_name >.ptiavg) and power report, this flow generates the following output files:

- a switching scenario file ( voltus_power.stateprop.switchsrc ) that contains the switching information of flip-flops.
- a report file ( voltus_power.stateprop.stats ) that contains the instance toggle coverage, that is, number of primary input, flop, combinational, macro, and idle instances selected at different clock frequencies.
Tcl command :

data:
read_lib ../DesignData//LIB/TYP_DRIVE.lib ../DesignData//LIB/TYP_PRIM.lib ../DesignData//LIB/TYP_SPECIAL.lib 
 
 read_lib -lef ../DesignData//LEF/lef.r_mod 
 
 read_verilog ../DesignData//VERILOG/my_chip1.v ../DesignData//VERILOG/my_chip2.v 
 
 set_top_module my_chip 
 
 specify_def { ../DesignData//DEF/def1.def ../DesignData//DEF/def2.def} 
 
 specify_spef { ../DesignData//SPEF/core.spef ../DesignData//SPEF/signal.spef} 
 
 read_twf ../DesignData//TWF/my_chip.twf 
 
 read_twf ../DesignData//TWF/mixed.twf -scope inst_A1 
 
 read_twf ../DesignData//TWF/mixed.twf -scope inst_B1 
 
 read_twf ../DesignData//TWF/mixed.twf -scope inst_C1 
 
 read_twf ../DesignData//TWF/mixed.twf -scope inst_D1 
 
 read_activity_file -format VCD -scope top/shell/soin -start 600ns -end 700ns ../mixed_vcd/blocka.vcd -block inst_A1 
 
 read_activity_file -format VCD -scope top/shell/soin -start 600ns -end 700ns ../vcd_hier/DesignData/VCD/blockb.vcd -block inst_B1 
 
 read_activity_file -format VCD -scope top/shell/soin -start 600ns -end 700ns ../vcd_hier/DesignData/VCD/blockc.vcd -block inst_C1 
 
 read_activity_file -format VCD -scope top/shell/soin -start 600ns -end 700ns ../mixed_vcd/blockd.vcd -block inst_D1 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -method dynamic_mixed_mode -disable_static false -power_grid_library ../DesignData//PGV/library.cl 
 
 set_default_switching_activity -input_activity 0.2 \ -seq_activity 0.1 
 
 report_power

lines: Noise margin calculation allows you to determine the allowable noise voltage on the input of a gate so that the output will not be affected. It is the amount of noise a circuit can resist without affecting its operation. These values are determined to ensure that the false noise signals are not causing any undesired operation. The noise values can be compared with the noise margins and if they are within the margins provided, it indicates the ability of the circuit to operate in the presence of noise.
The following diagram illustrates a chain of two inverters:
Here,
Voh (minimum output high voltage) = VDD_driver - VSS_receiver
Vol (maximum output low voltage) = VSS_driver - VSS_receiver
Vih (minimum input high voltage) = (1-noise_limit)*(VDD_receiver - VSS_receiver)
Vil (maximum input low voltage) = noise_limit*(VDD_receiver - VSS_receiver)
The following is the criteria for noise margin calculation:
Margin_High = NMH (V oh - Vih) - signal noise - guardband > 0
Margin_Low = NML (V il - Vol) - signal noise - guardband > 0
Effective Instance Voltage Based Noise Margin Calculation

Voltus uses the Effective Instance Voltage method for noise margin calculation. In this method, noise margins are calculated with worst voltages seen on the driver-receiver pins. Noise margins will be calculated for only the driver-receiver pairs where flop is a receiver, as shown below:
This method uses the elapsed worst case voltages on the power and ground pins of the driver and receiver instances. This method has the following stages:

- Power Analysis - The Power Analysis engine will generate the driver-receiver pairs. This will be limited to the state-elements (flops) as noise margins will be calculated only for the driver-receiver pairs where flop is a receiver. 
 You need to specify the set_power_analysis_mode -create_driver_db true command parameter to generate the driver-receiver database.
- Rail Analysis - The Rail Analysis engine automatically detects that this is for EIV-based noise margin calculation based on the availability of the driver-receiver database.
- Calculate Noise Margin - Specify the calculate_noise_margin command to calculate noise margin (NMH/NML). To run this command, you need to specify the default or cell-specific noise limit, signal noise, guardband, rail directory, and power directory. 
 The format of the noise limit file is: 
 default_noise_limit <value> 
 default_signal_noise <value> 
 default_guard_band <value> 
 <cell_name> <noise_limit> <signal_noise> <guardband>
Performing Noise Margin Calculation
This section describes the various inputs and outputs of the noise margin calculation flow. Noise margin calculation can be done in both the static and dynamic power/rail analysis flow.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Common Power Format (CPF) file
- SPEF
- Power-grid libraries (.cl)
Procedure
To calculate noise margin, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz
2. Specify the CPF file for power domain information. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_domain -cpf ../design/super_filter.cpf
3. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -decoupled\ ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
4. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVecLessPowerResults
5. Define the dynamic power analysis mode. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -reset 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorlessPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless 
 
 -create_driver_db true
6. Optional. Define the simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -reset 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps
7. Run power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 report_power -outfile dyn.rpt
8. Define the rail analysis mode. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method dynamic \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_on \ 
 
 -save_voltage_waveforms true \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 -temperature 125 \ 
 
 -gif_resolution 0 \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 }
9. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. Then, use set_rail_analysis_domain to define the power domains. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_AO -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_ring -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.05 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD_ring -pwrnets VDD_AO -gndnets VSS -threshold 0.10 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD_AO -pwrnets VDD_AO -gndnets VSS -threshold 0.10
10. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_AO -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_AO.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp
11. Specify the location of the current files generated during dynamic power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_rail_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 set_power_data \ 
 
 -format current \ 
 
 { \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_AO.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_column.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_ring.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_external.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VSS.ptiavg \ 
 
 }
12. Run rail analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_rail \ 
 
 -results_directory ./dynVecLessRailResults \ -type domain corecurrent \
13. Calculate noise margin. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 calculate_noise_margin -noise_limit_file ./noiselimit \ 
 
 -state_directory dynVecLessRailResults/core_125C_dynamic_1\ 
 
 -power_directory dynVecLessPowerResults
Output
The state directory will contain the following file:

data:
A report that gives a summary of the noise margin values for the driver-receiver pins. 
 
 # File Format for EIV-based method: 
 
 <Margin_High> <Margin_Low> <NMh> <NMl> <Voh> <Vol> <Vih> <Vil> <Signal_Noise> <Guardband> <Dr Inst/pin> <Rx Inst/pin>
