 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 }
9. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. Then, use set_rail_analysis_domain to define the power domains. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_AO -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_ring -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.85 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.05 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD_ring -pwrnets VDD_AO -gndnets VSS -threshold 0.10 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD_AO -pwrnets VDD_AO -gndnets VSS -threshold 0.10
10. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_AO -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_AO.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp
11. Specify the location of the current files generated during dynamic power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_rail_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 set_power_data \ 
 
 -format current \ 
 
 { \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_AO.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_column.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_ring.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VDD_external.ptiavg \ 
 
 dynVecLessPowerResults/dynamic_VSS.ptiavg \ 
 
 }
12. Run rail analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_rail \ 
 
 -results_directory ./dynVecLessRailResults \ -type domain corecurrent \
13. Calculate noise margin. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 calculate_noise_margin -noise_limit_file ./noiselimit \ 
 
 -state_directory dynVecLessRailResults/core_125C_dynamic_1\ 
 
 -power_directory dynVecLessPowerResults
Output
The state directory will contain the following file:

data:
A report that gives a summary of the noise margin values for the driver-receiver pins. 
 
 # File Format for EIV-based method: 
 
 <Margin_High> <Margin_Low> <NMh> <NMl> <Voh> <Vol> <Vih> <Vil> <Signal_Noise> <Guardband> <Dr Inst/pin> <Rx Inst/pin>

title: Hierarchical Power Integrity Analysis with Extreme Modeling

lines:
- Overview
- Using Extreme Modeling
- xPGV Generation
- xPGV Instantiation
- Setting up and Running the Hierarchical PI Analysis Flow

Voltus can perform hierarchical power integrity (PI) analysis of large designs using the Extreme Modeling (XM) technology. Voltus-XM allows you to create xPGV models for the IP blocks which accurately capture the demand current and electrical parasitics of the block. These xPGV models are smaller compared to the fully extracted block and when used in the chip-level analysis, it can help significantly reduce runtime and memory. The generated IP models can be reused in different designs or for multiple instantiations within a design. The modeling methodology used in Voltus-XM ensures minimal result difference relative to a fully flat analysis.

Voltus-XM (also known as xPGV generation) is an advanced PGV modeling flow that has been introduced for generating power-grid view (xPGV) models for large digital blocks. The hierarchical analysis flow with XM is intended primarily for reducing the compute resource requirements for the analysis of large designs. It can be used when there are multiple DEF files composing a design, and the blocks represented by the component DEF files can be analyzed independently.
The hierarchical analysis flow with XM has two steps, as shown in the following diagram:

- xPGV generation (Block Analysis) - perform chip-level or block-level rail analysis to obtain only block-level instance current data and generate xPGV model.
- xPGV instantiation (Top-Level Analysis) - instantiate the generated xPGVs and use them to perform top-level rail analysis.
This flow requires the Voltus-XM license. It is an option to the baseline license Voltus-XL. The licensing use model for Voltus-XM is:

- xPGV generation - One Voltus-XM license is required for generating one xPGV model.
- xPGV usage - One Voltus-XM license is required for each xPGV model that is used in the analysis, up to a maximum of 10 XM licenses. Usage of more than 10 xPGV models does not require more than 10 licenses. For example, 10 XM licenses will suffice for the use of 12 xPGV models. License requirement is based on number of models used instead of instantiations. If a CPU block’s xPGV model is instantiated four times, it will require only one XM license.

The block xPGV can be generated either after the signoff static/dynamic IR drop analysis or after the static/dynamic Early Rail Analysis (ERA) of the block. In both cases, the xPGV is generated using the R-C grid of the block and currents captured at the via layer during the analysis. The xPGV generation step creates a context-independent model that does not require a chip-level context for building the xPGV model. Therefore, there is no need to simulate the isolated partitions (surrounding blocks) of large designs during block-level analysis. It has the ability to dynamically create xPGV models for only those IP blocks of the design that you want to analyze.
The following diagram illustrates xPGV model generation:
In the xPGV model generation flow:

- the via cut layer for the upper layers is defined by users.
- top layers above the cut layer are preserved.
- tap currents at the device pin layers are preserved.
- massively reduced RC network is created between the specified via cut layer and current taps.
- the outside connection of the model is defined by LEF.
The reduced RC model is created between the instance taps (instance LEF pins) and the user-specified via cut layer, as shown in the following diagram:

The grid below the via layer, and any grid portion above the via layer which has become disconnected due to the removal of the lower layers are discarded. Any LEF ports below the via layer specified for cutting the grid are also discarded. This reduces the size of the view significantly, because the majority of the resistors in most blocks are on the lower metal layers. This also means that you will not see the lower layers of the block and switched supply nets when using xPGV views.

For block xPGV generation, you need to follow the usual steps of data preparation, power calculation, and rail analysis on the block-level DEF. The xPGV view is created from the results of the block-level rail analysis. To create the xPGV model, rail analysis keeps track of the currents flowing through the user-specified via cut layer in the block.

The block-level xPGV view can then be used to perform rail analysis of the DEFs at the next level up in the hierarchy or for top-level full-chip analysis. The next level rail analysis will run faster with the block-level xPGVs because these runs use the power-grid that is modeled inside the xPGV.

title: Setting up and Running the Hierarchical PI Analysis Flow

lines: For the hierarchical analysis flow, you need two Tcl scripts (first for xPGV generation and second for xPGV instantiation).
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Quantus technology file
- Power-grid libraries
Procedure
Following are the steps for running this flow:

1. In the first Tcl script, load the block-level design data. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 specify_def {/Data//def/top.def /Data//def/hier_block.def} 
 
 specify_spef {Data//spefs/top.spef.gz \ /Data//spefs/hier_block.spef.gz}
2. Specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVecLessPowerResults
3. Specify the power analysis commands. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorlessPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db false \ 
 
 -enable_mt_in_vectorbasedflow true \ 
 
 -power_grid_library /hierpgv_dyn_accurate/All_merge.cl\ 
 
 -enable_state_propagation true 
 
 set_default_switching_activity -input_activity 0.2 \ 
 
 -seq_activity 0.1 -clock_gates_output 2.0 \ 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -period 20 -resolution 20
4. Specify the rail analysis commands. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 -method dynamic \ 
 
 -temperature 125 \ 
 
 -ignore_shorts true \ 
 
 -limit_number_of_steps false \ 
 
 -use_em_view_list /Data//RAIL/submegacells.list \ 
 
 -snap_layer_for_current_taps \ /Data//RAIL/megacell.collapse_tap_current_layer \ 
 
 -power_grid_library $pgv_list \ 
 
 -report_power_in_parallel true \ 
 
 -xpgv_config_file xpgv.conf 
 
 set_power_pads -format defpin -net VDD1 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD1 -force -voltage 1.21 -threshold 1.08 
 
 set_power_pads -format defpin -net VSS 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -force -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.12 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name core -pwrnets VDD1 -gndnets VSS 
 
 analyze_rail -type domain -output dynamic_rail core 
 
 
 
 The following parameter is specific to the xPGV generation flow:
  - -xpgv_config_file file - specifies block details, such as, block name for which xPGV is generated, via cut layer name that indicates to preserve the layers about the cut layer, and block LEF file names. This parameter is specified during block-level analysis. 
 Configuration File Format : 
 CELL < block_name > 
 CUT_LAYER < cut_via_layer_name > 
 Lef < tech_lef_name block_lef_name > 
 END. 
 Example 
 CELL hier_block 
 CUT_LAYER V4 
 Lef {/Data/lefs/tech.lef /Data/lefs/hier_block.lef} 
 END
5. Generate the block-level xPGVs. 
 Tcl command: 
 
 
 
 generate_xpgv -cell hier_block -output_dir xpgv -state_directory dynamic_rail/core_125C_dynamic_1 
 
 
 
 The log file will generate the power-grid view generation statistics, as shown in the following log file snippet: 
 ** INFO: (VOLTUS_LGEN-3265): 
 Power Grid View Generation Statistics: 
 # Total number of cells: 1 
 # EARLY view created: 1 (100%) 
 # IR view created: 1 (100%) 
 # EM view created: 1 (100%) 
 Finished PGV Library Generator at 13:08:20 02/15/2019 (cpu=0:00:01, real=0:01:01, peak mem=511.77MB)
6. In the second Tcl script, specify the design loading (top-level design data), power analysis, and rail analysis commands. Following is a snippet of the top-level rail analysis commands using the generated xPGV model: 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 -method dynamic \ 
 
 -temperature 125 \ 
 
 -ignore_shorts true \ 
 
 -limit_number_of_steps false \ 
 
 -use_em_view_list /Data//RAIL/submegacells.list \ 
 
 -snap_layer_for_current_taps \ /Data//RAIL/megacell.collapse_tap_current_layer \ 
 
 -power_grid_library {/Data/hierpgv_dyn_accurate/All_merge.cl \ ../xpgv/hier_block/hier_block.cl} \ 
 
 -report_power_in_parallel true \ 
 
 -flatten_xpgv_block_instances use_def.txt 
 
 set_power_pads -format defpin -net VDD1 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD1 -force -voltage 1.21 -threshold 1.08 
 
 set_power_pads -format defpin -net VSS 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -force -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.12 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name core -pwrnets VDD1 -gndnets VSS 
 
 analyze_rail -type domain -output dynamic_rail core 
 
 
 
 The following parameters are specific to the xPGV instantiation flow:
  - -power_grid_library libraries - specifies the generated xPGV library and other PGV libraries being used in the flow.
  - -flatten_xpgv_block_instances file - specifies a file that includes a list of block instances that use DEF instead of xPGV for power and rail analysis, that is, specify the instances that are not to be used as xPGV. This parameter allows you exclude DEFs for xPGV blocks. You can specify a few instances of the same block that will use DEF, and the others that will use xPGV at the top-level. If you do not specify this parameter, and if both DEF and xPGV are given for a block, xPGV takes precedence over DEF. 
 Instance List File Format : 
 < block_name > < block_instance_name |All|None> 
 Example 
 For a design with three blocks, if you want to use the DEF and xPGV as following: 
 Block_1 - > A1 (xPGV) & A2 (DEF) 
 Block_2 - > B1 & B2 (xPGV for both instances) 
 Block_3 - > C1 & C2 (DEF for both instances) 
 The specified file will contain the following information: 
 Block_1 A2 
 Block_2 None 
 Block_3 All
Output
The xPGV output directory will contain the following information:

data: The name of the macro power-grid library is < cellname > .cl . It contains the physical and electrical representation of the hierarchical block that is required for rail analysis.

A report for the cell's power/ground net containing: 
 
 name of power and ground nets, voltage value for power pins 
 number of taps for the Early/EM/IR views
A report containing capacitance, the current of analyzed nets, resistors, and the number of current taps and layers for the Early/EM/IR views.

lines: The top-level analysis will generate the following:

- Static power reports and plots
- Dynamic currents and IR-drop plots

- Overview
- Data Requirements for ESD Effective Resistance Checks
- Specifying ESD Rules
  - Rule for computing clamp-to-clamp resistance
  - Rule for computing bump-to-clamp resistance
  - Rule for computing bump-to-clamp resistance (shorted clamps)
  - Rule for computing bump-to-bump resistance (shorted bumps)
  - Rule for computing bump-to-bump resistance (unshorted bumps)
  - Rule for computing bump-to-instance resistance
  - Rule for computing clamp-to-instance resistance
  - Rule for reporting isolated bump/clamps
  - EM Rule for all nets with zaps and sinks with all bumps shorted on respective nets
  - EM rule for zaps and sinks on specific bumps on specific nets
- Performing ESD Analysis
- Analyzing ESD Reports
- Viewing Effective Resistance Check Results
- CDM Based ESD Analysis

Electrostatic Discharge (ESD) is defined as the transient electric current between two objects at different electrostatic potentials causing an ESD event. Due to shrinking geometries of nanometer designs, devices are at a higher risk to ESD exposure and are prone to damage.
To prevent an ESD event, you can place ESD devices at power/ground pads to provide an efficient discharge path between one bump to another.

Calculation of effective resistance between a large pair of nodes is a difficult engineering problem with high computational complexity. Although there are graph-based search algorithms, they only identify the least resistance paths but not the effective resistance between power/ground bumps and ESD devices. Because of the presence of multiple alternate paths between bumps and ESD cells, the resistance of least resistive path will be considerably higher than the effective resistance.

The ESD Analysis ( analyze_esd_network ) flow in Voltus allows you to perform different types of effective resistance checks, such as bump to clamp, bump to bump, and clamp to clamp, and current density analysis during an ESD zap event. This command checks whether every power or ground bump has an ESD device, and the device is placed in such a way that it does not violate the effective resistance or current density limit. The objective of ESD Checker is to easily identify the violations caused by high effective resistance or the current density above the specified threshold value. It also performs isolation checks, that is, if any bump does not have a path to an ESD cell (indicating existence of isolated bumps), or if a clamp is not connected to any bump (indicating existence of isolated clamps).

In addition, you can perform the Charged Device Model (CDM) based ESD analysis ( analyze_esd_voltage ) that allows you to report violations where potential difference across driver/receiver is greater than the given threshold.
ESD analysis supports the following features:

- bump to clamp effective resistance check - Effective resistance is computed between each bump to all ESD devices in the design, or from each ESD device to all bumps in the design (if the report method is reverse). It also supports ESD analysis from shorted bumps to each clamp. Therefore, it allows you to perform two types of checks, one is shorted bumps to all clamps and second is each bump to each clamp. Both checks will be done with a different threshold value.
- bump to bump effective resistance check - Effective resistance is computed for all paths - from a power bump to an ESD device to a ground bump. It calculates and reports the effective least resistance path for each bump of a power net to each bump of a ground net through clamps. This rule works only for domain-based or multiple domain-based analysis since it calculates the least effective resistance path between power and ground nets taking into account multiple clamps in the path. 
 The ESD analysis flow also supports bump to bump ESD checks over a user-specified radius. The software can calculate bump to bump resistance only for bumps within the specified radius. This feature allows you to:
  - select and check bumps only in the given radius. This reduces the number of bumps and ESD output file size, and improves the runtime.
  - check for weak ESD cells within radius, and skip/fix them.
  - ignore uninterested large paths.
- clamp to clamp effective resistance check - Effective resistance is computed between all clamps for each net.
- bump to instance effective resistance check - Effective resistance is computed between every bump to instance, and will check if the resistance value is less than that of the same bump to clamp cell. If the resistance value of bump to clamp is more than the resistance value of bump to instance, then a fail status is given.
- clamp to instance effective resistance check - Effective resistance is computed between every instance to nearest clamp. If the resistance value of instance to clamp is more than the threshold value, then a fail status is given. This report is important to perform CDM check.
- Isolated bump/clamp check - Checks if there are any bumps that are not protected by an ESD cell, or checks if there are any clamps that are not connected to any bumps.
- Current density/EM analysis - Checks whether current densities are not exceeding limits that cause metal melting or fusion. 
 
You can perform ESD analysis using the read_def or specify_def flow. For information on running analyze _esd_network with specify_def , refer to the ESD Analysis with specify_def section in this chapter.

title: Data Requirements for ESD Effective Resistance Checks

lines: The following table lists the required inputs for performing ESD resistance checks in Voltus:

data: ESD cells are tagged using the -esd_cells or -esd_cells_list_file parameter of the set_advanced_pg_library_mode command, and ESD cell pins are tagged using the -esd_pin_list parameter of the set_advanced_pg_library_mode command. The ESD cell PGV contains cell pin information and ESD device electrical characteristics.

A text file to specify a set of rules that define all the ESD checks to be performed along with the effective resistance threshold limit. 
 
 Some of the characteristics of the rule file are: 
 
 All rules should be in a single file 
 A single rule specifies the type of check and the pass/fail criteria 
 The various rule types are: 
 clamp2clamp - computes clamp to clamp resistance 
 bump2clamp - computes bump to clamp resistance 
 bump2bump - computes bump to bump resistance 
 bump2instance - computes bump to instance resistance 
 clamp2instance - computes clamp to instance resistance 
 em - checks for current density 
 connectivity - checks for isolated bumps or clamps
This file has the same format as the ICT EM file. It contains the current density check limits for each metal layer.

The technology library contains the extraction technology file, area capacitance specification, decap/filler/powergate cells, and tech view for all the cells. ESD analysis requires only LEF-based PGVs.
The DEF files contain information about cell placement, power/ground grid, and signal routing.

lines: Voltus allows you to specify all the rules for computing effective resistance in a single rule file. The rule file is specified using the -config_file parameter of the analyze _esd_network command.
The rule syntax is case insensitive. The syntax of the rule file is:
Syntax for Resistance Checks
Syntax for EM Checks
Parameters

data: Allows to filter clamps, that is, only clamps with an effective resistance to bump value less than the specified arc threshold value will be considered for the bump-to-bump resistance check.
Specifies the length (x) and width (y) of the bump region in μm for radius-based bump-to-bump ESD checks.

Allows you to specify the bumps for which the rule is run. bumps is mutually exclusive with the power , ground , nets parameters. 
 
 The following are the two use models: 
 
 specify the bump names for the bump2clamp and clamp2clamp rule types: 
 bumps < list_of_bumps > 
 specify the bump names for the em and bump2bump rule types: 
 bumps -from < list_of_bumps > -to < list_of_bumps >

For bumps , you can specify the following parameters: 
 
 -from and -to parameters to indicate the list of starting and ending bumps. You must specify the bumps within the same net. This parameter is applicable to the em and bump2bump rule types. 
 -reporting {original | reverse | both} specifies the reporting format for sorting of the rule. The default value is original . 
 -report_threshold value specifies to report EM violations above the threshold value. The value is the ratio of current (avg) to the respective EM limit. The default value is 0.9 . This parameter is applicable to the em and bump2bump rule types.

Excludes or includes the list of cells given in the specified file. 
 
 If this parameter is not specified, ESD analysis is performed on all cells in the design. The specified cell list text file contains a list of cells. Each cell name must reside on a separate line in the file. 
 
 The following is the format of the specified file: 
 
 cell1 
 
 cell2
Specifies to check isolated bumps. The default value is false . This parameter is applicable only to the connectivity rule type.
Specifies to check isolated clamps. The default value is false . This parameter is applicable only to the connectivity rule type.
clamp_cells -from cell1 cell2 cell3 -to cell1 cell2 cell3

Specifies the clamp cells for which the rule is run. clamp_cells and clamp_types are mutually exclusive. 
 
 For clamp2clamp , you need to specify the -from and -to parameters to specify the starting and ending clamp cells. For the other types of checks, you need to specify individual clamp cells. The -from and -to parameters are not required.
clamp_types -from type1 type2 type3 ... -to type1 type2 type3 ...

Specifies the type of clamp for which the rule is run. The different clamp types are stored in PGV. clamp_cells and clamp_types are mutually exclusive. 
 
 For clamp2clamp , you need to specify the -from and -to parameters to specify the starting and ending clamp types. For the other types of checks, you need to specify individual clamp cell types. The -from and -to parameters are not required.

Specifies the zap current value. This parameter is applicable to the em and bump2bump rule types. 
 
 For bump2bump , this parameter must be specified when the IV curve PGV models are used to perform non-linear ESD analysis.

Enables shorting of clamps on the specified nets and performs effective resistance check from each bump to the shorted clamps. It also reports the number of shorted ESD clamps that are connected in parallel to each bump. 
 
 This parameter is applicable to the bump2clamp type.

EM analysis is performed when IV curve PGV based ESD analysis is performed. You can use this option to report those EM results if required. It is recommended to set em_results to false for saving disk space. The default value is false . 
 
 This parameter is applicable to the bump2bump rule type.

Specifies to do bump2bump analysis till taps. For this analysis, it is recommended to use ESD PGV generated using Voltus-Fi, and tag the ESD devices inside the PGV. The default value is false . 
 
 This parameter is applicable to the bump2bump type.
ground -nets { net1 net2 net3 ... | all} -short_bumps {true | false}

Specifies the list of ground nets to be analyzed. You can specify all to include all ground nets. 
 
 For bump2clamp , you can specify -short_bumps true to do a resistance check from a shorted bump to each clamp. If -short_bumps is set to false , resistance check is done for every bump to every clamp. You can have two rules for bump2clamp , one with -short_bumps set to true and the other with -short_bumps set to false . Both these rules will have a different value for reff_threshold .

Excludes or includes the list of instances given in the specified file. 
 
 If this parameter is not specified, ESD analysis is performed on all instances in the design. The specified instance list text file contains a list of instances. Each instance name must reside on a separate line in the file. 
 
 The following is the format of the specified file: 
 
 instance1 
 
 instance2

Specifies the threshold for least effective resistance path between bump2bump, beyond which failure will be reported. If a threshold value is not specified, all rules will be passed. 
 
 This parameter is applicable to the bump2bump type.

Allows you to specify the nets for which ESD analysis is to be performed. nets is mutually exclusive with the power , ground , bumps parameters. This parameter gives you the flexibility to specify any nets for the -from and -to options, and does not confine to the power nets for the -from list and the ground nets for the -to list. 
 
 The following are the two use models: 
 
 specify the net names for the bump2clamp and clamp2clamp rule types: 
 nets < list_of_nets |all> 
 specify the net names for the em and bump2bump rule types: 
 nets -from < list_of_nets |all> -to < list_of_nets |all>

For nets , you can specify the following options: 
 
 -from and -to parameters to indicate the list of starting and ending nets. You can either specify the list of nets or all to include all nets. 
 -short_net_bumps specifies to short bumps of the specified nets only. 
 -short_bumps specifies to short all bumps for each net. The default value is false . 
 -short_bumps and -short_net_bumps are mutually exclusive. 
 -reporting {original | reverse | both} specifies the reporting format for sorting of the rule. The default value is original . 
 -report_threshold value specifies to report EM violations above the threshold value. The value is the ratio of current (avg) to the respective EM limit. The default value is 0.9 . This parameter is applicable to the em and bump2bump rule types.

Specifies the minimum number of shorted clamps between bumps. The number of shorted clamps should be greater than or equal to the given threshold value, beyond which failure will be reported. This is an optional parameter that is used with the effective_calculation parameter. 
 
 This parameter is applicable to the bump2clamp type.

Specifies the resistance threshold to report the equivalent parallel resistance through multiple clamps in the bump-to-bump path. The equivalent parallel resistance is calculated by considering only those clamps with effective resistance less than the specified arc_reff_threshold.

Specifies the Pass/Fail criteria for bumps/clamps/nets. 
 
 all : criterion for a bump/clamp/net to pass is that all paths from that bump/clamp/net passes. 
 one : criterion for a bump/clamp/net to pass is if atleast one path from that bump/clamp/net passes. 
 
 The default value is all .

Specifies the resistance threshold for the bump-to-bump path's effective resistance to pass. A pass status is given when the effective resistance is below the specified threshold value, and a fail status is given when the effective resistance is above the specified threshold value.
power -nets { net1 net2 net3 ... | all} -short_bumps {true | false}

Specifies the list of power nets to be analyzed. You can specify all to include all power nets. 
 
 For bump2clamp , you can specify -short_bumps true to do a resistance check from a shorted bump to each clamp. If -short_bumps is set to false , resistance check is done for every bump to every clamp. You can have two rules for bump2clamp , one with -short_bumps set to true and the other with -short_bumps set to false . Both these rules will have a different value for reff_threshold .

Specifies the minimum allowed threshold for the resistance check information written to the report file. The default value is 0 . 
 
 This parameter is applicable to all effective resistance check rule types.

Reports bump-to-bump resistance for the power-to-ground pair. When this parameter is set to false , reports power-to-power and ground-to-ground bump-to-bump resistance with the power-to-ground pair bump-to-bump resistance. The default value is true .

Controls the size of the effective resistance check report by limiting the number of lines written to the report file. This parameter is useful in limiting the report size when you specify a very low resistance check threshold. The default value is 500 . 
 
 This parameter is applicable to all effective resistance check rule types.

Specifies the reporting format that can be used for sorting of the rule. This parameter gives you the flexibility to report the effective resistance in a different sequence. By default, it reports as per the rule type. 
 
 The options of this parameter are: 
 
 reverse - reports effective resistance from clamp to bump. It checks if any clamp is failing from clamp to bump. 
 original - reports effective resistance from bump to clamp. It checks if any bump is failing from bump to clamp. This is the default value. 
 both - reports effective resistance both from bump to clamp and clamp to bump. 
 
 Example for rule type bump2clamp : 
 
 When reporting is set to original , it reports effective resistance from bump to clamp. 
 When reporting is set to reverse , it reports effective resistance from clamp to bump. 
 When reporting is set to both , it reports effective resistance from bump to clamp and clamp to bump.
Specifies the name of the rule.

Specifies the resistance threshold for the rule to pass (rule pass criteria). A pass status is given when the effective resistance is below the specified threshold value, and a fail status is given when the effective resistance is above the specified threshold value. 
 
 This parameter is applicable to clamp2clamp , bump2bump , and bump2clamp types.
Specifies to short only the pins of the clamp pair for which the effective resistance is being calculated. By default, the tool shorts pins for all clamps. The default value is false .

The setup parameter is used to enable zap analysis on power or ground nodes of a cross-domain pair, wherein the driver-receiver pair have different power or ground domains. It generates current density report for each resistor on the grid for cross-domains with multiple nets between the source and sink bumps. 
 
 -ahdl_include , -spice_include , and -iv_lib are mutually exclusive. Use one of these parameters to provide the non-linear connections (ESD models) between the power and ground pins, instead of directly providing ESD Ron/Roff resistor values. 
 -iv_lib means inherit the model created in PGV run. 
 -cross_domain true indicates that all the domain nets that have ESD connections with the source and sink net will be taken into account during zap analysis. The default value of false indicates that the tool simulates only the network of the source and sink net.
Specifies to calculate only the least effective resistance path (LERP). When set to true , the effective resistance values will not be reported. The default value is false .
sink -type {power | ground} -nets { net1 net2 net3 ... | all}-short_bumps {true | false} -report_threshold value
source -type {power | ground} -nets { net1 net2 net3 ... | all} -short_bumps {true | false} -report_threshold value

For em check, source (the point where current is injected), and sink (the point from where the current comes out) details are provided. For both source and sink, you need to specify the type of net (power/ground), the nets to be analyzed, and whether bumps are to be shorted. You can specify EM rules for all nets or specific nets with zaps and sinks on bumps. 
 
 If -short_bumps is set to true , current is injected in the shorted node (vdd), and comes out of the shorted node (vss). 
 
 In the em rule check, the software runs a static simulation and checks current through each metal line against the ESD EM techfile.

Specifies a pair of nets separated by “:” that are skipped during bump2bump analysis. 
 
 This parameter is applicable to the bump2bump type.
type {clamp2clamp | bump2bump | bump2clamp | bump2instance | clamp2instance | connectivity | em}

This section describes the various inputs and outputs of the ESD analysis flow.
Required Input Files

- ESD Cell PGVs
- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- ESD Rule File
- ESD EM technology file
- Technology PGV
Procedure
To perform ESD analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Create PGVs for ESD cells. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef ../../design/BONDPAD52.lef ../../design/giolib045_modified.lef 
 
 set_pg_library_mode - celltype techonly -ground_pins { VSS } -power_pins { VDD 1 } - lef_layermap ./lefdef.map -extraction_tech_file ./qrcTechFile 
 
 set_advanced_pg_library_mode -esd_cells_list_file {esd_cell_list} -esd_parameters_file param_file.txt -esd_pin_list {VDD VSS VDDESD VSSESD} 
 
 generate_pg_library - output esd_pgv 
 
 
 
 Here, the following parameters are specific to the ESD analysis flow: 
 The format of the -esd_cells_list_file file is: 
 <clamp cell name> <clamp type>
esd_decap_vdd CLAMPIO #clamp type can be used to group clamps
esd_decap_core CLAMPCORE 
 The format of the -esd_parameters_file file is: 
 Cell <cell name> <Ron> <Roff> <power_pin> <ground_pin> <voltage> 
Cell esd_decap_vdd 0.9 10000 vdd_esd vss_esd 0.4
  - -esd_cells_list_file specifies the ESD clamp cells that are to be analyzed.
  - -esd_pin_list specifies the ESD clamp pins that are to be analyzed. Effective resistance analysis will be done only if the nets are connected to these pins.
  - -esd_parameters_file specifies the electrical characteristics of the clamp cells.
2. Load the design data. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 specify_def {/data/def/top_final.def /data/def/dmu.def}
3. Define the rail analysis mode. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method static \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 -power_grid_library ./data/pgv_dir/esd_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 -ignore_shorts true\ 
 
 -enable_rlrp_analysis true \ 
 
 -ict_em_models ./data/ESD_em.ict \
4. Specify the power/ground net voltages, and define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : . 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.8 -force 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0 -threshold 0.1 -force 
 
 set_power_pads -format xy -net VDD -file ./data/top_VDD.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -format xy -net VSS -file ./data/top_VSS.pp
5. Specify the rail analysis power domain. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name PD - pwrnets { VDD } - gndnets { VSS }
6. Run ESD analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_esd_network PD -config_file rule1.txt -output ESD_analysis -type domain -use_power_pad true 
 
 
 
 Instead of shorting all nodes of the lowest ESD cell pin layer, you can specify the – clamp_pin_short_file < pin_shorting_file > parameter to specify the list of pin layers to be shorted for bump-to-clamp and clamp-to-clamp resistance checks. 
 The format of the -clamp_pin_short_file file is: 
 #Cell specification
CELL <cell name> <pin> <layer>

#Instance specification
INST <instance name> <pin> <layer>
The log file will generate the ESD analysis statistics, as shown in the following log file snippet:

The analyze_esd_network command creates a state directory inside the < output_directory > specified with the command. The ESD directory is created inside this state directory. The ESD directory contains a detailed report for each rule along with a summary report for all the rules. Following is an example of the directory structure:
ESD_analysis/top_25C_esd_1/ESD
The ESD folder contains the following files:

- b2c_rule_CORE.reff
- b2c_rule_all_clamps.reff
- c2c_rule_core.reff
- esd_isolation.reff
- em_rule_all_nets_VDD_short_VSS_short
- summary.rpt
Output
The ESD directory will contain the following files:

data: A report that gives a summary of all rules and their pass/fail status.
< rule_name >.reff files that contain detailed reports of each type of rule.
This folder contains current density detailed results.

lines: The following reports are generated for ESD Analysis in Voltus:

- Effective Resistance Check Reports
- Current Density Check Report
Effective Resistance Check Reports
The following reports are generated for effective resistance checks:

- Bump-to-Clamp Report
- Clamp-to-Clamp Report
- Bump-to-Bump Report
- Clamp-to-Instance Report
- Bump-to-Instance Report
- Isolated Bumps or Clamps Report
- Summary Report
The report formats and samples are provided below.
Report of Bump-to-Clamp Type Rule
Sample filename: b2c_rule1.reff
Format:
Format Description:
bump specifies the bump name.
x y specifies the location of the bump.
layer specifies the metal layer on which the bump is located.

pass/fail specifies the status of the clamp instance - a pass status is given when the bump to clamp effective resistance is below the specified threshold value and a fail status is given when the effective resistance is above the specified threshold value.
R ohm specifies the bump to clamp effective resistance.
threshold specifies the minimum allowed effective resistance between a power or ground bump and an ESD device pair.
clamp cell specifies the name of the ESD clamp cell.
x y specifies the location of the clamp cell pin node. If there are multiple nodes on the pin and are shorted, this is the location of the shorted pin.
pin specifies the name of the ESD pin.
layer specifies the metal layer of the pin whose x,y location is given.
clamp instance specifies the name of the ESD clamp instance.
Sample Report:
Format (bump to shorted clamp):
Format Description:
bump specifies the bump name.
x y specifies the location of the bump.
layer specifies the metal layer on which the bump is located.

pass/fail specifies the status of the shorted clamp instance - a pass status is given when the bump to shorted clamp effective resistance is below the specified threshold value ( reff_threshold ) and the number of shorted clamps is greater than or equal to the specified threshold value ( parallel_clamp_threshold ). Similarly, a fail status is given when the bump to shorted clamp effective resistance is above the specified threshold value ( reff_threshold ) or the number of shorted clamps is less than the specified threshold value ( parallel_clamp_threshold ).
R ohm specifies the bump to clamp effective resistance.
threshold specifies the minimum allowed effective resistance between a power or ground bump and an ESD device pair.
parallel clamps specifies the number of shorted clamps.
pin specifies the name of the ESD pin.
Report of Clamp-to-Clamp Type Rule
Sample filename: c2c_rule1.reff
Format:
Format Description:
clamp cell specifies the from-clamp cell name.
x y specifies the location of the from-clamp cell.
pin specifies the name of the ESD pin of the from-clamp cell.
layer specifies the metal layer of the ESD pin of the from-clamp cell.
clamp instance specifies the name of the clamp instance.

pass/fail specifies the status of the to-clamp instance - a pass status is given when the clamp to clamp effective resistance is below the specified threshold value and a fail status is given when the effective resistance is above the specified threshold value.
R ohm specifies the clamp to clamp effective resistance.
threshold specifies the minimum allowed effective resistance between the from-clamp and to-clamp cells.
clamp cell specifies the to-clamp cell name.
x y specifies the location of the to-clamp cell.
pin specifies the name of the ESD pin of the to-clamp cell.
layer specifies the metal layer of the ESD pin of the to-clamp cell.
clamp instance specifies the name of the to-clamp instance.
Sample Report:
Report of Clamp to Instance Rule
Sample filename: c2i_core.reff
Format:
Format Description:
clamp cell specifies the clamp cell name.
x y specifies the location of the clamp cell.
pin specifies the name of the ESD pin of the clamp cell.
layer specifies the metal layer on which the pin is located.
clamp instance specifies the name of the clamp instance.

pass/fail specifies the status of the instance - a pass status is given when the clamp to instance effective resistance is below the specified threshold value and a fail status is given when the effective resistance is above the specified threshold value.
R ohm specifies the clamp to instance effective resistance.
threshold specifies the minimum allowed effective resistance between the clamp and instance.
cell specifies the cell name.
x y specifies the location of the cell.
pin specifies the name of the cell pin.
layer specifies the metal layer on which the pin is located.
instance specifies the name of the instance.
Sample Report:
Report of Bump to Instance Rule
Sample filename: b2i_core.reff
Format:
Format Description:
clamp cell specifies the clamp cell name.
x y specifies the location of the clamp cell.
pin specifies the name of the ESD pin of the clamp cell.
layer specifies the metal layer on which the clamp cell is located.
clamp instance specifies the name of the clamp instance.
bump specifies the bump name.
x y specifies the location of the bump.
layer specifies the metal layer on which the bump is located.

pass/fail specifies the status of the instance - a pass status is given when the bump to clamp is below the resistance value of bump to instance, and a fail status is given when the resistance value of bump to clamp is more than the resistance value of bump to instance.
B2C ohm specifies the bump to clamp effective resistance.
B2I ohm specifies the bump to instance effective resistance.
cell specifies the cell name.
x y specifies the location of the cell.
pin specifies the name of the cell pin.
layer specifies the metal layer on which the cell is located.
instance specifies the name of the instance.
Sample Report:
Report of Isolated Bump/Clamp Rule
Sample filename: esd_isolation.reff
Format:
Format Description:
Isolated Total Bumps specifies the total number of isolated bumps and lists the names of the isolated bumps.
Isolated Total Clamps specifies the total number of isolated clamps and lists the names of the isolated clamps.
Sample Report:
Current Density Check Report
Sample filename: VDD.rj.avg.rpt
Format:
Format Description:
I(mA) specifies the current value that flows through the Metal/Via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Ilimit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Layer specifies the metal/via layer name on which the resistor is located.
Location specifies the coordinates of the resistor.

ShortLength is also known as "Blech length" or "Path length". It can be explained as "the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer".
ShortLength = Drawn length of longest continuous same metal * Layout scale factor (from qrctechFile).
Width specifies the width of the resistor.
Via Area is the drawn dimension via area in square microns.
Via Cuts is the number of via cuts.

Fix Width/ Via The “Fix Width/Via” is recalculated by modifying the existing via width to eliminate EM violation. If I/Ilimit is greater than 1, which is a violation, Fix Width/Via will be: violation * via width
Current Direction is applicable only to via layers where 'D" denotes down, "U" denotes up.

Benefit Factor When certain constraints (blue-highlighted - “L>3W<0.4”) are met, its EM limit can be 2X, 3X, or multiple X of the base EM limit. These multiples are called Benefit Factor (red-highlighted in the above example - “1”, "2").
Here, W is the wire width and L is the Blech length.
J(A/cm^2) is the current density value.
Jmax(A/cm^2) is the current density limit.
ResistorID is the unique ID assigned to a resistor.

Sample Report:
The Summary report summarizes the PASS/FAIL status of each rule with rule details. This report allows you to assess the ESD impact on the design before referring to the individual detailed reports.
Sample filename: summary.rpt
Sample Report:
The following are snippets of the summary report:
- Clamp-to-Clamp Rule
Rule Status : Specifies the PASS / FAIL status of the rule. It will have the status PASS if all the nets under this rule pass, and will have the status FAIL if even one net fails.
Net : Nets analyzed under this rule
Type : Specifies the type of the net being analyzed
FromClamps : Number of clamps analyzed under the from section of the rule on each net
ToClamps : Number of clamps analyzed under the to section of the rule on each net

Total Paths : Number of paths analyzed. If from and to clamps are of the same clamp type, total paths = #FromClamps * (#Toclamps – 1) . If from and to are not of same clamp type, total paths = #Fromclamps * #Toclamps.
PassClamps : Number of pass clamps. Criterion for a clamp to pass is if all paths from that clamp passes.
FailClamps : Number of fail clamps.
Status : Status of each net. Status is set to Pass if FailClamps = 0 , and is set to Fail if FailClamps > 0 .
MinRes : The minimum resistance value for each net.
MaxRes : The maximum resistance value for each net.
- Bump-to-Clamp Rule
Rule Status : Specifies the PASS / FAIL status of the rule. It will have the status PASS if all the nets under this rule pass, and will have the status FAIL if even one net fails.
Net : Nets analyzed under this rule
Type : Specifies the type of the net being analyzed
Clamps : Clamp cells on each net
Bumps : Bumps on each net
Short : Specifies if bumps were shorted
Total Paths : Number of paths analyzed. If bumps are shorted, total paths is equal to the number of clamps. If bumps are not shorted, total paths is equal to #Bumps * #Clamps
PassPaths : Number of pass bumps in the B2C Summary, or number of pass clamps in the C2B Summary. Criterion for a bump/clamp to pass is if all paths from that bump/clamp passes.
FailPaths : Number of fail bumps/clamps.
Status : Status of each net. Status is set to Pass if FailPaths = 0 , and is set to Fail if FailPaths > 0 .
MinRes : The minimum resistance value for each net
MaxRes : The maximum resistance value for each net
- Isolated Bumps and ESD Clamps
Rule Status : Specifies the PASS / FAIL status of the rule. It will have the status PASS if all the nets under this rule pass, and will have the status FAIL if even one net fails.
Net : Nets analyzed under this rule
Type : Specifies the type of the net being analyzed
Clamps : Number of clamps on each net
Bumps : Number of bumps on each net
Isolated Clamps : Number of isolated clamps on each net
Isolated Bumps : Number of isolated bumps on each net
Status : Status of each net. Status is set to Pass if Isolated Clamps/Isolated Bumps = 0 , and is set to Fail if Isolated Clamps/Isolated Bumps > 0 .
- Bump-to-Bump Rule
Rule Status : Specifies the PASS / FAIL status of the rule. It will have the status PASS if all the nets under this rule pass, and will have the status FAIL if even one net fails.
FromNet : Specifies the from-power/ground net name
ToNet : Specifies the to-power/ground net name
FromBumps: Number of bumps defined under the from section of the rule on each net
ToBumps : Number of bumps defined under the to section of the rule on each net
TotalPaths : Number of paths analyzed
FailedPaths : Number of failed paths
Status : Status of each net to net loop. Status is set to Pass if FailedPaths = 0 , and is set to Fail if FailedPaths > 0 .
MinLoop : The minimum loop resistance value for each net
MaxLoop : The maximum loop resistance value for each net
- Bump-to-Instance Rule
Rule Status : Specifies the PASS / FAIL status of the rule. It will have the status PASS if all the nets under this rule pass, and will have the status FAIL if even one net fails.
Net : Nets analyzed under this rule
Type : Specifies the type of the net being analyzed
Clamps : Clamp cells on each net
Bump : Bumps on each net
Instances : Instances on each net
TotalPaths : Number of paths analyzed
FailInstances : Number of failed instances
Status : Status of each net. Status is set to Pass if FailInstances = 0 , and is set to Fail if FailInstances > 0 .
MinRes : The minimum resistance value for each net
MaxRes : The maximum resistance value for each net
- Clamp-to-Instance Rule
Rule Status : Specifies the PASS / FAIL status of the rule. It will have the status PASS if all the nets under this rule pass, and will have the status FAIL if even one net fails.
Net : Nets analyzed under this rule
Type : Specifies the type of the net being analyzed
Clamps : Clamp cells on each net
Instances : Instances on each net
TotalPaths : Number of paths analyzed
FailPaths : Number of failed paths
Status : Status of each net. Status is set to Pass if FailPaths = 0 , and is set to Fail if FailPaths > 0 .
MinRes : The minimum resistance value for each net
MaxRes : The maximum resistance value for each net
Voltus GUI supports the visual display of ESD resistance check and EM results. To view the ESD analysis results:

1. Select Power Rail - ESD Results . 
 The ESD Summary window appears. This window displays the list of rules that were analyzed.
2. Click the browse button to access the ESD directory under the state directory to load results, and click Load .
3. Click on a specific rule to view a summary of the rule. 
 The Rule Detail window appears. This window displays details of the rule selected in the ESD Summary window (top section), and the status summary for that rule (bottom section). 
 
 For the em rule type, the bottom section of the Rule Detail window shows the multiple nets associated with a domain.
4. Select a net (resistance check) in the lower section of the form to see the corresponding bumps/clamps and ESD cells. 
 For the em rule type, select a domain in the lower section to see the net-based current density information. The Domain window displays multiple tabs based on the nets in the domain. Each tab will include the EM ratio, current values for the wire segment, current limit values, layer name of the segment, location of the segment, and width of the segment. 
 The Net window appears. This window displays the From and To sections for the net selected in the Rule Detail window. 
 
 The top section displays the From Clamps/Bumps . You can select a row from the top section to display the corresponding To Clamps/Bumps in the bottom section. In addition, the GUI will highlight the fly-lines between from clamps/bumps and to clamps/bumps, as shown below: 
 
 You can select the Show Plot option to show or hide flight-lines when viewing ESD resistance-check results in the GUI.
ESD Analysis with specify_def
The analyze_esd_network command supports the specify_def flow where the design DEFs are not loaded at the outset which leads to performance gain.
Following is an example command list illustrating how to run analyze_esd_network with specify_def , including the PGV generation step:

Charged-device model (CDM) based ESD events occur when a chip is charged during manufacturing, for example, during board assembly, and it comes in contact with a conductive surface. Even with CDM robustness of advanced mixed signal ICs, CDM failures on gate oxides can occur on driver and receiver pairs within the same power and ground domain. A large voltage drop between the local ground of drivers and receivers can result in a high voltage drop on the receiver gate, resulting in oxide damage. Although, CDM ESD damage often occurs at power domain signal crossings, but it can also occur for high resistive ground connections between driver and receiver.

The CDM based ESD Analysis flow allows you to report the driver-receiver violation pairs and perform static IR Drop analysis to check the voltage drop over a given limit between all driver and receiver pairs. This flow helps to analyze the driver/receiver pairs for CDM failure.
The following inputs are required for CDM based ESD analysis:

- Net name
- Driver/receiver file of the net
- ESD pin location file
- Current distribution layer
- Total ESD current to be distributed
The output of the analysis is a text report (.rpt), which contains the driver/receiver voltage drop information for the specified net.
The following steps are performed to run the CDM based ESD Analysis flow:

1. Load the design data or the Innovus database.
2. Perform power calculation to generate the driver/receiver pairs. 
 You must specify the -create_driver_db parameter of the set_power_analysis_mode command attribute to determine all the drivers/receivers on the ground net.
3. Set up rail analysis for extraction.
4. Perform IR Drop analysis for every driver/receiver pair and report voltage drop above the given limit. Use the analyze_esd_voltage command to provide the current injection points (sinks), the current distribution layer (source), and the total ESD current to be distributed. 
 The command generates a report containing all the violations where potential difference across driver/receiver is greater than the given threshold.

For today’s system-on-chip (SoC) designs, it is essential that the IC power analysis includes the package effects for accurate simulation results from the early design stage to the signoff stage. To address this need, Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution supports the following two types of package models for both the static and dynamic rail analysis:

- Simple lumped resistance, capacitance, and inductance (RLC) pin model for the block-level or early design analysis
- Fully distributed and coupled RLCK SPICE model for full-chip signoff. This model can be generated from different IC packaging modeling products such as, Sigrity XtractIM.

To improve the chip-package co-simulation turn-around time and flow usability, Volltus IC Power Integrity Solution also provides a feature called as: Voltus-Sigrity Package Analysis (SPA). It allows the IC designers to access Sigrity products from the Voltus environment and seamlessly perform:

- Package model extraction for IC rail analysis
- Package-aware on-chip static and/or dynamic rail analysis
- IC-aware package IR drop analysis for validation and what-if analysis
- Chip-package result co-display and cross reference for debugging and what-if analysis

The license requirement for SPA is Voltus-L/XL + Voltus-AA + Voltus-SPA. For details on licensing, refer to Product and Licensing Information . You must install Sigrity version 2015 QIR3 or later. For details on Sigrity installation, refer to Cadence Sigrity 2015 Release Installation Guide .
Furthermore, to enable system-centric chip-package-PCB co-simulation, Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution is able to generate one of the following die models:

- An n-port reduced R model with static current profile after static rail analysis
- An n-port reduced RC model with dynamic switching current profile after dynamic rail analysis

These die models can be used with package designs, as well as PCB designs in the Sigrity Package Analysis products to verify whether or not the entire power network across chip, package, and PCB meets the targeted static and dynamic voltage noise margins and targeted impedances.
This chapter covers the following topics:

- Static and dynamic rail analysis including package model
- Voltus-SPA flow
- Die-model generation

title: Static and Dynamic Rail Analysis Including Package Model

lines: Package model information can be specified using the Voltus GUI or the TCL commands. When loading the RLC package pin models or SPICE package models, a confirmation of the values used or package model files loaded are written to the log file.

During static analysis, Voltus considers only the resistive elements. It ignores the capacitive and inductive effects by modeling them as opens and shorts respectively. During dynamic analysis, Voltus considers resistive, capacitive, and inductive effects. It shorts voltage sources of 0 volts and ignores resistance values above 1 megohm.

RDL0 or Off-Chip Package Tracing

Package Analysis lets you include RDL0 (off-chip package trace) resistance effects by tracing internal switched net bumps during static and dynamic rail analysis. A design will have dummy bumps on the switched nets to define chip terminals that are used for package to hook up RDL0 netlist to the power-grid, as shown below:
There are two types of RDL0 runs:

- Run without package - a separate RDL0 subcircuit is created and hooked to the power-grid.
- Run with package - an RDL0 subcircuit is provided inside the package netlist that is hooked to the power-grid.
The off-chip package tracing feature allows you to analyze the switched net bumps that are marked as RDL0 bumps. The inputs required to use this feature are:

- trace model names (names of switched net bumps)
- switched net bump location files
- SPICE subcircuit (RDL0 netlist or full-chip package netlist)
- mapping file containing mapping of the terminals of the SPICE subcircuit (SPICE circuit port name) in the package model file to the locations at the power-grid (voltage source name in switch net’s )

To enable off-chip package tracing, specify the set_offchip_package_trace command. When this command is specified, the software automatically generates switched net bump current and bump voltage waveforms in the voltus_rail_pad.tran.ptiavg file created in the switch net directory located under the rail state directory.

Package to Die Mapping
Voltus can perform automatic mapping between package and die in two different ways, as explained below:

- Package with die placement information : This information describes how a chip is placed on a package and is specified as die coordinates, flip, and rotation related to the package system. It is usually saved into a package design database, such as, .MCM or .SiP, and then gets carried over into the Model Connection Protocol (MCP) section of the Sigrity XtractIM package model via the keywords: [BBOX], [Flip] and [Rotation]. Using the die placement information, Voltus automatically determines the die shrink factor and creates a mapping between package and die. 
 Following is an example of the die placement information in a package model: 
 *[REM] [Rotation] 0
*[REM] [BBOX] -0.0039258 -0.003195 0.0039258 0.003195
*[REM] [Flip] No
- MCPEditor Utility : If the package model does not contain the BBOX information, Voltus can still perform automatic mapping using the MCP Editor utility. For more information, refer to Rail Analysis Including Package Model .
Voltus also allows the user-specified package die connections with the set_package parameters: [-mapping < file >] [-offset { x y }] [-rotate < rotation_angle_in_degress >] [-flip {true | false}]
Mapping File Format

Mapping File specifies the name of the package terminal mapping file, which contains the mapping of the terminals of the SPICE subcircuit in the package model file to the locations at the grid or top-level pins. The mapping file formats supported by Voltus are:

- Model Connection Protocol (MCP)
- Voltus-specific Format
For more information on the mapping file formats, refer to the Package Model and Mapping File section in the " File Formats " chapter.
Format of Voltus-specific terminal mapping file
Example of Voltus-specific terminal mapping file

Voltus-Sigrity Package Analysis (SPA), the IC-centric Chip-Package co-simulation flow, has Sigrity embedded in Voltus to provide an accurate and easy-to-use power signoff solution for a highly-coupled Power Delivery Network (PDN).
It allows you to access the Sigrity package analysis tools from the Voltus environment for:

- Package model generation
- Package-aware static and dynamic rail analysis
- Package IR drop analysis
- Chip-package result co-display and cross reference
- Access to Sigrity MCP editor, if required
Package Extraction

Voltus generates a package model for user-specified power nets by calling Sigrity XtractIM in the batch mode. XtractIM is a tool that provides a complete model extraction environment focused specifically on IC package applications.
To create a package model using GUI:

1. Select Set Advanced Package Options from the Package menu. 
 The following form appears:
2. Specify the full path to the bin folder of the package extraction and analysis tools (XtractIM and PowerDC) in the Sigrity Tool Path field.
3. Specify the name of the two column mapping file that contains the mapping between the net names of package with the net names of die in the Net Mapping File field. Following is a sample format: 
 vdd_ao VDD 
 vdd_external VDD
4. Click Save to save the settings as a .conf file (configuration file), and reuse it next time by loading it.
5. Click OK . 
 After specifying the tool path, access the Extract Package form to start package model extraction.
6. Select Extract Package from the Package menu. 
 The following form appears:
7. Specify the work space name (package model database) of the package extraction tool (XtractIM) in the Workspace field. The file name extension is .ximx .
8. Specify the domain name in the Rail Analysis Domain field.
9. Define the die circuit name in the Package Die Name field. When specified, the software automatically creates a mapping between the die name in the package extraction tool and the die name in the software.
10. Define the ball grid array (BGA) circuit name in the Package BGA Name field. It is the PCB component in the package model, which eventually connects to the PCB model.
11. You can specify the output/result directory in the Output Folder field. By default, the package model will be created in the workspace. However, if you specify an output directory, then a copy of the result will be made in the new directory.
12. Specify the ground net that is selected as reference net for pin-based model generation in the Reference Net field.
13. Click OK . 
 The result of the package model extraction, which is done by calling XtractIM from within Voltus, is a Spice model that will be stored in the specified output directory. The naming format of the file is: < xtract_im workspace >_< spd_filename >_Final_PinBaseSPICE.ckt .
The TCL command for the Set Advanced Package Options form is set_advanced_package_options , and for the Extract Package form is extract_package .
Rail Analysis Including Package Model

Rail analysis is performed with the package model included. Refer to the “ Static and Dynamic Rail Analysis Including Package Model ” section for details. In the SPA flow, rail analysis will automatically pick the package model generated using the extract_package command. Hence, you do not need to specify the package model file via the set_package command. You can specify whether or not to perform the auto chip-package connection by using the following command:

- If the -unconnected_die_pkg_pins parameter is set to “ ignore ”, then Voltus will continue simulation without pausing or stopping. This means that there are some unconnected pins between package and die, and you would like to continue with whatever connections are made.
- If the -unconnected_die_pkg_pins parameter is set to “ error ”, then Voltus stops the simulation by erroring out. This means that there are some unconnected pins between package and die, and you do not want to ignore them, but want to start the MCP Editor GUI to make manual connections.
- If the -unconnected_die_pkg_pins parameter is set to “ edit ”, then the Voltus simulation will pause, and the MCP Editor GUI will appear to allow you to make manual connections between package and die pins. This means that there are unconnected pins, and you want to start the MCP Editor GUI to try manual connections.
After performing manual connections, the simulation will continue irrespective of whether there is a 100% match or not.
To perform automatic mapping using the MCP Editor utility:

1. Select the Generate Mapping File option from the Package -> Sigrity -> MCP Editor menu. 
 The MCP Editor form appears:
2. You can select one of the following options: 
 When Voltage Source Files is selected : 
 When DIE MCP Header is selected : 
 Browse and select a circuit file with the MCP header in the DIE MCP Header field. 
 Browse and select a package model in the Package Model field. 
 Browse and select the output directory in the Output Folder field.
  1. Specify the net name and source file in the Net and Source File fields.
  2. Click Add to add details of the specific net and the corresponding source file to the power/ground nets table.
  3. Browse and select a package model in the Package Model field.
  4. Browse and select the output directory in the Output Folder field.
3. Click Launch . 
 The following form appears:
4. Click Auto Connect . 
 The MCP Auto Connection form appears.
5. Specify an option to match the die and package pins by selecting one of the following radio buttons: 
 In the above sample form, the disconnected pins are matched by first selecting the Pin name match radio button, then specifying the left and right pins in the respective fields, and clicking the Match button. 
 The Pin Name Match information will be displayed at the bottom of the MCP Auto Connection form.
  - Auto match
  - Pin name match
  - Ckt node name match
  - Net name match
  - Coord match
6. Click OK to close the MCP Auto Connection form and return to the previous form.
7. Click OK to close the MCP Editor form. 
 The command prompt displays the following message: 
 Successfully mapped die package model with the result mapping file
The TCL command for the MCP Editor form is map_die_package
The outputs for the Rail Analysis flow are:

- When the MCP Editor utility is internally invoked to establish a connection between die and package, a ploc file ( *ploc.ckt ) is generated that is available in the <state_dir>/Reports folder. This file is a dummy die model which contains the die pins and the MCP header.
- A two column mapping file ( *.map ) is generated, by default, which is in the <state_dir>/Reports folder. This file has the die pin names and the corresponding package pin names.
- If the MCP Editor utility is invoked, a mapping file will be saved in the <state_dir> which can be used as is in the set_package –spice <> –mapping <mapping_file> command.
- A die pin current voltage file ( *_iv.rpt ) is generated, by default, which is used in PowerDC to emulate the die model. The file is in the <state_dir>/Reports folder. The die pin current voltage file has three columns:
 <pkg_pin_name> <voltage_value> <current_value>
- The final results of Rail Analysis is in the <state_dir> .
Package Model Analysis

Voltus enables and runs package IR drop analysis by calling PowerDC from within the Voltus environment. The Sigrity PowerDC environment provides fast and accurate DC analysis for IC packages and PCBs.
To perform package model analysis in GUI:

1. Select Analyze Package from the Package menu. 
 The following form appears:
2. Specify the workspace name for package analysis (powerDC) in the Workspace field. The file name extension is .pdcx .
3. Specify the domain name in the Rail Analysis Domain field.
4. Define the die circuit name in the Package Die Name field. When specified, the software automatically creates a mapping between the die name in the package analysis tool and the die name in the software.
5. Define the BGA circuit name in the Package BGA Name field.
6. You can specify the package analysis results directory in the Output Folder field. By default, the package model results are created in the workspace.
7. Specify the name of the .xml result file in the Result Name field.
8. Specify the name of the generated pin current file from rail analysis that contains the package pin names and the current values in the Pin Current File field. It is automatically generated after the package model is created, and IR drop analysis is performed using the package model.
9. Click OK . 
 The PowerDC GUI appears. Using this GUI, you can view the Electrical-field distribution once the simulation has completed. The 2D/3D E-Distributions are the static IR drop results in 2D (X/Y) or 3D (X/Y/Z). If you select 2D distribution, you can see the voltage/current on one layer at a time. Alternatively, if you select 3D distribution, you can see the X/Y (layer) information as well as the Z direction information.
The TCL command for the Analyze Package form is analyze_package .
View Package Analysis Results
You can use the Package Plots form to view the package IR drop analysis results in the PowerDC GUI which will be invoked from within Voltus.
To view package analysis results in GUI:

1. Select Package Plots from the Package menu. 
 The following form appears:
2. Specify the workspace name for package analysis (powerDC) in the Workspace field. The file name extension is .pdcx.
3. Specify the name of the .xml result file in the Result Name field.
4. Click OK to view the package IR drop analysis results in the PowerDC GUI, as shown below:
The TCL command for the Package Plots form is view_package_results .
Access Sigrity Tools from Voltus
You can access the following Sigrity tools from within Voltus using the Package -> Sigrity menu:

- MCP Editor
- XtractIM
- PowerDC

These are additional options that allow you to access the package extraction (XtractIM) and analysis (PowerDC) tools to have more control over package modeling and to easily make modifications (if needed), and then proceed with extraction or package analysis.
Cross Probing between Voltus and Sigrity Results in GUI

When you run the Voltus-SPA flow, the Package IR drop results are displayed in the Sigrity (PowerDC) GUI, and the die results are displayed in the Voltus GUI. The software allows cross probing between the Voltus and Sigrity results, while viewing them in their respective GUIs, in the following ways:

- Zoom Synchronization between Voltus and PowerDC : When you zoom into a portion of the design in Voltus, the corresponding design area in PowerDC will be selected and zoomed in. Similarly, when you zoom into a portion of the design in PowerDC, the corresponding design area in Voltus will be selected and zoomed in.
- Bump Synchronization between Voltus and PowerDC : When you select a bump in Voltus, the corresponding bump in PowerDC will be selected and zoomed in. Similarly, when you select a bump in PowerDC, the corresponding bump in Voltus will be selected and zoomed in.
- Net Enabled Synchronization between Voltus and PowerDC : When you enable/disable nets in the Power & Rail Layers/Nets form in Voltus and click Apply , the corresponding nets will be enabled/disabled in PowerDC automatically. Similarly, when you enable/disable nets in PowerDC, the corresponding nets will be enabled/disabled in the Power & Rail Layers/Nets form automatically.
The following is a snapshot of bump synchronization:
The following is a snapshot of net synchronization:

Voltus generates a package model for user-specified power nets by calling Sigrity XtractIM in the batch mode. XtractIM is a tool that provides a complete model extraction environment focused specifically on IC package applications.
To create a package model using GUI:

1. Select Set Advanced Package Options from the Package menu. 
 The following form appears:
2. Specify the full path to the bin folder of the package extraction and analysis tools (XtractIM and PowerDC) in the Sigrity Tool Path field.
3. Specify the name of the two column mapping file that contains the mapping between the net names of package with the net names of die in the Net Mapping File field. Following is a sample format: 
 vdd_ao VDD 
 vdd_external VDD
4. Click Save to save the settings as a .conf file (configuration file), and reuse it next time by loading it.
5. Click OK . 
 After specifying the tool path, access the Extract Package form to start package model extraction.
6. Select Extract Package from the Package menu. 
 The following form appears:
7. Specify the work space name (package model database) of the package extraction tool (XtractIM) in the Workspace field. The file name extension is .ximx .
8. Specify the domain name in the Rail Analysis Domain field.
9. Define the die circuit name in the Package Die Name field. When specified, the software automatically creates a mapping between the die name in the package extraction tool and the die name in the software.
10. Define the ball grid array (BGA) circuit name in the Package BGA Name field. It is the PCB component in the package model, which eventually connects to the PCB model.
11. You can specify the output/result directory in the Output Folder field. By default, the package model will be created in the workspace. However, if you specify an output directory, then a copy of the result will be made in the new directory.
12. Specify the ground net that is selected as reference net for pin-based model generation in the Reference Net field.
13. Click OK . 
 The result of the package model extraction, which is done by calling XtractIM from within Voltus, is a Spice model that will be stored in the specified output directory. The naming format of the file is: < xtract_im workspace >_< spd_filename >_Final_PinBaseSPICE.ckt .

Access Sigrity Tools from Voltus
You can access the following Sigrity tools from within Voltus using the Package -> Sigrity menu:

- MCP Editor
- XtractIM
- PowerDC

These are additional options that allow you to access the package extraction (XtractIM) and analysis (PowerDC) tools to have more control over package modeling and to easily make modifications (if needed), and then proceed with extraction or package analysis.
Cross Probing between Voltus and Sigrity Results in GUI

When you run the Voltus-SPA flow, the Package IR drop results are displayed in the Sigrity (PowerDC) GUI, and the die results are displayed in the Voltus GUI. The software allows cross probing between the Voltus and Sigrity results, while viewing them in their respective GUIs, in the following ways:

- Zoom Synchronization between Voltus and PowerDC : When you zoom into a portion of the design in Voltus, the corresponding design area in PowerDC will be selected and zoomed in. Similarly, when you zoom into a portion of the design in PowerDC, the corresponding design area in Voltus will be selected and zoomed in.
- Bump Synchronization between Voltus and PowerDC : When you select a bump in Voltus, the corresponding bump in PowerDC will be selected and zoomed in. Similarly, when you select a bump in PowerDC, the corresponding bump in Voltus will be selected and zoomed in.
- Net Enabled Synchronization between Voltus and PowerDC : When you enable/disable nets in the Power & Rail Layers/Nets form in Voltus and click Apply , the corresponding nets will be enabled/disabled in PowerDC automatically. Similarly, when you enable/disable nets in PowerDC, the corresponding nets will be enabled/disabled in the Power & Rail Layers/Nets form automatically.
