This section covers the following topics:

- Need for die-model generation
- Die-model generation features
- Voltus flow to create die-model
- Time domain analysis of package and die-model
- Frequency domain analysis of package and die-model
Need for die-model generation

An n-port reduced RC chip model with dynamic switching profile can be created during analysis. This die-model can be used with the package model in Sigrity products to verify whether package and on-chip dynamic profile meets the target impedance across wide frequency spectrum.

- Resistance in package routing results in voltage loss at chip level
  - Inductance in package routing results in dynamic power changes in die
  - The impact can be significant especially in low power designs
- Die model generation enables:
  - Chip-package resonance analysis
  - package decap estimate
  - System in Package (SiP) design and analysis
A cross section of a typical chip package is shown in the following diagram:
Die, package, and board
Die-model generation features

- Supports n-port die-model generation
- Supports die-model generation for TSV/SiP designs
- Eliminates iterations of long full-chip dynamic simulations with package
- Enables efficient resonance analysis of the design with different package and die parameters
- Die-model can be fed to Sigrity PowerSI to do freq domain analysis using SpeedSim
- Die-model and package can be simulated in Spectre to do freq domain analysis
- Allows package designer to optimize package in context of die to achieve target impedance at wide range of frequencies
- Allows circuit designers to optimize die (on-chip decaps and dynamic transient currents) in context of package to achieve the target impedance
- Additionally die + package model is generated in SPICE format to perform frequency and time domain analysis using Spectre to perform resonance analysis and verify accuracy of die-model
Die + package model
Voltus flow to create die-model
Die model generation flow is shown in the following diagram:
Die model generation flow in Voltus
Tcl command to generate die-model
Example Tcl Flow

Note that create_die_model command is incremental and you can generate die-model in a new session without re-analyzing dynamic IR drop. As long as state directory containing dynamic IRdrop analysis result is available the create_die_model will use it to generate die-model. You will have to re-define power nets and domain using set_pg_nets and set_rail_analysis_domain again. Die Model generation can use multiple CPUs that have been set earlier in the script using the set_multi_cpu_usage –localCpu command.
TCL command to generate die-model for a TSV/SiP design

A TSV/SiP design has multiple die in the design. After performing dynamic IR drop analysis for the design, you can run the die-model generation flow to create a complete die model. As a result, you are able to use the complete die model for system simulation. The Tcl command to generate die-model is as follows:
Example Tcl Flow
Time domain analysis of package and die-model
The following figure shows how the package is mapped to the die to perform full-chip dynamic transient analysis using Voltus.
Package + full-chip transient simulation

However, this transient analysis is expensive and limited to only a few clock cycles. In order to simulate the package + die for longer duration to analyze the impact of package inductance (Ldi/dt) beyond initial clock cycles, you can run transient analysis on package + die-model, as shown in the following figure:
Package + die-model transient simulation

The package + die-model transient simulation setup is captured in .sp file in the output directory specified by create_die_model command. This setup can be simulated in SPICE. Although original dynamic IR drop simulation is performed over limited number of cycles, it may not fully capture on-chip current and voltage oscillation due to package. You can choose to repeat the original dynamic current profile of the chip (captured as .isrc file in output directory) using the -repeat <time in ns> option in create _die_model command. This enables you to perform transient simulation of package and die-model for longer periods of time:

You can also measure the die-model accuracy by probing the current through power-pad on die form SPICE simulation and correlating i t with VC_SUM signal generated by Voltus transient simulation. This comparison is shown below:
Current measurement at power pads
Frequency domain analysis of package and die-model

- At resonant frequency, on-chip capacitance and package inductance forms a circuit with high admittance that results in power supply oscillation beyond permissible threshold (+- 5-10%)
- The on-chip capacitance provides decoupling at high frequencies (low impedance because capacitor impedance in inversely proportional to frequency). However, components operating at low frequencies do not benefit from on-chip capacitance due to high impedance and hence current to these components primarily comes from package which is inductive in nature. So designs producing high transient current at low frequencies tend to oscillate the power supply and it will resonate at 
 F0 = 1/2*pi*sqrt(LC) = resonance frequency
- Target impedance must be met at wide range of frequencies (1Hz->10GHz) to avoid power supply oscillation beyond threshold (+- 5-10%) 
 Target Impedance = Ztarget = 10%VDD/Ipeak 
 e.g. VDD=1v and Ipeak = 0.1Amp ; Ztarget = 1Ohm
The resonance analysis with die-model can be done in the Sigrity environment using SpeedSim or using stand-alone Spectre circuit simulator. See results in the following form:
Chip package frequency response
The package + die-model frequency domain simulation setup is captured in .sp file in the output directory specified by create _die_model command. This setup can be simulated in SPICE.
Note that you can also feed only the die-model ( domain_25C_dyanamic_1/die_model/domain_without_package.sp ) to the Sigrity products to perform the analysis.

- Scaling Resistance of the Power-Grid
- Scaling Static and Dynamic Currents
- Creating Static and Dynamic Current Regions
- Scaling Capacitance
- Creating Virtual Shapes

Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution offers wide range of what-if analysis capabilities to guide power-grid optimization. This analysis is used primarily to change electrical parameters of the power-grid on-the-fly to estimate eventual optimization efforts to satisfy IRdrop and ElectroMigration limits.
The following power-grid what-if analysis features are available in Voltus:

- Scaling Resistance of the Power-Grid
- Scaling Static and Dynamic Currents
- Creating Static and Dynamic Current Regions
- Scaling Capacitance
What-if analysis can be driven from the GUI as well as TCL commands. In the GUI, it is invoked from the Run Rail Analysis - Advanced form, as shown below:
What-If Analysis Setup Button
What-if analysis is also available through TCL commands:

- scale_what_if_current
- scale_what_if_capacitance
- scale_what_if_resistance
- create_current_region

During what-if analysis, you can enable sensitivity analysis (rs) (see Viewing Static Rail Analysis Results in the Static Power, IRdrop and EM Analysis chapter). It highlights power-grid segments which when optimized, improves the IRdrop of the design. Based on the IRdrop - ir, the Resistor Sensitivity Analysis - rs, Current Density - rj plots, and Generating Text Reports , you can scale resistance of a given LEF layer on-the-fly to see whether changing the wire resistance improves the IRdrop. You can scale resistance of a layer in a region or globally for the design. Using the " What-If Analysis Setup - Resistance " form, you can interactively draw a box in the layout canvas in which scaling is to be applied. If multiple nets are being analyzed, you can specify the net name for the same.
What-If Resistance Analysis Form

IRdrop problems inside a power-gated domain can be resolved by possibly increasing the drive strength of power-gates or adding more power-gates rather than optimizing power-grid mesh of the power-gated net. You can quickly perform what-if analysis by scaling the on-resistance of the already placed power-gates.
Example TCL flow

When power-grid optimization is not feasible in the regions of IRdrop and electromigration violations, you can choose to scale static or dynamic currents on-the-fly to do what-if analysis. This analysis can help determine whether the designer should reduce activity, simultaneous switching and placement in the design to meet IRdrop and electromigration threshold. You can scale static or dynamic currents using the " What-If Analysis Setup - Current " form. You have the option to scale current inside the design hierarchy and regions. You can interactively draw regions on the layout canvas. Additionally, there is an option to not scale currents for instances associated with clock network, however, this option is only available when scaling currents for design hierarchy. The current will be scaled for all components inside region.
What-If Current Analysis Form
Example TCL flow

title: Creating Static and Dynamic Current Regions

lines: You can specify current regions on the routed power-grid layer to analyze the impact of unplaced or placed custom macros (without accurate power-grid view) on the global power-grid. Both static and dynamic current regions can be specified using the " What-If Analysis Setup - Regions " form by interactively drawing box in the layout canvas. The dynamic current regions require PWL current format with time (ns) and current (mA) pairs. In addition, dynamic current regions require specification of internal and loading capacitance to be used for the region.
What-If Current Region Form
Example TCL Flow

During dynamic analysis, you can perform what-if analysis to scale capacitance to see its impact on dynamic IRdrop caused by simultaneous switching currents. This feature can be used to perform what-if decap analysis by scaling intrinsic capacitance in the region to estimate decap optimization efforts. Additionally, you have the option to scale loading and power-grid capacitance. The capacitance scaling can be performed in GUI using the " What-If Analysis Setup - Capacitance " form.
What-If Capacitance Analysis Form
Example TCL Flow

You can create what-if wires/vias to fix voltage drop and analyze the new grid for various type of existing analyses. The analysis with what-if wires/vias can be done in both early and signoff mode rail analysis. In the sign-off mode what-if analysis, only possible virtual vias between what-if wires and design original wires are automatically generated. You can also skip the generation of these automatic “whatif” vias using the -era_skip_virtual_via_by_type parameter of the set_rail_analysis_ mode command. The virtual vias are dropped using the default layer cut size and cut spacing.
Virtual shapes can be created in GUI using the " What-If Analysis Setup - Virtual Shape " form.
What-If Virtual Shape Creation Form
Example TCL Flow
What-If Analysis Flow
This section describes how to set up the what-if analysis mode and then run rail analysis.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Power database generated during power calculation
- State directory created during rail analysis
Procedure
To perform what-if analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data and then run rail analysis. Refer to Static Power Analysis Flow .
2. Select Power & Rail - Run Rail Analysis . 
 The Run Rail Analysis form appears.
3. Click the Advanced tab.
4. Click the What-If Analysis Setup button. 
 The What-If Analysis Setup form appears.
5. Specify the resistance scaling options through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 scale_what_if_resistance -global -net VDD_AO -layer Metal4 -scale 4 -auto_scale_adjacent_via_layers true
  1. Click the Resistance tab.
  2. Select the Scale Resistance check box.
  3. Select the Auto Scale Adjacent Via Layers check box.
  4. Select a layer from the Layer Name drop-down list.
  5. Specify the scale value in the Scale field.
  6. Specify the net name in the Net Name field.
  7. Click the Global radio button to scale all the rings and stripes of the selected layer.
  8. Click the Add button to save the changes.
6. Specify the current scaling options through the command line or through the GUI. 
 GUI : 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 scale_what_if_current -global -scale 10
  1. Click the Current tab.
  2. Select the Scale Hierarchy Currents check box.
  3. Select the Scale Global Current check box.
  4. Specify the scale value in the Scale field.
  5. Click the Add button to save the changes.
7. Click OK in the What-If Analysis Setup form.
8. In the Voltus console, re-run rail analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_rail -output ./staticRailResults/whatif -type domain ALL
9. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots to open Power & Rail Plots form (Rail Results Viewer).
10. Click the DB Setup button to open the Power & Rail Setup form.
11. Click Browse next to the Rail Database field, and select the latest state directory from the Rail State Directory browser window.
12. Click OK .
13. In the Power & Rail Plots form, select the Rail radio button and select the ir rail analysis type from the drop-down list.
14. Review the analysis results in the layout window. 
 Similarly, you can create current regions or change the resistance or current scale factors, and rerun rail analysis.

In deep sub-micron designs, power has become a major concern as cell leakage increases with shrinking dimensions. The leakage power has increased from roughly 10% of total power in 130nm to 35% in 90nm to 55% in 65nm designs. As a way to address power consumption, designers use different methodologies to minimize power.

In modern low power IC designs, one common method of conserving power is to shut off portions of the chip when they are not being used. One of the ways to do this is power gating. In this design style, the portion of the chip to be powered down is driven by a local power supply rail. This local rail is then connected to a global power supply rail through one or more power gates (also known as power switches). Power gates are typically large transistors which can be turned off when the block is not being used. By shutting off the power supply to the block, the leakage current of the block is significantly reduced. Since the block is shut off, the switching current of the block will be zero.
Types of Power Gates
There are two methodologies of using Power Gating using sleep transistors to control power consumptions.

- Coarse-grain sleep transistors are used to control the power to a large section of logic.
- Fine-grain sleep transistors are used to control the power of specific instances.
These two methods are shown below:
Two ways to reduce power consumption using sleep transistors

To implement coarse-grain sleep transistors in a standard place and route environment, power gates (containing sleep transistors) are inserted between the power rails of a block of logic and the main power rails of the design. This method offers the following benefits and drawbacks:

- Less area needed for implementation
- Greater control of leakage
- Degraded noise margins
- More difficult to guarantee performance
- Requires more custom design

Fine-grain sleep transistors are integrated as part of the circuit design of the cell and therefore provides greater control over each instance that contains a sleep transistor. This method offers the following benefits and drawbacks:

- Increased area for implementation
- Less control of leakage
- Guaranteed performance
- Requires a standard cell library with sleep transistor logic.

In all cases where sleep transistors are used to power down sections of logic, there is a need to return to the last known state when the power is re-applied. For coarse-grain sleep transistors, this can be achieved by using input latch cells that are outside the power down region. For fine-grain sleep transistors, this is typically achieved using specially designed state retention power gating (SRPG) cells for registers and latches.
Power Gate Analysis
When considering rail analysis of a design that includes power-gates, following analysis should be performed:

- Analysis of how the insertion of the power-gates impacts power, IR drop and electromigration when the power-gates are turned completely on (steady state analysis)
- Analysis of the total power savings and chip leakage when power-gates are turned off (steady state analysis)
- Analyze the time required to turn on all power-gates (powerup analysis) from its powered down state to its nominal voltage. The functional logic inside the power-gated block can not be activated until this time.
- Analyze the impact of rush current generated by the powering up logic on global (always-on) power-grid voltage (rush current analysis).
Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution addresses the power-gate analysis requirements using two approaches:

- A steady state analysis, where the state of the power-gates is not dynamically changing, instead, all power-gates are either on or off. It is used to understand how the insertion of the power gates impacts IR drop and electromigration when all power-gates are on and power-savings when all power-gates are off. The static or dynamic power can be used to do steady state analysis.
- A rush current analysis, to understand the impact of turning on the power-gates dynamically to measure turn on time and analyze the impact of rush current on the IRdrop of the always-on power-grid.

During library generation of the power-gate cell, Voltus characterizes the power gate's on-resistance (Ron), the leakage current (Ileakage) when the power-gate is off, and the saturation current (Idsat) of the power gate. The information is then stored in the cell library, which is then used by Voltus during IR-drop analysis. Optionally you have the ability to override the Ron, leakage and saturation currents for the power-gates if desired.
The library generation of power-gate cells requires:

- LEF
- Extraction tech file
- Layermap of LEF and extraction tech file
- SPICE netlist and SPICE device models 
 For power gate cell PGV characterization, the SPICE netlist with device x, y coordinates is recommended to be used to generate the detail EM view having the exact Ron resistors in the specific locations to represent the power gate transistors. It is important to have a clear and accurate resistance network of power gate cells so that the overall IR-drop and EM analysis of the low-power design is accurate.
- Power-gate cell's always on and switched pin

If SPICE netlist is not available, Voltus can not characterize Ron, Idsat and Ileakage parameters for the power-gate. In this case, you must specify estimated parameters for each power-gate cell manually.
To perform power-gate cell characterization:

1. Select Power & Rail - Set PowerGrid Library Mode . 
 The following form appears:
2. Set Cell Type to Std Cells to use SPICE subcircuit for view generation.
3. Specify Filler Cell Names and Decap Cell Names (optional) to perform decap optimization during rail analysis.
4. Select Simulation , and specify the netlist in the Spice subckt netlist field to characterize cell capacitance and current for the power-gate cell.
5. Specify Spice Model and Corners for device current and capacitance characterization.
6. Specify Extraction Tech File and LEF Layermap .
7. Specify Power Pins , Voltages , and Ground Pins . 
Ensure that the always-on and switched pin voltages are same.
8. Click the Advanced tab. 
 The following form appears:
9. Cell List File and Cell Decap File are optional fields.
10. Select Power Gate Cell Characterization .
11. Specify power-gate cell name
12. Specify always-on power pin name
13. Specify switch power pin name
14. If spice subckts are provided, Ron, Idsat and Ileakage will be disabled.
15. Click Add to add the power-gate cell information to the listbox.
16. Similarly add all power-gate cell information to the listbox.
17. Click OK to characterize the power-gate library.
Below is an example TCL script to generate power-gate cell library.
Fine-grain memory characterization
For fine-grain power gate support, each cell has an internal switched rail. To use fine-grain power gates, the following needs to be done:

- Specify the set_pg_library_mode -powergate_finegrain_simulation true and - powergate_parameters {{cell supply switched [Ron Idsat Ileak]}+}
- Add a label for the switched rail manually using the set_advanced_pg_library_mode -add_port_labels file_name parameter. This file contains details about the label of a switched rail. Use this parameter if the switched rail is not labeled.
Below is an example TCL script to generate a power-gate cell library for fine grain cells:
-powergate_parameters {{block1 vdd vdds1} {block1 vdd vdds2}}
The power-gate library report can be generated from GUI or using TCL command as follows:
check_pg_library -output report -report /../pg.cl

The generated report lists power-gate cell, power pins, voltage, coupled capacitance, and characterized power-gate parameters like Ron, Idsat and Ileakage for each enable pins. The example report is shown below:

For steady state analysis of power-gates, Voltus models the power-gate transistor as a linear resistance. The value of the resistance is determined from the device model during power grid library generation of the power gate. Voltus can analyze the impact of this resistance during static or dynamic power and rail analysis to ascertain whether sufficient power-gates are placed in the design to satisfy the current requirement of the switched block.

Voltus determines whether each power-gate cell is operating within the non-saturated (linear resistance) region, and reports when it is not. Power-gates operating outside of the non-saturation region are considered to be drawing too much current to be reliable. In addition to graphical plots, detailed power-gate text report and ECO file for Innovus is generated after the analysis.
Voltus can also be used to calculate the power savings and leakage power for the design when the power-gated block is powered down.
Usage of Common Power Format (CPF)

The power-gate implementation is part of the low-power MSMV design methodology. As power and voltage constraint become more aggressive, the low-power designs are becoming more complex with multiple voltage islands and power-gated domains to save power. The Common Power Format (CPF) allows single specification of power intent used throughout the design implementation and analysis. It is an ASCII file that captures various power domains, power logic and power modes of the low-power design. More information about CPF and how to create this specification file can be found in Common Power Format User Guide shipped with Innovus.

Using CPF enables power to be captured in the specification and the design process. This enables all the tools in the chain to utilize the same power description, facilitating easy exploration of different power architectures with each tool automatically seeing the same description of the power architecture. Also, by separating this information from the RTL, it enables easy re-use of IP or blocks that have the same functionality but different power profiles and this CPF can be handed off to physical implementation so that physical implementation and analysis team uses the same representation as the logic design team. Finally, power-aware DFT can be automated through this, enabling higher quality silicon and less power consumed on the tester.
Following is an example of a low-power design that can be described using CPF:
Example of low-power design

It is a small design with two instances of the MAC - for instance, one MAC could be for ethernet and another WLAN. We can use CPF to define the power domains VDDm (0.84v) and VDD (1.08v). We can also specify power-gated domains VDDlu and VDDau which are powering MAC I and MAC II. Notice how the two instances of the MAC are in different power domains - this is because typically both will not be used at the same time. We also use different state retention policies, where MAC2 retains state for all registers for fast recovery from shutdown, while MAC1 only retains state on a few registers to save power and area. If you were to specify this in the RTL, you would need to maintain two different versions of the MAC RTL, which also makes what-if analysis more complicated.

In addition to state retention, power controls and isolation logic are defined in CPF. The power modes are defined here, with different combinations of MAC1 and MAC2 being on and off. Finally the technology information is specified by associating different operating conditions, liberty files, and SDC to different power modes.
Voltus supports CPF to drive power and rail analysis on low-power design. You can load CPF using read_power_domain -cpf command described in chapter Importing Design .

Based on the power-mode (analysis view) specification in CPF, Voltus power and rail analysis associates timing libraries, PVT corner, SDC and state of the power domains, as depicted in the figure below:
CPF Power and Rail Analysis flow for Power Gates
Steadystate Power Analysis

Steady state power analysis can be done on a power-gated design to analyze total power savings and leakage power when the power-gated block is powered down. The powered down domain does not consume any switching power. The leakage power for the powered down block is only the leakage of the power-gates.

Static and dynamic power can be computed during steadystate analysis and fed to steadystate rail analysis to analyze the impact on IRdrop. The GUI and TCL instructions to run static and dynamic power analysis are same as in Static Power, IRdrop and EM Analysis and Dynamic Power and IRDrop Analysis .

If CPF is loaded, you can specify set_power_analysis_mode -analysis_view view to drive power analysis. It will automatically pick up associated timing libraries, SDC, power net voltages and off domains to do power analysis. Note that if you want to specify "analysis view" using the set_power_analysis_mode command, the specified analysis view must be active during the session. This can be done by executing the following command in the Console tab in GUI, or TCL script:
Also the signal parasitic file at the desired RC corner must be read after activating the analysis view.
If CPF is not available, you can turn off power domain(s) manually by specifying the switched net using set_power_analysis_mode -off_pg_nets net_names .
Use report_power -leakage command to report leakage when the power domain is shut off.
Steadystate Rail Analysis

Steadystate rail analysis can be done on power-gated design to analyze whether placed power-gates are sufficient to accommodate the power need of the power-gated block. The GUI and TCL instructions to run steadystate rail analysis on power-gated design is same as described in Static Power, IRdrop and EM Analysis and Dynamic Power and IRDrop Analysis . The static or dynamic power can be fed in the form of current files of all associated nets to do this analysis.

If CPF is used, you can specify the analysis view using set_rail_analysis_mode -analysis_view view . This will automatically derive all power domains defined in CPF and voltage of all power nets in the domain.

If a switched net is off in an analysis view, this information is also automatically derived from CPF. However, if CPF is not loaded, you must specify off power nets manually using set_rail_analysis_mode -off_rails net_name . In addition, you must specify all power nets, its voltages and domain using the set_pg_nets and set_rail_analysis_domain commands.

It is important to note that when analyzing power-gated domain, you do not have to specify a switched net in the domain definition ( set_rail_analysis_domain ). This definition should only contain always-on net names. The switched net is automatically traced by the program and the state of this switched net is either driven by set_rail_analysis_ mode -off_rails or directly from CPF.
In non-CPF steadystate analysis of power-gated domain,

Once the analysis is complete, load the results using report_power_rail_results command and view the graphical plots " power-gate current I/Idsat - pi " and " power-gate voltage -pv ". Both plots are explained in chapter Static Power, IRdrop and EM Analysis .
In addition, review the pi.report text file generated by the analysis.

Steadystate rail analysis can be done on power-gated design to analyze whether placed power-gates are sufficient to accommodate the power need of the power-gated block. The GUI and TCL instructions to run steadystate rail analysis on power-gated design is same as described in Static Power, IRdrop and EM Analysis and Dynamic Power and IRDrop Analysis . The static or dynamic power can be fed in the form of current files of all associated nets to do this analysis.

If CPF is used, you can specify the analysis view using set_rail_analysis_mode -analysis_view view . This will automatically derive all power domains defined in CPF and voltage of all power nets in the domain.

If a switched net is off in an analysis view, this information is also automatically derived from CPF. However, if CPF is not loaded, you must specify off power nets manually using set_rail_analysis_mode -off_rails net_name . In addition, you must specify all power nets, its voltages and domain using the set_pg_nets and set_rail_analysis_domain commands.

It is important to note that when analyzing power-gated domain, you do not have to specify a switched net in the domain definition ( set_rail_analysis_domain ). This definition should only contain always-on net names. The switched net is automatically traced by the program and the state of this switched net is either driven by set_rail_analysis_ mode -off_rails or directly from CPF.
In non-CPF steadystate analysis of power-gated domain,

Once the analysis is complete, load the results using report_power_rail_results command and view the graphical plots " power-gate current I/Idsat - pi " and " power-gate voltage -pv ". Both plots are explained in chapter Static Power, IRdrop and EM Analysis .
In addition, review the pi.report text file generated by the analysis.

Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution supports native power-up analysis for performing rush current analysis without having to invoke UltraSim. The UltraSim-based power-up flow has the following disadvantages:

- Long simulation time for UltraSim
- Inability to take effect of the always-on net parasitics while doing power-up analysis
- Customizing the Spice deck and UltraSim options is cumbersome
- Need for a separate UltraSim license

The power-grid library generation engine of Voltus supports the native power-up flow, where the power-gates are modelled as voltage controlled current sources (VCCS). There is no use model change during library generation of the power-gate cell. The same data inputs and commands, as previously used, are required to generate the power-gate library (PGV).
The following diagram illustrates the modelling of a power-gate cell as VCCS:

The Power Analysis engine in Voltus writes out the arrival (rise/fall) times at the enable pin of the power-gate cells along with the transition times (rise/fall) for all the power-gate cells in the design. This information is then further used by the Rail Analysis engine downstream to do rush current analysis. At the power analysis stage, it is assumed that you will provide an accurate Liberty file for the power-gate cells along with proper constraints for the enable circuitry in the SDC file. The inbuilt timer will then use this information to calculate the correct arrival and transition times for the power-gate cells in the context of the whole design.

The Rail Analysis engine reads and uses the VCCS models for power switches along with the enable pin stimulus generated during the power analysis run. You can perform rush current analysis using the following methods:

- powering up the block of interest without taking into account the always-on net, that is, assuming an ideal always-on net. This flow only allows you to analyze one powering up net at a time.
- powering up the block(s) of interest taking into account the parastitcs of the always-on net, that is, assuming a non-ideal always-on net. This flow allows you to analyze more than one power-up net at a time. Therefore, you can simultaneously analyze the IR drop on the always-on net and power up one or more switched nets in the same run.

At the end of the analysis, Voltus reports the turn-on time of the switched net, peak current and a text based report having firing time of each switch. This report is similar to the text based report written out by the UltraSim-based flow. Also, a binary file is generated which has the current profile of the powering-up net. This can be viewed using the SimVision waveform viewer.

In native power-up analysis, multiple powering up rails are found in the set_rail_analysis_mode command. Each powering up rail requires a dedicated CPU and all powering up rails are analyzed in parallel. If you specify only one powering up rail, you do not require a multi-CPU license. You can use the set_multi_cpu_usage command to specify the number of CPUs required.
Example
Setup Native Powerup Analysis using GUI
To set up native power-up analysis, perform the following steps:

1. Perform library generation of power-gate cells. See Power-Grid Library Generation .
2. Set up and run Dynamic Power Analysis. See Dynamic Vector-based Power Analysis Flow .
3. Select Power & Rail - Set Rail Analysis Mode . 
 The following form appears:
4. Set Analysis Method as Dynamic .
5. Set Accuracy . 
 XD uses LEF based views, and HD uses GDS based accurate views.
6. Specify Power-grid library. See Power-Grid Library Generation for details on generating power-grid libraries.
7. If CPF is loaded, select Analysis View .
8. Select Switched Off Nets to specify a list of nets to be excluded from analysis. 
 Switched Off Nets and Block Power Up Net are mutually exclusive.
9. Select Powering Up Nets . This option takes into account the IR drop effect of always-on nets. You can specify a list of powering up nets. You can select Block Power Up Net to specify a single net for block power-up analysis. 
 Powering Up Nets and Block Power Up Net are mutually exclusive.
10. Specify EM Analysis Models if analyzing Electromigration 
 If you do not specify an Electromigration model file, the software will select Electromigration models from the QRC technology file. You can use the command set_rail_analysis_mode -em_models file to override these settings.
11. Select Generate Movies . This will provide the ability to view plots over simulation time.
12. Select the Advanced tab of the Set Rail Analysis Mode form.
13. Select Generate Boundary Voltage File if using Hierarchical view for the block
14. Specify GDS for Flip-chip RDL or Full-chip GDS if needed.
15. Fill in the decap fields as needed.
16. Click OK or Apply .

View Rush Currents (powering-up nets)

Upon successful completion of rail analysis for rush current analysis, Voltus generates a power-up rush current file and a power-up text based report for the net under the <net_name> directory in the state directory of the power nets.

The software generates a text report file called <powerup_net>_powering_up.report in the <net_name> directory with measurement of turn on time and peak rush current. It highlights the wake-up time of individual power-gates in the design. If any power-gate is not turned on during simulation, it is also highlighted in the report. An example power-up report is shown below:
#This is a Power-up Report File.
Voltus generates a current waveform called dynamic_powerup_<powerup net>_<always-on net>.ptiavg in the <net_name> directory.
To view peak rush current after analysis:

1. Open Power & Rail > Report > Dynamic Waveforms form and select Power Waveforms .
2. Load the current waveform file named as dynamic_powerup_< powerup net >_< always-on net >.ptiavg . For example, dynamic_powerup_VDD_SW1_VDD.ptiavg .
3. Select Total Current from the Composite Waveform drop-down listbox.
To view the probed waveforms on each instance, load the dynamic_powerup_< powerup net >_< always-on net >.ptiavg file in SimVision waveform viewer using File > Open Database form.

- Overview to Through-Silicon Via
  - Domain-based Through-Silicon Via Flow
- Overview to System-in-Package
  - Domain-based System-in-Package Flow
- Mixed Approach - a Combination of TSV and SiP

Through-Silicon Vias (TSVs) is a method of multi-die analysis, wherein, two or more chips are stacked vertically and are connected using via objects running through the stack. The via objects take signals/power/ground from the back-side of a die through its substrate to the main routing areas above the substrate. Back side metal routes enable routing on the back-side of the die to make better alignment for contact to a package. The TSV connects between the highest of the back-side metals through the substrate to the traditional Metal1.
Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution supports the following two types of TSV stacked die configuration:

- faceDown Configuration (top metal to top metal) - this configuration utilizes TSV only in the lower die with the upper die connecting through the top level regular metals. For example, Metal7 is using landing pads (like bump cells) on the highest metal aligned to connect between the 2 die.
- faceUp Configuration (top metal to the back-side metal) - this configuration utilizes TSV on both die. Signals power and ground go from the lower die's upper metal through back-side metal at TSVs of the upper die to reach its regular interconnect layers.

In both types of configuration, one die is the slave of the other die relying on the connections through the master die to draw current for power and ground from the package. The slave does not have direct connections to the package. Current is drawn from the package through the master die. As a result, the master die sees the slave as additional loading from a power/ground network perspective and sensitivity to IR drop is increased. Designing the power/ground network of the lower master die in order to satisfy the current demands of both itself and then also supplying or feeding current through to the upper slave die must be taken into account during the design phase. Analyzing and verifying the overall power and ground network is even more essential to ensure satisfactory power delivery and working die.
The following diagram illustrates a die containing TSV:
The following diagrams illustrate the stacked die configuration in detail:

Format of the Mapping File

The mapping file from Innovus is a virtual mapping that provides the connections between any 2 stacked die with communication directly between the die (master/slave configuration). Depending on the stacking die configuration, Innovus generates one or more mapping files. For example, if you are stacking 3 die, one on top of each other, Innovus generates 2 mapping files, die1-die2 and die2-die3. Alternatively, if you are stacking two smaller die on top of one large die, Innovus generates a single mapping file, die1-die2/die3.
The following diagram illustrates these two cases:
The format of the mapping file is as follows:
where, <mapping_pair_*> for power means:
The components are separated with a white space or tab. One line has one mapping pair.
<design1> = instName1(cellName1) for one of the die
<net_in_design1> is the name of the power net connected to the power bump in design1
<Vsrc_in_design1> is the name of the power bump in design1

System-in-Package (SiP) is similar to TSV multi-die analysis as it performs simultaneous analysis of two die. In case of SIP, the dies connect only through the package and there are no virtual connections between each die. In Voltus, to accurately simulate the interactive effect between each die, all the dies could be combined to perform IR drop analysis simultaneously and do not need to simulate one die at a time.
The following diagram illustrates the flow:
SiP can generate a single package model containing ports to multiple die. The naming of ports is driven by DEFs read by SiP from Innovus.

title: Mixed Approach - a Combination of TSV and SiP

lines: Voltus supports a mix of traditional SiP (multi-die connected through package) combined with stacked die using TSVs to enable die-die communication. The following diagram illustrates an example of a mixed approach using traditional SiP techniques (both 3D-IC and side-by-side) as well as 3D-IC using TSV to enable communication between the stacked die:

In this example, the package connects to 4 die, Logic1, Logic2, Logic3, and RF1. RF1 is connected to the package using wire-bonding. The others are connected to the package using bumps. Logic1 (master) is connected to Mem1 (slave). Logic2 (master) is connected to Mem2 (slave). Mem1 and Mem2 are two instances of the same design. Logic3 and RF1 communicate only with the package and any inter-die communication occurs through the package. Mem1 communicates 100% through Logic1 and Mem2 communicates 100% through Logic2. Any communication between Mem1 and Mem2 to Logic3, RF1, or the board, occurs through the master chips Logic1 and Logic2, respectively.
The following diagram illustrates how electrically the interfaces can be handled using both stacked 3D-IC TSV die and traditional SiP stacked die:

In this example, the package connects to 3 die, namely Logic1, SOC1, and RF1. RF1 is connected to the package using wire-bonding. The others are connected to the package using bumps. The package model describes the electrical circuit between the die with a mapping file to connect between the die.

Here, one pair of stacked 3D-IC chips is utilizing TSV. Logic1 (master) is connected to Mem1 (slave). The mapping file from Innovus describes the virtual mapping that provides the connections between any two stacked die with communication directly between the die (master/slave configuration). The RLC for microbump electrical characteristics is added in the voltage source file for the slave die.

- Introduction
- Basic Voltus/Tempus Flow
- IR Drop Effect on Delays
- Voltus Setup for Power and IR Drop
- Library Setup
- Skew Analysis
- Jitter Analysis
- IR Drop Aware Critical Path Analysis Using Spice
- IR Drop Aware Clock Jitter Analysis Using Spice
- Example Flow Scripts
IR drop on power rails can significantly affect cell and net delays. IR drop transitions dynamically vary with varying switching activities in the design.
The variation can span from within a clock cycle or clock cycle to clock cycle. The delay variations due to IR drop affects both cell and net delays in clock and data paths.

The delay variance in data paths and clock paths account in setup and hold timing checks. Tempus supports skew analysis to check IR drop effects on clock arrival times at different register pins. It also supports jitter analysis to check late and early arrival times at a particular register.

Voltus performs min and max IR drop analysis based on low and high switching activities and derives effective min/max operating voltages for every instance in the design. These instance specific effective operating voltages are written into files which are referred as min or max IR drop files. The min/max IR drop files can be read in Tempus using the update_delay_corner command. These instance based min and max voltages are used during delay computation for respective corners and for performing variance checks using these delays.

Tempus also runs Spice simulations for the given timing path and compares delays against delays computed using the cell-level delay models. It creates a constant voltage source for each instance with voltage specified in the IR drop files. On the other hand, Voltus generates detailed power and ground voltage waveforms for each instance, which can be input into Tempus. Tempus uses these waveforms to create piece-wise linear voltage waveform for each instance.

IR drop can affect both net delays and cell delays. Since the peak voltage is reduced, IR drop voltage swing range will be smaller than the nominal voltage, which affects the net delay and input slew at the receiver.
In the following figure, assume that the driver is operating at the nominal voltage Vdd and receiver is operating at VIRdrop voltage.
Also assume the following trip points set in the timing library:

- Lower slew threshold: 20%
- Upper slew threshold: 80%
- Input threshold: 50%
- output threshold: 50%

The net delay with nominal voltage swing is smaller than the delay computed with IR drop voltage as the signal waveform propagates across the net. This example also shows the variance in input slew at the receiver. As the cell delay is the function of input slew and output load, the cell delay will also be affected.

Apart from input slew, the cell delays derived from the library which is characterized for nominal voltage will be derated to IR drop voltages. For accurate delay modeling, it is recommended to use multiple libraries characterized with different operating voltages. These libraries are referred as triLibs in this document.

title: Voltus Setup for Power and IR Drop

lines: Clock jitter analysis requires minimum and maximum voltage for each instance in the design. To achieve this, IR drop analysis is run with minimum and maximum activity which translates to min and max power for the design. This power is fed to IR drop analysis to generate min and max voltage for each instance in the design, as shown in the following figure:

The min activity during power analysis is defined by turning off activity of combinational logic and toggling only clock network. This is achieved by setting negligible activity (1%) on primary inputs and output of sequential logic. If the design has gated clock, it should be enabled. Refer to the set_default_switching_activity command in the script below. Note that specifying 0% activity does not work at this time, as power analysis will revert to default activity of 20% in this case.
The max activity during power analysis is defined as expected average activity on primary inputs and output of sequential logic. In addition, gated clocks are disabled.

Alternately, if VCD is available with low and high activity cycles, it can be specified in Voltus using read_activity_file command with start and stop times. Optionally, you can find high or low activity cycles by profiling VCD file. For information on vector profiling, refer to Dynamic Vector-based Power Analysis Flow .
Example Voltus script for the flow is shown below.

The instance voltage file has .iv extension. It is saved under the IR drop results directory, for example, " core_25C_dynamic_1/Reports/VDD_VSS.iv ". The .iv and .iv.gif files are located in the Reports folder under the state directory. The instance voltage is determined using power and ground IR drop waveforms inside the switching window of the instance, as shown below. It is also referred to as effective instance voltage.

This file can be read in Tempus/Voltus using update_delay_corner command as discussed in the subsequent sections of clock jitter and skew analysis. The instance voltage can be displayed in the context of layout using the set_power_rail_display command as well.
Example :

In addition to instance voltage, IR drop waveforms are also stored inside the IR drop results directory, for example, core_25C_dynamic_1/VDD/VDD.ptiavg and VSS/VSS.ptiavg . These voltage waveforms can be used for critical path analysis using SPICE. If dynamic power and IR drop analysis is done using the vector-based method, you may be required to shift these waveforms during critical path analysis using the create_spice_deck command. This will be required to align IR drop waveform with the clock edges of SDC. The IR drop waveform for instance can be viewed using view_dynamic_waveform TCL command in Voltus, as shown below:

You can use the set_rail_analysis_mode -eiv_method {best worst} command to generate min and max instance voltage files within the timing window file in Voltus. The min/max IR drop files are fed into Static Timing Analyzer (STA) to perform clock jitter analysis in Tempus.
To ensure compatibility with Tempus to run jitter analysis, the following command should not be used:
set_rail_analysis_mode –report_voltage_drop –eiv_detail_report –eiv_threshold –eiv_max_instances

Multiple libraries characterized with different operating voltages ( triLibs ) can be read into Tempus. Delay calculator computes an instance delay by interpolating delays between two libraries that are close to IR drop voltage of that particular instance.
For example, if you load libraries characterized for 1.0 volts, 0.9 volts, and 0.8 volts, the delay calculator uses1.0v and 0.9v libraries for all instances with IR drop < 1.0v and > 0.9v.
The triLibs can be loaded into Tempus in the following three ways
Reading Libraries Using read_lib Commands

Libraries with same cells characterized for different voltages should be grouped into separate list. From the above example, libraries scmetro_cmos10lp_hvt_ss_* , scmetro_cmos10lp_lvt_ss_* and scmetro_cmos10lp_rvt_ss_* which has three libraries characterized for 1.0v, 0.9v and 0.8v are grouped into a list. The list can be mixed with other libraries like pllclk_slow.lib , which is not characterized with multiple operating voltages.

triLibs can be specified while creating library sets for particular corner.

Libraries can be loaded into Tempus via configuration file which exists in the saved database. The following syntax should be used in the configuration file to load triLibs via read_ design

It is recommended that for better accuracy, the difference between two adjacent libraries should not be more than 20%. Also better delay accuracy can be achieved by reducing this difference at lower operating voltages.

Skew is the difference in arrival times of clock at launch and capture registers. In the on-chip variation mode, the launch flop uses late paths and capture flop uses early paths. Late path delays are computed based on IR drop observed during high switching activity and Early path delays are computed based on IR drop observed during low switching activity.

High switching activity results into higher voltage drop means lower operating voltages. Lower operating voltages result in larger net/cell delays. Low switching activity results into lower voltage drop means higher operating voltages (but less than nominal voltage). Higher operating voltages result in smaller net/cell delays.
Clock skew analysis can be performed in Tempus using the report_clock_timing -type skew command.
Clock Skew Report for a Register Pair
Without IR Drop Delays
With IR Drop Delays
report_clock_timing command has the -verbose option, which generates detailed reports.
Detailed Report without IR Drop Delays
Detailed Report with IR Drop Delays

Delay reported for instance DTMF_INST/RESULTS_CONV_INST/g6001 without IR drop is 0.277ns for both late and early paths. When min/max IR drop files are provided, IR drop caused the instance DTMF_INST/RESULTS_CONV_INST/g6001 delay to increase from 0.277 to 0.298ns for late path (using max delays) and 0.284ns for early path (using min delays).
Overall there is 22ps timing jump due to IR drop.

Jitter is the difference in the late and early arrival times of clock phase at a register clock pin. Latest arrival time can be calculated based on the cell and net delays of longest clock path to the register clock pin during the high switching activity. Earliest arrival time can be calculated based on the cell and net delays of shortest clock path to the register clock pin during the low switching activity.

The Voltus Clock Jitter Analysis flow leverages the power and rail capabilities of Voltus, and integrates it with Tempus static timing analysis and Spectre simulation capabilities to perform the full analysis using a single script. It enables you to compute clock jitter for all the clock end points or user-defined end points. This flow includes profiling the cycle-to-cycle EIV values, applying the worst-case cycle-to-cycle EIV values for two simulations, measuring the delta delays for each of the clock tree cells, and computing the effective jitter for the clock path.
The following diagram shows cycle-to-cycle EIV profiling in Voltus:
This flow includes the following steps:

1. Creates the power profile using the vectorless or vector-based dynamic power calculation method.
2. Generates cycle-to-cycle EIV report file. The following command is used to generate the cycle-to-cycle EIV file: 
 set_rail_analysis_mode –eiv_average_per_window_list clock_network.insts 
 The following is a snippet of the clock_network.insts input file: 
 INST CLK_L2_I1 
 INST CLK_L2_I13 
 ... 
 The output EIV format appears as follows: 
 #INST_NAME START_TIME:END_TIME EIV_AVG 
 clk_L2_I1 1.32e-09:1.78e-09 1.06354 6.38e-09:6.8e-09 1.06217 
 clk_L2_I13 1.32e-09:1.68e-09 1.06273 6.38e-09:6.72e-09 1.06854
3. Reads the IR drop generated by Voltus, profiles and identifies the cycle-to-cycle worst IR drop, and uses the worst two cycles to do the jitter analysis.
4. Calculate jitter for all the clock end points or user-defined end points.
This section describes the various inputs and outputs of the clock jitter analysis flow.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF)
- Technology PGV
- EIV File (generated from IR drop analysis)
- Clocks or Path Collection
- Spice netlist and model file
- LSF job details (queue resources for simulation)
Procedure
To perform jitter analysis, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter -ignore_undefined_cell 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz 
 
 read_sdc ../design/timing.sdc
2. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -decoupled\ ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
3. Specify the directory where the power results will be stored. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVecLessPowerResults
4. Define the power analysis mode. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless \ 
 
 -enable_state_propagation true \ 
 
 -create_binary_db false \ 
 
 -report_instance_switching_info all \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } -corner max
5. Specify jitter frame and simulation time. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_default_switching_activity -name low -input_activity 0.05 \ 
 
 -seq_activity 0.1 -clock_gates_output 0.2 \ 
 
 set_default_switching_activity -name high -input_activity 0.1 \ 
 
 -seq_activity 0.2 -clock_gates_output 2.0 \ 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -period 15ns -resolution 50ps \ 
 
 -activity_pattern {0 high 5ns low 10ns high}
6. Define the rail analysis mode. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method dynamic \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } \ 
 
 -eiv_method { worst best avg } \ 
 
 -work_directory_name VOLTUS_OUTPUT/extraction_output \ 
 
 -report_power_in_parallel true \ 
 
 -eiv_average_per_window_list all_cell.list
7. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. Then, use set_rail_analysis_domain to define the power domains. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.810 -force 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.010 -force 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain - name PD - pwrnets VDD - gndnets VSS
8. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD -format xy -file ../design/VDD.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../design/VSS.pp
9. Run rail analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_rail -type domain -output OUTPUT/IRDROP PD
10. Perform Tempus delay calculation. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 report_timing 
 
 report_timing -net
11. Run jitter analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_jitter -eiv_vdd_vss {VDD VSS} \ 
 
 -eiv_file /IRDROP/PD_25C_dynamic_1/Reports/VDD_VSS.win_avg.rpt \ 
 
 -model_file /../include.ss -subckt_file /../worst.spi \ 
 
 -spectre /../tools.lnx86/bin/spectre \ 
 
 -no_hard_stop_at_to_pins true -outdir jitter_output \ 
 
 -from i_REC_CLK -spectre_cmd_options {+aps +spice +mt=8 \ 
 
 +postlayout +dcopt } -lsf_job_string {/grid/sfi/farm/bin/bsub \ 
 
 -q rnd -P VOLTUS:16.1:PV:regTest -W 140:00 -R \ 
 
 "OSNAME==Linux && OSBIT==64 && (OSREL==EE60||OSREL==EE50)" -Is } \ 
 
 -run_simulation \ 
 
 -levels_combine 2 \ 
 
 -max_spectre_lic 100
The Voltus console will display jitter analysis statistics, as shown in the following snippet:
Output
The jitter analysis directory will contain the following files:

data: A report that gives a list of all the start and end point pair clock branches and the corresponding cycle-to-cycle jitter value in picosecond. If the -through point is used, it is also given in the report.

A detailed report for each start and end point pair branch. For each branch, it includes stage by stage details as follows: 
 
 Cycle-to-Cycle Jitter Value (ps) 
 Cell input pin name and output pin name with rise/fall transition 
 EIV value used for cycle 1 and cycle 2 
 Simulations measured cell delay for cycle 1 and cycle 2

title: IR Drop Aware Critical Path Analysis Using Spice

lines: Tempus supports IR drop aware spice analysis of timing paths. It writes out sensitized spice netlist for given timing path and invokes Spectre simulator to do spice simulations. Upon completion of spice simulations, Tempus reads back the spice report and generates delay comparison table between spice delays and Tempus internal delay calculator.

When IR drop files are read into Tempus, Tempus automatically creates a constant voltage source for each instance in the spice netlist with voltage specified in the IR drop file for that particular instance.

These spice decks can be created using the following command:

During setup timing analysis launch clock path traverses through longest path, and hold analysis launch path traverses through shortest path. Tempus runs spice simulations on these paths and reports arrival times at the register pin computed by timing analysis engine and spice. Difference between the arrival times reported by these two simulations gives clock-jitter.

lines:
- Tempus Power Integrity Analysis Overview
- Licensing Requirements
- Tempus Power Integrity Flow
- Performing Tempus PI Analysis
- Tempus PI Result Analysis and Reporting

The Tempus Power Integrity Analysis (Tempus PI) flow is a combination of the Tempus static timing analysis and Voltus power and IR drop technologies. Tempus and Voltus are built on a common database with common runtime and memory model, and are fully integrated for better accuracy. Therefore, the IR drop and static timing analysis information can easily be shared between the two tools.

The primary objective of the Tempus PI flow is to identify IR-sensitive timing paths, to accurately predict IR drop on these paths with vectorless analysis, and to enable automated fixing of these critical paths using Innovus and Tempus IR-ECO. The IR-sensitive timing paths are paths that have sufficient positive slack but degrade significantly with IR drop. This timing/sensitivity-driven IR analysis is multi-cycle (transient IR simulation), functional, and vectorless. It is not only functional for the paths under analysis but it also creates a worst-case but feasible switching scenario for neighbors around it. The IR drop and timing events are natively aligned within the tool because Tempus and Voltus share all information, such as IR drop, timing windows, and switching within the timing windows.
The following diagram illustrates the Tempus-Voltus integration in the Tempus PI flow:
The following are the key features of the Tempus PI flow:

- Identify IR-sensitive paths - the critical paths are timed using the traditional VDD margins on cell delays using max IR drop. The IR-sensitive paths are not timing critical paths but can be critical in the IR conditions. These IR-sensitive paths are identified among many paths, such as the resistance sensitive paths, power sensitive paths, timing sensitive paths, IR-sensitive paths for neighboring cells, and user-defined paths. The IR-sensitive critical paths are scheduled to switch in all cycles of the transient IR simulation.
- Create functional path switching scenarios - it creates realistic worst-case switching scenarios for the path and for the environment around it. In this flow, the software is aware of the delays along the path, and also the delays of the paths in the neighborhood. It creates switching scenarios that are timing slack and timing path aware.
- Identify proximity aggressors - it finds proximity aggressors around voltage and time-critical instances based on power-grid resistance and timing sensitivity. These proximity aggressors are modeled to switch at different times within the timing windows from cycle to cycle. The other aggressors are modeled using the vectorless algorithm to meet activity target and package effects.
- Calculate impact of delay/timing on voltage and time critical paths using cycle-cycle voltage statistic.

Following is a comparative analysis of a slack report (histogram of timing and number of paths/endpoints for the given design) from the traditional timing signoff flow and a slack report from the Tempus PI flow:
Here, you can observe the following:

- There are a total of 2092 paths.
- In the case of the traditional timing signoff flow, there are no negative slacks, and the slack bin starts at the +50ps to +60ps range. The worst path is in the +10 to +20ps slack range.
- In the case of the Tempus PI true signoff flow, 71 paths are failing timing with negative slack values. The worst path is in the -30ps to -40ps range.
- Using the Tempus PI generated vectors with a more aggressive path analysis in the neighborhood, slack has moved to a negative range. This indicates a major shift in the overall delay. 
 These 71 failing paths that can affect performance on silicon can be fixed using the Tempus PI ECO feature.
Licensing Requirements

Tempus PI can be invoked from either a Tempus or a Voltus session. This feature requires 3 licenses: Tempus XL (TPS200), Voltus XL (VTS200), and the Tempus Power Integrity Option (TPS600). The following table describes the licensing use model:
Tempus Power Integrity Flow
The following diagram illustrates the Tempus PI flow:
To perform Tempus PI analysis, perform the following steps:

1. DB Signoff: Load the input design database.
2. Critical Path Analysis - Identify the critical paths that have timing violations in the Tempus STA stage.
3. IR drop Analysis - Report IR drop hotspots (Effective Instance Voltage - EIV) and identify worst violation victims and their aggressors in the Voltus IR Drop analysis stage.
4. Tempus ECO Optimization - Perform both downsizing of cells or aggressors that cause IR drop and ECO timing optimization in the Tempus ECO Fixing stage.
5. IR Fix Validation - Run Tempus PI analysis again to re-check the result after Tempus ECO.
This flow generates a database with better timing and potentially better IR drop.
Performing Tempus PI Analysis
This section describes the various inputs and outputs of the Tempus PI analysis flow.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, timing libraries, netlist, SDC, DEF, SPEF)
- Technology PGV
Procedure
To perform Tempus PI analysis, complete the following steps:
Step 1: Tempus STA Analysis Using IR Derates

1. Load the design data. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz 
 
 
 
 Tcl command - Snippet of viewDefinition.tcl : 
 
 
 
 create_library_set -name LS_wc_1p00 -timing ./standard_cells_1p00_125C.lib 
 
 create_library_set -name LS_wc_0p90 -timing ./standard_cells_0p90_125C.lib ;# This library set will be used to improve interpolation later in the flow 
 
 create_library_set -name LS_wc_0p85 -timing ./standard_cells_0p85_125C.lib 
 
 create_rc_corner -name RC_wc_125 -T 125 
 
 
 
 create_delay_corner -name DC_wc_1p00 -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -library_set LS_wc_1p00 
 
 create_delay_corner -name DC_wc_0p85 -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -library_set LS_wc_0p85 
 
 create_constraint_mode -name CM_wc -sdc_files ./design.sdc 
 
 
 
 create_analysis_view -name AV_wc_1p00 -constraint_mode CM_wc -delay_corner DC_wc_1p00 ;# This is the non-derated/non-margined view at 1.0v for IR drop analysis 
 
 create_analysis_view -name AV_wc_0p85 -constraint_mode CM_wc -delay_corner DC_wc_0p85 ;# This is the traditional STA view at 0.85V with voltage margined/derated for IR drop analysis
2. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
3. Define the analysis views to be used for setup and hold analysis, and update timing for the design. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_analysis_view -setup {AV_wc_1p00 AV_wc_0p85 } -hold {AV_wc_1p00 AV_wc_0p85 }
Step 2: Voltus Power and IR Drop Analysis (EIV DB Generation)

1. Specify the directory where the power results will be stored. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVecLessPowerResults
2. Define the power analysis mode. The Tempus PI analysis flow is applicable only to the dynamic vectorless power calculation method. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless \ 
 
 -disable_static true \ 
 
 -enable_state_propagation true \ 
 
 -honor_combinational_logic_on_clock_net false \ 
 
 -report_instance_switching_info all \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } \ 
 
 -enable_tempus_pi true \ 
 
 -ir_derated_timing_view AV_wc_0p85 \ 
 
 -enable_pba_for_tempus_pi true 
 
 
 
 
 
 The following are the parameters specific to the Tempus PI analysis flow:
  - -enable_tempus_pi true - specifies to enable the Tempus PI analysis flow.
  - -ir_derated_timing_view view_name - specifies the name of the timing view used to calculate timing slack and to rank the flops. This parameter must be specified if there are multiple views. 
 The -ir_derated_timing_view parameter defines the timing view used to detect the IR-sensitive paths using a lower voltage than the actual analysis. This lower voltage simulates the IR drop for each instance in the design. The software uses this view to prioritize the end-points that need to be prioritized for switching.
  - -enable_pba_for_tempus_pi true - specifies to enable the Path-Based Analysis (PBA) for slack calculation. By default ( false ), the Graph-Based Analysis (GBA) is used.
3. Optional. Define default activities for various points in the design. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_default_switching_activity -input_activity 0 \ 
 
 -period 4.0 -clock_gates_output_ratio 0.5 -seq_activity 0.2 
 
 
 
 The default switching activity for sequential cells ( -seq_activity ) in percentage (ratio 0.0 to 1.0) specifies the number of fully expanded data paths that will be scheduled to switch in the Tempus PI flow.
4. Optional, but recommended. Define the power simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps -period 320ns 
 
 
 
 The dynamic power simulation period defines the number of power analysis cycles to be run. 
 The user-defined sequential activity per cycle ( set_default_switching_activity -seq_activity ) and the total power analysis simulation period ( set_dynamic_power_simulation -period ) control the number of critical path end-points that will be covered per simulation cycle and the total simulation time. Allowing a larger power simulation period will allow Tempus PI to cover all the end-points.
5. Run power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 report_power
6. Define the rail analysis mode. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method dynamic \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_1p00 \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } \ 
 
 -enable_rlrp_analysis true \ 
 
 -verbosity true \ 
 
 -temperature 125 \ 
 
 -eiv_eval_window switching \ 
 
 -eiv_method {bestavg worstavg}
7. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. Then, use set_rail_analysis_domain to define the power domains. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_AO -voltage 1 -threshold 0.95 -force 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_column -voltage 1 -threshold 0.95 -force 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_ring -voltage 1 -threshold 0.95 -force 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_external -voltage 1 -threshold 0.95 -force 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.05 -force 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name ALL -pwrnets { VDD_AO VDD_external } -gndnets VSS
8. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_AO -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_AO.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_external -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_external.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp
9. Define the rail simulation resolution, and specify the location of the binary current files generated through dynamic power analysis (step 5). 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_rail_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 set_power_data -scale 1 -format current [glob dynVecLessPowerResults/*.ptiavg]
10. Run rail analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze _rail -output ./dynVecLessRailResults -type domain ALL 
 
 
 
 The Voltus console will display rail analysis statistics, as shown in the following snippet: 
 Rail Analysis completed successfully. 
 Finished Rail Analysis at 19:32:45 02/10/2019 (cpu=0:01:12, real=0:02:33, peak mem=1697.00MB) 
 Current Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution resource usage: (total cpu=0:09:48, real=1:06:51, mem=1697.00MB) 
 
 ***Info: Found 70 instances with IR drop violations in design based on eiv_method: Worst and eiv_eval_window: elapse.
Step 3: Tempus IR-Aware STA Using EIV Database (any IR Derates Removed)

1. Calculate IR-aware slack by specifying the IR analysis output as input to the Tempus run: 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_delay_cal_mode -early_irdrop_data_type best_average \ 
 
 -late_irdrop_data_type worst_average 
 
 update_delay_corner -name DC_wc_1p00 \ 
 
 -library_set {LS_wc_1p00 LS_wc_0p90 LS_wc_0p85} \ 
 
 -irdrop_data ./dyn_rail_phaseIII/ALL_25C_dynamic_1 
 
 set_analysis_view -setup {AV_wc_1p00} -hold {AV_wc_1p00} 
 
 update_timing -full 
 
 report_timing -max_paths 200000 -retime path_slew_propagation -retime_mode exhaustive > pi_timing.pba.rpt 
 
 
 
 The final analysis within Tempus PI is done at the non-derated voltage corner (that is, 1.0V in the viewDefinition.tcl example). Therefore, the AV_wc_1p00 view is selected for analysis, and the corresponding delay corner ( DC_wc_1p00 ) is updated to enable voltage tri-lib interpolation by specifying multiple library sets ( -library_set )at various voltages (1.00V, 0.90V and 0.85V). 
 The following are the parameters specific to the Tempus PI analysis flow:
  - -irdrop_data directory_name - specifies the IR drop files or directory of files to apply to both the early and late delay calculation for the specified delay corner object.
Step 4: Tempus ECO Fixing
Output
The rail output directory will contain the following information:

data:
For each run, an output directory is generated with the default name < domainName >_< temperature >_dynamic_< X >. < domainName > will be replaced with ALL if all domains are to be analyzed ( analyze _rail -output dir -type domain ALL ), or with < netName > if a power net is to be analyzed. The output directory contains state directories for each net, such as VDD and VSS. 
 
 Each state directory contains the following: 
 
 results – a file that gives a summary of the rail analysis results for various plots, such as tc (tap current), rc (resistor current), and so on. 
 Reports – a folder that contains a list of useful report files that contain all power-grid integrity data. 
 Auto-generated GIF files of the rail analysis plots

lines: The current work directory contains the timing report:

data: A timing report that provides information about the various paths in the design. The report_timing > < timing_report >.rpt command is used to generate the report and write it to the specified file name. For more information on Tempus commands, refer to Tempus Text Command Reference .

When you compare the timing reports in the following snapshots, you will observe that the timing path that had positive slack in the traditional analysis flow has negative slack in the Tempus PI analysis flow:
