Tempus PI can be invoked from either a Tempus or a Voltus session. This feature requires 3 licenses: Tempus XL (TPS200), Voltus XL (VTS200), and the Tempus Power Integrity Option (TPS600). The following table describes the licensing use model:
Tempus Power Integrity Flow
The following diagram illustrates the Tempus PI flow:
To perform Tempus PI analysis, perform the following steps:

1. DB Signoff: Load the input design database.
2. Critical Path Analysis - Identify the critical paths that have timing violations in the Tempus STA stage.
3. IR drop Analysis - Report IR drop hotspots (Effective Instance Voltage - EIV) and identify worst violation victims and their aggressors in the Voltus IR Drop analysis stage.
4. Tempus ECO Optimization - Perform both downsizing of cells or aggressors that cause IR drop and ECO timing optimization in the Tempus ECO Fixing stage.
5. IR Fix Validation - Run Tempus PI analysis again to re-check the result after Tempus ECO.
This flow generates a database with better timing and potentially better IR drop.
Performing Tempus PI Analysis
This section describes the various inputs and outputs of the Tempus PI analysis flow.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, timing libraries, netlist, SDC, DEF, SPEF)
- Technology PGV
Procedure
To perform Tempus PI analysis, complete the following steps:
Step 1: Tempus STA Analysis Using IR Derates

1. Load the design data. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_view_definition ../design/viewDefinition.tcl 
 
 read_verilog ../design/postRouteOpt.enc.dat/super_filter.v.gz 
 
 set_top_module super_filter 
 
 read_def ../design/super_filter.def.gz 
 
 
 
 Tcl command - Snippet of viewDefinition.tcl : 
 
 
 
 create_library_set -name LS_wc_1p00 -timing ./standard_cells_1p00_125C.lib 
 
 create_library_set -name LS_wc_0p90 -timing ./standard_cells_0p90_125C.lib ;# This library set will be used to improve interpolation later in the flow 
 
 create_library_set -name LS_wc_0p85 -timing ./standard_cells_0p85_125C.lib 
 
 create_rc_corner -name RC_wc_125 -T 125 
 
 
 
 create_delay_corner -name DC_wc_1p00 -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -library_set LS_wc_1p00 
 
 create_delay_corner -name DC_wc_0p85 -rc_corner RC_wc_125 -library_set LS_wc_0p85 
 
 create_constraint_mode -name CM_wc -sdc_files ./design.sdc 
 
 
 
 create_analysis_view -name AV_wc_1p00 -constraint_mode CM_wc -delay_corner DC_wc_1p00 ;# This is the non-derated/non-margined view at 1.0v for IR drop analysis 
 
 create_analysis_view -name AV_wc_0p85 -constraint_mode CM_wc -delay_corner DC_wc_0p85 ;# This is the traditional STA view at 0.85V with voltage margined/derated for IR drop analysis
2. Specify the SPEF file for signal net parasitic information. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner RC_wc_125 ../design/postRouteOpt_RC_wc_125.spef.gz
3. Define the analysis views to be used for setup and hold analysis, and update timing for the design. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_analysis_view -setup {AV_wc_1p00 AV_wc_0p85 } -hold {AV_wc_1p00 AV_wc_0p85 }
Step 2: Voltus Power and IR Drop Analysis (EIV DB Generation)

1. Specify the directory where the power results will be stored. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynVecLessPowerResults
2. Define the power analysis mode. The Tempus PI analysis flow is applicable only to the dynamic vectorless power calculation method. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless \ 
 
 -disable_static true \ 
 
 -enable_state_propagation true \ 
 
 -honor_combinational_logic_on_clock_net false \ 
 
 -report_instance_switching_info all \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } \ 
 
 -enable_tempus_pi true \ 
 
 -ir_derated_timing_view AV_wc_0p85 \ 
 
 -enable_pba_for_tempus_pi true 
 
 
 
 
 
 The following are the parameters specific to the Tempus PI analysis flow:
  - -enable_tempus_pi true - specifies to enable the Tempus PI analysis flow.
  - -ir_derated_timing_view view_name - specifies the name of the timing view used to calculate timing slack and to rank the flops. This parameter must be specified if there are multiple views. 
 The -ir_derated_timing_view parameter defines the timing view used to detect the IR-sensitive paths using a lower voltage than the actual analysis. This lower voltage simulates the IR drop for each instance in the design. The software uses this view to prioritize the end-points that need to be prioritized for switching.
  - -enable_pba_for_tempus_pi true - specifies to enable the Path-Based Analysis (PBA) for slack calculation. By default ( false ), the Graph-Based Analysis (GBA) is used.
3. Optional. Define default activities for various points in the design. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_default_switching_activity -input_activity 0 \ 
 
 -period 4.0 -clock_gates_output_ratio 0.5 -seq_activity 0.2 
 
 
 
 The default switching activity for sequential cells ( -seq_activity ) in percentage (ratio 0.0 to 1.0) specifies the number of fully expanded data paths that will be scheduled to switch in the Tempus PI flow.
4. Optional, but recommended. Define the power simulation period and resolution. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation -resolution 50ps -period 320ns 
 
 
 
 The dynamic power simulation period defines the number of power analysis cycles to be run. 
 The user-defined sequential activity per cycle ( set_default_switching_activity -seq_activity ) and the total power analysis simulation period ( set_dynamic_power_simulation -period ) control the number of critical path end-points that will be covered per simulation cycle and the total simulation time. Allowing a larger power simulation period will allow Tempus PI to cover all the end-points.
5. Run power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 report_power
6. Define the rail analysis mode. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method dynamic \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 -analysis_view AV_wc_1p00 \ 
 
 -power_grid_library { \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/tech_pgv/techonly.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/stdcell_pgv/stdcells.cl \ 
 
 ../data/pgv_dir/macro_pgv/macros_pll.cl \ 
 
 } \ 
 
 -enable_rlrp_analysis true \ 
 
 -verbosity true \ 
 
 -temperature 125 \ 
 
 -eiv_eval_window switching \ 
 
 -eiv_method {bestavg worstavg}
7. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. Then, use set_rail_analysis_domain to define the power domains. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_AO -voltage 1 -threshold 0.95 -force 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_column -voltage 1 -threshold 0.95 -force 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_ring -voltage 1 -threshold 0.95 -force 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD_external -voltage 1 -threshold 0.95 -force 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.05 -force 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name ALL -pwrnets { VDD_AO VDD_external } -gndnets VSS
8. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_AO -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_AO.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD_external -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VDD_external.pp 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../design/super_filter_VSS.pp
9. Define the rail simulation resolution, and specify the location of the binary current files generated through dynamic power analysis (step 5). 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_dynamic_rail_simulation -resolution 50ps 
 
 set_power_data -scale 1 -format current [glob dynVecLessPowerResults/*.ptiavg]
10. Run rail analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze _rail -output ./dynVecLessRailResults -type domain ALL 
 
 
 
 The Voltus console will display rail analysis statistics, as shown in the following snippet: 
 Rail Analysis completed successfully. 
 Finished Rail Analysis at 19:32:45 02/10/2019 (cpu=0:01:12, real=0:02:33, peak mem=1697.00MB) 
 Current Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution resource usage: (total cpu=0:09:48, real=1:06:51, mem=1697.00MB) 
 
 ***Info: Found 70 instances with IR drop violations in design based on eiv_method: Worst and eiv_eval_window: elapse.
Step 3: Tempus IR-Aware STA Using EIV Database (any IR Derates Removed)

1. Calculate IR-aware slack by specifying the IR analysis output as input to the Tempus run: 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_delay_cal_mode -early_irdrop_data_type best_average \ 
 
 -late_irdrop_data_type worst_average 
 
 update_delay_corner -name DC_wc_1p00 \ 
 
 -library_set {LS_wc_1p00 LS_wc_0p90 LS_wc_0p85} \ 
 
 -irdrop_data ./dyn_rail_phaseIII/ALL_25C_dynamic_1 
 
 set_analysis_view -setup {AV_wc_1p00} -hold {AV_wc_1p00} 
 
 update_timing -full 
 
 report_timing -max_paths 200000 -retime path_slew_propagation -retime_mode exhaustive > pi_timing.pba.rpt 
 
 
 
 The final analysis within Tempus PI is done at the non-derated voltage corner (that is, 1.0V in the viewDefinition.tcl example). Therefore, the AV_wc_1p00 view is selected for analysis, and the corresponding delay corner ( DC_wc_1p00 ) is updated to enable voltage tri-lib interpolation by specifying multiple library sets ( -library_set )at various voltages (1.00V, 0.90V and 0.85V). 
 The following are the parameters specific to the Tempus PI analysis flow:
  - -irdrop_data directory_name - specifies the IR drop files or directory of files to apply to both the early and late delay calculation for the specified delay corner object.
Step 4: Tempus ECO Fixing
Output
The rail output directory will contain the following information:

data:
For each run, an output directory is generated with the default name < domainName >_< temperature >_dynamic_< X >. < domainName > will be replaced with ALL if all domains are to be analyzed ( analyze _rail -output dir -type domain ALL ), or with < netName > if a power net is to be analyzed. The output directory contains state directories for each net, such as VDD and VSS. 
 
 Each state directory contains the following: 
 
 results – a file that gives a summary of the rail analysis results for various plots, such as tc (tap current), rc (resistor current), and so on. 
 Reports – a folder that contains a list of useful report files that contain all power-grid integrity data. 
 Auto-generated GIF files of the rail analysis plots

lines: The current work directory contains the timing report:

data: A timing report that provides information about the various paths in the design. The report_timing > < timing_report >.rpt command is used to generate the report and write it to the specified file name. For more information on Tempus commands, refer to Tempus Text Command Reference .

lines: Tempus PI Result Analysis and Reporting

You can analyze the timing reports generated from the traditional timing signoff and the Tempus PI flows to determine the instances in the high IR drop region. When you compare the timing reports in the following snapshots, you will observe that the timing path that had positive slack in the traditional analysis flow has negative slack in the Tempus PI analysis flow:
Worst-case Path Analysis (Traditional)
Worst-case path analysis (Tempus PI)

The following snapshot shows the top five register2register critical paths ( Timing SI -> Debug Timing menu option) and the corresponding IR drop heat map ( Power Rail -> Power Rail Plots menu option):
Following is the Tempus PI performance data for a design with 5.05M standard cell instances:
The following diagram illustrates the Tempus PI flow:
To perform Tempus PI analysis, perform the following steps:

1. DB Signoff: Load the input design database.
2. Critical Path Analysis - Identify the critical paths that have timing violations in the Tempus STA stage.
3. IR drop Analysis - Report IR drop hotspots (Effective Instance Voltage - EIV) and identify worst violation victims and their aggressors in the Voltus IR Drop analysis stage.
4. Tempus ECO Optimization - Perform both downsizing of cells or aggressors that cause IR drop and ECO timing optimization in the Tempus ECO Fixing stage.
5. IR Fix Validation - Run Tempus PI analysis again to re-check the result after Tempus ECO.
This flow generates a database with better timing and potentially better IR drop.
Performing Tempus PI Analysis
This section describes the various inputs and outputs of the Tempus PI analysis flow.

 analyze _rail -output ./dynVecLessRailResults -type domain ALL 
 
 
 
 The Voltus console will display rail analysis statistics, as shown in the following snippet: 
 Rail Analysis completed successfully. 
 Finished Rail Analysis at 19:32:45 02/10/2019 (cpu=0:01:12, real=0:02:33, peak mem=1697.00MB) 
 Current Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution resource usage: (total cpu=0:09:48, real=1:06:51, mem=1697.00MB) 
 
 ***Info: Found 70 instances with IR drop violations in design based on eiv_method: Worst and eiv_eval_window: elapse.
Step 3: Tempus IR-Aware STA Using EIV Database (any IR Derates Removed)

1. Calculate IR-aware slack by specifying the IR analysis output as input to the Tempus run: 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_delay_cal_mode -early_irdrop_data_type best_average \ 
 
 -late_irdrop_data_type worst_average 
 
 update_delay_corner -name DC_wc_1p00 \ 
 
 -library_set {LS_wc_1p00 LS_wc_0p90 LS_wc_0p85} \ 
 
 -irdrop_data ./dyn_rail_phaseIII/ALL_25C_dynamic_1 
 
 set_analysis_view -setup {AV_wc_1p00} -hold {AV_wc_1p00} 
 
 update_timing -full 
 
 report_timing -max_paths 200000 -retime path_slew_propagation -retime_mode exhaustive > pi_timing.pba.rpt 
 
 
 
 The final analysis within Tempus PI is done at the non-derated voltage corner (that is, 1.0V in the viewDefinition.tcl example). Therefore, the AV_wc_1p00 view is selected for analysis, and the corresponding delay corner ( DC_wc_1p00 ) is updated to enable voltage tri-lib interpolation by specifying multiple library sets ( -library_set )at various voltages (1.00V, 0.90V and 0.85V). 
 The following are the parameters specific to the Tempus PI analysis flow:
  - -irdrop_data directory_name - specifies the IR drop files or directory of files to apply to both the early and late delay calculation for the specified delay corner object.
Step 4: Tempus ECO Fixing
Output
The rail output directory will contain the following information:

data:
For each run, an output directory is generated with the default name < domainName >_< temperature >_dynamic_< X >. < domainName > will be replaced with ALL if all domains are to be analyzed ( analyze _rail -output dir -type domain ALL ), or with < netName > if a power net is to be analyzed. The output directory contains state directories for each net, such as VDD and VSS. 
 
 Each state directory contains the following: 
 
 results – a file that gives a summary of the rail analysis results for various plots, such as tc (tap current), rc (resistor current), and so on. 
 Reports – a folder that contains a list of useful report files that contain all power-grid integrity data. 
 Auto-generated GIF files of the rail analysis plots

Overview

Due to resistive power grid or simultaneous switching of close instances, there is reduction in voltage on VDD nets and increase in voltage on VSS nets. These deviation in voltage compared to the original values is IR Drop (on VDD nets) or Ground Bounce (on VSS nets). The reason IR drop analysis is so important is that the resistance increases with advanced nodes, and power increases with high operating frequencies, both resulting in IR drop violations. IR drop may cause timing issues if they are higher than 5%-10% of effective supply voltage, or cause functional failures if they are more than 20%-30% of effective supply voltage.
The following are some of the existing methods to fix IR Drop:

- Add power pads to the hot spot regions. This method is restricted by the package design.
- Add decap cells near aggressors. However, effective series resistance increases in case of advanced nodes, which makes the decap cells less effective.
- Add power straps, but this can only be done if sufficient routing resources are available.

This chapter covers the IR aware placement method for fixing IR drop issues. In this method, the aggressors are moved to a different location. This reduces current requirements at the hot spot regions, and therefore decreasing IR drop.
Data Requirements for IR Drop Aware Placement
The table below lists the inputs that are required for IR Drop Aware Placement in Voltus and Innovus:

data: Files for the Rail Analysis stage
Power Domain and its associated Power Nets
Reports generated by Hotspot Debugger after Rail Analysis

lines: IR Drop Aware Placement Flow
The following diagram illustrates the IR Drop Aware Placement analysis flow:
The flow has three fundamental steps:

1. Restore the post CTS or post-routed database using the following command: 
 restoreDesign
2. Set up power and rail analysis modes and environment using the following commands: 
 set_power_output_dir dir_name
set_power_analysis_mode ...
set_default_switching_activity ...
set_dynamic_power_simulation ...
set_rail_analysis_mode ...
set_pg_nets ...
set_rail_analysis_domain ...
set_power_pads ...
3. Perform IR aware placement analysis using the following command: 
 setPlaceMode ...
refinePlace ...
ecoRoute

In this flow, the refinePlace command performs power and rail analysis, identifies hotspots, and refines the placement of instances. After checking for IR Drop violations, if there are still some critical violations, a second iteration of IR aware placement is done which is followed by IR Drop analysis.
Hotspot Debugger

In the IR Drop Aware Placement flow, you need to identify the hotspots with aggressors (instances) that demand high currents. These instances in the hotspot regions are reported by the refinePlace command, and the report is then used by Innovus to perform IR Aware Placement. The command divides the design into regions with the hotspots, and identifies aggressors (worst instances) in those hotspots, as shown in the following diagram:
The values of the following two parameters of the debug_irdrop command are key to get the best results for IR Drop improvement with lesser affect to timing:

- The -nregion value (default: 3 ) parameter specifies the number of regions that the design needs to be divided into. The value of - nregion will depend on the number of hotspot regions that can be seen in the design. The higher the value of -nregion , the more likely the hotspot regions will be concentrated resulting in efficient results.
- The -nworst_instances value (default: 10 ) are the number of instances that are reported in each regions. For - nworst_instances, a higher value will cause more movement of the instances and a smaller value will cause lesser movement of the instances.
Based on the values of -nregion and - nworst_instances, you can control the movement of instances and the number of instances that are being moved.

The hotspot regions are reported in the main Hotspot Debugger report ( report.rpt ), located in the VoltusDebugIRdropResults directory, generated after running the refinePlace command. The following is a snippet of the main report:
For each region, there are region-based reports (red-highlighted in the above diagram) that have instances with the worst IR drops and their layer-based and net-based IR drop values.
IR Aware Placement

The aim of IR Aware Placement analysis is to spread the high IR drop instances away from hotspots to improve IR drop. The high IR drop instances are vertically moved high as it is more efficient than horizontal movement.
This step is performed in Innovus. IR Aware placement requires the following commands and variable:

- setPlaceMode -place_detail_irdrop_aware_effort{none | low | medium high} - Specifies the IR drop optimization effort level to be used during refinePlace . The possible arguments are:
  - none : Disables the feature. (Default)
  - low : Moves the aggressor instances to nearby rows, and the aggressor has the same priority as the other instances during legalization.
  - medium : Moves the aggressor instances to nearby rows, and the aggressor has higher priority than the other instances during legalization.
  - high : Moves the aggressor instances to nearby rows, and the aggressor has higher priority than the other instances during legalization. high will treat the rule more strictly during placement legalization.
- refinePlace - Performs the placement of instances based on the reports generated by Hotspot Debugger. The refinePlace command performs power and rail analysis, identifies hotspots, and refines the placement of instances. Therefore, you do not have to specify the report_power , analyze _rail, and debug_irdrop standalone commands. The use model of this command is given below. 
 In case of the post-routed database, you need to re-route the signal nets using the ecoRoute command after all the optimization iterations are complete. 
 
For information on the timing analysis and placement commands used in the Innovus software, refer to Innovus Text Command Reference .
  - For Post-CTS Database: refinePlace
  - For Post-routed Database: 
 setPlaceMode -place_detail_preserve_routing true
refinePlace
The following 4 directories are generated in the current directory after refinePlace :

- VoltusPowerAnalysisResults
- VoltusRailAnalysisResults
- VoltusDebugIRdropResults
- work
Rechecking IR Drop and Performing Iterations
As part of the first iteration after refinePlace , you need to recheck the IR drop in the design. For rechecking IR Drop, specify the refinePlace command multiple times:
You must run the analyze_rail command after the final iteration using the refinePlace command. That is, you need to run rail analysis after the final placement of instances.
Rerouting the Signal Nets and Saving the Design

Rerouting needs to be done in the post-routed database after refinePlace as the instances are moved to a different location, and the signal nets need to be rerouted. For rerouting, use the following command: ecoRoute
You can do ecoRoute after every refinePlace iteration but this will increase the turnaround time. It is therefore recommended to do ecoRoute after all the iterations are done.

At every stage, it is recommended to save the design after refinePlace as there will be changes in the design that needs to be stored. For saving the design, use the following command: saveDesign design_name.enc
Debugging IR Aware Placement

After the first Power and Rail Analysis, you will be able to see the hotspots in the GUI. The following image shows that some of the instances, such as clock buffer cells and flip flops, are switching simultaneously causing these highlighted hotspot regions:
You need to use Hotspot Debugger to recognize the hotspots. The following is a snapshot of the main Hotspot Debugger report:

Here, the design is divided into seven regions. The report states the location of these regions, timing libraries, power-grid views and power-grid integrity checks, IR drop consumption, and report for each region with root cause analysis. Based on the XY location of each region, you can plot boxes to mark the regions that are reported. For that you can use the following command:
add_gui_shape -rect {x1 y1 x2 y2} -layer layer_name -width value
This will plot rectangles as shown in the following image:

# Define Voltage Source Location
# Initial Timing Analysis

#------------------------------------------ # Use deleteFiller to delete fillers if there are any fillers in the design to make space for instances to move
# Final Timing Analysis

 In case of the post-routed database, you need to re-route the signal nets using the ecoRoute command after all the optimization iterations are complete. 
 
For information on the timing analysis and placement commands used in the Innovus software, refer to Innovus Text Command Reference .
  - For Post-CTS Database: refinePlace
  - For Post-routed Database: 
 setPlaceMode -place_detail_preserve_routing true
refinePlace
The following 4 directories are generated in the current directory after refinePlace :

- VoltusPowerAnalysisResults
- VoltusRailAnalysisResults
- VoltusDebugIRdropResults
- work
Rechecking IR Drop and Performing Iterations
As part of the first iteration after refinePlace , you need to recheck the IR drop in the design. For rechecking IR Drop, specify the refinePlace command multiple times:
You must run the analyze_rail command after the final iteration using the refinePlace command. That is, you need to run rail analysis after the final placement of instances.
Rerouting the Signal Nets and Saving the Design

Rerouting needs to be done in the post-routed database after refinePlace as the instances are moved to a different location, and the signal nets need to be rerouted. For rerouting, use the following command: ecoRoute
You can do ecoRoute after every refinePlace iteration but this will increase the turnaround time. It is therefore recommended to do ecoRoute after all the iterations are done.

At every stage, it is recommended to save the design after refinePlace as there will be changes in the design that needs to be stored. For saving the design, use the following command: saveDesign design_name.enc
Debugging IR Aware Placement

After the first Power and Rail Analysis, you will be able to see the hotspots in the GUI. The following image shows that some of the instances, such as clock buffer cells and flip flops, are switching simultaneously causing these highlighted hotspot regions:
You need to use Hotspot Debugger to recognize the hotspots. The following is a snapshot of the main Hotspot Debugger report:

Here, the design is divided into seven regions. The report states the location of these regions, timing libraries, power-grid views and power-grid integrity checks, IR drop consumption, and report for each region with root cause analysis. Based on the XY location of each region, you can plot boxes to mark the regions that are reported. For that you can use the following command:
add_gui_shape -rect {x1 y1 x2 y2} -layer layer_name -width value
This will plot rectangles as shown in the following image:

The Timing-aware IR drop fixing flow allows you to fix the IR drop violations using the Tempus ECO feature. Some of the challenges related to IR drop analysis and fixing are:

- Power-grid is consuming an increasing amount of chip area
- Libraries that are characterized at given voltages are getting unusable
- Huge time is spent in fixing of IR drops through traditional means

The manual techniques used to fix the IR drop hotspots are time consuming and iterative because finding the right manual ECO can be challenging when designers do not have the complete timing and physical information.

Tempus-ECO is a physical-aware optimization tool that can work on the sign-off timing analysis data (Graph Base Analysis or Path Base Analysis) and can provide very accurate slack information. This can make the IR drop fixing more predictive, avoiding several iterations which are otherwise required in manual techniques. In this flow, the IR drop analysis is run using Voltus Rail Analysis, which provides feedback on hotspots to Tempus-ECO to make the ECO changes and to reduce the IR drop. Rail Analysis will output a binary database containing the IR drop information, as shown below:
The IR drop hotspots will be fixed by sizing logic cells in the timing-aware mode, as shown below:
IR-aware ECO Flow
The following diagram illustrates the combined setup and steps to perform the Tempus IR-aware ECO flow:
The flow has the following steps:

1. Perform static timing analysis.
2. Set up power and rail analysis modes and environment using the following commands: 
 set_power_output_dir dir_name 
 set_power_analysis_mode ... 
 set_default_switching_activity ... 
 set_dynamic_power_simulation ... 
 set_rail_analysis_mode ... 
 set_pg_nets ... 
 set_rail_analysis_domain ... 
 set_power_pads ...
3. Perform power and rail analysis using the following commands: 
 report_power 
 analyze_rail
4. Run Hotspot Debugger to recognize hotspots using the following command: 
 debug_irdrop
5. Perform Tempus ECO analysis using the following commands: 
 set_eco_opt_mode 
 eco_opt_design 
 The ECO flow generates the eco_innovus.tcl file.
6. Run rail analysis again to re-check the result after Tempus ECO. 
 To perform post-ECO rail analysis: 
 OR 
 Run rail analysis in Voltus. In this case, use the DEF file from Innovus to run post-ECO rail analysis within Voltus.
  1. Source the eco_innovus.tcl file in Innovus.
  2. Run rail analysis in Innovus.
Hotspot Debugger and ECO Analysis

Hotspot Debugger ( debug_irdrop ) identifies the hotspots with aggressors that demand high currents. These instances in the hotspot regions are reported by the debug_irdrop command, and later used by Tempus to perform Tempus ECO. This command divides all the hotspots in the design into the specified number of regions, and reports the worst instances in that region. The values of the -nregion and -nworst_instances parameters of the debug_irdrop command are key to get the best results for IR Drop improvement with lesser affect to timing.
The following two commands are used in the Tempus ECO analysis part of the flow:

- set_eco_opt_mode is a command to set the global parameters for ECO. You need to specify these parameters before running the eco_opt_design command.
- eco_opt_design automatically uses these global parameters to run ECO optimization.
The following ECO command parameters can be specified in this flow:

- set_eco_opt_mode -fix_ir_drop {true | false} - fixes IR drop violations.
- set_eco_opt_mode -max_slack <> -max_paths <> -nworst <> - these options are configurable based on need, and are active only when the -retime parameter is set.
- set_eco_opt_mode -load_irdrop_db dir - specifies the directory path where previously generated debug_irdrop files are kept. This directory must be same as specified with the debug_irdrop -output_directory parameter.
- set_eco_opt_mode -allow_multiple_incremental {true | false} - allows to run multiple types of optimization in the incremental mode.
- eco_opt_design -drv - triggers ECO.

Depending on the set_eco_opt_mode settings, the software will either auto-generate the ECO Timing DB, or load the existing one. Then, it will proceed to the timing fixing phase, and finally output an ECO file and detailed diagnostics in the log file.
Rechecking IR Drop and Result Analysis

You need to recheck the IR drop in the design after Tempus ECO to see improvement in IR drop violations. The same activity file and switching scenario that was used during the first iteration should be used in the second iteration.
There are multiple ways to analyze the quality of results before and after Tempus ECO, such as using voltage drop map, checking the number of IR drop violations, and analyzing the timing report.
Check the ivdd map to see hotspots
To view the ivdd map in the GUI:

1. Select Power & Rail Plots option from the Power & Rail menu. 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears.
2. Open the Power & Rail Setup form, accessed by clicking the DB Setup button.
3. Click the Browse button next to the Rail Database field to load the rail data.
4. Click OK .
5. In the Power & Rail Plots form, select the Rail radio button, and select the ivdd rail analysis plot type from the drop-down list to view the ivdd map.
The following two snapshots show the ivdd map before and after running Tempus ECO:
Check the IR drop violations reported in the log file
You can open the log file to check the number of violations reported after running the debug_irdrop command.
The following snippet shows IR drop violations before the Tempus ECO flow:
From the above two snippets, it is observed that this flow has fixed a total 362 IR drop violations.
Check the timing reported in the log file
You can open the log file to check timing quality of results before and after running Tempus ECO. The following snippet shows IR drop violations before and after the Tempus ECO flow:
These two snippets indicate that timing is not degraded.
Check the eco.ird file

You can check the eco.ird file before and after the Tempus ECO flow to compare the IR drop numbers of each instance and to see an impact of the Tempus ECO flow. The eco.ird file is located at <debug_irdrop_output_dir>/eco.ird.

Overview

The charged particles of a conductor in an electric field give up kinetic energy when they collide with atomic ions. The increase in this kinetic energy of the ions manifests itself as heat and a rise in temperature. Wire self-heating or Joule Heating describes a phenomenon where high AC currents flowing through the resistive interconnects causes extreme temperature. The following diagram illustrates signal net experiencing AC current as load capacitance is charged and discharged:

In order to prevent wire self-heating or AC signal electromigration, signal interconnects should be analyzed for their AC current carrying capacity and measured against the AC current limits specified by foundry.

Earlier, the AC current density limits could only be defined in the Technology LEF file, and only root-mean-square (RMS) current limit check was supported. Now, the software supports RMS current limit, peak AC current, and average AC current limit checks. However, for peak AC current and average AC current limits, these limits must be defined in the QRC Technology file. For RMS current limits, you can define them in both the Technology LEF and QRC Technology files. To achieve more accurate results, it is recommended to use the ECSM timing library.
Calculating Irms Waveform
The default Advanced Analysis Engine (AAE) delay calculator uses the internal integration method to calculate Irms.
The following diagram shows the I(t) waveform for a given net:
The equation is:
Where, S = Switching Factor
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
S/Tsw = Feff , which means the effective frequency.
Calculating Ipeak Waveform
The software estimates the peak AC current waveform from the default AAE delay calculator. The peak current is checked against the peak limit ( I_peak_ac_limit ) provided by the foundry as below:
Ipeak_ac_limit = Ipeak_dc_limit/sqrt(r)

To check peak EM violation, the tool compares “ Ipeak_measured ” against “ Ipeak_ac_limit ”. An equivalent alternative is to compare “ Ipeak_measured * sqrt(r) ” against “ Ipeak_ac_limit * sqrt(r) = Ipeak_dc_limit ”. By default, the tool uses the alternative and reports “ Ipeak_measured * sqrt(r) ” as peak current, and “ Ipeak_ac_limit * sqrt(r) ” as peak limit.
ICT EM Model (Old Format Example) :
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 123.12*(w-0.002)
In this old ICT EM file format, " 123.12*(w-0.002) " is in fact Ipeak_dc_limit .
ICT EM Model (New Format Example) :
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 123.12*(w-0.002)/( r ) ^ 0.5
In this new ICT EM file format, " 123.12*(w-0.002)/( r ) ^ 0.5 " is Ipeak_ac_limit , and “ em_peak_with_duty_ratio explicit ” is present in the process header.
The following diagram shows the I(t) waveform for a given net:
Td calculation equation is given below:
Where,
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
Ipeak_measured = abs.max(Ipeak(rising), Ipeak(falling))

According to the DRM from foundry, only signal nets with effective frequency equal or larger than the minimum effective frequency (1MHz by default) will be checked for their peak AC current. If the signal nets have an effective frequency lower than the minimum frequency, the software will issue a warning message stating that the net will be ignored for peak current check. You can use the verify_AC_limit -minPeakFreq parameter to change the minimum effective frequency. If the duty ratio r for a signal net is equal or lower than the minimum duty ratio (0.05 by default), then the minimum duty ratio will be used. You can use the verify_AC_limit -minPeakDutyRatio parameter to change the default value.
Calculating Iavg Waveform

The software uses the default AAE delay calculator to calculate the average current considering the recovery factor. For a rising (positive) and falling (negative) waveform, Iavg is calculated using the following equation:
Iavg = abs. max (rising, falling) - EM Recovery factor * abs. min (rising, falling)

where, the EM recovery factor is defined in the QRC Technology file. You can overwrite this value. The default value of the EM recovery factor is 1, that is, Iavg will be zero. If you do not specify the EM recovery factor, the software will issue a warning message when avg analysis is selected and no EM recovery factor is defined.
For digital CMOS circuits that are charging and discharging a fixed load, by definition, abs.Iavg(rising) = abs.Iavg(falling).
where, S = Switching Factor
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
S/Tsw = Feff, which means the effective frequency
Example:
In this example

- Iavg(rising) = 2mA
- Iavg(falling) = -2mA
- em_recovery_factor = 0.5
Based on the equation, Iavg calculation is as follows:
Calculating Effective Frequency
For clock nets, Tsw is already given in the timing constraints, and S , the switching factor, is always 2.0, so the effective frequency is, Feff = 1/T sw.
For signal nets, there are two choices:

- You can specify the switching factor ( S ) for the signal nets using the verify_AC_limit -toggle parameter. The specified switching factor will be applied to all signal nets and multiplied with the switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) to calculate the effective frequency of the signal net. The default switching factor of 1.0 (100%) signifies that the signal net will switch once at every period of the related clock cycle ( rising + falling ). The switching factor of the clock net will not change with the verify_AC_limit -toggle parameter as it always equals to 2.0 . The switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) for the net is derived from the frequency of the associated clock (fastest clock will be used if more than one is associated) using the integrated static timing analysis engine when running this software. 
 # default switching factor of 100% on data network 
 verify_AC_limit \
-method rms \
-detailed \
-toggle 1.0 \
-report AC.1.0.rpt
- If using the global activity on all nets is too pessimistic or optimistic, you can set the effective frequency ( frequency * activity ) using the TCF, VCD, or FSDB file generated by netlist simulators. Optionally, you can set input and flop activities and propagate them to generate a TCF file as follows: 
 set_default_switching_activity \
-input_activity 0.2 \
-seq_activity 0.15 \
-clock_gates_output 2.0 \
-period 7.0
 
propagate_activity

write_tcf <design>.tcf
 
read_activity_file \
-format TCF \
-set_net_freq true dma_mac.tcf
verify_AC_limit \
-method avg \
-detailed \
-avgRecovery 0.5 \
-useQRCTech \
-use_db_freq \
-report AVG.tcf.rpt
Computing Irms/Ipeak/Iavg for each Routing Segment

For a routed net, the wire widths can taper as they reach the sink pins, and the routing can branch to multiple sinks. In order to avoid false violations, a local Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value must be computed for each wire segment of the net.

The Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value of each wire segment should only be the current required to charge the capacitance of the "downstream" wire segments and sinks. The following diagram illustrates Irms/Ipeak/Iavg calculation on "downstream" wire segments:

In this diagram, a) shows that the wire segment s1 must carry current to charge/discharge the entire net, so the Irms/Ipeak/Iavg at the driver output is the correct value to check for s1. In b), the wire segment s2 only carries current to charge/discharge s2, s4, s5 and the pin-caps of i2 and i3. In c), wire segment s5 only carries current for s5 and the pin-cap of i3.

The total wire capacitance (Cwire) comes from extraction (SPEF), and the pin-capacitance for each instance pin from the timing libraries. The total Irms/Ipeak/Iavg will be calculated using the total wire capacitance (Cwire). The software estimates the "downstream capacitance" that must be charged/discharged through individual wire segments by using the total area of the "downstream wire segments" relative to the total area of the wire plus the pin-caps of the sinks.
In example b) for Irms , the result is:
Cwire(total net) is already known from extraction
Cnet(total net) = Cwire(total net) + Cpin for all the sinks (driver's Cpin is not included)
Irms (total net) is computed from Cnet , the slew rate and frequency is as described earlier
Irms is proportional to Cnet , so we can estimate Irms(s2) as:
Checking the AC Current Limits
You can verify AC current violations on signal nets by using the verify_AC_limit command. verify_AC_limit checks for the following types of AC current violations on signal nets:

- Root-mean-square (RMS) current limit violations ( Irms violations)
- Peak current limit violations ( Ipeak violations)
In addition, verify_AC_limit can be used for average current limit (Iavg) analysis.

AC current limit violations are sometimes also referred as wire self-heat violations. Design Rule Check (DRC) manuals have these current limits to avoid over-heating a signal-net wire with too much AC current. To prevent wire self-heating or AC signal electromigration, signal interconnects should be analyzed for their AC current carrying capacity and measured against the AC current limits specified by foundry.
Use the verify_AC_limit -method parameter to specify the waveform calculation method to be used (rms, peak, or avg).
RMS/Peak/Avg Current Limit Violations

By default, verify_AC_limit only checks for Irms violations. The software calculates the Irms at the driver output and compares it to the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables in the LEF file, which contains the I rms limits for each routing layer.

If the computed Irms for a net exceeds the ACCURRENTDENSITY limit defined in LEF for any of the layers/widths used by the net, an error occurs and a violation marker is attached to the net. Each routing segment is checked separately. If more than one driver exists, the check starts at an arbitrary driver. The layers and widths of pins are not checked for AC current limits.
The software support checks the RMS table for all layers. To get access to the width-dependent syntax, we must add an arbitrary single frequency value. Therefore, a typical table might look like:
The tables are interpreted as piece-wise-linear tables indexed by width and frequency. In the example above, a wire of width 0.15um can carry 10.0 mA/um * .15um = 1.5mA of current.

The Irms limits in the table can also be scaled for other factors like temperature. For example, the Irms limits table should be computed for a specific "maximum allowed temperature change" ( maxTchange ). The I rms limits are normally proportional to the square-root of the maxTchange . Therefore, if the table used a maxTchange of 20 degrees, and you want a maxTchange of 10 degrees, you would use a scale value of sqrt(10/20) = 0.71 .
The software checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions:

- If more than one frequency is given, verify_AC_limit will warn that it is only using width values from the first frequency in the table.
- There must be at least two width values, otherwise verify_AC_limit gives an error message and quits.
- The widths must be increasing in value, and the current limits must be increasing in value, otherwise the software gives an error message and quits.
- If no table exists for a routing layer, a warning message is given, and the limit is assumed to be infinite for that layer.
verify_AC_limit also checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions and takes the following actions:

- If there is no table for a routing layer, the software gives a warning and assumes an infinite limit for the layer.
- If PEAK and AVERAGE tables are present, the software ignores them.

For Irms , signal EM analysis not only supports the rules defined in the technology LEF file, but also the rules defined in the QRC Technology file. But for Ipeak and Iavg , only the rules defined in the QRC Technology file are supported. If no EM rule is defined in the QRC Technology file, the software will stop checking and give an error message.

These rules defined in the QRC Technology file are represented in the form of an equation, as opposed to PWL, which when processed for each wire segment individually results in a more accurate EM limit calculation.

To perform this analysis, you must load the QRC tech file in Voltus using the -qx_tech_file parameter of create_rc_corner or update_rc_corner . verify_AC_limit processes the limits defined as polynomials inside the QRC tech file to determine layer-based AC current density limits. The QRC technology file attached to the current timing view (current RC corner) is used.

The QRC tech file is generated from an ASCII input file called the ICT file. For each layer, the ICT file includes em_model construct that defines AC and DC current density polynomials. Here's a sample em_model construct for a metal layer with current limits as an equation ( EQU ):
The peak, RMS, and average current limits for this layer are computed using the equations for the following three em_jmax_ac models.

- em_jmax_ac_peak : Peak current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_rms : RMS current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_avg : Average current limits for this layer. 
 
Signal AC EM limit depends on transition time ( Td - time between half peaks in current waveforms ) and duty ratio ( r ) in advanced process nodes. Additionally, foundries require to apply a specific duty ratio value regardless of the calculated duty ratio. 
To support this, the following AC peak EM rule should be used:
 em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.5 apply r = 1
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.001 Td < 0.5
 In conditional statements, always use the qualifier before numerical values. For example, use Td >= 0.5 , instead of 0.5 <= Td .
The syntax of EQU for these three em_jmax_ac equations is a numeric expression ending in a newline. The expression follows normal expression syntax with normal precedence rules:

1. exponentiation (^)
2. * or /
3. + or -
The following reserved names (case-insensitive) can be used in the equations to pass in parameters.

- w : Width of the wire being checked (required for metal layers that should be checked)
- deltaT : Delta temperature allowed. Normally only used for RMS limits to specify the max change in temperature allowed (optional).
- jmax_factor : For temperature based derating (optional).
- jmax_lifetime : Maximum years/hours of operation for lifetime derating (optional)
The following built-in functions are supported:

- ^ (exponentiation)
- * / (multiply, divide)
- + - (addition, subtraction)
- ( ) (grouping of expressions for precedence)
- sqrt
- log
Specification of derating for lifetime or hours of operation in ICT:
jmax_lifetime lifetime1scale1 [ lifetime2scale2 .......]

jmax_lifetime provides the ability to set the scaling factor that applies to the current density limits for different lifetimes. Based on the lifetime value that you specify (using the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process section of the ICT file), the appropriate multiplication factor is applied to the nominal current density limit. The unit should be specified in accordance with the setting defined by the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process definition of the ICT file.
Sample extract from the ICT file:
Specification for temperature based derating:
jmax_factor temp1scale1 [ temp2scale2 ...]

jmax_factor is an optional scaling factor that can be used at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature (defined by em_tref in the process definition). The temperature for jmax_factor should be specified in degrees Celsius. Scaling factor is a positive integer: >1 to scale up, <1 to scale down, or 1 for no scale effect.
Sample extract from the ICT file:
Area dependent parameters for via layers:
Area-based EM limits for vias can be defined using a PWL pair of area value. The PWL keyword in the syntax below signifies the use of area based EM limits.
em_jmax_ac_peak [ value | PWL value1area1 | EQU fn(E) ]

For more information on the ICT file, see the EM_Model section in the " Creating the ICT File " chapter of the Quantus Techgen Reference Manual .

For an MMMC design, if you want to choose one view to perform Irms/Ipeak/Iavg check, you need to use the set_default_view command to specify the view name or else the first defined view will be used by default.
Prevention of AC Signal EM Violations

Clock nets are generally the first nets to fail the AC current density limits because they have the highest frequency and net loads. It is impractical to "repair" them later without affecting the clock-skew, so clock tree synthesis should avoid creating AC current limit violation. Therefore, CTS inside Innovus has an optional max_cap value to the CTS specification file. CTS will avoid creating any clock net that exceeds the max_cap limit and therefore avoid any possible AC current limit violation.
Repair of AC Signal EM Violations

In Innovus, a fixACLimitViolation command exists that will repair any violations found by the verify_AC_limit command. It will do this initially by adding a buffer to split up the net load to any net marked with a violation by verify_AC_limit . Note that most users avoid fixing violations on clock network as it will impact clock skew.

Top Scope Signal EM Analysis

During iterative design refinement, Electromigration (EM) analysis is performed on the modules and the top-level logic for a large hierarchical design. For top-level analysis, use the create_top_scope command to perform abstraction of the top-level hierarchical instances for analysis of only the top-level logic instead of running the EM analysis of the fullchip netlist. The command allows you to perform abstraction of all the hierarchical instances in the top-level, or specify the top-level hierarchical instances that should be abstracted. When a hierarchical instance is abstracted, all the internal logic is ignored while retaining the interface paths.
The following diagram illustrates the top scope signal EM flow:
The create_top_scope command must be specified before the read_sdc command. The following is an example script for the top scope signal EM flow:

## Create Top Scope
## Set the process node before EM analysis

When the create_top_scope command is specified, Voltus generates current waveforms for the top-level nets, and nets interfacing with the hierarchical blocks. All the other nets are ignored from the current generation process. The clock network is traced for the design to calculate accurate slews for the nets being driven from inside the block.
Boundary Model Based Signal EM Analysis

The boundary model for a block provides sufficient details of its interface paths to accurately analyze these paths when loading the model into the signal EM analysis of the top-level module. These details include interface netlist, SPEF, and side inputs. Aggressors that are not on the boundary paths are not included in the model, and any coupling capacitance to such nets is grounded. To create the cell boundary model, use the create_cell_signal_em_model command. After the boundary model is generated, it is automatically loaded to the higher-level module.
The following figure shows the top scope with the block boundary model:
Here,

- Red nets indicate signal EM analysis is performed
- Black nets indicate skipped from signal EM analysis
- Yellow logic is removed during boundary model generation
- Full clock propagation such that slew and clock marking are done correctly

In the top-level signal EM run, the boundary model is loaded instead of the full block contents. Boundary models can be loaded for multiple blocks. If you specify a Verilog for a block that already has a boundary model, the specified Verilog will override the netlist in the boundary model. The boundary model should not be overridden with a Verilog definition.

You can specify the 
 create_cell_signal_em_model 
 command before or after running the 
 set_top_module 
 command. The details of the two use models are given below:

- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified before set_top_module , the command will generate the boundary model for a sub-cell and load it. For this use model, the -cell , -dir , -spef , and -verilog parameters must be specified.
- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified after set_top_module , the command will generate the model for the top cell for use in a subsequent top-level run. In this case, the command is issued in a block-level run, and the SPEF should be loaded prior to the create_cell_signal_em_model command. For this use model, the -dir parameter must be specified. Since the design is already loaded, the relevant information is obtained from the current session.

Viewing Signal EM Analysis Results
The signal EM results can be debugged in the context of physical layout using the interactive signal EM analysis plots. To view the signal EM plots:

1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots. 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the signal EM analysis results. 
 
 
 To enable the signal EM GUI display, you must specify the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter. This parameter writes the signal EM data into a database file ( filename.db ) after performing signal EM analysis. This file contains the information required for loading and displaying signal EM data in GUI.
2. Click the DB Setup button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
3. Click the Browse button next to the SEM Database field to load the signal EM database file. You need to load the database file generated by the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter.
4. Click OK .
5. To set the visibility of the signal EM layers, click the Layers/Nets button. 
 The Power & Rail Layers/Nets form appears.
6. In the SemLayers tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the signal EM layers to modify the display of layers.
7. Click OK .
8. Select the Sem radio button and select a signal EM analysis plot type from the drop-down list. 
 
 
 The sem_rms , sem_avg , and sem_peak plots are enabled based on the verify_AC_limit -method command parameter. If you specify “ -method {rms peak avg} ”, then all the three plots are enabled. If you specify “ -method {rms avg} ”, then only I rms and I avg can be plotted in the GUI. 
 The following image displays the sem_avg plot: 
 
 
 The color of each wire segment represents its average current density that was determined after running the verify_AC_limit command.
9. Similarly, select the other types of signal EM plots and analyze the signal EM analysis results.
10. You can query signal wire segment related data, such as net information or current density value, by double-clicking a wire segment. 
 The Attribute Viewer form appears: 
 
 
 The Attribute Viewer form can also be displayed by selecting a wire segment and pressing q .
11. Click Close .

-method {rms peak avg}
 parameter enables the 
sem_rms
,

, and

 plots in the 
Power & Rail Plots
 form

report 
 dma_design.rpt

# display the RMS current density plot

Signal EM Report Format
Sample filename: signal.rpt
Format:
Format Description:
Irms/Limit specifies the RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Irms specifies the actual RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Ipeak/Limit specifies the peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Ipeak specifies the actual peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Iavg/Limit specifies the average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Iavg specifies the actual average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.

Width/CutArea If the wire segment is metal, it is calculated as the actual width perpendicular to the current flow of the wire segment. If the wire segment is via, it is calculated as the area of the cut in the via.

FixWidth/FixCutNum is the width (for metal segment) or cutNum (for via) required to fix the violation. When there is a violation, it is the new width of the metal wire or the new number of cuts of via (drawn area).
Cap is the capacitance of the wire segment.
Layer specifies the metal/via layer name on which the resistor is located.
(X1 Y1 X2 Y2) specifies the coordinates of the resistor.
PathLength of a wire segment is the distance traced from the first pin shape of driver to the center of the wire segment.
Blechlength is the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer.
Current Direction is applicable only to via layers where 'D" denotes down, "U" denotes up.
RMS_V2VFactor is the EM benefit from the via-to-via spacing rule for metal layers.

The charged particles of a conductor in an electric field give up kinetic energy when they collide with atomic ions. The increase in this kinetic energy of the ions manifests itself as heat and a rise in temperature. Wire self-heating or Joule Heating describes a phenomenon where high AC currents flowing through the resistive interconnects causes extreme temperature. The following diagram illustrates signal net experiencing AC current as load capacitance is charged and discharged:

In order to prevent wire self-heating or AC signal electromigration, signal interconnects should be analyzed for their AC current carrying capacity and measured against the AC current limits specified by foundry.

Earlier, the AC current density limits could only be defined in the Technology LEF file, and only root-mean-square (RMS) current limit check was supported. Now, the software supports RMS current limit, peak AC current, and average AC current limit checks. However, for peak AC current and average AC current limits, these limits must be defined in the QRC Technology file. For RMS current limits, you can define them in both the Technology LEF and QRC Technology files. To achieve more accurate results, it is recommended to use the ECSM timing library.
Calculating Irms Waveform
The default Advanced Analysis Engine (AAE) delay calculator uses the internal integration method to calculate Irms.
The following diagram shows the I(t) waveform for a given net:
The equation is:
Where, S = Switching Factor
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
S/Tsw = Feff , which means the effective frequency.
Calculating Ipeak Waveform
The software estimates the peak AC current waveform from the default AAE delay calculator. The peak current is checked against the peak limit ( I_peak_ac_limit ) provided by the foundry as below:
Ipeak_ac_limit = Ipeak_dc_limit/sqrt(r)

To check peak EM violation, the tool compares “ Ipeak_measured ” against “ Ipeak_ac_limit ”. An equivalent alternative is to compare “ Ipeak_measured * sqrt(r) ” against “ Ipeak_ac_limit * sqrt(r) = Ipeak_dc_limit ”. By default, the tool uses the alternative and reports “ Ipeak_measured * sqrt(r) ” as peak current, and “ Ipeak_ac_limit * sqrt(r) ” as peak limit.
ICT EM Model (Old Format Example) :
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 123.12*(w-0.002)
In this old ICT EM file format, " 123.12*(w-0.002) " is in fact Ipeak_dc_limit .
ICT EM Model (New Format Example) :
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 123.12*(w-0.002)/( r ) ^ 0.5
In this new ICT EM file format, " 123.12*(w-0.002)/( r ) ^ 0.5 " is Ipeak_ac_limit , and “ em_peak_with_duty_ratio explicit ” is present in the process header.
The following diagram shows the I(t) waveform for a given net:
Td calculation equation is given below:
Where,
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
Ipeak_measured = abs.max(Ipeak(rising), Ipeak(falling))

According to the DRM from foundry, only signal nets with effective frequency equal or larger than the minimum effective frequency (1MHz by default) will be checked for their peak AC current. If the signal nets have an effective frequency lower than the minimum frequency, the software will issue a warning message stating that the net will be ignored for peak current check. You can use the verify_AC_limit -minPeakFreq parameter to change the minimum effective frequency. If the duty ratio r for a signal net is equal or lower than the minimum duty ratio (0.05 by default), then the minimum duty ratio will be used. You can use the verify_AC_limit -minPeakDutyRatio parameter to change the default value.
Calculating Iavg Waveform

The software uses the default AAE delay calculator to calculate the average current considering the recovery factor. For a rising (positive) and falling (negative) waveform, Iavg is calculated using the following equation:
Iavg = abs. max (rising, falling) - EM Recovery factor * abs. min (rising, falling)

where, the EM recovery factor is defined in the QRC Technology file. You can overwrite this value. The default value of the EM recovery factor is 1, that is, Iavg will be zero. If you do not specify the EM recovery factor, the software will issue a warning message when avg analysis is selected and no EM recovery factor is defined.
For digital CMOS circuits that are charging and discharging a fixed load, by definition, abs.Iavg(rising) = abs.Iavg(falling).
where, S = Switching Factor
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
S/Tsw = Feff, which means the effective frequency
Example:
In this example

- Iavg(rising) = 2mA
- Iavg(falling) = -2mA
- em_recovery_factor = 0.5
Based on the equation, Iavg calculation is as follows:
Calculating Effective Frequency
For clock nets, Tsw is already given in the timing constraints, and S , the switching factor, is always 2.0, so the effective frequency is, Feff = 1/T sw.
For signal nets, there are two choices:

- You can specify the switching factor ( S ) for the signal nets using the verify_AC_limit -toggle parameter. The specified switching factor will be applied to all signal nets and multiplied with the switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) to calculate the effective frequency of the signal net. The default switching factor of 1.0 (100%) signifies that the signal net will switch once at every period of the related clock cycle ( rising + falling ). The switching factor of the clock net will not change with the verify_AC_limit -toggle parameter as it always equals to 2.0 . The switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) for the net is derived from the frequency of the associated clock (fastest clock will be used if more than one is associated) using the integrated static timing analysis engine when running this software. 
 # default switching factor of 100% on data network 
 verify_AC_limit \
-method rms \
-detailed \
-toggle 1.0 \
-report AC.1.0.rpt
- If using the global activity on all nets is too pessimistic or optimistic, you can set the effective frequency ( frequency * activity ) using the TCF, VCD, or FSDB file generated by netlist simulators. Optionally, you can set input and flop activities and propagate them to generate a TCF file as follows: 
 set_default_switching_activity \
-input_activity 0.2 \
-seq_activity 0.15 \
-clock_gates_output 2.0 \
-period 7.0
 
propagate_activity

write_tcf <design>.tcf
 
read_activity_file \
-format TCF \
-set_net_freq true dma_mac.tcf
verify_AC_limit \
-method avg \
-detailed \
-avgRecovery 0.5 \
-useQRCTech \
-use_db_freq \
-report AVG.tcf.rpt
Computing Irms/Ipeak/Iavg for each Routing Segment

For a routed net, the wire widths can taper as they reach the sink pins, and the routing can branch to multiple sinks. In order to avoid false violations, a local Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value must be computed for each wire segment of the net.

The Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value of each wire segment should only be the current required to charge the capacitance of the "downstream" wire segments and sinks. The following diagram illustrates Irms/Ipeak/Iavg calculation on "downstream" wire segments:

In this diagram, a) shows that the wire segment s1 must carry current to charge/discharge the entire net, so the Irms/Ipeak/Iavg at the driver output is the correct value to check for s1. In b), the wire segment s2 only carries current to charge/discharge s2, s4, s5 and the pin-caps of i2 and i3. In c), wire segment s5 only carries current for s5 and the pin-cap of i3.

The total wire capacitance (Cwire) comes from extraction (SPEF), and the pin-capacitance for each instance pin from the timing libraries. The total Irms/Ipeak/Iavg will be calculated using the total wire capacitance (Cwire). The software estimates the "downstream capacitance" that must be charged/discharged through individual wire segments by using the total area of the "downstream wire segments" relative to the total area of the wire plus the pin-caps of the sinks.
In example b) for Irms , the result is:
Cwire(total net) is already known from extraction
Cnet(total net) = Cwire(total net) + Cpin for all the sinks (driver's Cpin is not included)
Irms (total net) is computed from Cnet , the slew rate and frequency is as described earlier
Irms is proportional to Cnet , so we can estimate Irms(s2) as:
Checking the AC Current Limits
You can verify AC current violations on signal nets by using the verify_AC_limit command. verify_AC_limit checks for the following types of AC current violations on signal nets:

- Root-mean-square (RMS) current limit violations ( Irms violations)
- Peak current limit violations ( Ipeak violations)
In addition, verify_AC_limit can be used for average current limit (Iavg) analysis.

AC current limit violations are sometimes also referred as wire self-heat violations. Design Rule Check (DRC) manuals have these current limits to avoid over-heating a signal-net wire with too much AC current. To prevent wire self-heating or AC signal electromigration, signal interconnects should be analyzed for their AC current carrying capacity and measured against the AC current limits specified by foundry.
Use the verify_AC_limit -method parameter to specify the waveform calculation method to be used (rms, peak, or avg).
RMS/Peak/Avg Current Limit Violations

Therefore, if the table used a maxTchange of 20 degrees, and you want a maxTchange of 10 degrees, you would use a scale value of sqrt(10/20) = 0.71 .
The software checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions:

- If more than one frequency is given, verify_AC_limit will warn that it is only using width values from the first frequency in the table.
- There must be at least two width values, otherwise verify_AC_limit gives an error message and quits.
- The widths must be increasing in value, and the current limits must be increasing in value, otherwise the software gives an error message and quits.
- If no table exists for a routing layer, a warning message is given, and the limit is assumed to be infinite for that layer.
verify_AC_limit also checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions and takes the following actions:

- If there is no table for a routing layer, the software gives a warning and assumes an infinite limit for the layer.
- If PEAK and AVERAGE tables are present, the software ignores them.

jmax_lifetime lifetime1scale1 [ lifetime2scale2 .......]

jmax_lifetime provides the ability to set the scaling factor that applies to the current density limits for different lifetimes. Based on the lifetime value that you specify (using the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process section of the ICT file), the appropriate multiplication factor is applied to the nominal current density limit. The unit should be specified in accordance with the setting defined by the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process definition of the ICT file.

Specification for temperature based derating:
jmax_factor temp1scale1 [ temp2scale2 ...]

jmax_factor is an optional scaling factor that can be used at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature (defined by em_tref in the process definition). The temperature for jmax_factor should be specified in degrees Celsius. Scaling factor is a positive integer: >1 to scale up, <1 to scale down, or 1 for no scale effect.

Area-based EM limits for vias can be defined using a PWL pair of area value. The PWL keyword in the syntax below signifies the use of area based EM limits.
em_jmax_ac_peak [ value | PWL value1area1 | EQU fn(E) ]

For an MMMC design, if you want to choose one view to perform Irms/Ipeak/Iavg check, you need to use the set_default_view command to specify the view name or else the first defined view will be used by default.
Prevention of AC Signal EM Violations

Clock nets are generally the first nets to fail the AC current density limits because they have the highest frequency and net loads. It is impractical to "repair" them later without affecting the clock-skew, so clock tree synthesis should avoid creating AC current limit violation. Therefore, CTS inside Innovus has an optional max_cap value to the CTS specification file. CTS will avoid creating any clock net that exceeds the max_cap limit and therefore avoid any possible AC current limit violation.
Repair of AC Signal EM Violations

In Innovus, a fixACLimitViolation command exists that will repair any violations found by the verify_AC_limit command. It will do this initially by adding a buffer to split up the net load to any net marked with a violation by verify_AC_limit . Note that most users avoid fixing violations on clock network as it will impact clock skew.

Top Scope Signal EM Analysis

During iterative design refinement, Electromigration (EM) analysis is performed on the modules and the top-level logic for a large hierarchical design. For top-level analysis, use the create_top_scope command to perform abstraction of the top-level hierarchical instances for analysis of only the top-level logic instead of running the EM analysis of the fullchip netlist. The command allows you to perform abstraction of all the hierarchical instances in the top-level, or specify the top-level hierarchical instances that should be abstracted. When a hierarchical instance is abstracted, all the internal logic is ignored while retaining the interface paths.
The following diagram illustrates the top scope signal EM flow:
The create_top_scope command must be specified before the read_sdc command. The following is an example script for the top scope signal EM flow:

## Create Top Scope
## Set the process node before EM analysis

When the create_top_scope command is specified, Voltus generates current waveforms for the top-level nets, and nets interfacing with the hierarchical blocks. All the other nets are ignored from the current generation process. The clock network is traced for the design to calculate accurate slews for the nets being driven from inside the block.
Boundary Model Based Signal EM Analysis

The boundary model for a block provides sufficient details of its interface paths to accurately analyze these paths when loading the model into the signal EM analysis of the top-level module. These details include interface netlist, SPEF, and side inputs. Aggressors that are not on the boundary paths are not included in the model, and any coupling capacitance to such nets is grounded. To create the cell boundary model, use the create_cell_signal_em_model command. After the boundary model is generated, it is automatically loaded to the higher-level module.
The following figure shows the top scope with the block boundary model:
Here,

- Red nets indicate signal EM analysis is performed
- Black nets indicate skipped from signal EM analysis
- Yellow logic is removed during boundary model generation
- Full clock propagation such that slew and clock marking are done correctly

In the top-level signal EM run, the boundary model is loaded instead of the full block contents. Boundary models can be loaded for multiple blocks. If you specify a Verilog for a block that already has a boundary model, the specified Verilog will override the netlist in the boundary model. The boundary model should not be overridden with a Verilog definition.

You can specify the 
 create_cell_signal_em_model 
 command before or after running the 
 set_top_module 
 command. The details of the two use models are given below:

- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified before set_top_module , the command will generate the boundary model for a sub-cell and load it. For this use model, the -cell , -dir , -spef , and -verilog parameters must be specified.
- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified after set_top_module , the command will generate the model for the top cell for use in a subsequent top-level run. In this case, the command is issued in a block-level run, and the SPEF should be loaded prior to the create_cell_signal_em_model command. For this use model, the -dir parameter must be specified. Since the design is already loaded, the relevant information is obtained from the current session.

Viewing Signal EM Analysis Results
The signal EM results can be debugged in the context of physical layout using the interactive signal EM analysis plots. To view the signal EM plots:

1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots. 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the signal EM analysis results. 
 
 
 To enable the signal EM GUI display, you must specify the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter. This parameter writes the signal EM data into a database file ( filename.db ) after performing signal EM analysis. This file contains the information required for loading and displaying signal EM data in GUI.
2. Click the DB Setup button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
3. Click the Browse button next to the SEM Database field to load the signal EM database file. You need to load the database file generated by the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter.
4. Click OK .
5. To set the visibility of the signal EM layers, click the Layers/Nets button. 
 The Power & Rail Layers/Nets form appears.
6. In the SemLayers tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the signal EM layers to modify the display of layers.
7. Click OK .
8. Select the Sem radio button and select a signal EM analysis plot type from the drop-down list. 
 
 
 The sem_rms , sem_avg , and sem_peak plots are enabled based on the verify_AC_limit -method command parameter. If you specify “ -method {rms peak avg} ”, then all the three plots are enabled. If you specify “ -method {rms avg} ”, then only I rms and I avg can be plotted in the GUI. 
 The following image displays the sem_avg plot: 
 
 
 The color of each wire segment represents its average current density that was determined after running the verify_AC_limit command.
9. Similarly, select the other types of signal EM plots and analyze the signal EM analysis results.
10. You can query signal wire segment related data, such as net information or current density value, by double-clicking a wire segment. 
 The Attribute Viewer form appears: 
 
 
 The Attribute Viewer form can also be displayed by selecting a wire segment and pressing q .
11. Click Close .

read_verilog <verilog netlist>
set_top_module <design name>
read_def <def netlist>
