-method {rms peak avg}
 parameter enables the 
sem_rms
, 
sem_avg
, and 
sem_peak
 plots in the 
Power & Rail Plots
 form
verify_AC_limit 
 -method {rms peak avg} -detailed -report_db -
report 
 dma_design.rpt

# display the RMS current density plot 
set_power_rail_display -plot sem_rms
Signal EM Report Format
Sample filename: signal.rpt
Format:
Format Description:
Irms/Limit specifies the RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Irms specifies the actual RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Ipeak/Limit specifies the peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Ipeak specifies the actual peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Iavg/Limit specifies the average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Iavg specifies the actual average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.

Width/CutArea If the wire segment is metal, it is calculated as the actual width perpendicular to the current flow of the wire segment. If the wire segment is via, it is calculated as the area of the cut in the via.

FixWidth/FixCutNum is the width (for metal segment) or cutNum (for via) required to fix the violation. When there is a violation, it is the new width of the metal wire or the new number of cuts of via (drawn area).
Cap is the capacitance of the wire segment.
Layer specifies the metal/via layer name on which the resistor is located.
(X1 Y1 X2 Y2) specifies the coordinates of the resistor.
PathLength of a wire segment is the distance traced from the first pin shape of driver to the center of the wire segment.
Blechlength is the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer.
Current Direction is applicable only to via layers where 'D" denotes down, "U" denotes up.
RMS_V2VFactor is the EM benefit from the via-to-via spacing rule for metal layers.
The default Advanced Analysis Engine (AAE) delay calculator uses the internal integration method to calculate Irms.
The following diagram shows the I(t) waveform for a given net:
The equation is:
Where, S = Switching Factor
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
S/Tsw = Feff , which means the effective frequency.
Calculating Ipeak Waveform
The software estimates the peak AC current waveform from the default AAE delay calculator. The peak current is checked against the peak limit ( I_peak_ac_limit ) provided by the foundry as below:
Ipeak_ac_limit = Ipeak_dc_limit/sqrt(r)

To check peak EM violation, the tool compares “ Ipeak_measured ” against “ Ipeak_ac_limit ”. An equivalent alternative is to compare “ Ipeak_measured * sqrt(r) ” against “ Ipeak_ac_limit * sqrt(r) = Ipeak_dc_limit ”. By default, the tool uses the alternative and reports “ Ipeak_measured * sqrt(r) ” as peak current, and “ Ipeak_ac_limit * sqrt(r) ” as peak limit.
ICT EM Model (Old Format Example) :
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 123.12*(w-0.002)
In this old ICT EM file format, " 123.12*(w-0.002) " is in fact Ipeak_dc_limit .
ICT EM Model (New Format Example) :
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 123.12*(w-0.002)/( r ) ^ 0.5
In this new ICT EM file format, " 123.12*(w-0.002)/( r ) ^ 0.5 " is Ipeak_ac_limit , and “ em_peak_with_duty_ratio explicit ” is present in the process header.
The following diagram shows the I(t) waveform for a given net:
Td calculation equation is given below:
Where,
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
Ipeak_measured = abs.max(Ipeak(rising), Ipeak(falling))

According to the DRM from foundry, only signal nets with effective frequency equal or larger than the minimum effective frequency (1MHz by default) will be checked for their peak AC current. If the signal nets have an effective frequency lower than the minimum frequency, the software will issue a warning message stating that the net will be ignored for peak current check. You can use the verify_AC_limit -minPeakFreq parameter to change the minimum effective frequency. If the duty ratio r for a signal net is equal or lower than the minimum duty ratio (0.05 by default), then the minimum duty ratio will be used. You can use the verify_AC_limit -minPeakDutyRatio parameter to change the default value.
Calculating Iavg Waveform

The software uses the default AAE delay calculator to calculate the average current considering the recovery factor. For a rising (positive) and falling (negative) waveform, Iavg is calculated using the following equation:
Iavg = abs. max (rising, falling) - EM Recovery factor * abs. min (rising, falling)

where, the EM recovery factor is defined in the QRC Technology file. You can overwrite this value. The default value of the EM recovery factor is 1, that is, Iavg will be zero. If you do not specify the EM recovery factor, the software will issue a warning message when avg analysis is selected and no EM recovery factor is defined.
For digital CMOS circuits that are charging and discharging a fixed load, by definition, abs.Iavg(rising) = abs.Iavg(falling).
where, S = Switching Factor
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
S/Tsw = Feff, which means the effective frequency
Example:
In this example

- Iavg(rising) = 2mA
- Iavg(falling) = -2mA
- em_recovery_factor = 0.5
Based on the equation, Iavg calculation is as follows:
Calculating Effective Frequency
For clock nets, Tsw is already given in the timing constraints, and S , the switching factor, is always 2.0, so the effective frequency is, Feff = 1/T sw.
For signal nets, there are two choices:

- You can specify the switching factor ( S ) for the signal nets using the verify_AC_limit -toggle parameter. The specified switching factor will be applied to all signal nets and multiplied with the switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) to calculate the effective frequency of the signal net. The default switching factor of 1.0 (100%) signifies that the signal net will switch once at every period of the related clock cycle ( rising + falling ). The switching factor of the clock net will not change with the verify_AC_limit -toggle parameter as it always equals to 2.0 . The switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) for the net is derived from the frequency of the associated clock (fastest clock will be used if more than one is associated) using the integrated static timing analysis engine when running this software. 
 # default switching factor of 100% on data network 
 verify_AC_limit \
-method rms \
-detailed \
-toggle 1.0 \
-report AC.1.0.rpt
- If using the global activity on all nets is too pessimistic or optimistic, you can set the effective frequency ( frequency * activity ) using the TCF, VCD, or FSDB file generated by netlist simulators. Optionally, you can set input and flop activities and propagate them to generate a TCF file as follows: 
 set_default_switching_activity \
-input_activity 0.2 \
-seq_activity 0.15 \
-clock_gates_output 2.0 \
-period 7.0
 
propagate_activity

write_tcf <design>.tcf
 
read_activity_file \
-format TCF \
-set_net_freq true dma_mac.tcf
verify_AC_limit \
-method avg \
-detailed \
-avgRecovery 0.5 \
-useQRCTech \
-use_db_freq \
-report AVG.tcf.rpt
Computing Irms/Ipeak/Iavg for each Routing Segment

For a routed net, the wire widths can taper as they reach the sink pins, and the routing can branch to multiple sinks. In order to avoid false violations, a local Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value must be computed for each wire segment of the net.

The Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value of each wire segment should only be the current required to charge the capacitance of the "downstream" wire segments and sinks. The following diagram illustrates Irms/Ipeak/Iavg calculation on "downstream" wire segments:

In this diagram, a) shows that the wire segment s1 must carry current to charge/discharge the entire net, so the Irms/Ipeak/Iavg at the driver output is the correct value to check for s1. In b), the wire segment s2 only carries current to charge/discharge s2, s4, s5 and the pin-caps of i2 and i3. In c), wire segment s5 only carries current for s5 and the pin-cap of i3.

The total wire capacitance (Cwire) comes from extraction (SPEF), and the pin-capacitance for each instance pin from the timing libraries. The total Irms/Ipeak/Iavg will be calculated using the total wire capacitance (Cwire). The software estimates the "downstream capacitance" that must be charged/discharged through individual wire segments by using the total area of the "downstream wire segments" relative to the total area of the wire plus the pin-caps of the sinks.
In example b) for Irms , the result is:
Cwire(total net) is already known from extraction
Cnet(total net) = Cwire(total net) + Cpin for all the sinks (driver's Cpin is not included)
Irms (total net) is computed from Cnet , the slew rate and frequency is as described earlier
Irms is proportional to Cnet , so we can estimate Irms(s2) as:
Checking the AC Current Limits
You can verify AC current violations on signal nets by using the verify_AC_limit command. verify_AC_limit checks for the following types of AC current violations on signal nets:

- Root-mean-square (RMS) current limit violations ( Irms violations)
- Peak current limit violations ( Ipeak violations)
In addition, verify_AC_limit can be used for average current limit (Iavg) analysis.

AC current limit violations are sometimes also referred as wire self-heat violations. Design Rule Check (DRC) manuals have these current limits to avoid over-heating a signal-net wire with too much AC current. To prevent wire self-heating or AC signal electromigration, signal interconnects should be analyzed for their AC current carrying capacity and measured against the AC current limits specified by foundry.
Use the verify_AC_limit -method parameter to specify the waveform calculation method to be used (rms, peak, or avg).
RMS/Peak/Avg Current Limit Violations

By default, verify_AC_limit only checks for Irms violations. The software calculates the Irms at the driver output and compares it to the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables in the LEF file, which contains the I rms limits for each routing layer.

If the computed Irms for a net exceeds the ACCURRENTDENSITY limit defined in LEF for any of the layers/widths used by the net, an error occurs and a violation marker is attached to the net. Each routing segment is checked separately. If more than one driver exists, the check starts at an arbitrary driver. The layers and widths of pins are not checked for AC current limits.
The software support checks the RMS table for all layers. To get access to the width-dependent syntax, we must add an arbitrary single frequency value. Therefore, a typical table might look like:
The tables are interpreted as piece-wise-linear tables indexed by width and frequency. In the example above, a wire of width 0.15um can carry 10.0 mA/um * .15um = 1.5mA of current.

The Irms limits in the table can also be scaled for other factors like temperature. For example, the Irms limits table should be computed for a specific "maximum allowed temperature change" ( maxTchange ). The I rms limits are normally proportional to the square-root of the maxTchange . Therefore, if the table used a maxTchange of 20 degrees, and you want a maxTchange of 10 degrees, you would use a scale value of sqrt(10/20) = 0.71 .
The software checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions:

- If more than one frequency is given, verify_AC_limit will warn that it is only using width values from the first frequency in the table.
- There must be at least two width values, otherwise verify_AC_limit gives an error message and quits.
- The widths must be increasing in value, and the current limits must be increasing in value, otherwise the software gives an error message and quits.
- If no table exists for a routing layer, a warning message is given, and the limit is assumed to be infinite for that layer.
verify_AC_limit also checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions and takes the following actions:

- If there is no table for a routing layer, the software gives a warning and assumes an infinite limit for the layer.
- If PEAK and AVERAGE tables are present, the software ignores them.

For Irms , signal EM analysis not only supports the rules defined in the technology LEF file, but also the rules defined in the QRC Technology file. But for Ipeak and Iavg , only the rules defined in the QRC Technology file are supported. If no EM rule is defined in the QRC Technology file, the software will stop checking and give an error message.

These rules defined in the QRC Technology file are represented in the form of an equation, as opposed to PWL, which when processed for each wire segment individually results in a more accurate EM limit calculation.

To perform this analysis, you must load the QRC tech file in Voltus using the -qx_tech_file parameter of create_rc_corner or update_rc_corner . verify_AC_limit processes the limits defined as polynomials inside the QRC tech file to determine layer-based AC current density limits. The QRC technology file attached to the current timing view (current RC corner) is used.

The QRC tech file is generated from an ASCII input file called the ICT file. For each layer, the ICT file includes em_model construct that defines AC and DC current density polynomials. Here's a sample em_model construct for a metal layer with current limits as an equation ( EQU ):
The peak, RMS, and average current limits for this layer are computed using the equations for the following three em_jmax_ac models.

- em_jmax_ac_peak : Peak current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_rms : RMS current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_avg : Average current limits for this layer. 
 
Signal AC EM limit depends on transition time ( Td - time between half peaks in current waveforms ) and duty ratio ( r ) in advanced process nodes. Additionally, foundries require to apply a specific duty ratio value regardless of the calculated duty ratio. 
To support this, the following AC peak EM rule should be used:
 em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.5 apply r = 1
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.001 Td < 0.5
 In conditional statements, always use the qualifier before numerical values. For example, use Td >= 0.5 , instead of 0.5 <= Td .
The syntax of EQU for these three em_jmax_ac equations is a numeric expression ending in a newline. The expression follows normal expression syntax with normal precedence rules:

1. exponentiation (^)
2. * or /
3. + or -
The following reserved names (case-insensitive) can be used in the equations to pass in parameters.

- w : Width of the wire being checked (required for metal layers that should be checked)
- deltaT : Delta temperature allowed. Normally only used for RMS limits to specify the max change in temperature allowed (optional).
- jmax_factor : For temperature based derating (optional).
- jmax_lifetime : Maximum years/hours of operation for lifetime derating (optional)
The following built-in functions are supported:

- ^ (exponentiation)
- * / (multiply, divide)
- + - (addition, subtraction)
- ( ) (grouping of expressions for precedence)
- sqrt
- log
Specification of derating for lifetime or hours of operation in ICT:
jmax_lifetime lifetime1scale1 [ lifetime2scale2 .......]

jmax_lifetime provides the ability to set the scaling factor that applies to the current density limits for different lifetimes. Based on the lifetime value that you specify (using the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process section of the ICT file), the appropriate multiplication factor is applied to the nominal current density limit. The unit should be specified in accordance with the setting defined by the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process definition of the ICT file.

Specification for temperature based derating:
jmax_factor temp1scale1 [ temp2scale2 ...]

jmax_factor is an optional scaling factor that can be used at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature (defined by em_tref in the process definition). The temperature for jmax_factor should be specified in degrees Celsius. Scaling factor is a positive integer: >1 to scale up, <1 to scale down, or 1 for no scale effect.

Area-based EM limits for vias can be defined using a PWL pair of area value. The PWL keyword in the syntax below signifies the use of area based EM limits.

Viewing Signal EM Analysis Results
The signal EM results can be debugged in the context of physical layout using the interactive signal EM analysis plots. To view the signal EM plots:

1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots. 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the signal EM analysis results. 
 
 
 To enable the signal EM GUI display, you must specify the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter. This parameter writes the signal EM data into a database file ( filename.db ) after performing signal EM analysis. This file contains the information required for loading and displaying signal EM data in GUI.
2. Click the DB Setup button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
3. Click the Browse button next to the SEM Database field to load the signal EM database file. You need to load the database file generated by the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter.
4. Click OK .
5. To set the visibility of the signal EM layers, click the Layers/Nets button. 
 The Power & Rail Layers/Nets form appears.
6. In the SemLayers tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the signal EM layers to modify the display of layers.
7. Click OK .
8. Select the Sem radio button and select a signal EM analysis plot type from the drop-down list. 
 
 
 The sem_rms , sem_avg , and sem_peak plots are enabled based on the verify_AC_limit -method command parameter. If you specify “ -method {rms peak avg} ”, then all the three plots are enabled. If you specify “ -method {rms avg} ”, then only I rms and I avg can be plotted in the GUI. 
 The following image displays the sem_avg plot: 
 
 
 The color of each wire segment represents its average current density that was determined after running the verify_AC_limit command.
9. Similarly, select the other types of signal EM plots and analyze the signal EM analysis results.
10. You can query signal wire segment related data, such as net information or current density value, by double-clicking a wire segment. 
 The Attribute Viewer form appears: 
 
 
 The Attribute Viewer form can also be displayed by selecting a wire segment and pressing q .
11. Click Close .
Example Script for Displaying the Signal EM Plot in GUI

# read design 
read_lib -lef <lefs>
read_lib <timing libs>
read_verilog <verilog netlist>
set_top_module <design name>
read_def <def netlist>

# write the signal EM data into a database file. The 
-method {rms peak avg}
 parameter enables the 
sem_rms
, 
sem_avg
, and 
sem_peak
 plots in the 
Power & Rail Plots
 form
verify_AC_limit

report 
 dma_design.rpt

# display the RMS current density plot 
set_power_rail_display -plot sem_rms
Signal EM Report Format
Sample filename: signal.rpt
Format:
Format Description:
Irms/Limit specifies the RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Irms specifies the actual RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Ipeak/Limit specifies the peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Ipeak specifies the actual peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Iavg/Limit specifies the average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Iavg specifies the actual average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.

Width/CutArea If the wire segment is metal, it is calculated as the actual width perpendicular to the current flow of the wire segment. If the wire segment is via, it is calculated as the area of the cut in the via.

FixWidth/FixCutNum is the width (for metal segment) or cutNum (for via) required to fix the violation. When there is a violation, it is the new width of the metal wire or the new number of cuts of via (drawn area).
Cap is the capacitance of the wire segment.
Layer specifies the metal/via layer name on which the resistor is located.
(X1 Y1 X2 Y2) specifies the coordinates of the resistor.
PathLength of a wire segment is the distance traced from the first pin shape of driver to the center of the wire segment.
Blechlength is the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer.
Current Direction is applicable only to via layers where 'D" denotes down, "U" denotes up.
RMS_V2VFactor is the EM benefit from the via-to-via spacing rule for metal layers.
The software estimates the peak AC current waveform from the default AAE delay calculator. The peak current is checked against the peak limit ( I_peak_ac_limit ) provided by the foundry as below:
Ipeak_ac_limit = Ipeak_dc_limit/sqrt(r)

To check peak EM violation, the tool compares “ Ipeak_measured ” against “ Ipeak_ac_limit ”. An equivalent alternative is to compare “ Ipeak_measured * sqrt(r) ” against “ Ipeak_ac_limit * sqrt(r) = Ipeak_dc_limit ”. By default, the tool uses the alternative and reports “ Ipeak_measured * sqrt(r) ” as peak current, and “ Ipeak_ac_limit * sqrt(r) ” as peak limit.
ICT EM Model (Old Format Example) :
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 123.12*(w-0.002)
In this old ICT EM file format, " 123.12*(w-0.002) " is in fact Ipeak_dc_limit .
ICT EM Model (New Format Example) :
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 123.12*(w-0.002)/( r ) ^ 0.5
In this new ICT EM file format, " 123.12*(w-0.002)/( r ) ^ 0.5 " is Ipeak_ac_limit , and “ em_peak_with_duty_ratio explicit ” is present in the process header.
The following diagram shows the I(t) waveform for a given net:
Td calculation equation is given below:
Where,
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
Ipeak_measured = abs.max(Ipeak(rising), Ipeak(falling))

According to the DRM from foundry, only signal nets with effective frequency equal or larger than the minimum effective frequency (1MHz by default) will be checked for their peak AC current. If the signal nets have an effective frequency lower than the minimum frequency, the software will issue a warning message stating that the net will be ignored for peak current check. You can use the verify_AC_limit -minPeakFreq parameter to change the minimum effective frequency. If the duty ratio r for a signal net is equal or lower than the minimum duty ratio (0.05 by default), then the minimum duty ratio will be used. You can use the verify_AC_limit -minPeakDutyRatio parameter to change the default value.
Calculating Iavg Waveform

The software uses the default AAE delay calculator to calculate the average current considering the recovery factor. For a rising (positive) and falling (negative) waveform, Iavg is calculated using the following equation:
Iavg = abs. max (rising, falling) - EM Recovery factor * abs. min (rising, falling)

where, the EM recovery factor is defined in the QRC Technology file. You can overwrite this value. The default value of the EM recovery factor is 1, that is, Iavg will be zero. If you do not specify the EM recovery factor, the software will issue a warning message when avg analysis is selected and no EM recovery factor is defined.
For digital CMOS circuits that are charging and discharging a fixed load, by definition, abs.Iavg(rising) = abs.Iavg(falling).
where, S = Switching Factor
Tsw = Period of one switching cycle (rising + falling)
S/Tsw = Feff, which means the effective frequency
Example:
In this example

- Iavg(rising) = 2mA
- Iavg(falling) = -2mA
- em_recovery_factor = 0.5
Based on the equation, Iavg calculation is as follows:
Calculating Effective Frequency
For clock nets, Tsw is already given in the timing constraints, and S , the switching factor, is always 2.0, so the effective frequency is, Feff = 1/T sw.
For signal nets, there are two choices:

- You can specify the switching factor ( S ) for the signal nets using the verify_AC_limit -toggle parameter. The specified switching factor will be applied to all signal nets and multiplied with the switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) to calculate the effective frequency of the signal net. The default switching factor of 1.0 (100%) signifies that the signal net will switch once at every period of the related clock cycle ( rising + falling ). The switching factor of the clock net will not change with the verify_AC_limit -toggle parameter as it always equals to 2.0 . The switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) for the net is derived from the frequency of the associated clock (fastest clock will be used if more than one is associated) using the integrated static timing analysis engine when running this software. 
 # default switching factor of 100% on data network 
 verify_AC_limit \
-method rms \
-detailed \
-toggle 1.0 \
-report AC.1.0.rpt
- If using the global activity on all nets is too pessimistic or optimistic, you can set the effective frequency ( frequency * activity ) using the TCF, VCD, or FSDB file generated by netlist simulators. Optionally, you can set input and flop activities and propagate them to generate a TCF file as follows: 
 set_default_switching_activity \
-input_activity 0.2 \
-seq_activity 0.15 \
-clock_gates_output 2.0 \
-period 7.0
 
propagate_activity

write_tcf <design>.tcf
 
read_activity_file \
-format TCF \
-set_net_freq true dma_mac.tcf
verify_AC_limit \
-method avg \
-detailed \
-avgRecovery 0.5 \
-useQRCTech \
-use_db_freq \
-report AVG.tcf.rpt
Computing Irms/Ipeak/Iavg for each Routing Segment

For a routed net, the wire widths can taper as they reach the sink pins, and the routing can branch to multiple sinks. In order to avoid false violations, a local Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value must be computed for each wire segment of the net.

The Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value of each wire segment should only be the current required to charge the capacitance of the "downstream" wire segments and sinks. The following diagram illustrates Irms/Ipeak/Iavg calculation on "downstream" wire segments:

In this diagram, a) shows that the wire segment s1 must carry current to charge/discharge the entire net, so the Irms/Ipeak/Iavg at the driver output is the correct value to check for s1. In b), the wire segment s2 only carries current to charge/discharge s2, s4, s5 and the pin-caps of i2 and i3. In c), wire segment s5 only carries current for s5 and the pin-cap of i3.

The total wire capacitance (Cwire) comes from extraction (SPEF), and the pin-capacitance for each instance pin from the timing libraries. The total Irms/Ipeak/Iavg will be calculated using the total wire capacitance (Cwire). The software estimates the "downstream capacitance" that must be charged/discharged through individual wire segments by using the total area of the "downstream wire segments" relative to the total area of the wire plus the pin-caps of the sinks.
In example b) for Irms , the result is:
Cwire(total net) is already known from extraction
Cnet(total net) = Cwire(total net) + Cpin for all the sinks (driver's Cpin is not included)
Irms (total net) is computed from Cnet , the slew rate and frequency is as described earlier
Irms is proportional to Cnet , so we can estimate Irms(s2) as:
Checking the AC Current Limits
You can verify AC current violations on signal nets by using the verify_AC_limit command. verify_AC_limit checks for the following types of AC current violations on signal nets:

- Root-mean-square (RMS) current limit violations ( Irms violations)
- Peak current limit violations ( Ipeak violations)
In addition, verify_AC_limit can be used for average current limit (Iavg) analysis.

AC current limit violations are sometimes also referred as wire self-heat violations. Design Rule Check (DRC) manuals have these current limits to avoid over-heating a signal-net wire with too much AC current. To prevent wire self-heating or AC signal electromigration, signal interconnects should be analyzed for their AC current carrying capacity and measured against the AC current limits specified by foundry.
Use the verify_AC_limit -method parameter to specify the waveform calculation method to be used (rms, peak, or avg).
RMS/Peak/Avg Current Limit Violations

By default, verify_AC_limit only checks for Irms violations. The software calculates the Irms at the driver output and compares it to the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables in the LEF file, which contains the I rms limits for each routing layer.

If the computed Irms for a net exceeds the ACCURRENTDENSITY limit defined in LEF for any of the layers/widths used by the net, an error occurs and a violation marker is attached to the net. Each routing segment is checked separately. If more than one driver exists, the check starts at an arbitrary driver. The layers and widths of pins are not checked for AC current limits.
The software support checks the RMS table for all layers. To get access to the width-dependent syntax, we must add an arbitrary single frequency value. Therefore, a typical table might look like:
The tables are interpreted as piece-wise-linear tables indexed by width and frequency. In the example above, a wire of width 0.15um can carry 10.0 mA/um * .15um = 1.5mA of current.

The Irms limits in the table can also be scaled for other factors like temperature. For example, the Irms limits table should be computed for a specific "maximum allowed temperature change" ( maxTchange ). The I rms limits are normally proportional to the square-root of the maxTchange . Therefore, if the table used a maxTchange of 20 degrees, and you want a maxTchange of 10 degrees, you would use a scale value of sqrt(10/20) = 0.71 .
The software checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions:

- If more than one frequency is given, verify_AC_limit will warn that it is only using width values from the first frequency in the table.
- There must be at least two width values, otherwise verify_AC_limit gives an error message and quits.
- The widths must be increasing in value, and the current limits must be increasing in value, otherwise the software gives an error message and quits.
- If no table exists for a routing layer, a warning message is given, and the limit is assumed to be infinite for that layer.
verify_AC_limit also checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions and takes the following actions:

- If there is no table for a routing layer, the software gives a warning and assumes an infinite limit for the layer.
- If PEAK and AVERAGE tables are present, the software ignores them.

For Irms , signal EM analysis not only supports the rules defined in the technology LEF file, but also the rules defined in the QRC Technology file. But for Ipeak and Iavg , only the rules defined in the QRC Technology file are supported. If no EM rule is defined in the QRC Technology file, the software will stop checking and give an error message.

These rules defined in the QRC Technology file are represented in the form of an equation, as opposed to PWL, which when processed for each wire segment individually results in a more accurate EM limit calculation.

To perform this analysis, you must load the QRC tech file in Voltus using the -qx_tech_file parameter of create_rc_corner or update_rc_corner . verify_AC_limit processes the limits defined as polynomials inside the QRC tech file to determine layer-based AC current density limits. The QRC technology file attached to the current timing view (current RC corner) is used.

The QRC tech file is generated from an ASCII input file called the ICT file. For each layer, the ICT file includes em_model construct that defines AC and DC current density polynomials. Here's a sample em_model construct for a metal layer with current limits as an equation ( EQU ):
The peak, RMS, and average current limits for this layer are computed using the equations for the following three em_jmax_ac models.

- em_jmax_ac_peak : Peak current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_rms : RMS current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_avg : Average current limits for this layer. 
 
Signal AC EM limit depends on transition time ( Td - time between half peaks in current waveforms ) and duty ratio ( r ) in advanced process nodes. Additionally, foundries require to apply a specific duty ratio value regardless of the calculated duty ratio. 
To support this, the following AC peak EM rule should be used:
 em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.5 apply r = 1
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.001 Td < 0.5
 In conditional statements, always use the qualifier before numerical values. For example, use Td >= 0.5 , instead of 0.5 <= Td .
The syntax of EQU for these three em_jmax_ac equations is a numeric expression ending in a newline. The expression follows normal expression syntax with normal precedence rules:

1. exponentiation (^)
2. * or /
3. + or -
The following reserved names (case-insensitive) can be used in the equations to pass in parameters.

- w : Width of the wire being checked (required for metal layers that should be checked)
- deltaT : Delta temperature allowed. Normally only used for RMS limits to specify the max change in temperature allowed (optional).
- jmax_factor : For temperature based derating (optional).
- jmax_lifetime : Maximum years/hours of operation for lifetime derating (optional)
The following built-in functions are supported:

- ^ (exponentiation)
- * / (multiply, divide)
- + - (addition, subtraction)
- ( ) (grouping of expressions for precedence)
- sqrt
- log
Specification of derating for lifetime or hours of operation in ICT:
jmax_lifetime lifetime1scale1 [ lifetime2scale2 .......]

jmax_lifetime provides the ability to set the scaling factor that applies to the current density limits for different lifetimes. Based on the lifetime value that you specify (using the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process section of the ICT file), the appropriate multiplication factor is applied to the nominal current density limit. The unit should be specified in accordance with the setting defined by the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process definition of the ICT file.
Sample extract from the ICT file:
Specification for temperature based derating:
jmax_factor temp1scale1 [ temp2scale2 ...]

jmax_factor is an optional scaling factor that can be used at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature (defined by em_tref in the process definition). The temperature for jmax_factor should be specified in degrees Celsius. Scaling factor is a positive integer: >1 to scale up, <1 to scale down, or 1 for no scale effect.
Sample extract from the ICT file:
Area dependent parameters for via layers:
Area-based EM limits for vias can be defined using a PWL pair of area value. The PWL keyword in the syntax below signifies the use of area based EM limits.
em_jmax_ac_peak [ value | PWL value1area1 | EQU fn(E) ]
Sample ICT file for a via layer:

You can control the EM units in the ICT file using the following parameters:
The default units are highlighted in bold.
For more information on the ICT file, see the EM_Model section in the " Creating the ICT File " chapter of the Quantus Techgen Reference Manual .

For an MMMC design, if you want to choose one view to perform Irms/Ipeak/Iavg check, you need to use the set_default_view command to specify the view name or else the first defined view will be used by default.
Prevention of AC Signal EM Violations

Clock nets are generally the first nets to fail the AC current density limits because they have the highest frequency and net loads. It is impractical to "repair" them later without affecting the clock-skew, so clock tree synthesis should avoid creating AC current limit violation. Therefore, CTS inside Innovus has an optional max_cap value to the CTS specification file. CTS will avoid creating any clock net that exceeds the max_cap limit and therefore avoid any possible AC current limit violation.
Repair of AC Signal EM Violations

In Innovus, a fixACLimitViolation command exists that will repair any violations found by the verify_AC_limit command. It will do this initially by adding a buffer to split up the net load to any net marked with a violation by verify_AC_limit . Note that most users avoid fixing violations on clock network as it will impact clock skew.

Top Scope Signal EM Analysis

During iterative design refinement, Electromigration (EM) analysis is performed on the modules and the top-level logic for a large hierarchical design. For top-level analysis, use the create_top_scope command to perform abstraction of the top-level hierarchical instances for analysis of only the top-level logic instead of running the EM analysis of the fullchip netlist. The command allows you to perform abstraction of all the hierarchical instances in the top-level, or specify the top-level hierarchical instances that should be abstracted. When a hierarchical instance is abstracted, all the internal logic is ignored while retaining the interface paths.
The following diagram illustrates the top scope signal EM flow:
The create_top_scope command must be specified before the read_sdc command. The following is an example script for the top scope signal EM flow:
## Import Design
## Create Top Scope
## Set the process node before EM analysis

When the create_top_scope command is specified, Voltus generates current waveforms for the top-level nets, and nets interfacing with the hierarchical blocks. All the other nets are ignored from the current generation process. The clock network is traced for the design to calculate accurate slews for the nets being driven from inside the block.
Boundary Model Based Signal EM Analysis

The boundary model for a block provides sufficient details of its interface paths to accurately analyze these paths when loading the model into the signal EM analysis of the top-level module. These details include interface netlist, SPEF, and side inputs. Aggressors that are not on the boundary paths are not included in the model, and any coupling capacitance to such nets is grounded. To create the cell boundary model, use the create_cell_signal_em_model command. After the boundary model is generated, it is automatically loaded to the higher-level module.
The following figure shows the top scope with the block boundary model:
Here,

- Red nets indicate signal EM analysis is performed
- Black nets indicate skipped from signal EM analysis
- Yellow logic is removed during boundary model generation
- Full clock propagation such that slew and clock marking are done correctly

In the top-level signal EM run, the boundary model is loaded instead of the full block contents. Boundary models can be loaded for multiple blocks. If you specify a Verilog for a block that already has a boundary model, the specified Verilog will override the netlist in the boundary model. The boundary model should not be overridden with a Verilog definition.

You can specify the 
 create_cell_signal_em_model 
 command before or after running the 
 set_top_module 
 command. The details of the two use models are given below:

- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified before set_top_module , the command will generate the boundary model for a sub-cell and load it. For this use model, the -cell , -dir , -spef , and -verilog parameters must be specified.
- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified after set_top_module , the command will generate the model for the top cell for use in a subsequent top-level run. In this case, the command is issued in a block-level run, and the SPEF should be loaded prior to the create_cell_signal_em_model command. For this use model, the -dir parameter must be specified. Since the design is already loaded, the relevant information is obtained from the current session.

The switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) for the net is derived from the frequency of the associated clock (fastest clock will be used if more than one is associated) using the integrated static timing analysis engine when running this software. 
 # default switching factor of 100% on data network 
 verify_AC_limit \
-method rms \
-detailed \
-toggle 1.0 \
-report AC.1.0.rpt
- If using the global activity on all nets is too pessimistic or optimistic, you can set the effective frequency ( frequency * activity ) using the TCF, VCD, or FSDB file generated by netlist simulators. Optionally, you can set input and flop activities and propagate them to generate a TCF file as follows: 
 set_default_switching_activity \
-input_activity 0.2 \
-seq_activity 0.15 \
-clock_gates_output 2.0 \
-period 7.0
 
propagate_activity

write_tcf <design>.tcf
 
read_activity_file \
-format TCF \
-set_net_freq true dma_mac.tcf
verify_AC_limit \
-method avg \
-detailed \
-avgRecovery 0.5 \
-useQRCTech \
-use_db_freq \
-report AVG.tcf.rpt
Computing Irms/Ipeak/Iavg for each Routing Segment

For a routed net, the wire widths can taper as they reach the sink pins, and the routing can branch to multiple sinks. In order to avoid false violations, a local Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value must be computed for each wire segment of the net.

The Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value of each wire segment should only be the current required to charge the capacitance of the "downstream" wire segments and sinks. The following diagram illustrates Irms/Ipeak/Iavg calculation on "downstream" wire segments:

In this diagram, a) shows that the wire segment s1 must carry current to charge/discharge the entire net, so the Irms/Ipeak/Iavg at the driver output is the correct value to check for s1. In b), the wire segment s2 only carries current to charge/discharge s2, s4, s5 and the pin-caps of i2 and i3. In c), wire segment s5 only carries current for s5 and the pin-cap of i3.

The total wire capacitance (Cwire) comes from extraction (SPEF), and the pin-capacitance for each instance pin from the timing libraries. The total Irms/Ipeak/Iavg will be calculated using the total wire capacitance (Cwire). The software estimates the "downstream capacitance" that must be charged/discharged through individual wire segments by using the total area of the "downstream wire segments" relative to the total area of the wire plus the pin-caps of the sinks.
In example b) for Irms , the result is:
Cwire(total net) is already known from extraction
Cnet(total net) = Cwire(total net) + Cpin for all the sinks (driver's Cpin is not included)
Irms (total net) is computed from Cnet , the slew rate and frequency is as described earlier
Irms is proportional to Cnet , so we can estimate Irms(s2) as:
Checking the AC Current Limits
You can verify AC current violations on signal nets by using the verify_AC_limit command. verify_AC_limit checks for the following types of AC current violations on signal nets:

- Root-mean-square (RMS) current limit violations ( Irms violations)
- Peak current limit violations ( Ipeak violations)
In addition, verify_AC_limit can be used for average current limit (Iavg) analysis.

AC current limit violations are sometimes also referred as wire self-heat violations. Design Rule Check (DRC) manuals have these current limits to avoid over-heating a signal-net wire with too much AC current. To prevent wire self-heating or AC signal electromigration, signal interconnects should be analyzed for their AC current carrying capacity and measured against the AC current limits specified by foundry.
Use the verify_AC_limit -method parameter to specify the waveform calculation method to be used (rms, peak, or avg).
RMS/Peak/Avg Current Limit Violations

By default, verify_AC_limit only checks for Irms violations. The software calculates the Irms at the driver output and compares it to the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables in the LEF file, which contains the I rms limits for each routing layer.

If the computed Irms for a net exceeds the ACCURRENTDENSITY limit defined in LEF for any of the layers/widths used by the net, an error occurs and a violation marker is attached to the net. Each routing segment is checked separately. If more than one driver exists, the check starts at an arbitrary driver. The layers and widths of pins are not checked for AC current limits.
The software support checks the RMS table for all layers. To get access to the width-dependent syntax, we must add an arbitrary single frequency value. Therefore, a typical table might look like:
The tables are interpreted as piece-wise-linear tables indexed by width and frequency. In the example above, a wire of width 0.15um can carry 10.0 mA/um * .15um = 1.5mA of current.

The Irms limits in the table can also be scaled for other factors like temperature. For example, the Irms limits table should be computed for a specific "maximum allowed temperature change" ( maxTchange ). The I rms limits are normally proportional to the square-root of the maxTchange . Therefore, if the table used a maxTchange of 20 degrees, and you want a maxTchange of 10 degrees, you would use a scale value of sqrt(10/20) = 0.71 .
The software checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions:

- If more than one frequency is given, verify_AC_limit will warn that it is only using width values from the first frequency in the table.
- There must be at least two width values, otherwise verify_AC_limit gives an error message and quits.
- The widths must be increasing in value, and the current limits must be increasing in value, otherwise the software gives an error message and quits.
- If no table exists for a routing layer, a warning message is given, and the limit is assumed to be infinite for that layer.
verify_AC_limit also checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions and takes the following actions:

- If there is no table for a routing layer, the software gives a warning and assumes an infinite limit for the layer.
- If PEAK and AVERAGE tables are present, the software ignores them.

For Irms , signal EM analysis not only supports the rules defined in the technology LEF file, but also the rules defined in the QRC Technology file. But for Ipeak and Iavg , only the rules defined in the QRC Technology file are supported. If no EM rule is defined in the QRC Technology file, the software will stop checking and give an error message.

These rules defined in the QRC Technology file are represented in the form of an equation, as opposed to PWL, which when processed for each wire segment individually results in a more accurate EM limit calculation.

To perform this analysis, you must load the QRC tech file in Voltus using the -qx_tech_file parameter of create_rc_corner or update_rc_corner . verify_AC_limit processes the limits defined as polynomials inside the QRC tech file to determine layer-based AC current density limits. The QRC technology file attached to the current timing view (current RC corner) is used.

The QRC tech file is generated from an ASCII input file called the ICT file. For each layer, the ICT file includes em_model construct that defines AC and DC current density polynomials. Here's a sample em_model construct for a metal layer with current limits as an equation ( EQU ):
The peak, RMS, and average current limits for this layer are computed using the equations for the following three em_jmax_ac models.

- em_jmax_ac_peak : Peak current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_rms : RMS current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_avg : Average current limits for this layer. 
 
Signal AC EM limit depends on transition time ( Td - time between half peaks in current waveforms ) and duty ratio ( r ) in advanced process nodes. Additionally, foundries require to apply a specific duty ratio value regardless of the calculated duty ratio. 
To support this, the following AC peak EM rule should be used:
 em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.5 apply r = 1
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.001 Td < 0.5
 In conditional statements, always use the qualifier before numerical values. For example, use Td >= 0.5 , instead of 0.5 <= Td .
The syntax of EQU for these three em_jmax_ac equations is a numeric expression ending in a newline. The expression follows normal expression syntax with normal precedence rules:

1. exponentiation (^)
2. * or /
3. + or -
The following reserved names (case-insensitive) can be used in the equations to pass in parameters.

- w : Width of the wire being checked (required for metal layers that should be checked)
- deltaT : Delta temperature allowed. Normally only used for RMS limits to specify the max change in temperature allowed (optional).
- jmax_factor : For temperature based derating (optional).
- jmax_lifetime : Maximum years/hours of operation for lifetime derating (optional)
The following built-in functions are supported:

- ^ (exponentiation)
- * / (multiply, divide)
- + - (addition, subtraction)
- ( ) (grouping of expressions for precedence)
- sqrt
- log
Specification of derating for lifetime or hours of operation in ICT:
jmax_lifetime lifetime1scale1 [ lifetime2scale2 .......]

jmax_lifetime provides the ability to set the scaling factor that applies to the current density limits for different lifetimes. Based on the lifetime value that you specify (using the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process section of the ICT file), the appropriate multiplication factor is applied to the nominal current density limit. The unit should be specified in accordance with the setting defined by the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process definition of the ICT file.
Sample extract from the ICT file:
Specification for temperature based derating:
jmax_factor temp1scale1 [ temp2scale2 ...]

jmax_factor is an optional scaling factor that can be used at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature (defined by em_tref in the process definition). The temperature for jmax_factor should be specified in degrees Celsius. Scaling factor is a positive integer: >1 to scale up, <1 to scale down, or 1 for no scale effect.
Sample extract from the ICT file:
Area dependent parameters for via layers:
Area-based EM limits for vias can be defined using a PWL pair of area value. The PWL keyword in the syntax below signifies the use of area based EM limits.
em_jmax_ac_peak [ value | PWL value1area1 | EQU fn(E) ]
Sample ICT file for a via layer:
Units for EM limits:
You can control the EM units in the ICT file using the following parameters:
The default units are highlighted in bold.
For more information on the ICT file, see the EM_Model section in the " Creating the ICT File " chapter of the Quantus Techgen Reference Manual .

For an MMMC design, if you want to choose one view to perform Irms/Ipeak/Iavg check, you need to use the set_default_view command to specify the view name or else the first defined view will be used by default.
Prevention of AC Signal EM Violations

Clock nets are generally the first nets to fail the AC current density limits because they have the highest frequency and net loads. It is impractical to "repair" them later without affecting the clock-skew, so clock tree synthesis should avoid creating AC current limit violation. Therefore, CTS inside Innovus has an optional max_cap value to the CTS specification file. CTS will avoid creating any clock net that exceeds the max_cap limit and therefore avoid any possible AC current limit violation.
Repair of AC Signal EM Violations

In Innovus, a fixACLimitViolation command exists that will repair any violations found by the verify_AC_limit command. It will do this initially by adding a buffer to split up the net load to any net marked with a violation by verify_AC_limit . Note that most users avoid fixing violations on clock network as it will impact clock skew.

Top Scope Signal EM Analysis

During iterative design refinement, Electromigration (EM) analysis is performed on the modules and the top-level logic for a large hierarchical design. For top-level analysis, use the create_top_scope command to perform abstraction of the top-level hierarchical instances for analysis of only the top-level logic instead of running the EM analysis of the fullchip netlist. The command allows you to perform abstraction of all the hierarchical instances in the top-level, or specify the top-level hierarchical instances that should be abstracted. When a hierarchical instance is abstracted, all the internal logic is ignored while retaining the interface paths.
The following diagram illustrates the top scope signal EM flow:
The create_top_scope command must be specified before the read_sdc command. The following is an example script for the top scope signal EM flow:
## Import Design
## Create Top Scope
## Set the process node before EM analysis

When the create_top_scope command is specified, Voltus generates current waveforms for the top-level nets, and nets interfacing with the hierarchical blocks. All the other nets are ignored from the current generation process. The clock network is traced for the design to calculate accurate slews for the nets being driven from inside the block.
Boundary Model Based Signal EM Analysis

The boundary model for a block provides sufficient details of its interface paths to accurately analyze these paths when loading the model into the signal EM analysis of the top-level module. These details include interface netlist, SPEF, and side inputs. Aggressors that are not on the boundary paths are not included in the model, and any coupling capacitance to such nets is grounded. To create the cell boundary model, use the create_cell_signal_em_model command. After the boundary model is generated, it is automatically loaded to the higher-level module.
The following figure shows the top scope with the block boundary model:
Here,

- Red nets indicate signal EM analysis is performed
- Black nets indicate skipped from signal EM analysis
- Yellow logic is removed during boundary model generation
- Full clock propagation such that slew and clock marking are done correctly

In the top-level signal EM run, the boundary model is loaded instead of the full block contents. Boundary models can be loaded for multiple blocks. If you specify a Verilog for a block that already has a boundary model, the specified Verilog will override the netlist in the boundary model. The boundary model should not be overridden with a Verilog definition.

You can specify the 
 create_cell_signal_em_model 
 command before or after running the 
 set_top_module 
 command. The details of the two use models are given below:

- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified before set_top_module , the command will generate the boundary model for a sub-cell and load it. For this use model, the -cell , -dir , -spef , and -verilog parameters must be specified.
- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified after set_top_module , the command will generate the model for the top cell for use in a subsequent top-level run. In this case, the command is issued in a block-level run, and the SPEF should be loaded prior to the create_cell_signal_em_model command. For this use model, the -dir parameter must be specified. Since the design is already loaded, the relevant information is obtained from the current session.

Viewing Signal EM Analysis Results
The signal EM results can be debugged in the context of physical layout using the interactive signal EM analysis plots. To view the signal EM plots:

1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots. 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the signal EM analysis results. 
 
 
 To enable the signal EM GUI display, you must specify the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter. This parameter writes the signal EM data into a database file ( filename.db ) after performing signal EM analysis. This file contains the information required for loading and displaying signal EM data in GUI.
2. Click the DB Setup button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
3. Click the Browse button next to the SEM Database field to load the signal EM database file. You need to load the database file generated by the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter.
4. Click OK .
5. To set the visibility of the signal EM layers, click the Layers/Nets button. 
 The Power & Rail Layers/Nets form appears.
6. In the SemLayers tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the signal EM layers to modify the display of layers.
7. Click OK .
8. Select the Sem radio button and select a signal EM analysis plot type from the drop-down list. 
 
 
 The sem_rms , sem_avg , and sem_peak plots are enabled based on the verify_AC_limit -method command parameter. If you specify “ -method {rms peak avg} ”, then all the three plots are enabled. If you specify “ -method {rms avg} ”, then only I rms and I avg can be plotted in the GUI. 
 The following image displays the sem_avg plot: 
 
 
 The color of each wire segment represents its average current density that was determined after running the verify_AC_limit command.
9. Similarly, select the other types of signal EM plots and analyze the signal EM analysis results.
10. You can query signal wire segment related data, such as net information or current density value, by double-clicking a wire segment. 
 The Attribute Viewer form appears: 
 
 
 The Attribute Viewer form can also be displayed by selecting a wire segment and pressing q .
11. Click Close .

# read design 
read_lib -lef <lefs>
read_lib <timing libs>
read_verilog <verilog netlist>
set_top_module <design name>
read_def <def netlist>

# write the signal EM data into a database file. The 
-method {rms peak avg}
 parameter enables the 
sem_rms
, 
sem_avg
, and 
sem_peak
 plots in the 
Power & Rail Plots
 form
verify_AC_limit 
 -method {rms peak avg} -detailed -report_db -
report 
 dma_design.rpt

# display the RMS current density plot 
set_power_rail_display -plot sem_rms
Signal EM Report Format
Sample filename: signal.rpt
Format:
Format Description:
Irms/Limit specifies the RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Irms specifies the actual RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Ipeak/Limit specifies the peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Ipeak specifies the actual peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Iavg/Limit specifies the average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Iavg specifies the actual average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.

Width/CutArea If the wire segment is metal, it is calculated as the actual width perpendicular to the current flow of the wire segment. If the wire segment is via, it is calculated as the area of the cut in the via.

FixWidth/FixCutNum is the width (for metal segment) or cutNum (for via) required to fix the violation. When there is a violation, it is the new width of the metal wire or the new number of cuts of via (drawn area).
Cap is the capacitance of the wire segment.
Layer specifies the metal/via layer name on which the resistor is located.
(X1 Y1 X2 Y2) specifies the coordinates of the resistor.
PathLength of a wire segment is the distance traced from the first pin shape of driver to the center of the wire segment.
Blechlength is the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer.
Current Direction is applicable only to via layers where 'D" denotes down, "U" denotes up.
RMS_V2VFactor is the EM benefit from the via-to-via spacing rule for metal layers.
For clock nets, Tsw is already given in the timing constraints, and S , the switching factor, is always 2.0, so the effective frequency is, Feff = 1/T sw.
For signal nets, there are two choices:

- You can specify the switching factor ( S ) for the signal nets using the verify_AC_limit -toggle parameter. The specified switching factor will be applied to all signal nets and multiplied with the switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) to calculate the effective frequency of the signal net. The default switching factor of 1.0 (100%) signifies that the signal net will switch once at every period of the related clock cycle ( rising + falling ). The switching factor of the clock net will not change with the verify_AC_limit -toggle parameter as it always equals to 2.0 . The switching frequency ( 1/Tsw ) for the net is derived from the frequency of the associated clock (fastest clock will be used if more than one is associated) using the integrated static timing analysis engine when running this software.

 verify_AC_limit \
-method rms \
-detailed \
-toggle 1.0 \
-report AC.1.0.rpt
- If using the global activity on all nets is too pessimistic or optimistic, you can set the effective frequency ( frequency * activity ) using the TCF, VCD, or FSDB file generated by netlist simulators. Optionally, you can set input and flop activities and propagate them to generate a TCF file as follows: 
 set_default_switching_activity \
-input_activity 0.2 \
-seq_activity 0.15 \
-clock_gates_output 2.0 \
-period 7.0
 
propagate_activity

The temperature for jmax_factor should be specified in degrees Celsius. Scaling factor is a positive integer: >1 to scale up, <1 to scale down, or 1 for no scale effect.

For an MMMC design, if you want to choose one view to perform Irms/Ipeak/Iavg check, you need to use the set_default_view command to specify the view name or else the first defined view will be used by default.
Prevention of AC Signal EM Violations

Clock nets are generally the first nets to fail the AC current density limits because they have the highest frequency and net loads. It is impractical to "repair" them later without affecting the clock-skew, so clock tree synthesis should avoid creating AC current limit violation. Therefore, CTS inside Innovus has an optional max_cap value to the CTS specification file. CTS will avoid creating any clock net that exceeds the max_cap limit and therefore avoid any possible AC current limit violation.
Repair of AC Signal EM Violations

In Innovus, a fixACLimitViolation command exists that will repair any violations found by the verify_AC_limit command. It will do this initially by adding a buffer to split up the net load to any net marked with a violation by verify_AC_limit . Note that most users avoid fixing violations on clock network as it will impact clock skew.

Top Scope Signal EM Analysis

During iterative design refinement, Electromigration (EM) analysis is performed on the modules and the top-level logic for a large hierarchical design. For top-level analysis, use the create_top_scope command to perform abstraction of the top-level hierarchical instances for analysis of only the top-level logic instead of running the EM analysis of the fullchip netlist. The command allows you to perform abstraction of all the hierarchical instances in the top-level, or specify the top-level hierarchical instances that should be abstracted. When a hierarchical instance is abstracted, all the internal logic is ignored while retaining the interface paths.
The following diagram illustrates the top scope signal EM flow:
The create_top_scope command must be specified before the read_sdc command. The following is an example script for the top scope signal EM flow:
## Import Design
## Create Top Scope
## Set the process node before EM analysis

When the create_top_scope command is specified, Voltus generates current waveforms for the top-level nets, and nets interfacing with the hierarchical blocks. All the other nets are ignored from the current generation process. The clock network is traced for the design to calculate accurate slews for the nets being driven from inside the block.
Boundary Model Based Signal EM Analysis

The boundary model for a block provides sufficient details of its interface paths to accurately analyze these paths when loading the model into the signal EM analysis of the top-level module. These details include interface netlist, SPEF, and side inputs. Aggressors that are not on the boundary paths are not included in the model, and any coupling capacitance to such nets is grounded. To create the cell boundary model, use the create_cell_signal_em_model command. After the boundary model is generated, it is automatically loaded to the higher-level module.
The following figure shows the top scope with the block boundary model:
Here,

- Red nets indicate signal EM analysis is performed
- Black nets indicate skipped from signal EM analysis
- Yellow logic is removed during boundary model generation
- Full clock propagation such that slew and clock marking are done correctly

In the top-level signal EM run, the boundary model is loaded instead of the full block contents. Boundary models can be loaded for multiple blocks. If you specify a Verilog for a block that already has a boundary model, the specified Verilog will override the netlist in the boundary model. The boundary model should not be overridden with a Verilog definition.

You can specify the 
 create_cell_signal_em_model 
 command before or after running the 
 set_top_module 
 command. The details of the two use models are given below:

- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified before set_top_module , the command will generate the boundary model for a sub-cell and load it. For this use model, the -cell , -dir , -spef , and -verilog parameters must be specified.
- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified after set_top_module , the command will generate the model for the top cell for use in a subsequent top-level run. In this case, the command is issued in a block-level run, and the SPEF should be loaded prior to the create_cell_signal_em_model command. For this use model, the -dir parameter must be specified. Since the design is already loaded, the relevant information is obtained from the current session.

Viewing Signal EM Analysis Results
The signal EM results can be debugged in the context of physical layout using the interactive signal EM analysis plots. To view the signal EM plots:

1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots. 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the signal EM analysis results. 
 
 
 To enable the signal EM GUI display, you must specify the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter. This parameter writes the signal EM data into a database file ( filename.db ) after performing signal EM analysis. This file contains the information required for loading and displaying signal EM data in GUI.
2. Click the DB Setup button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
3. Click the Browse button next to the SEM Database field to load the signal EM database file. You need to load the database file generated by the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter.
4. Click OK .
5. To set the visibility of the signal EM layers, click the Layers/Nets button. 
 The Power & Rail Layers/Nets form appears.
6. In the SemLayers tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the signal EM layers to modify the display of layers.
7. Click OK .
8. Select the Sem radio button and select a signal EM analysis plot type from the drop-down list. 
 
 
 The sem_rms , sem_avg , and sem_peak plots are enabled based on the verify_AC_limit -method command parameter. If you specify “ -method {rms peak avg} ”, then all the three plots are enabled. If you specify “ -method {rms avg} ”, then only I rms and I avg can be plotted in the GUI. 
 The following image displays the sem_avg plot: 
 
 
 The color of each wire segment represents its average current density that was determined after running the verify_AC_limit command.
9. Similarly, select the other types of signal EM plots and analyze the signal EM analysis results.
10. You can query signal wire segment related data, such as net information or current density value, by double-clicking a wire segment. 
 The Attribute Viewer form appears: 
 
 
 The Attribute Viewer form can also be displayed by selecting a wire segment and pressing q .
11. Click Close .
