Signal EM Report Format
Sample filename: signal.rpt
Format:
Format Description:
Irms/Limit specifies the RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Irms specifies the actual RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Ipeak/Limit specifies the peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Ipeak specifies the actual peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Iavg/Limit specifies the average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Iavg specifies the actual average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.

Width/CutArea If the wire segment is metal, it is calculated as the actual width perpendicular to the current flow of the wire segment. If the wire segment is via, it is calculated as the area of the cut in the via.

FixWidth/FixCutNum is the width (for metal segment) or cutNum (for via) required to fix the violation. When there is a violation, it is the new width of the metal wire or the new number of cuts of via (drawn area).
Cap is the capacitance of the wire segment.
Layer specifies the metal/via layer name on which the resistor is located.
(X1 Y1 X2 Y2) specifies the coordinates of the resistor.
PathLength of a wire segment is the distance traced from the first pin shape of driver to the center of the wire segment.
Blechlength is the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer.
Current Direction is applicable only to via layers where 'D" denotes down, "U" denotes up.
RMS_V2VFactor is the EM benefit from the via-to-via spacing rule for metal layers.

title: Computing Irms/Ipeak/Iavg for each Routing Segment

lines: For a routed net, the wire widths can taper as they reach the sink pins, and the routing can branch to multiple sinks. In order to avoid false violations, a local Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value must be computed for each wire segment of the net.

The Irms/Ipeak/Iavg value of each wire segment should only be the current required to charge the capacitance of the "downstream" wire segments and sinks. The following diagram illustrates Irms/Ipeak/Iavg calculation on "downstream" wire segments:

In this diagram, a) shows that the wire segment s1 must carry current to charge/discharge the entire net, so the Irms/Ipeak/Iavg at the driver output is the correct value to check for s1. In b), the wire segment s2 only carries current to charge/discharge s2, s4, s5 and the pin-caps of i2 and i3. In c), wire segment s5 only carries current for s5 and the pin-cap of i3.

The total wire capacitance (Cwire) comes from extraction (SPEF), and the pin-capacitance for each instance pin from the timing libraries. The total Irms/Ipeak/Iavg will be calculated using the total wire capacitance (Cwire). The software estimates the "downstream capacitance" that must be charged/discharged through individual wire segments by using the total area of the "downstream wire segments" relative to the total area of the wire plus the pin-caps of the sinks.
In example b) for Irms , the result is:
Cwire(total net) is already known from extraction
Cnet(total net) = Cwire(total net) + Cpin for all the sinks (driver's Cpin is not included)
Irms (total net) is computed from Cnet , the slew rate and frequency is as described earlier
Irms is proportional to Cnet , so we can estimate Irms(s2) as:
Checking the AC Current Limits
You can verify AC current violations on signal nets by using the verify_AC_limit command. verify_AC_limit checks for the following types of AC current violations on signal nets:

- Root-mean-square (RMS) current limit violations ( Irms violations)
- Peak current limit violations ( Ipeak violations)
In addition, verify_AC_limit can be used for average current limit (Iavg) analysis.

AC current limit violations are sometimes also referred as wire self-heat violations. Design Rule Check (DRC) manuals have these current limits to avoid over-heating a signal-net wire with too much AC current. To prevent wire self-heating or AC signal electromigration, signal interconnects should be analyzed for their AC current carrying capacity and measured against the AC current limits specified by foundry.
Use the verify_AC_limit -method parameter to specify the waveform calculation method to be used (rms, peak, or avg).
RMS/Peak/Avg Current Limit Violations

By default, verify_AC_limit only checks for Irms violations. The software calculates the Irms at the driver output and compares it to the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables in the LEF file, which contains the I rms limits for each routing layer.

If the computed Irms for a net exceeds the ACCURRENTDENSITY limit defined in LEF for any of the layers/widths used by the net, an error occurs and a violation marker is attached to the net. Each routing segment is checked separately. If more than one driver exists, the check starts at an arbitrary driver. The layers and widths of pins are not checked for AC current limits.
The software support checks the RMS table for all layers. To get access to the width-dependent syntax, we must add an arbitrary single frequency value. Therefore, a typical table might look like:
The tables are interpreted as piece-wise-linear tables indexed by width and frequency. In the example above, a wire of width 0.15um can carry 10.0 mA/um * .15um = 1.5mA of current.

The Irms limits in the table can also be scaled for other factors like temperature. For example, the Irms limits table should be computed for a specific "maximum allowed temperature change" ( maxTchange ). The I rms limits are normally proportional to the square-root of the maxTchange . Therefore, if the table used a maxTchange of 20 degrees, and you want a maxTchange of 10 degrees, you would use a scale value of sqrt(10/20) = 0.71 .
The software checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions:

- If more than one frequency is given, verify_AC_limit will warn that it is only using width values from the first frequency in the table.
- There must be at least two width values, otherwise verify_AC_limit gives an error message and quits.
- The widths must be increasing in value, and the current limits must be increasing in value, otherwise the software gives an error message and quits.
- If no table exists for a routing layer, a warning message is given, and the limit is assumed to be infinite for that layer.
verify_AC_limit also checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions and takes the following actions:

- If there is no table for a routing layer, the software gives a warning and assumes an infinite limit for the layer.
- If PEAK and AVERAGE tables are present, the software ignores them.

For Irms , signal EM analysis not only supports the rules defined in the technology LEF file, but also the rules defined in the QRC Technology file. But for Ipeak and Iavg , only the rules defined in the QRC Technology file are supported. If no EM rule is defined in the QRC Technology file, the software will stop checking and give an error message.

These rules defined in the QRC Technology file are represented in the form of an equation, as opposed to PWL, which when processed for each wire segment individually results in a more accurate EM limit calculation.

To perform this analysis, you must load the QRC tech file in Voltus using the -qx_tech_file parameter of create_rc_corner or update_rc_corner . verify_AC_limit processes the limits defined as polynomials inside the QRC tech file to determine layer-based AC current density limits. The QRC technology file attached to the current timing view (current RC corner) is used.

The QRC tech file is generated from an ASCII input file called the ICT file. For each layer, the ICT file includes em_model construct that defines AC and DC current density polynomials. Here's a sample em_model construct for a metal layer with current limits as an equation ( EQU ):
The peak, RMS, and average current limits for this layer are computed using the equations for the following three em_jmax_ac models.

- em_jmax_ac_peak : Peak current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_rms : RMS current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_avg : Average current limits for this layer. 
 
Signal AC EM limit depends on transition time ( Td - time between half peaks in current waveforms ) and duty ratio ( r ) in advanced process nodes. Additionally, foundries require to apply a specific duty ratio value regardless of the calculated duty ratio. 
To support this, the following AC peak EM rule should be used:
 em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.5 apply r = 1
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.001 Td < 0.5
 In conditional statements, always use the qualifier before numerical values. For example, use Td >= 0.5 , instead of 0.5 <= Td .
The syntax of EQU for these three em_jmax_ac equations is a numeric expression ending in a newline. The expression follows normal expression syntax with normal precedence rules:

1. exponentiation (^)
2. * or /
3. + or -
The following reserved names (case-insensitive) can be used in the equations to pass in parameters.

- w : Width of the wire being checked (required for metal layers that should be checked)
- deltaT : Delta temperature allowed. Normally only used for RMS limits to specify the max change in temperature allowed (optional).
- jmax_factor : For temperature based derating (optional).
- jmax_lifetime : Maximum years/hours of operation for lifetime derating (optional)
The following built-in functions are supported:

- ^ (exponentiation)
- * / (multiply, divide)
- + - (addition, subtraction)
- ( ) (grouping of expressions for precedence)
- sqrt
- log
Specification of derating for lifetime or hours of operation in ICT:
jmax_lifetime lifetime1scale1 [ lifetime2scale2 .......]

jmax_lifetime provides the ability to set the scaling factor that applies to the current density limits for different lifetimes. Based on the lifetime value that you specify (using the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process section of the ICT file), the appropriate multiplication factor is applied to the nominal current density limit. The unit should be specified in accordance with the setting defined by the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process definition of the ICT file.
Sample extract from the ICT file:
Specification for temperature based derating:
jmax_factor temp1scale1 [ temp2scale2 ...]

jmax_factor is an optional scaling factor that can be used at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature (defined by em_tref in the process definition). The temperature for jmax_factor should be specified in degrees Celsius. Scaling factor is a positive integer: >1 to scale up, <1 to scale down, or 1 for no scale effect.
Sample extract from the ICT file:
Area dependent parameters for via layers:
Area-based EM limits for vias can be defined using a PWL pair of area value. The PWL keyword in the syntax below signifies the use of area based EM limits.
em_jmax_ac_peak [ value | PWL value1area1 | EQU fn(E) ]

The default units are highlighted in bold.

Prevention of AC Signal EM Violations

Clock nets are generally the first nets to fail the AC current density limits because they have the highest frequency and net loads. It is impractical to "repair" them later without affecting the clock-skew, so clock tree synthesis should avoid creating AC current limit violation. Therefore, CTS inside Innovus has an optional max_cap value to the CTS specification file. CTS will avoid creating any clock net that exceeds the max_cap limit and therefore avoid any possible AC current limit violation.

In Innovus, a fixACLimitViolation command exists that will repair any violations found by the verify_AC_limit command. It will do this initially by adding a buffer to split up the net load to any net marked with a violation by verify_AC_limit . Note that most users avoid fixing violations on clock network as it will impact clock skew.

By default, verify_AC_limit only checks for Irms violations. The software calculates the Irms at the driver output and compares it to the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables in the LEF file, which contains the I rms limits for each routing layer.

If the computed Irms for a net exceeds the ACCURRENTDENSITY limit defined in LEF for any of the layers/widths used by the net, an error occurs and a violation marker is attached to the net. Each routing segment is checked separately. If more than one driver exists, the check starts at an arbitrary driver. The layers and widths of pins are not checked for AC current limits.
The software support checks the RMS table for all layers. To get access to the width-dependent syntax, we must add an arbitrary single frequency value. Therefore, a typical table might look like:
The tables are interpreted as piece-wise-linear tables indexed by width and frequency. In the example above, a wire of width 0.15um can carry 10.0 mA/um * .15um = 1.5mA of current.

The Irms limits in the table can also be scaled for other factors like temperature. For example, the Irms limits table should be computed for a specific "maximum allowed temperature change" ( maxTchange ). The I rms limits are normally proportional to the square-root of the maxTchange . Therefore, if the table used a maxTchange of 20 degrees, and you want a maxTchange of 10 degrees, you would use a scale value of sqrt(10/20) = 0.71 .
The software checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions:

- If more than one frequency is given, verify_AC_limit will warn that it is only using width values from the first frequency in the table.
- There must be at least two width values, otherwise verify_AC_limit gives an error message and quits.
- The widths must be increasing in value, and the current limits must be increasing in value, otherwise the software gives an error message and quits.
- If no table exists for a routing layer, a warning message is given, and the limit is assumed to be infinite for that layer.
verify_AC_limit also checks the ACCURRENTDENSITY tables for the following conditions and takes the following actions:

- If there is no table for a routing layer, the software gives a warning and assumes an infinite limit for the layer.
- If PEAK and AVERAGE tables are present, the software ignores them.

For Irms , signal EM analysis not only supports the rules defined in the technology LEF file, but also the rules defined in the QRC Technology file. But for Ipeak and Iavg , only the rules defined in the QRC Technology file are supported. If no EM rule is defined in the QRC Technology file, the software will stop checking and give an error message.

These rules defined in the QRC Technology file are represented in the form of an equation, as opposed to PWL, which when processed for each wire segment individually results in a more accurate EM limit calculation.

To perform this analysis, you must load the QRC tech file in Voltus using the -qx_tech_file parameter of create_rc_corner or update_rc_corner . verify_AC_limit processes the limits defined as polynomials inside the QRC tech file to determine layer-based AC current density limits. The QRC technology file attached to the current timing view (current RC corner) is used.

The QRC tech file is generated from an ASCII input file called the ICT file. For each layer, the ICT file includes em_model construct that defines AC and DC current density polynomials. Here's a sample em_model construct for a metal layer with current limits as an equation ( EQU ):
The peak, RMS, and average current limits for this layer are computed using the equations for the following three em_jmax_ac models.

- em_jmax_ac_peak : Peak current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_rms : RMS current limits for this layer.
- em_jmax_ac_avg : Average current limits for this layer. 
 
Signal AC EM limit depends on transition time ( Td - time between half peaks in current waveforms ) and duty ratio ( r ) in advanced process nodes. Additionally, foundries require to apply a specific duty ratio value regardless of the calculated duty ratio. 
To support this, the following AC peak EM rule should be used:
 em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.5 apply r = 1
em_jmax_ac_peak EQU 58.10 * ( w - 0.02 ) w >= 0.1 Td >= 0.001 Td < 0.5
 In conditional statements, always use the qualifier before numerical values. For example, use Td >= 0.5 , instead of 0.5 <= Td .
The syntax of EQU for these three em_jmax_ac equations is a numeric expression ending in a newline. The expression follows normal expression syntax with normal precedence rules:

1. exponentiation (^)
2. * or /
3. + or -
The following reserved names (case-insensitive) can be used in the equations to pass in parameters.

- w : Width of the wire being checked (required for metal layers that should be checked)
- deltaT : Delta temperature allowed. Normally only used for RMS limits to specify the max change in temperature allowed (optional).
- jmax_factor : For temperature based derating (optional).
- jmax_lifetime : Maximum years/hours of operation for lifetime derating (optional)
The following built-in functions are supported:

- ^ (exponentiation)
- * / (multiply, divide)
- + - (addition, subtraction)
- ( ) (grouping of expressions for precedence)
- sqrt
- log
Specification of derating for lifetime or hours of operation in ICT:
jmax_lifetime lifetime1scale1 [ lifetime2scale2 .......]

jmax_lifetime provides the ability to set the scaling factor that applies to the current density limits for different lifetimes. Based on the lifetime value that you specify (using the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process section of the ICT file), the appropriate multiplication factor is applied to the nominal current density limit. The unit should be specified in accordance with the setting defined by the em_lifetime_units parameter defined in the process definition of the ICT file.
Sample extract from the ICT file:
Specification for temperature based derating:
jmax_factor temp1scale1 [ temp2scale2 ...]

jmax_factor is an optional scaling factor that can be used at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature (defined by em_tref in the process definition). The temperature for jmax_factor should be specified in degrees Celsius. Scaling factor is a positive integer: >1 to scale up, <1 to scale down, or 1 for no scale effect.
Sample extract from the ICT file:
Area dependent parameters for via layers:
Area-based EM limits for vias can be defined using a PWL pair of area value. The PWL keyword in the syntax below signifies the use of area based EM limits.
em_jmax_ac_peak [ value | PWL value1area1 | EQU fn(E) ]
Sample ICT file for a via layer:
Units for EM limits:
You can control the EM units in the ICT file using the following parameters:
The default units are highlighted in bold.
For more information on the ICT file, see the EM_Model section in the " Creating the ICT File " chapter of the Quantus Techgen Reference Manual .

For an MMMC design, if you want to choose one view to perform Irms/Ipeak/Iavg check, you need to use the set_default_view command to specify the view name or else the first defined view will be used by default.
Prevention of AC Signal EM Violations

Clock nets are generally the first nets to fail the AC current density limits because they have the highest frequency and net loads. It is impractical to "repair" them later without affecting the clock-skew, so clock tree synthesis should avoid creating AC current limit violation. Therefore, CTS inside Innovus has an optional max_cap value to the CTS specification file. CTS will avoid creating any clock net that exceeds the max_cap limit and therefore avoid any possible AC current limit violation.
Repair of AC Signal EM Violations

In Innovus, a fixACLimitViolation command exists that will repair any violations found by the verify_AC_limit command. It will do this initially by adding a buffer to split up the net load to any net marked with a violation by verify_AC_limit . Note that most users avoid fixing violations on clock network as it will impact clock skew.

Top Scope Signal EM Analysis

During iterative design refinement, Electromigration (EM) analysis is performed on the modules and the top-level logic for a large hierarchical design. For top-level analysis, use the create_top_scope command to perform abstraction of the top-level hierarchical instances for analysis of only the top-level logic instead of running the EM analysis of the fullchip netlist. The command allows you to perform abstraction of all the hierarchical instances in the top-level, or specify the top-level hierarchical instances that should be abstracted. When a hierarchical instance is abstracted, all the internal logic is ignored while retaining the interface paths.
The following diagram illustrates the top scope signal EM flow:
The create_top_scope command must be specified before the read_sdc command. The following is an example script for the top scope signal EM flow:
## Import Design
## Create Top Scope
## Set the process node before EM analysis

When the create_top_scope command is specified, Voltus generates current waveforms for the top-level nets, and nets interfacing with the hierarchical blocks. All the other nets are ignored from the current generation process. The clock network is traced for the design to calculate accurate slews for the nets being driven from inside the block.
Boundary Model Based Signal EM Analysis

The boundary model for a block provides sufficient details of its interface paths to accurately analyze these paths when loading the model into the signal EM analysis of the top-level module. These details include interface netlist, SPEF, and side inputs. Aggressors that are not on the boundary paths are not included in the model, and any coupling capacitance to such nets is grounded. To create the cell boundary model, use the create_cell_signal_em_model command. After the boundary model is generated, it is automatically loaded to the higher-level module.
The following figure shows the top scope with the block boundary model:
Here,

- Red nets indicate signal EM analysis is performed
- Black nets indicate skipped from signal EM analysis
- Yellow logic is removed during boundary model generation
- Full clock propagation such that slew and clock marking are done correctly

In the top-level signal EM run, the boundary model is loaded instead of the full block contents. Boundary models can be loaded for multiple blocks. If you specify a Verilog for a block that already has a boundary model, the specified Verilog will override the netlist in the boundary model. The boundary model should not be overridden with a Verilog definition.

You can specify the 
 create_cell_signal_em_model 
 command before or after running the 
 set_top_module 
 command. The details of the two use models are given below:

- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified before set_top_module , the command will generate the boundary model for a sub-cell and load it. For this use model, the -cell , -dir , -spef , and -verilog parameters must be specified.
- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified after set_top_module , the command will generate the model for the top cell for use in a subsequent top-level run. In this case, the command is issued in a block-level run, and the SPEF should be loaded prior to the create_cell_signal_em_model command. For this use model, the -dir parameter must be specified. Since the design is already loaded, the relevant information is obtained from the current session.
The following is an example script for the boundary model based top scope signal EM flow:

Viewing Signal EM Analysis Results
The signal EM results can be debugged in the context of physical layout using the interactive signal EM analysis plots. To view the signal EM plots:

1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots. 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the signal EM analysis results. 
 
 
 To enable the signal EM GUI display, you must specify the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter. This parameter writes the signal EM data into a database file ( filename.db ) after performing signal EM analysis. This file contains the information required for loading and displaying signal EM data in GUI.
2. Click the DB Setup button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
3. Click the Browse button next to the SEM Database field to load the signal EM database file. You need to load the database file generated by the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter.
4. Click OK .
5. To set the visibility of the signal EM layers, click the Layers/Nets button. 
 The Power & Rail Layers/Nets form appears.
6. In the SemLayers tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the signal EM layers to modify the display of layers.
7. Click OK .
8. Select the Sem radio button and select a signal EM analysis plot type from the drop-down list. 
 
 
 The sem_rms , sem_avg , and sem_peak plots are enabled based on the verify_AC_limit -method command parameter. If you specify “ -method {rms peak avg} ”, then all the three plots are enabled. If you specify “ -method {rms avg} ”, then only I rms and I avg can be plotted in the GUI. 
 The following image displays the sem_avg plot: 
 
 
 The color of each wire segment represents its average current density that was determined after running the verify_AC_limit command.
9. Similarly, select the other types of signal EM plots and analyze the signal EM analysis results.
10. You can query signal wire segment related data, such as net information or current density value, by double-clicking a wire segment. 
 The Attribute Viewer form appears: 
 
 
 The Attribute Viewer form can also be displayed by selecting a wire segment and pressing q .
11. Click Close .
Example Script for Displaying the Signal EM Plot in GUI

# read design 
read_lib -lef <lefs>
read_lib <timing libs>
read_verilog <verilog netlist>
set_top_module <design name>
read_def <def netlist>

# write the signal EM data into a database file. The 
-method {rms peak avg}
 parameter enables the 
sem_rms
, 
sem_avg
, and 
sem_peak
 plots in the 
Power & Rail Plots
 form
verify_AC_limit 
 -method {rms peak avg} -detailed -report_db -
report 
 dma_design.rpt

# display the RMS current density plot 
set_power_rail_display -plot sem_rms
Signal EM Report Format
Sample filename: signal.rpt
Format:
Format Description:
Irms/Limit specifies the RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Irms specifies the actual RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Ipeak/Limit specifies the peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Ipeak specifies the actual peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Iavg/Limit specifies the average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Iavg specifies the actual average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.

Width/CutArea If the wire segment is metal, it is calculated as the actual width perpendicular to the current flow of the wire segment. If the wire segment is via, it is calculated as the area of the cut in the via.

FixWidth/FixCutNum is the width (for metal segment) or cutNum (for via) required to fix the violation. When there is a violation, it is the new width of the metal wire or the new number of cuts of via (drawn area).
Cap is the capacitance of the wire segment.
Layer specifies the metal/via layer name on which the resistor is located.
(X1 Y1 X2 Y2) specifies the coordinates of the resistor.
PathLength of a wire segment is the distance traced from the first pin shape of driver to the center of the wire segment.
Blechlength is the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer.
Current Direction is applicable only to via layers where 'D" denotes down, "U" denotes up.
RMS_V2VFactor is the EM benefit from the via-to-via spacing rule for metal layers.

 The Attribute Viewer form appears:

Sample filename: signal.rpt
Format:
Format Description:
Irms/Limit specifies the RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Irms specifies the actual RMS current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Ipeak/Limit specifies the peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Ipeak specifies the actual peak current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.
Iavg/Limit specifies the average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Iavg specifies the actual average current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.

Width/CutArea If the wire segment is metal, it is calculated as the actual width perpendicular to the current flow of the wire segment. If the wire segment is via, it is calculated as the area of the cut in the via.

FixWidth/FixCutNum is the width (for metal segment) or cutNum (for via) required to fix the violation. When there is a violation, it is the new width of the metal wire or the new number of cuts of via (drawn area).
Cap is the capacitance of the wire segment.
Layer specifies the metal/via layer name on which the resistor is located.
(X1 Y1 X2 Y2) specifies the coordinates of the resistor.
PathLength of a wire segment is the distance traced from the first pin shape of driver to the center of the wire segment.
Blechlength is the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer.
Current Direction is applicable only to via layers where 'D" denotes down, "U" denotes up.
RMS_V2VFactor is the EM benefit from the via-to-via spacing rule for metal layers.

Top Scope Signal EM Analysis

During iterative design refinement, Electromigration (EM) analysis is performed on the modules and the top-level logic for a large hierarchical design. For top-level analysis, use the create_top_scope command to perform abstraction of the top-level hierarchical instances for analysis of only the top-level logic instead of running the EM analysis of the fullchip netlist. The command allows you to perform abstraction of all the hierarchical instances in the top-level, or specify the top-level hierarchical instances that should be abstracted. When a hierarchical instance is abstracted, all the internal logic is ignored while retaining the interface paths.
The following diagram illustrates the top scope signal EM flow:
The create_top_scope command must be specified before the read_sdc command. The following is an example script for the top scope signal EM flow:
## Import Design
## Create Top Scope
## Set the process node before EM analysis

When the create_top_scope command is specified, Voltus generates current waveforms for the top-level nets, and nets interfacing with the hierarchical blocks. All the other nets are ignored from the current generation process. The clock network is traced for the design to calculate accurate slews for the nets being driven from inside the block.
Boundary Model Based Signal EM Analysis

The boundary model for a block provides sufficient details of its interface paths to accurately analyze these paths when loading the model into the signal EM analysis of the top-level module. These details include interface netlist, SPEF, and side inputs. Aggressors that are not on the boundary paths are not included in the model, and any coupling capacitance to such nets is grounded. To create the cell boundary model, use the create_cell_signal_em_model command. After the boundary model is generated, it is automatically loaded to the higher-level module.
The following figure shows the top scope with the block boundary model:
Here,

- Red nets indicate signal EM analysis is performed
- Black nets indicate skipped from signal EM analysis
- Yellow logic is removed during boundary model generation
- Full clock propagation such that slew and clock marking are done correctly

In the top-level signal EM run, the boundary model is loaded instead of the full block contents. Boundary models can be loaded for multiple blocks. If you specify a Verilog for a block that already has a boundary model, the specified Verilog will override the netlist in the boundary model. The boundary model should not be overridden with a Verilog definition.

You can specify the 
 create_cell_signal_em_model 
 command before or after running the 
 set_top_module 
 command. The details of the two use models are given below:

- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified before set_top_module , the command will generate the boundary model for a sub-cell and load it. For this use model, the -cell , -dir , -spef , and -verilog parameters must be specified.
- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified after set_top_module , the command will generate the model for the top cell for use in a subsequent top-level run. In this case, the command is issued in a block-level run, and the SPEF should be loaded prior to the create_cell_signal_em_model command. For this use model, the -dir parameter must be specified. Since the design is already loaded, the relevant information is obtained from the current session.
The following is an example script for the boundary model based top scope signal EM flow:
## Import Design
## Read top-level Verilog
## BM generation for blocks
The following is the command for creating a boundary model and loading it into the current session:
The following is the command for creating a boundary model after running set_top_module in a block-level session:
## Create Top Scope
## Boundary model Verilog files to be read for blocks “automatically”
## Boundary model SPEF files to be read for blocks “automatically”
## Set the process node before EM analysis
Viewing Signal EM Analysis Results
The signal EM results can be debugged in the context of physical layout using the interactive signal EM analysis plots. To view the signal EM plots:

1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots. 
 The Power & Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the signal EM analysis results. 
 
 
 To enable the signal EM GUI display, you must specify the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter. This parameter writes the signal EM data into a database file ( filename.db ) after performing signal EM analysis. This file contains the information required for loading and displaying signal EM data in GUI.
2. Click the DB Setup button on the Power & Rail Plots form. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
3. Click the Browse button next to the SEM Database field to load the signal EM database file. You need to load the database file generated by the verify_AC_limit -report_db command parameter.
4. Click OK .
5. To set the visibility of the signal EM layers, click the Layers/Nets button. 
 The Power & Rail Layers/Nets form appears.
6. In the SemLayers tab, select or clear the check boxes adjacent to the signal EM layers to modify the display of layers.
7. Click OK .
8. Select the Sem radio button and select a signal EM analysis plot type from the drop-down list. 
 
 
 The sem_rms , sem_avg , and sem_peak plots are enabled based on the verify_AC_limit -method command parameter. If you specify “ -method {rms peak avg} ”, then all the three plots are enabled. If you specify “ -method {rms avg} ”, then only I rms and I avg can be plotted in the GUI. 
 The following image displays the sem_avg plot: 
 
 
 The color of each wire segment represents its average current density that was determined after running the verify_AC_limit command.
9. Similarly, select the other types of signal EM plots and analyze the signal EM analysis results.
10. You can query signal wire segment related data, such as net information or current density value, by double-clicking a wire segment. 
 The Attribute Viewer form appears: 
 
 
 The Attribute Viewer form can also be displayed by selecting a wire segment and pressing q .
11. Click Close .

# read design 
read_lib -lef <lefs>
read_lib <timing libs>
read_verilog <verilog netlist>
set_top_module <design name>
read_def <def netlist>

# write the signal EM data into a database file. The 
-method {rms peak avg}
 parameter enables the 
sem_rms
, 
sem_avg
, and 
sem_peak
 plots in the 
Power & Rail Plots
 form
verify_AC_limit 
 -method {rms peak avg} -detailed -report_db -
report 
 dma_design.rpt

# display the RMS current density plot 
set_power_rail_display -plot sem_rms

Overview

Hot-carrier injection or DC signal electromigration can occur whenever a transistor switches. Whenever there are Ids (drain-source current), there is some probability that the carriers get enough kinetic energy to be injected into the gate oxide and cause a Vth shift. The calculation of this probability is a complex function of the Vgs (gate-source voltage) and the Vds (drain-source voltage) and Ids.

The most common problem occurs when there is a slow output transition with a large output capacitance which would mean a high Vds for a long time with a high Vgs and Ids. This type of operation causes a higher probability of hot-carrier injection, and therefore, Vth shifts and could result in reliability problems. The following diagram illustrates that within a cell, some segments only experience DC current:
The current discharged through wire A and B is a pulsed DC current that is proportional to the frequency of the signal net and the load capacitance:
Idc = frequency x Cout

Since the wire inside the cell has a fixed width, it also has a fixed maximum DC current electromigration limit. A high-frequency output needs a smaller Cout to keep Idc under the DC electromigration limit, while a low-frequency output can tolerate a higher Cout.
The most useful way to express this limit is as a maxCap versus frequency limit for the cell.
DC Signal EM Analysis using maxCap and maxTran Limits

The software manages DC electromigration using maximum capacitance limit based on switching frequency. This is driven by maxCapPerFreq data in liberty. In addition, it also supports maxCapPerTran and maxCapPerFreqTran . The violations can be reported using the report_design_rule -cap and report_design_rule -tran commands.
Optionally these constraints can be set for cell pins manually, as follows:
Frequency based Cell EM Limits in Liberty
The electromigration "group" in liberty supports 1-D and 2-D look-up tables that lists frequency based electromigration limits based on input transition time and output loading capacitance.

- Input Pin: 1-D table listing frequency limits based on input transition time
- Output Pin: 2-D table listing frequency limits based on input transition time and output load capacitance
The following figure displays cell-based DC electromigration limit templates in liberty :

The report_freq_violation command invokes static timing analysis and computes effective frequency (switching factor * clock domain freq) on each instance pin. The switching factor is determined by either propagating the activities or by specifying a TCF, VCD, or FSDB file. The clock domain frequency for the instance pin is derived from static timing analysis. The software will then compute frequency limit at the output pin from liberty based on slew on the related input pin and output load to determine frequency violation.
Repair of DC Signal EM Violations

In Innovus, the optDesign command can be used to upsize cell instances and minimize load capacitance to fix DC signal EM violations. This command is not available inside Voltus. The auto-fixing is not available for violations reported by the report_freq_violation command at this time.
Example Script for DC Signal EM Analysis
# read design
# check max transition violations
# check max capacitance violations
# check max cap violations based on frequency
# manually set max cap limits per freq in liberty using set_max_cap_per_freq command
# note that lib name is library name inside dotlib, e.g. library (tcbn65lplvtwc)
# read activity file for frequency annotation
# check max tran violations based on frequency
# manually set max tran limits per freq in liberty using set_max_tran_per_freq command
# check frequency violation
# requires dotlib with em templates

Hot-carrier injection or DC signal electromigration can occur whenever a transistor switches. Whenever there are Ids (drain-source current), there is some probability that the carriers get enough kinetic energy to be injected into the gate oxide and cause a Vth shift. The calculation of this probability is a complex function of the Vgs (gate-source voltage) and the Vds (drain-source voltage) and Ids.

The most common problem occurs when there is a slow output transition with a large output capacitance which would mean a high Vds for a long time with a high Vgs and Ids. This type of operation causes a higher probability of hot-carrier injection, and therefore, Vth shifts and could result in reliability problems. The following diagram illustrates that within a cell, some segments only experience DC current:
The current discharged through wire A and B is a pulsed DC current that is proportional to the frequency of the signal net and the load capacitance:
Idc = frequency x Cout

Since the wire inside the cell has a fixed width, it also has a fixed maximum DC current electromigration limit. A high-frequency output needs a smaller Cout to keep Idc under the DC electromigration limit, while a low-frequency output can tolerate a higher Cout.
The most useful way to express this limit is as a maxCap versus frequency limit for the cell.
DC Signal EM Analysis using maxCap and maxTran Limits

The software manages DC electromigration using maximum capacitance limit based on switching frequency. This is driven by maxCapPerFreq data in liberty. In addition, it also supports maxCapPerTran and maxCapPerFreqTran . The violations can be reported using the report_design_rule -cap and report_design_rule -tran commands.
Optionally these constraints can be set for cell pins manually, as follows:
Frequency based Cell EM Limits in Liberty
The electromigration "group" in liberty supports 1-D and 2-D look-up tables that lists frequency based electromigration limits based on input transition time and output loading capacitance.

- Input Pin: 1-D table listing frequency limits based on input transition time
- Output Pin: 2-D table listing frequency limits based on input transition time and output load capacitance
The following figure displays cell-based DC electromigration limit templates in liberty :

The report_freq_violation command invokes static timing analysis and computes effective frequency (switching factor * clock domain freq) on each instance pin. The switching factor is determined by either propagating the activities or by specifying a TCF, VCD, or FSDB file. The clock domain frequency for the instance pin is derived from static timing analysis. The software will then compute frequency limit at the output pin from liberty based on slew on the related input pin and output load to determine frequency violation.
Repair of DC Signal EM Violations

In Innovus, the optDesign command can be used to upsize cell instances and minimize load capacitance to fix DC signal EM violations. This command is not available inside Voltus. The auto-fixing is not available for violations reported by the report_freq_violation command at this time.
Example Script for DC Signal EM Analysis
# read design
# check max transition violations
# check max capacitance violations
# check max cap violations based on frequency
# manually set max cap limits per freq in liberty using set_max_cap_per_freq command
# note that lib name is library name inside dotlib, e.g. library (tcbn65lplvtwc)
# read activity file for frequency annotation
# check max tran violations based on frequency
# manually set max tran limits per freq in liberty using set_max_tran_per_freq command
# check frequency violation
# requires dotlib with em templates

- Overview
  - FEOL Self-Heat (delta T FEOL)
  - BEOL Self-Heat (delta T RMS)
- Data Requirements for SHE Analysis
- Self-Heating Analysis Flow
  - Sample Run Script
- View SHE Analysis Plots in Voltus
- Delta Temperature File Formats

The 16nm FinFET process node is rapidly becoming the preferred choice for advanced Integrated Circuit (IC) designs. The self-heating effects (SHE) of FinFET has become very significant and need to be taken into account in device modeling and circuit simulation. Conventional self-heating compact models, such as those that use auxiliary thermal resistance and capacitance RC circuits, are inefficient in simulation because of the additional nodes introduced in RC thermal network and therefore increased circuit matrix size. In addition, FinFET compact models also become very expensive to evaluate because large amount of temperature-dependency terms and derivatives must be computed.
Self-Heating Effect is driven by front-end-of-line (FEOL) self-heat and back-end- of-line (BEOL) self-heat.

- FEOL is the first portion of IC fabrication where the individual devices (transistors, capacitors, resistors, etc.) are patterned in the semiconductor. FEOL generally covers everything up to (but not including) the deposition of metal interconnects layers.
- BEOL is the second portion of IC fabrication where the individual devices (transistors, capacitors, resistors, etc.) get interconnected with wiring on the wafer. Both contribute towards the delta T for self-heating.
FEOL Self-Heat (delta T FEOL)

Temperature increase is a function of # of fins and # of fingers, given the same FinFET structure. Also, temperature increase is linear to power. Both contribute towards self-heating. To calculate delta T FEOL:

- it must be calculated based on Instance
- depending on instance, power and cell-based fin # and finger # is calculated from SHF
- thermal resistance depends on cell type, core versus IO
BEOL Self-Heat (delta T RMS)
Temperature increase is a function of Irms through metal and the metal width. Interconnect wires create the self-heating effect. To calculate delta T BEOL, you need to:
PG Net Analysis:

1. Perform dynamic power and IR drop analysis.
2. Calculate resistor I rms current.
3. Calculate delta T on each PG resistor (fractured wire segment)
For Signal Net Analysis:

1. Perform signal EM analysis based on same switching activities
2. Get I rms on each wire segment
3. Calculate delta T on each signal wire segment
To calculate final delta T on BEOL, the software considers the RMS current on BEOL and interaction effect from FEOL.

Temperature increase is a function of # of fins and # of fingers, given the same FinFET structure. Also, temperature increase is linear to power. Both contribute towards self-heating. To calculate delta T FEOL:

- it must be calculated based on Instance
- depending on instance, power and cell-based fin # and finger # is calculated from SHF
- thermal resistance depends on cell type, core versus IO
BEOL Self-Heat (delta T RMS)
Temperature increase is a function of Irms through metal and the metal width. Interconnect wires create the self-heating effect. To calculate delta T BEOL, you need to:
PG Net Analysis:

1. Perform dynamic power and IR drop analysis.
2. Calculate resistor I rms current.
3. Calculate delta T on each PG resistor (fractured wire segment)
For Signal Net Analysis:

1. Perform signal EM analysis based on same switching activities
2. Get I rms on each wire segment
3. Calculate delta T on each signal wire segment
To calculate final delta T on BEOL, the software considers the RMS current on BEOL and interaction effect from FEOL.
Temperature increase is a function of Irms through metal and the metal width. Interconnect wires create the self-heating effect. To calculate delta T BEOL, you need to:
PG Net Analysis:

1. Perform dynamic power and IR drop analysis.
2. Calculate resistor I rms current.
3. Calculate delta T on each PG resistor (fractured wire segment)
For Signal Net Analysis:

1. Perform signal EM analysis based on same switching activities
2. Get I rms on each wire segment
3. Calculate delta T on each signal wire segment
To calculate final delta T on BEOL, the software considers the RMS current on BEOL and interaction effect from FEOL.
The table below lists the inputs that are required for SHE analysis in Voltus:

data: Synopsys libraries (dot libs) and PVT corner for analysis.
Full VCD file for vector-based analysis
Technology LEF, standard cells, IOs, memories and IP LEF.
DEF or Multiple DEFs for design top level and blocks
Flattened or multiple SPEFs for top level and blocks
Spice netlist for all cells in the design along with the Spice models. It is recommended that the supplied Spice netlist for macros/memories and IO cells have device X/Y location file.
Design components for IOs, memories, and IPs
Current limits per unit area for each process layer. Usually available through foundry. It should contain the IRMS equation.
Need Alpha parameter for all layers. It is the metal layer effect.
A file for Instance and its static power. Use half of total power for SHE table look-up. It is a simple 2 column format of <instance_name> and <total_power>

A file containing instance name, finger number, and fin number. It is to derive the FOEL delta T calculation. Characterization on <finger_num> and <fin_num> is based on PMOS only, thus only half of total power should be used.

lines: The following diagram illustrates the self-heating analysis flow:
The following key tasks are performed for the SHE flow:

1. Load the design (LEF/DEF/Verilog/Timing Libs/SDC)
2. Specify activity for the design (TCF/VCD/FSDB)
3. Run report_power to calculate instance-based static power and dynamic currents on power network.
4. Run the analyze_self_heat command to specify the number of tiles, layer-based α coefficients, β coefficients, and list of cells with number of fins/fingers. This command will use the static power data to compute delta T FEOL for all instances in the design. This command:
  - computes the RMS currents on signal wires
  - computes the RMS currents on power network wires
  - generates Detail Delta Temperature File (ddt), Instance Delta Temperature File (idt), and Tile Delta Temperature File (tdt) 
 
Instead of using the analyze_self_heat command, you can use the analyze_joule_heat command if you need to analyze only the BEOL self-heat (delta T RMS) without analyzing the FEOL self-heat and any coupling effects from FEOL to BEOL.
Sample Run Script
##Setup Design
## Rail Analysis
##Analyze Self-heat
The following is an example for the analyze_joule_heat command:
analyze_joule_heat -domain ALL -tile_delta_temperature_file joule_ttm.txt -tiles {10 10} -detail_delta_temperature_file joule_heat_detail.rpt -report_conn_pin_wire joule_heat.rpt
##Setup Design
## Rail Analysis
##Analyze Self-heat
The following is an example for the analyze_joule_heat command:
analyze_joule_heat -domain ALL -tile_delta_temperature_file joule_ttm.txt -tiles {10 10} -detail_delta_temperature_file joule_heat_detail.rpt -report_conn_pin_wire joule_heat.rpt

title: View SHE Analysis Plots in Voltus

lines: To view the SHE analysis plots using command line:

1. Load the detail/instance/tile delta T files using the following parameters of the read_power_rail_results command:
  - -detail_delta_temperature_file
  - -instance_delta_temperature_file
  - -tile_delta_temperature_file
2. Specify the ddt / idt / tdt plot type using the following command: 
 set_power_rail_display -plot ddt
To view the SHE analysis plots using GUI:

1. Select Power & Rail - Power & Rail Plots . 
 The following form appears:
2. Click DB Setup . 
 The following form appears:
3. Click the Browse button next to the Rail Database field to load the rail data.
4. Load either detail, instance, or tile delta T files in the corresponding field in the Instance Files section.
5. Click OK .
6. In the Power & Rail Plots form, click the Rail radio button.
7. Select the plot type ( ddt/idt/tdt ) corresponding to the loaded delta T file from the drop-down list.
8. Click Apply . 
 The temperature map appears in the Layout tab.
The following types of SHE analysis plots are available:

- Detail Delta Temperature File (ddt) - Reports the increase in temperature (deltaT) for all metal layers in the entire design. This plot provides a detailed view of the temperature variations on the design.
- Instance Delta Temperature File (idt) - Reports the increase in temperature (deltaT) for all instances in the entire design.
- Tile Delta Temperature File (tdt) - Reports the increase in temperature (deltaT) per tile based on the number of tiles, specified using the analyze_self_heat -tiles parameter. The design on the layout is divided into sections based on the number of tiles that are specified, for example, 100x100. On the layout, the tiles are colored on the basis of the temperature increase in each tile. This is shown in the figure below:
Detail Delta Temperature File (ddt) - Self-Heat Analysis
Detail Delta Temperature File (ddt) - Joule Heat Analysis
Instance Delta Temperature File (idt)
Tile Delta Temperature File (tdt)

data: xmin(um) ymin(um) xmax(um) ymax(um) nx(int) ny(int)

lines:
where 
,

- DIE_AREA specifies the coordinates of the die area. nx and ny is the tile number in the temperature map file.
- NUMBER_OF_LAYERS is the number of layers in each die instance.
- LAYER specifies the LEF layer name.
- XCOR and YCOR are coordinates of each layer
- T(0,0) …. is the temperature value assigned to a tile at a specific coordinate.

- Introduction to Statistical ElectroMigration Budgeting
- Statistical ElectroMigration Budgeting Flow
- TCL Command Parameters for Running the SEB Flow
- Setting up and Running Power EM and Signal EM Analyses for FIT Calculation
- Output Reports
- Viewing FIT GUI Map

Statistical ElectroMigration budgeting (SEB) enables you to meet the ElectroMigration (EM) reliability targets by performing a statistical interconnect EM analysis based on the current modeling and thermal analysis of the design. This feature leverages the statistical nature of EM reliability to compute the level of the EM risk for all design rule violations. SEB analysis requires fixing of only those violations that add significant risk. This reduces the design verification time and improves performance while ensuring that the design meets its chip-level reliability goals.
The Voltus SEB flow involves:

- Measuring the total chip failure rate by EM analysis
- Checking if the total failure rate meets the product requirement
Failures in Time (FIT) is a factor to represent the chip failure rate. Following is the definition of FIT:
1 FIT = One failure per 109 product hours
= One failure in 1000 products through 106 hours
= One failure in 105 products through 104 hours
= or any other combination between products and hours as long as the product of the two is 109
Voltus will calculate the FIT value for each resistor of metal and via. The tool will follow the foundry EM definition to calculate FIT.
The ElectroMigration FIT equation is given below:
The following is the detailed description of the Median Time to Failure (MTF) equation:

- LT = lifetime of product in hour
- erf() = error function
- S DC = DC EM severity ratio, I DC /I limit
  - I DC : DC current of design
  - I limit : DC EM limit from foundry design rule manual and calculated at T ambient
- MTF(T ambient ) = median time to failure in hour (from foundry)
- σ= sigma, spread in lifetime distribution (from foundry)
- n = current density exponent (from foundry)
- Ea = activate energy (from foundry)
- k = Boltzmann's constant
- T = absolute temperature in K
The following diagram illustrates the SEB analysis flow:
The following key tasks are performed for the SEB flow:

1. Load the design (LEF/DEF/Verilog/Timing Libs/SDC) and apply the signal EM settings.
2. Perform static and/or dynamic power analysis. The static power analysis step is optional and is not required if you have an instance power file.
3. Perform self-heating analysis to evaluate the BEOL and FEOL self-heat and any coupling effects from FEOL to BEOL, or just Joule-heating analysis ( delta_T_RMS ), to calculate the final BEOL temperature rise effect. 
 Since the VIA layer does not have the RMS EM rules, delta_T_RMS cannot be calculated for the VIA layer. VIA’s delta_T is always decided by the max delta_T of the connecting upper and lower metal.
4. Perform SEB FIT calculation based on the final BEOL temperature rise to generate the power and signal SEB report.
TCL Command Parameters for Running the SEB Flow
The following parameters are specified using the set_rail_analysis_mode command for the PG SEB flow or the set_signal_em_analysis_mode command for the signal SEB flow:

- -check_thermal_aware_em
- –enable_seb
- –env_temperature
- -read_detail_delta_temperature_file
- -seb_lifetime
- –seb_table
- –seb_temperature
- –use_rms_delta_t
For detailed information on these parameters, refer to Voltus Text Command Reference .

title: Setting up and Running Power EM and Signal EM Analyses for FIT Calculation

lines: This section describes how to set up the power EM and signal EM analysis modes, and then calculate FIT.
Required Input Files

- Design data (LEF, netlist, DEF, SPEF, SDC)
- Power-grid libraries (.cl)
- Current files created during static/dynamic power calculation
In addition to these generic inputs for the EM analysis flow, the following specific inputs are required ( provided by foundry ):

- ICT-EM file - this file contains the EM model/rules to calculate the EM limits for power net EM analysis.
- Cell thermal resistance (Rth) file - this file contains the list of cells and their corresponding thermal resistance value that is used to calculate FEOL delta T.
- Thermal coupling coefficients - these parameters specify the thermal coupling factor between devices and metal layers.
Procedure
To perform power EM and signal EM analyses, complete the following steps:
Section 1: Self-Heat Analysis

1. Load the design data. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_lib $lib 
 
 read_verilog $verilog 
 
 set_top_module $top_module 
 
 read_def $def 
 
 read_sdc $sdc
2. Specify the MMMC mode settings to apply the power EM settings. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 create_library_set -name libs_min -timing $lib 
 
 create_rc_corner -name nominal -qx_tech_file $qrcTechfile 
 
 create_delay_corner -name corner_min -library_set libs_min -rc_corner nominal 
 
 create_constraint_mode -name setup_func_mode -sdc_files $sdc 
 
 create_analysis_view -constraint_mode setup_func_mode -name setup_func -delay_corner corner_min 
 
 create_analysis_view -constraint_mode setup_func_mode -name hold_func -delay_corner corner_min 
 
 set_analysis_view -setup [list setup_func] -hold [list hold_func] 
 
 set_analysis_mode -analysisType onChipVariation -checkType hold 
 
 read_spef -rc_corner nominal SPEF/top.spef 
 
 set_design_mode -process 7 
 
 set_delay_cal_mode
3. Optional. Define the static power analysis mode, and specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. If you have the instance power file, the static power analysis step is not required. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode -method static 
 
 set_power_output_dir static_db 
 
 report_power -outfile static_power.rpt
4. Define the dynamic power analysis mode, and specify the sub-directory that will contain the power results. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -disable_static false \ 
 
 -write_static_currents true \ 
 
 -binary_db_name dynvectorlessPower.db \ 
 
 -create_binary_db true \ 
 
 -method dynamic_vectorless \ 
 
 set_power_output_dir dynamic_db
5. You can define default activities for the unassigned nets. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_default_switching_activity -global_activity 0.15 \ 
 
 -clock_gates_output_ratio 2.0
6. Specify the activity file. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 propagate_activity -set_net_freq true 
 
 write_tcf out.tcf 
 
 
 
 write_tcf is an optional command that is specified to perform sanity checks.
7. Run power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 report_power
8. Optional. If you do not load the CPF file, use the set_pg_nets command to define the power nets in the design, and assign attributes to these nets. Then, use set_rail_analysis_domain to define the power domains. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.72 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.08 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name ALL -pwrnets {VDD} -gndnets VSS
9. Specify the location of the current files generated during dynamic power analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_data -format current -scale 1 \ 
 
 -bias_voltage 1.0\ 
 
 $dynamic_current_list
10. Define the location of the voltage sources for each power/ground net. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD -format xy -file ../PLOC/VDD.ploc 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../PLOC/VSS.ploc
11. Specify the parameters for power RMS analysis: 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode \ 
 
 -method dynamic \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 - power_grid_library $pgv\ 
 
 -temperature 125\ 
 
 -ict_em_models $ictem 
 
 
 
 Dynamic and RMS analyses are required to calculate Joule heat.
12. Perform self-heat analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_self_heat –domain ALL \ 
 
 -alpha_parameters {{M1 $alpha_factor1 $alpha_factor2}...} \ 
 
 -beta_parameters {$beta_factor1 $beta_factor2 $beta_factor3}\ 
 
 -cell_thermal_resistance_file TRF.txt\ 
 
 -instance_power_file ./static_db/static_power.rpt \ 
 
 -instance_delta_temperature_file Inst_Delta_Temp_Report.txt \ 
 
 -tile_delta_temperature_file sh_ttm.txt \ 
 
 -tiles {10 10} \ 
 
 -detail_delta_temperature_file power_detail.rpt \ 
 
 -report_conn_pin_wire self_heat.rpt
Section 2: Power FIT Analysis

1. Define the rail analysis mode with the SEB parameters. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_rail_analysis_mode\ 
 
 -method static \ 
 
 -accuracy hd \ 
 
 -temperature 125 \ 
 
 -power_grid_library $pgv \ 
 
 -enable_seb true \ 
 
 -seb_table seb_table.txt \ 
 
 -env_temperature 85 \ 
 
 -em_threshold 0.0 \ 
 
 -check_thermal_aware_em true \ 
 
 -read_detail_delta_temperature_file power_detail.rpt \ 
 
 -ict_em_models $ictem
2. Specify the power nets, power domain, location of the voltage sources, and static current files created during power analysis:. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VDD -voltage 0.9 -threshold 0.7 
 
 set_pg_nets -net VSS -voltage 0.0 -threshold 0.05 
 
 set_rail_analysis_domain -name ALL -pwrnets {VDD} -gndnets {VSS} 
 
 set_power_data -format current { \ 
 
 static_db/static_VDD.ptiavg \ 
 
 static_db/static_VSS.ptiavg \ 
 
 } 
 
 set_power_pads -net VDD -format xy -file ../PLOC/VDD.ploc 
 
 set_power_pads -net VSS -format xy -file ../PLOC/VSS.ploc
3. Run rail analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 analyze_rail -type domain -output static_rail core 
 
 
 
 The Voltus console will display rail analysis statistics, as shown in the following snippet: 
 Rail Analysis completed successfully. 
 
 Finished Rail Analysis at 11:56:53 08/20/2018 (cpu=0:00:20, real=0:00:34, peak mem=1218.10MB) 
 Current Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution resource usage: (total cpu=0:03:56, real=0:58:07, mem=1218.10
Section 3: Signal FIT Analysis

1. Run signal EM analysis. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_signal_em_analysis_mode 
 
 -method {avg} \ 
 
 -avgRecovery 0 \ 
 
 -detailed \ 
 
 -useQrcTech \ 
 
 -use_db_freq \ 
 
 -report seb_reduce.rpt \ 
 
 -enable_seb \ 
 
 -seb_table seb_table.txt \ 
 
 -env_temperature 85 \ 
 
 -seb_lifetime 43800 \ 
 
 -check_thermal_aware_em \ 
 
 -read_detail_delta_temperature_file power_detail.rpt \ 
 
 -ict_em_models $ictem 
 
 verify_AC_limit 
 
 
 
 
 
 The Voltus console will display the number of violations, as shown in the following snippet: 
 Num Violations: 72 
 
 **************End VerifyACLimit**************** 
 (CPU Time =0:03:56 MEM=23.020M)
Power SEB Report
The rail output directory contains the power SEB report:

data:
For each run, the state directory is generated in the rail output directory. The directory has the default name < domainName >_< temperature >_dynamic_< X > for the dynamic analysis run, and < domainName >_< temperature >_avg_< X > for the static analysis run. 
 
 < domainName > will be replaced with ALL if all domains are to be analyzed ( analyze_rail -output dir -type domain ALL ), or with < netName > if a power net is to be analyzed. 
 
 Following is an example of the report path: ALL_125C_avg_1/Reports/VDD/VDD.rj.avg.rpt

lines: Sample filename: VDD.rj.avg.rpt
Format:
Format Description:
I/Ilimit specifies the current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
I(mA) specifies the current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Ilimit(mA) specifies the current limit value based on DC EM rules that is provided by the foundry. It depends upon the width and thickness of the wire segment.
Layer specifies the metal/via layer name on which the resistor is located.
Location {X1 Y1 X2 Y2} specifies the coordinates of the resistor.

ShortLength is also known as "Blech length" or "Path length". It can be explained as "the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer".
ShortLength is the drawn length of the longest continuous same metal * layout scale factor (from qrctechFile).
Width specifies the width of the resistor.
Via Area is the drawn dimension via area in square microns.
Via Cuts is the number of via cuts.

Fix Width/ Via The “Fix Width/Via” is recalculated by modifying the existing via width to eliminate the EM violation. If I/Ilimit is greater than 1, which is a violation, Fix Width/Via will be: violation * via width
Current Direction is applicable only to via layers where "D" denotes down and "U" denotes up.

Benefit Factor When certain constraints (blue-highlighted - “L>3W<0.4”) are met, its EM limit can be 2X, 3X, or multiple X of the base EM limit. These multiples are called Benefit Factor (red-highlighted in the following example - “1”, "2").
Here, W is the wire width and L is the Blech length.
J(A/cm^2) is the current density value.
Jmax(A/cm^2) is the current density limit.
ResistorID is the unique ID assigned to a resistor.
DT_FEOL is the FEOL self-heat value derived from the thermal resistance of cells.
DT_RMS is the Joule-heat value derived from the resistor Irms current.
EM_Temperature is the temperature within delta_T used to calculate the Ilimit.
SEB_Temperature is the temperature used to update MTF with respect to the ambient temperature.
Sdc is the DC EM severity ratio calculated for the resistor. This value is based on the user-specified env_temperature.
MTF is the median time to failure that is calculated at seb_temperature by considering delta_T used for EM analysis.
T (seb_temperature) = T (env_temperature) + delta_T

- without self-heating 
 delta_T = delta_T_RMS
  - with self-heating 
 delta_T = delta_T_RMS + delta_T_SHE
FIT is the failure in time calculated for each resistor of metal and via.
Reference_FIT_R is the resistor that is used as a reference.
Sample Report: The snapshot of the report has been split into 2 parts, Part A and Part B.
Part A of the report
Part B of the same report
The total FIT value is displayed in the header of VDD.rj.avg.rpt as following:
Signal SEB Report
By default, the current working directory contains the violations report file:

data:
This file contains all the violations arranged in the descending order of the ratio of current calculated to the current limit. The violations are marked by ‘* ’ at the end of the calculated current values. When the result shows that the current calculated is higher than the limit, a violation can be seen for that net. 
 
 If there are no violations, there will be no entry in the report.
The file contains the detailed report for all the nets, and is generated when the -detailed parameter is specified.

lines: Sample filename: seb_signal.detailed.rpt
Format:
Format Description:
Iavg/Limit specifies the current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool divided by the current limit value provided by the foundry.
Iavg specifies the actual current value that flows through the metal/via resistor that is calculated by the tool.
Limit(mA) specifies the current limit value that is provided by the foundry depending upon its width and thickness.

Width/CutArea If the wire segment is metal, it is calculated as the actual width perpendicular to the current flow of the wire segment. If the wire segment is via, it is calculated as the area of the cut in the via.

FixWidth/FixCutNum is the width (for metal segment) or cutNum (for via) required to fix the violation. When there is a violation, it is the new width of the metal wire or the new number of cuts of via (drawn area).
Cap is the capacitance of the wire segment.
Layer specifies the metal/via layer name on which the resistor is located.
(X1 Y1 X2 Y2) specifies the coordinates of the resistor.
PathLength of a wire segment is the distance traced from the first pin shape of the driver to the center of the wire segment.
Blechlength is the longest and continuous centerline path from edge to edge among the connected wire shapes on the same metal layer.
Current Direction is applicable only to via layers where "D" denotes down and "U" denotes up.
FIT is the failure in time calculated for each resistor of metal and via.
ID is the original resistor ID.
Ref_ID is the ID of the resistor that is used as a reference.
SdcSEB is the DC EM severity ratio calculated for the resistor. This value is based on user-specified env_temperature.
TdcSEB is the temperature used to update MTF with respect to the ambient temperature.
dTrmsSEB is the Joule-heat value derived from the resistor Irms current for SEB temperature.
MtfNew is the median time to failure that is calculated at seb_temperature by considering delta_T used for EM analysis.
T (seb_temperature) = T (env_temperature) + delta_T

- without self-heating 
 delta_T = delta_T_RMS
  - with self-heating 
 delta_T = delta_T_RMS + delta_T_SHE
Tdc is the temperature within delta_T used to calculate the Ilimit.
dTrms is the Joule-heat value derived from the resistor Irms current.
Sample Report: The snapshot of the report has been split into 2 parts, Part A and Part B.
Part A of the report
Part B of the same report
The total FIT value is displayed at the end of the seb_signal_detailed.rpt file as the following:

The major portion of the total FIT at the design level is from the power and ground nets. The portion from the signal nets is usually negligible because of the small average current due to the behavior of the rise and fall currents canceling each other.
To visualize FIT values using the rail results viewer, complete the following steps:

1. Load the design data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_lib -lef $lefs 
 
 read_lib $lib 
 
 read_verilog $verilog 
 
 set_top_module $top_module 
 
 read_def $def 
 
 
 
 
 
 GUI : Select File - Read Design . 
 This step is required only if FIT analysis is running in a different Voltus session. If the analysis is running in the same session, perform step 2.
2. Load the rail data through the command line or through the GUI. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 read_power_rail_results -rail_directory VDD_125C_avg_1 
 
 
 
 GUI :
  1. Select Power Rail - Power Rail Plots . 
 The Power Rail Plots form appears. This form is the main window for displaying the rail analysis results.
  2. Click the DB Setup button. 
 The Power & Rail Setup form appears.
  3. You can enter the rail output directory or click Browse next to the Rail Database field, and select a location from the Rail State Directory browser window.
  4. Click OK .
3. In the Power Rail Plots form, select the Rail radio button and select the fit rail analysis plot type from the drop-down list. 
 Tcl command : 
 
 
 
 set_power_rail_display -plot fit 
 
 
 
 The fit plot will be overlaid on the design.

- Overview
- Library Generation
- Power Analysis
- Rail Analysis
Body biasing is used at advanced nodes for the following reasons:

- Avoid latch-up
- Optimize sub-threshold leakage
- Optimize performance

Generally, WELL contacts in a design are tied to core VDD and VSS. In order to optimize sub-threshold leakage power consumption, body voltage is applied to control Vth of the transistor using dedicated body bias supply rails. Reverse body bias is applied to low Vth devices during idle mode to reduce leakage, and forward body bias is applied to high Vth devices during active mode to achieve higher performance. The body voltage is generally much lower than the voltage of the core supply rails because high reverse body bias will produce higher leakage, and high forward body bias can result in excessive current through device.
Example of back-bias supply with NMOS is shown below.
When VPW < Source , Vth increases, Id and leakage decreases.
Following is a pictorial representation of well tap cells and body bias connectivity:

For body bias analysis, cells with body bias pins needs to be included during power-grid view library generation. The body bias pins should be defined in the cell LEF. Following is an example of a standard cell (CORE) LEF, where WELLPIN has no connections within the cell, and would be an additional physical-only LEF pin:
Following is an example of a tap cell with body bias pin:

Since the WELL layers are not processed during power-grid view library generation, you will have to map these layers to the diffusion layer in order to extract connectivity. This mapping can be specified using LEF-Technology layer map file specified using the following command:
set_pg_library_mode –lef_layermap < LEF:QRC technology mapping file >
An example of LEF layer map is given below:
You need to define bias pins in the library using the following command:
set_pg_library_mode –power_pins {pin1 voltage1} –ground_pins {pin}
set_advanced_pg_library_mode -process_bulk_diffusion_ports true
Once library generation is completed, check < library >.report to ensure that body bias pins are present for these cells and current taps are generated.
Power Analysis

The bias pin definitions are read from the liberty (dotlibs) libraries. Generally, no additional commands or parameters are needed to calculate power for designs with body bias as long as the liberty files are properly characterized with body bias power domain definitions.
Example of a liberty file with body bias pin definitions is as follows:

With body bias pin definitions in liberty, the power and ground pins with type pwell and nwell will be regarded as body bias pins. The absolute voltage of these bias pins will be used as reference voltage to calculate leakage and internal power for these pins.

If body bias pins are defined in LEF, but missing in the liberty file, power analysis cannot rely on LEF to infer if a PG pin belongs to a primary power domain or a body bias domain. In this case, power analysis calculates power for the cell and splits it between primary domain (VDD:VSS) and body bias domain (VNW:VPW) which is not desirable. To avoid this, you can specify body bias pins using the following command parameter during power analysis:
set_power_analysis_mode –bulk_pins { VNW VPW }
In this case, if a liberty file does not have body bias definition, power analysis does not distribute power to the body bias domain.
Rail Analysis

Once the power-grid view library is generated for cells with body bias pins as described above and power analysis is run to compute power dissipation on these pins, you can run static and dynamic IRdrop analysis on body bias network. Through library generation, current taps (sinks) are added on diffusion ports.
In order to extract body bias PG network connectivity to diffusion ports of cell, and process current taps on body bias pins, specify the following command parameters during rail analysis:

- set_rail_analysis_mode -process_bulk_pins_for_body_bias true 
 Default: false 
 This parameter processes current taps assigned to bulk pins in power-grid view library. It is used during body bias analysis where current taps are assigned to bulk pins.
- set_rail_analysis_mode -extractor_include ./mybbias.cmd 
 In the mybbias.cmd file, specify the following variable: 
 setvar enable_toplevel_diff_extract true 
 Default: false 
 Extracts ports defined on the diffusion layer in power-grid view library. This variable is used during body bias analysis to extract connectivity in the diffusion layer.
Following is an example script for rail analysis:
Example of R network of a body bias power net:

title: Leakage Power Scaling using Liberate Library Files

lines: At lower technology nodes, leakage power is a substantial component of the total power in a design. Therefore, it is important to accurately calculate the leakage power at different voltage and temperature corners. This section focuses on the use model for running power calculation for leakage power scaling in Voltus using Liberate generated liberty files.
The flow for generating the liberty files using Liberate to enable leakage power scaling is given below:
