title: RTL Activity File Flow in Static Power Calculation

lines: RTL Activity File Flow in Static Power Calculation

The toggle data from RTL simulations are applied to gate-level netlists in Voltus for power analysis. You can use the map_activity_file command of Voltus to specify instance name mapping between RTL netlist and GATE level netlist. The mapping file used for instance name mapping is generated by Conformal.
The following diagram illustrates the flow:

Conformal flattens and models the Golden and Revised designs and automatically maps the key points. In Conformal, the REPORT MAPPED POINTS command is used to display the mapped points that were automatically identified or added with the Conformal software. The mapping file generated using the report mapped points command is used in Voltus to increase the annotation of the RTL activity file. This mapping is required to annotate waveform from the RTL switching activity (that is, VCD), to do power calculation on a post-layout netlist. RTL level VCD or TCF are obtained from simulation on the RTL Verilog netlist. The mapping file has information for each mapped point from the Golden and Revised design, and a summary of all Golden and Revised mapped points. The tool assumes that the Golden (G) refers to the RTL netlist and Revised (R) refers to the GATE level netlist.
By default, Conformal uses the primitive instance name of the D flip-flops (DFF) during reporting. For example:

Names like U$1 are pin names of the DFF cells referred by Conformal when creating mapping. Sometimes, it is useful to know which library pin a particular DFF instance is referencing, especially when dealing with multibit DFFs. You can use the set gate report -USE_LIBRARY_PIN_NAME command option before generating a report. With this option, Conformal uses the library pin name instead of the primitive name in a gate report, if the gate comes from the library.
In the TCL mode, the command option will be:
Note the following when using the -USE_LIBRARY_PIN_NAME option:

- The option only affects the gate netlist and not the RTL because there are no library pins in the RTL to report.
- This option affects all the report commands. For example, report mapped point , report gate , report path , write map point , and so on.
- Use this option before saving a mapping file ( report mapped points -long ) for use in Voltus because the mapping file will contain the library pin name. However, this mapping file cannot be read back in Conformal.
- This command option is available in Conformal version 15.10-s180 or later.

Conformal makes use of the SET NAMING RULE command to specify special text handling rules, such as hierarchical separators, tristate naming rules, register naming rules, array delimiters, instance names, or variable names. This command makes the mapping more runtime efficient and reliable. However, these name changes can make the mapping file unusable.

To resolve the issues with the mapping file, add the comment syntax to block of the code related to the SET NAMING RULE command in the Conformal script (and force LEC to map renamed signals by function, and not by names). Although this produces a useable mapping file, it leads to higher runtime for Conformal.

This section lists the LibGen file formats in alphabetical order.
Current Region File

A current region file is the definition of current for an area bounded by x,y coordinates. It includes a list of regions and the amount of current to be distributed within them for the power-grid. This will override any values calculated by Voltus library simulator. The dynamic current region file is used for dynamic power-grid view generation.
Sample file name : current_region
Command : set_pg_library_mode {-current_distribution current_region region_file }
Format :
Format Description :

UNIT defines the default scale to be used with the current and capacitance values read from the current region file. These scales apply to any value that is not followed by an explicit unit specification. Valid current values are: A (Amps), mA (miliAmps), uA (microAmps), and nA (nanoAmps). Valid capacitance values are: F (Farads), mF (miliFarads), uF (microfarads), nF (nanofarads), pF (picoFarads), and fF (femtoFarads).

CELL specifies the name of the cell. All the following NET , LAYER , and REGION commands will apply to this CELL until another CELL specification is encountered. Wildcards can be used in the cell_name .

PROP [FLAT | HIER] If the keyword FLAT is applied, LibGen will not perform hierarchical tracing for the given net in this cell. If the keyword HIER is presented, LibGen will perform hierarchical tracing for the given net in this cell. This provides the ability for LibGen to automatically specify a current value to all instances of a cell in the design hierarchy. The assumption is that all of these instances of the cell will consume the same power. The default is HIER .

NET specifies that all the LAYER and REGION commands following it applies to the net_name . This stays in effect until another NET or CELL specification is encountered. Wildcards can be used in the net_name .

LAYER specifies that all the REGION commands following it apply to the layer_name . This stays in effect until another LAYER , NET , or CELL command is encountered. The layer_name must be a library layer name. If the layer is not found, LibGen will issue an error message and exit. You must provide a valid layer name for LibGen to run. If you specified a valid layer, but LibGen does not find any via in the assigned current region, LibGen will issue a warning message, but the process will still continue and exit successfully. In this case, you may get a 0 current value for the specified cell.

REGION specifies that the current value and optionally the capacitance value to be associated with the rectangular region specified by the bounding box ( x1 y1 x2 y2 ). Current taps will be created at the locations of the vias or contacts specified by the most recent LAYER command. These current taps will be attached to the layer that is below the specified via or contact layer. Coordinate values are assumed to be in the GDSII coordinate units and will be interpreted according to the scale and precision settings contained in the GDSII file (or spice netlist with device XY location) that contains the cell. The value of the current drawn by each current tap will be 1/n of the value specified for the current region, where n is the number of taps within the current region. The second variation of the REGION command without the bounding box is valid only if there is only one current region. In this case, LibGen can automatically use the bounding box of the cell for the region.
Example :
Cell Pin Net Map File

A file containing the mappings between a cell's LEF pin names and the internal names of the net to which those pins are attached. This file is used when the LEF file contains multiple unique power pins that are connected to the same power net.
Sample file name : pin_map.txt
Command : set_advanced_pg_library_mode -cell_pinnet_map_file filename
Format :
Format Description :
cellname specifies the name of the cell. net_name specifies the internal name of the net. pin_name specifies the name of the LEF pin.
Example :
Cell Accura Data File

The Cell Accura Data file contains data about the electrical characteristics of a cell. It supports include statements for hierarchical descriptions. Specifying clock, instance power, and toggle count data is optional. Give only one specification for each type of cell. If you give more than one specification, LibGen issues a warning message and uses the last specification given.
Sample file name : cell_char.file
Command : set_advanced_pg_library_mode -cell_ accura_data_file filename
Format :
Format Description :
CELL cellname specifies the name of the cell.
FREQ= value specifies the clock frequency for the cell used to compute the average currents. The default unit is hertz.
CLK_FILE= filename specifies the name of the file containing the clock nodes, subcircuit definitions, and subcircuit instance names and their frequencies. You can specify multiple file names.

FA_VALUE= value specifies the default nodal activity to be used instead of Accura. This value is used at each node unless it is overridden by a value in the file specified by FA_FILE . It is given as an activity factor. A value of 0.20 would be 20% of the default frequency.

FA_FILE= filename specifies the name of the file containing nodes and their activity. It is given as an activity factor. A value of 0.20 would be 20% of the default frequency. You can specify multiple file names.
AVG_PWR= value specifies the total average power dissipation of the cell connected to the voltage source. The default unit is watts.
AVG_PWR_FILE= filename specifies the name of the file containing the subcircuit definitions, subcircuit instance names, and average power consumption. You can specify multiple file names.
Example :
Clock File Format
The clock file has the format shown in the following example:
CLK is a keyword.

- If you use the CLK keyword, the node name is treated as the root of a clock running at the given frequency.
- If you use the INST keyword, all input and output nodes--that is, node names on the instance card--are set to toggle at the specified frequency. The activity of the nodes internal to the subcircuit is computed by using Accura. If you give an optional activity value, Accura is disabled, and the activities of all the nodes are set to the specified value; all nodes toggle at the time represented by the product of the specified frequency and the activity.
- If you use the CELL keyword, all instances of the cell are processed instead of a single instance.
CLK has precedence over INST , and INST overrides CELL information to resolve redundant specifications.
Frequency Activity File Format
The frequency activity file has the format shown in the following example. The column on the left shows the net names, and the column on the right shows the activity.
Average Power File Format
The average power file has the format shown in the following example:

The CELL keyword specifies that the name following it is a subcircuit definition and that all instances of the cell dissipate the same average power. If the file does not contain the CELL keyword, the name is an instance of a subcircuit dissipating the specified average power.
Cell Decap File
A file containing a list of cells and the decoupling capacitance value for each.
Sample file name : decaps
Command : set_pg_library_mode - cell_decap_file filename
Format :
Format Description :
UNIT is optional. If no UNIT statement is present at the beginning of the file, all capacitance values not followed by a unit specification are assumed to be in femtofarads.
CELL specifies the total decoupling capacitance for a cell. The CELL specification stays in effect until another CELL specification is encountered.
NET specifies the decoupling capacitance for a net.
Example :
Cell Well Cap File
A file containing a list of cells and the well capacitance value for each.
Sample file name : wellcaps
Command : set_advanced_pg_library_mode -well_cap_file filename
Format :
Format Description :
UNIT is optional. If no UNIT statement is present at the beginning of the file, all capacitance values not followed by a unit specification are assumed to be in femtofarads.
CELL specifies the well capacitance value for a cell. The CELL specification stays in effect until another CELL specification is encountered.
Example :
Circuit Include File

Specifies the name of the Voltus library simulator input file. This file is used for current estimation and contains information about the cells in the design, additional MODEL cards for passive devices like resistors, and additional DC voltages.
Sample file name : simulator.ckt
Command : set_advanced_pg_library_mode -circuit_include_file filename
Format :
Spice format
Example :
GDS Layermap File

A layer mapping file is required for the Rail Analysis library generation flow. The GDS layermap file contains the mapping information to map the GDS layer number to the layer names in the technology file. This file is required when library generation reads a GDS file. This file format is also used by the GDS2DEF utility ( convert_gds_to_def ).
Sample file name : gds.layermap
Command : set_pg_library_mode - gds_layermap filename
Format :
Format Description :
LibGen Type specifies the type of library layer, for example, metal or polycide. The various layer types are:

- diff - diffusion layer
- poly - polycide layer
- via - via layer
- metal - metal layer
- layer - layer name used in the technology file 
 Use this for layers that are not diffusion, metal, poly, or via layers. You can use it a number of times for each layer name to assign a different GDSII layer number to a cell library layer.
Library Layer Name specifies the name of the library layer to map to the GDSII layer number. The library layers are technology layers which can be dumped using the Techgen –techinfo command.
Keyword specifies the keyword for the GDS layer mapping.
GDSII Layer Number specifies the layer number of the GDSII layer.

GDS Datatype specifies the optional number of the subclass for the GDSII layer. If you do not specify a datatype, the software uses all datatypes for that GDSII layer. If GDS datatype is not defined, the software considers it as a regular layout routing layer where all the shapes will be extracted.
The GDS layer may have additional information/attribute assigned using the following keywords:

- PORT : Converts text labels in this layer to ports.
- TEXT_ONLY : Includes only the text in the named layer and ignores all geometries.
- NO_TEXT : Excludes text in the named layer.
Example :
LEF-DEF Layermap File

A layer mapping file is required for the Rail Analysis library generation flow. The LEF-DEF layermap file contains the mapping information to map the layer names in the LEF file to the layer names in the technology file. This file is required when LibGen reads a LEF file.
Sample file name : lefdef.layermap
Command : set_pg_library_mode -lef_layermap filename
Format :
Format Description :
LibGen Type specifies the type of library layer, for example, metal or polycide.
Library Layer Name specifies the name of the library layer to map to the LEF/DEF layer.
Keyword specifies the keyword for the LEF-DEF layer mapping.
LEFDEF Layer Name specifies the name of the LEF/DEF layer to map to the library layer.
Example :
Pad Voltage Source File
A file containing the locations of the voltage sources to be connected to the pad cells.
Sample file name : voltsrc_533a.txt
Command : set_advanced_pg_library_mode -source_location_file file
Format :
Format Description :
This source location file syntax includes the following parameters:

- MIN_SPACING spacing_value 
 DENSITY density_value 
 These values are used when you specify the source location as a rectangle. LibGen attempts to generate a grid of voltage sources with the dimensions density_value x density_value y in the rectangle. However, if this grid would place the voltage sources closer together than the value set by MIN_SPACING , LibGen generates fewer voltage sources to avoid violating the MIN_SPACING setting. 
 Since these variables are global to the source location file, you can specify them anywhere in the file. If you specify them more than once, LibGen uses the last setting. The default value for MIN_SPACING is 1 micron. The default value for DENSITY is 10.
- CELL cell_name 
 NET net_name 
 These specifications apply to the voltage source locations that follow until LibGen encounters a new CELL or NET line. You can specify voltage sources as single points or as rectangles. If you specify them as rectangles, LibGen generates a grid of voltage sources inside these rectangles.
- PORT|DETAILED 
 The source location file can specify voltage sources to be used with port or detailed views. If you do not use the PORT and DETAILED keywords, LibGen uses the voltage source data for detailed views. LibGen issues a warning instructing you to update the format of your source location file to include the PORT and DETAILED keywords. 
 You can specify the PORT and DETAILED keywords on the cell level, on the net level, or on an individual voltage source location definition. LibGen uses specifications at the cell level unless they are overridden at the net level. It uses specifications at the net level unless they are overridden by the individual voltage source specifications.
- lef_layer_name <PORT|DETAILED> x y [ x y ] 
 Lines beginning with a LEF layer name specify the actual location of the voltage sources. Usually you can specify this location as a single point by providing one set of values for x and y. These x and y coordinates are specified in microns and use the same coordinate system as that used for LEF ports in the LEF file. 
 Alternatively, you can specify the voltage source locations as a rectangle by giving two sets of x and y locations. In this case, LibGen attempts to generate a 10-by-10 grid of voltage sources inside this area. From a rectangle specification, it does not create voltage sources closer than 1 micron apart, so if the rectangle is less than about 10 microns on a side, it generates fewer voltage sources. 
 In the source location file, the location of the voltage sources is specified by using a LEF layer name and location in LEF coordinate space. However, because of the way LibGen uses this data internally, no LEF geometries are actually required to exist at the location where the voltage sources are to be placed. LibGen uses this data internally with the layout of the block (that is, the GDS data) during detailed power-grid view generation, so it only requires that there be layout (GDS) geometries at the location of the voltage sources. Similarly, the LEF layer name used in the source location file does not actually have to be included in any of the input LEF files; it must be defined only in the layer map used by LibGen.
Example :

- Following is an example of the contents of the voltage source location file: 
 CELL an2_120
NET vcc
 MET1 11.0 16.0 
 MET1 0.0 15.0 22.1 17.5
NET gnd
 MET1 11.0 1.0
 MET1 0 -0.5 22.1 2.0
 
CELL xor
NET vcc
 MET1 10.0 5.0
NET gnd
 MET1 10.0 1.0
- Following is an example file that uses only cell-level specifications to define voltage sources for the port view of the NAND1 cell: 
 CELL NAND1
PORT
 NET VDD
 MET1 5 95
 MET2 5 95
 NET VSS
 MET1 5 5
 MET2 5 5
- Following is an example file that uses net-level specifications to define voltage sources for both the port and detailed views of the NAND1 cell: 
 CELL NAND1
 NET VDD
 PORT
 MET1 5 95
 MET1 DETAILED 5 92

Port File
A file containing the details about the label of a switched rail. It contains all information on ports that you want to create and load.
Sample file name : fire_ports
Command : set_advanced_pg_library_mode -add_port_labels filename
Format :
The format of the file is as follows:
Format Description :
MACRO The MACRO statement is followed by a cell name.
PIN The PIN statement is followed by a pin name and a direction. These are the valid directions:

- B--Bidirectional
- I--Input
- O--Output

Trigger File

A file containing the trigger conditions for each cell. The trigger file contains information on the input vectors and waveform triggering input pin information. The trigger condition is used to identify the functional mode (read/write/idle) of the custom macro in the Power and Rail Analysis flow.
Sample file name : trig.cell
Command : set_pg_library_mode -current_distribution {dynamic_simulation trigger_file }
Format :
The format of the trigger file is:

data:
SIMULATION -> Vector-based flow 
 DOTLIB -> Liberty based Flow 
 PWL - User-defined PWL flow 
 FSDB - Spice total current output captured in the FSDB format 
 DATASHEET - Datasheet details of any memory 
 SPICE_DECK_BASED - User-specified Spice deck flow
MODE_NAME <mode1> CONDITIONAL_INPUT < conditional input statement for cellname1 >
Mode name and conditional input statement for the cell. Conditional input of current for the given mode.
Conditional pin statement for the cell. Signal pin for which the tool should rise or fall.
Simulation vector detail in the given mode.
User-specified PWL file that contains information about the PWL waveforms (power/ground currents)
DATASHEET_PARAMETER < { pg_pin1 Capacitance Leakage } { pg_pin2 Capacitance Leakage }>
DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW < time > DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW < time > DATASHEET_DELAY < time > DATASHEET_PARAMETER < { power_pin cap leakage_pwr } >

DATASHEET_INPUT_SLEW - Clock input slew of the conditional pin 
 DATASHEET_OUTPUT_SLEW - Rising slew of the output pin Q 
 DATASHEET_DELAY - Mode delay as the read_access and write_access time 
 DATASHEET_PARAMETER - Datasheet parameters include power pin voltage, load capacitance, and leakage power. Load capacitance is mode dependent.
FSDB_PATH < path > FSDB_NET_NAME <net> [< generic_name >] FSDB_START_TIME < time > FSDB_END_TIME < time > FSDB_HIER_CHAR < hier_char, for hier DB >

FSDB file path 
 Power net name and its FSDB probe name 
 FSDB start and end time 
 Hierarchical separator for hierarchical database. The default separator is "." 
 
 Single FSDB file having total current for ALL power/ground pins with all mode current in it

Time ranges for different modes in the PWL-based current characterization flow. These keywords allow you to use a single PWL input file for multiple modes and specify different time ranges for these modes in which the dynamic PGV is being created.
DECK_FILE < filename > SIM_OPTIONS < option_name > FSDB_FILE < filename >

Specify a single deck file for all modes, or a unique deck file for each mode. 
 User-specified Spectre simulator options. The default value of this keyword is +aps . 
 Voltus executes the Spice deck and reads the current waveform generated by simulation. After simulation of the deck file, an FSDB file is generated. You must specify the FSDB file name with the FSDB_FILE keyword.

SIMULATOR <type of simulator> DOTLIB_LIST {a list of memory dotlibs files} MODE_COUNT MODE_CONFIG_FILE <file_path> SIM_START_TIME <time> SIM_STOP_TIME <time> SIM_STEP_SIZE <step_size> STIMULUS_FILE <file_path> INCLUDE_FILE <file_path>

SIMULATOR - Specifies the simulator to be used for characterizing current waveforms for Macro EM views. You can specify the following simulators: Accelerated Parallel Simulator (APS), SPECTRE, and UltraSim. APS is the default simulator. 
 DOTLIB_LIST - Specifies a list of memory dotlibs. 
 MODE_COUNT - Specifies the maximum number of modes to be generated. Valid values are either a positive number or `all'. `all' means stimulus for all power groups are to be generated. The default value is 4. If you set this parameter to all, it may result in high runtime as it may generate too many modes. 
 MODE_CONFIG_FILE - Specifies the file containing extra information for memory cell. This file contains the following information - signal slew, signal active value and memory write information. 
 SIM_START_TIME - Specifies the simulation start time. The default unit is in nanoseconds (ns). 
 SIM_STOP_TIME - Specifies the simulation stop time. The default unit is in nanoseconds (ns).

SIM_STEP_SIZE - Specifies the step size for power-grid analysis. The default value of step size is 20 picoseconds (ps). This parameter supports other units such as, ns and fs. This parameter supports unit-prefixes (pico, femto, nano) combined with units of measure (ps, fs, ns), but does not support units itself ("second", "Ohm", "Farad") without unit-prefix. 
 
 STIMULUS_FILE - Specifies the stimulus file containing the trigger information for all input ports. 
 
 INCLUDE_FILE - Specifies the name of the simulation include file.

Trigger File Format - Dotlib

For dotlib-based detailed dynamic view characterization, the trigger and stimulus files are automatically generated from an existing dotlib. This flow is applicable only for memory cells. Voltus generates stimuli for the worst power groups (rise_power, fall_power or power) in the specified dotlib. It reads only the internal power groups having the " when " condition and constructs stimulus vectors. Each of the generated stimulus belongs to one of the following groups:

- pin power based (this group is associated with input pin)
- arc-based (this group is associated with output pin)
The file format of the trigger file is:

If the dotlib contains arc-based power groups, you must specify MODE_CONFIG_FILE and include the memory_write condition in the memory description file. Otherwise, arc-based power groups will be skipped. The memory description file contains the following information:

- Signals slew: specify slew for signals. These slew values will be used in case rise or fall is to be applied on signal. The slew value specified using this construct takes priority over the slew value saved for the group. The default slew unit in the slews construct is ns. The syntax is: 
 slews {
<signal_1> <slew_value_1>
<signal_2> <slew_value_2>
....
}
- Signals: specify signals' active values irrespective of memory_write . The syntax is: 
 signals {
<sig_1_name> <active_value> 
<sig_2_name> <active_value> 
....
 }
where <active value> can be H or L
- Memory write information: specify information required to write data to memory through memory_write block. 
 memory_write {
clock <clock_name> <type>, where <type> can be R, F, H or L
data_in <data_bus_name>
address <address bus name>
signal <sig_1 name> <active_value>, where <active value> can be H or L
signal <sig_2 name> <active_value>, where <active value> can be H or L
....
}

This section lists the Power Analysis file formats in alphabetical order.
Instance ASCII Power File

A file containing the peak or average power-consumption values for each cell type. The values are specified in watts. It enables you to scale the cell peak power values up or down when you want to limit the peak power that an instance can draw at any time. This file is optional for both static and dynamic analysis.
Sample file name : pg22e2_cellpwr.txt
Command : set_power_data -format ascii vdd_vdd.ptipeak
Format :
Cell names cannot contain wildcards.
Format Description :
instancename specifies the instance to apply the power value to.
power_value specifies the power value in watts.
power_pin_name specifies the power pin name of the instance.
Example :
Instance Temperature File
A temperature file is used to support thermal aware leakage. You can use this file to perform temperate aware leakage and internal power calculation. This file is generated by the thermal engine.
Sample file name : temperaturefile
Command : set_inst_temperature_file filename
Format :
Format Description :
Default_temperature specifies the default temperature, in degrees.
instance_name specifies the instance to apply the temperature to.
temperature is the temperature of the specified instance.
Example :
TCF File

A toggle count format (TCF) file contains switching activity information that is needed for power optimization and power analysis in low-power synthesis. The switching activity includes the toggle count information and the probability of the net or pin being in the logic 1 state.
A TCF file contains the switching activity information in a hierarchical representation. In a hierarchical representation, the net and pin names are specified within the instance scope.
This file is used to provide activity information during power calculation and can be read in using the read_activity_file command.
Sample file name : design.tcf
Command : read_activity_file -format TCF design.tcf
Format :
Format Description :
tcfversion identifies the TCF version.
generator identifies the utility that generated this TCF file. Possible values are " BGPower Verilog PLI " and " BGPower Verilog VHDL PLI ." This parameter is optional.
genversion identifies the version of the utility that generated the TCF file.
date specifies the date and time when the data in the TCF file was created.
duration specifies the simulation capture period.
unit specifies the time unit used for the simulation duration.
instance is identified through a string. For each pin and net , the TCF file lists two strings:

- The first string represents the name (hierarchical name or name with respect to the instance).
- The second string contains a real number representing the probability of that net or pin being logic 1, and an integer representing the toggle count.

TWF File

The timing windows file (TWF) contains timing window and slew data from timing analysis of the design that is used to constrain noise analysis for more realistic results based on the timing dependency of switching signals.

TWF is required when you perform a vectorless dynamic power consumption calculation. For each net or pin, TWF contains the ranges of signal arrival times and transition times derived from the static timing analysis output by Tempus. It compresses all vectors into one result. This file is in ASCII format.
Sample file name: dtmf.twf
Command: This file can be generated using the Tempus command write_twf and can be read in using the read_twf command.
Format:
Format Description:
Header Section
The header section of a TWF file contains information relevant to the entire file, including the design name, the tool used to generate the TWF, and scaling factors for the values in the file.

The design name, date, and program entries are for documentation purposes and do not affect the meaning of the data in the rest of the file. The version, delimiters, and scaling factors do affect how the data in the file is interpreted.
Syntax
TWF Version
The VERSION construct identifies the version of the TWF specification to which the file conforms.
Syntax
Example
Design Name
The DESIGN construct specifies the name of the design to which the TWF file applies.
Syntax
Example
Date
The DATE construct indicates the date and time when the data was generated.
Syntax
Example
Program
The PROGRAM construct indicates the name of the program that created or last modified the TWF file, the version number of that program, and the company that produced the program.
Syntax
Example
Delimiters
The DELIMITERS construct specifies the characters that are used as delimiters in design names for hierarchy delimiter character, left index character and right index character.
Syntax
Example
Scaling Factors
Scaling factor constructs specify the multiplier to use to scale the values for the specified physical property.
Syntax
The default time scale is 1 second. If TIME_SCALE is specified, all delay numbers in the TWF are multiplied by the specified value. For example, 1.0E-12 corresponds to delay values in picoseconds.

The default capacitance scale is 1 Farad. If CAP_SCALE is specified, all capacitance numbers in the TWF are multiplied by the specified value. For example, 1.0E-12 corresponds to capacitance values in picofarads.

The default resistance scale is 1 ohm. If RES_SCALE is specified, all resistance numbers in the TWF are multiplied by the specified value. For example, 1.0E-3 corresponds to resistance values in milli-ohms.

The default voltage scale is 1 volt. If VOLTAGE_SCALE is specified, all voltage numbers in the TWF are multiplied by the specified value. For example, 1.0E-3 corresponds to voltage values in millivolts.
Voltage Threshold

The VOLTAGE_THRESHOLD construct specifies the two measurement points on a waveform that were used in calculating the slews in the TWF file. These measurement points are defined as a percentage of the change in voltage from the start of the transition to the end of the transition.
Syntax
Example
Lifetime
The LIFETIME construct specifies the required operating lifetime for the design in years. This value is not scaled by the TIME_SCALE statement.
Syntax
Example
Operating Conditions
The operating conditions constructs specify environment corners and related process, voltage, and temperature derating points.
Syntax
One string (representing typical) or three strings (representing minimum, typical, and maximum) or asterisks (representing placeholders) can be specified for OPERATING CONDITIONS a_string values .
One or three values can be specified for the single_or_triple_number parameters.

- When one value is specified, it applies to the typical value.
- When three values are specified, the first represents the minimum value, the second represents the typical value, and the third represents the maximum value.
Example
Default Input Slew
The DEFAULT_INPUT_SLEW construct specifies the default minimum rise, minimum fall, maximum rise, and maximum fall times for input slews for all primary input and bidirectional pins of the design.
Syntax
The slews are time values in the units defined by the TIME_SCALE . Each slew value may be specified as a numeric value or an asterisk, which serves as a placeholder.
Body Section
Statements in the body section specify timing data and constraints for the design.
Syntax
CTLF Files
The locations of the Timing Library Format (TLF) or compiled TLF (CTLF) files for the design are specified in an ext_ctlf_files statement.
Syntax
Example
Waveform
The WAVEFORM construct defines an abstract periodic waveform.
Syntax

The name of the waveform must be unique. The period describes the interval at which the waveform repeats. The edge_position gives the time at which the transition takes place relative to the start of each period. edge_position and period are represented in units defined by TIME_SCALE in the header section.
Example
Clock
Each clock is described by relating a previously defined waveform to a port (the source of that waveform). These ports are usually the roots of a clock network.
Syntax
Example
Clock Groups
Clocks within a group are identified by their waveform names. The definitions of the waveforms must precede the clock group specification.
Syntax
Example
Driver Cell Section
The DRIVER_CELL section contains information about the cells driving the input ports of the design, specifying the cell type and the slews at the driver output pins.
Syntax

The library name indicates the library that contains the driver cell. The pos_neg_edge item indicates whether the sink pin transitions on the rising ( POSEDGE ) or falling ( NEGEDGE ) of the source pin.
The input slew must be specified in the units defined by the TIME_SCALE .
port specifies the input or bidirectional port being driven.
Example

This example specifies the INBUF cell from the vixen library. Sink pin COREPIN transitions on the falling edge of source pin BONDING . An input slew of 0 applies to the falling edge of the driver output.
Driver Strength Section
The DRIVER_STRENGTH section specifies the holding resistance of the cells driving the input ports.
Syntax
The driver strength is a resistance value and must be specified in the units defined by the RES_SCALE .
External Load Construct
The EXTERNAL_LOAD construct specifies the external capacitance, or load, at the specified port (pin).
Syntax
The external load is a capacitance value in the units defined by the RESISTANCE_SCALE construct. Each a_number value may be specified as a number or an asterisk placeholder.
Example
Constant Section
The Constant section specifies all non-switching nets and their values.
Syntax

Assigns a logical constraint (0 or 1) to the net(s) or pin(s) connected to port . The rise_k value represents the rising signal source resistance and the fall_k represents the falling signal source resistance.
Example
This example sets all nets connected to usb_bus_out[21] to a logical value of 1 .
Input Slew Section
The INPUT_SLEW section specifies slews at the input ports of the design.
Syntax
Caused By Section
Nets and pins that are timing constrained have arrival times (timing windows) caused by clock pins.

- All nets/pins constrained by the same clock pin are grouped under a CAUSED_BY section for that port .
- A time-constrained net/pin can be contained under multiple CAUSED_BY sections.
Nets and pins that are not timing constrained are grouped under the CAUSED_BY NULL section.
Syntax
The following definitions apply to the CAUSED_BY parameters:

data: Rising signal arrival time. For CAUSED_BY NULL statements, this value defaults to *.
Falling signal arrival time. For CAUSED_BY NULL statements, this value defaults to *.

Value that signifies whether the net or pin is part of the clock network. 
 
 ( C = Clock; D = Data; U = Undefined; Z = Tristate)

lines: Example

In this example, NetA and NetB have their arrival times (timing windows) caused by ClkA , and NetB and NetC are caused by ClkB . NetB is constrained by multiple clocks and occurs in both CAUSED_BY sections.
Example
In this example, net NETD is not time constrained.

This section lists the Rail Analysis file formats in alphabetical order.
Dynamic Trigger File During Rail Analysis

A file that contains the trigger conditions for a detailed dynamic view block. When a detailed dynamic view block is used in a hierarchical design, it needs to be determined when this block is triggered. The dynamic trigger file is used to determine this. At the trigger time, the dynamic current waveforms of the detailed dynamic view block will be applied.
Sample file name : trigger.txt
Command : set_rail_analysis_ mode -dynamic_trigger_file filename
Format :
Format Description :

INSTANCE and CELL keywords should be used to identify whether the trigger should be applied to specific instance or all the instances of the cell name. By default it uses s (for example, " top/insta 2e-9 ").
Example :
EM Models File

The EM Models file specifies the models needed for Electromigration analysis. If you do not not specify an Electromigration model file, the software will select Electromigration models from the QRC technology file.
Sample file name: em_models.txt
Command: set_rail_analyis_mode -em_models file and set_rail_analyis_mode -process_techgen_em_rules {true | false}
Format:
Limit-based EM Model Syntax
Risk-based EM Model Syntax

em_model { 
# Width Independent parameters are not affected by width
 
[em_vcwidth <value>]
[em_W_n <value>]
[em_m <value>]
[em_n <value>]
[em_sigma <value>]

 
# Parameters applying specifically to narrow or wide wires
 
[em_A_n <value>] [em_A_w <value>] [em_Ea_n <value>] [em_Ea_w <value>] 

# Width/Area PWL parameters
 
[em_A <value>]
[em_Ea <value>]
}
Format Description:
Parameters for both Risk-based and Limit-based Analysis

data:
Optional parameter for Via layers that defines the width of a standard via in microns. The analysis tool assumes that the via is square, so it squares a side to obtain the area. Do not specify this parameter for conductor or diffusion layers. If PWL value/area pairs or an equation is defined then vcwidth is not required to calculate current density limits and it is ignored. 
 
 Default: 0 for metal layers and 0.5 for cut layers Range: Any integer or floating point number 
 
 vcwidth is specified as microns instead of meters to be consistent with the rest of the ICT file.

Optional parameter that specifies the wire width in microns below which a line is considered narrow. Note: all lines are considered wide unless you specify otherwise using this parameter. Default: 0 Range: Any integer or floating point number.

lines: Parameters for Risk-based Analysis

data:

 Default: 700 Range: A positive integer or floating point number

 Default: 700 Range: A positive integer or floating point number

Specifies the value of A to use for wide lines. Its unit is specified as: A 2 h/kcm 4 . 
 
 Default: 45000 Range: A positive integer or floating point number

Specifies the activation energy value to use for narrow lines. Its unit is specified in electron volts.

Optional parameter that specifies the temperature coefficient used by Black’s equation for electromigration risk assessment.

Optional parameter that specifies the current exponent used by Black’s equation for electromigration risk assessment.

Optional parameter that specifies the value to be used in the lognormal failure rate model for predicting the lifetime of a circuit.

lines: Parameters for Limit-based Analysis:

data:
Optional parameter that specifies the maximum AC average current density which can either be defined as a single value, a PWL table, or an equation ( EQU ). 
 
 For a conductor or diffusion layer the PWL table is defined as the current density limit value based on the wire width. For via layers it is defined as the current density limit value based on the via area. 
 
 Default: None 
 
 Range: A positive integer or floating point number, a PWL table, or an equation ( EQU ). The value should be specified before any qualifiers ( jmax_factor , current_direction , and via_range ). The qualifiers themselves are not order dependent and may be specified as needed.

This optional parameter is essentially the same as em_jmax_ac_avg , with the exception that it is specified for narrow wires (as defined by em_W_n ). In addition, this parameter only accepts a single value, not a PWL table or an equation (EQU).

This optional parameter is essentially the same as em_jmax_ac_avg , with the exception that it is specified for wide wires (as defined by em_W_n ). In addition, this parameter only accepts a single value, not a PWL table or an equation (EQU).
