 Default: 1.0e+6 A/cm 2 for conductor layers, 0.001A/via for via layers. 
 
 Range: A positive integer or floating point number, a PWL table, or an equation ( EQU ). The value should be specified before any qualifiers ( jmax_factor , current_direction , and via_range ). The qualifiers themselves are not order dependent and may be specified as needed.

This optional parameter is essentially the same as em_jmax_dc_rms , with the exception that it is specified for narrow wires (as defined by em_W_n ). In addition, this parameter only accepts a single value, not a PWL table or an equation (EQU).

This optional parameter is essentially the same as em_jmax_dc_rms , with the exception that it is specified for wide wires (as defined by em_W_n ). In addition, this parameter only accepts a single value, not a PWL table or an equation (EQU).

Optional parameter that specifies the recovery factor used for calculating the average current density with recovery. You can set the recovery factor to -1.0 when you want to account for the average of the absolute current (avg(abs(current))) so that the negative current does not cancel the positive current values in arriving at the average current result. 
 
 Default: 1 
 
 Range: A positive integer or floating point number

lines: Example:
EM Only ICT File
The EM only ICT file provides the process and EM model information for EM analysis. This is a text-based file, which means you can use any text editor to enter information in this file.
Sample file name : file.ict
Command : set_rail_analysis_mode –process_techgen_em_rules true –ict_em_models ICT-EM file
Format :
Format Description :
background_dielectric_constant value - Specifies the dielectric constant for the region above the top or last dielectric layer. This field is required.
layout_scale value - Specifies the ratio of the scaled dimensions divided by the drawn dimensions. The ICT file is defined based on the scaled dimensions.
temp_reference value - Specifies the reference temperature in Celsius. This is the nominal temperature used when the tech file resistance information is calculated.
em_tref <value> - Specifies the nominal temperature for EM analysis in Celsius. The default value is 25C, and can be specified as any integer value.

em_output_wlt [ silicon | drawn ] - This determines whether the width and length parameters are written out as drawn dimensions or silicon dimensions, and whether erosion tables for thickness variation are output. The default setting is drawn.
em_variables - This parameter is used to globally declare variables that are used within the various em_models. w and delta_T are used normally.

- w : width of the conductor
- delta_T : ΔT(°C) is the temperature rise due to Joule heating. delta_T is an input for the following command parameters:
  - For P/G EM, set_rail_analysis_mode –rms_em_delta_T < value >
  - For signal EM, verify_AC_limit –deltaTemp < value >
em_conductor_unit mA - Specifies the units to use for current density limits on conductor layers. Only mA is supported.
em_via_unit mA - Specifies the units to use for current density limits on via layers on a simple per via basis. Only mA is supported.
em_via_area_unit mA - Specifies the units to use for piecewise linear (PWL) current density limits for via layers using density/area value pairs. Only mA is supported.

em_segment_length [perimeter | centerline] - Determines how to calculate the length of a wire segment. This parameter determines whether the segment length is of the type perimeter or centerline . The difference between the perimeter and centerline segment length types is as follows:

- perimeter - specifies that the length calculation is along the wire outer edge boundary. This value is used for 28-nm process nodes.
- centerline - specifies that the length is extended to the edge of the segment along the centerline path. This value is used for 20-nm process nodes.
Rules for EM model in Conductor layer
- Equations in EM Model

The conductor layer usually uses equation (EQU) to describe the EM model. EQU is a function of the conductor/metal width, fn(w). The operators used in EQU are: +, -, *, /, ^, sqrt, (). You must specify the EQU statements in the same fn(w) form with different beneficial factors, and the EQU statements must be listed in the ascending order of beneficial factors.
In the above EQU statements:
Beneficial factors are 1 , 2 , and 3 , respectively. The beneficial factor must be preceded to fn(w).
- jmax_factor
jmax_factor <temp1> <scale1> [<temp2> <scale2> ...]

EQU may have the temperature scaling table, jmax_factor . jmax_factor is the optional scaling factor to use at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature (defined by em_tref in the process definition). The temperature for jmax_factor should be specified in degrees Celsius. Scaling factor is a positive integer: >1 to scale up, <1 to scale down, or 1 for no scale effect. Normally, the lower temperature has a bigger scale factor, while the higher temperature has a smaller scale factor for the current limit.
The temperature in jmax_factor for EM calculation is the input by set_rail_analysis_mode -em_temperature < value > .
- Length or Width Condition

EQU may have the width (W) and/or length (L) of conductor/metal as the condition. The L parameter specifies the length-based jmax values in microns. The W parameter specifies the width-based jmax values in microns. L and W are applicable for both metals and vias.
The qualifiers of W or L must be preceded to inequality symbols.
W >= 2 W <= 4; acceptable 2 <= W <= 4; not acceptable
- delta_T Variable

EQU may have the variable, delta_T , which is the input by set_rail_analysis_mode –rms_em_delta_T < value > . delta_T is the maximum temperature rise permitted in Celsius degree. This value is used for root-mean-square (rms) Electronmigration current limit analysis.

data: em_model { em_jmax_ac_rms EQU sqrt( 1.23 * delta_T * ( w - 0.001 )^2 ) }

lines: - Implicit /sqrt(r) in em_jmax_ac_peak
For AC peak current limit, it may have EQU as shown below:

When the AC peak current limit is calculated, the formulation of “ 123.12*(w-0.002)/sqrt(r) ” is used inside the tool. That is /sqrt(r) is always implicit in the EQU of em_jmax_ac_peak . “r” is duty ratio.
Rules for EM model in Via layer
- Piecewise Linear Interpolation

The via layer usually uses Piecewise Linear (PWL) to describe the EM model. The keyword, PWL, must be defined explicitly before the piecewise linear table. In the PWL statements, there must be a base PWL table without any conditions. In the following PWL statements, the blue line is the base PWL table without any conditions. Generally, it should have the lowest current limit, as others have the current limit with the multiplier of beneficial factors (>1.0).

The base PWL table should contain all the possible cut areas as the index. For example, in the blue line, it is 0.0016 (Vx), 0.0032 (VxBAR), 0.0064 (VxLRG). A PWL table’s index is the area of cut. The unit is µm2. The PWL table syntax is:

Normally, a via layer has 1x square via, rectangular via, and 2x square via. Therefore, the area index will follow area of 1x square via, area of rectangular via, and area of 2x square via. The area index must be listed in the ascending order. Ensure that all the areas of the possible cut shapes are listed in the table; interpolation will not be performed for calculation.

For the PWL tables with conditions (not the base table), the current limits of different cut areas must have the identical beneficial factor against the respective values in the base table. For example, in the first table, the beneficial factor is 3=0.666/0.222=0.999/0.333; in the second table, the beneficial factor is 2=0.222/0.111=0.444/0.222=0.666/0.333.

The parameter, em_vcwidth , is only used for a via/cut layer, and not used for a conductor or diffusion layer. It is the cut width of the square via. You must define this parameter explicitly for a via layer.
- jmax_factor

PWL table may have the temperature scaling table, jmax_factor . jmax_factor is the optional scaling factor to use at different temperatures compared to the reference temperature (defined by em_tref in the process definition). The temperature for jmax_factor should be specified in degrees Celsius. Scaling factor is a positive integer: >1 to scale up, <1 to scale down, or 1 for no scale effect. Normally, the lower temperature has a bigger scale factor, while the higher temperature has a smaller scale factor for the current limit.
The temperature in jmax_factor for EM calculation is the input by set_rail_analysis_mode -em_temperature < value > .
- Conditions
PWL table may have the width (W) and/or length (L) of of the upper/bottom metal of the via as the condition.
The various conditions are:

- The L parameter specifies the length-based jmax values in microns.
- The W parameter specifies the width-based jmax values in microns.
- Lu , Lb , Wu , and Wb are applicable for vias only.
Where, L = length W = Width Lu = Upper metal length of the via Lb = Bottom metal length of the via Wu = Upper metal width of the via Wb = Lower metal width of the via
- Current Direction
PWL table may have different current limits if the current through the cut has a specific direction. The current_direction syntax is:
current_direction <up|down>
up - means the current flows from the bottom metal to the upper metal, and specifies to use the length and width of the metal below Lb/Wb for the VIA Jmax factor
down - means the current flows from the upper metal to the bottom metal, and specifies to use the length and width of the metal above Lu/Wu for VIA Jmax factor
The current direction has the converse direction of electrons flow.
- single Keyword for Single Square Cut on Metal Layers
PWL table may have a specific current limit for single square cut via (not via array).

data: em_model { em_vcwidth 0.040 em_jmax_dc_avg PWL 0.222 0.0016 jmax_factor 100 2.0 105 1.5 110 1.0 115 0.8 120 0.6 125 0.4 Lu > 2 Lu <= 4 Lb <= 4 current_direction up single }

lines: Example :
Package Model and Mapping File
The Package Model file contains a SPICE subcircuit describing a package model. It enables Voltus to use package models during full-chip static and dynamic power-grid analysis.

The Mapping file specifies the name of the package terminal mapping file, which contains the mapping of the terminals of the SPICE subcircuit in the package model file to the locations at the grid or top-level pins. The mapping file formats supported by Voltus are:

- Voltus-specific Format
- Model Connection Protocol (MCP)
Command : set_package -spice model_file -mapping mapping_file
Voltus-specific Format
The format of the package terminal mapping file is as follows:
Format Description :

subckt_terminal_name specifies the name of the terminals of the SPICE subcircuit in the package model file to be mapped to the grid locations or the top-level pins. subckt_terminal_name without mapping information indicates that the terminal should be kept floating. subckt_terminal_name specified with the power or ground keyword indicates that the subcircuit terminal should be connected to a virtual power or ground node during power-grid analysis.
pin toplevel_pin_name specifies the name of the top-level pin to which to connect the SPICE subcircuit terminal.
cell pad_cell_instance_name specifies the name of the pad cell instance to which to connect the SPICE subcircuit terminal.
x,y specifies the location, in microns, where the specific SPICE subcircuit terminal should be attached.
power_pin net_name specifies the power pin name and net name to which to connect the SPICE subcircuit terminal.
ground_pin net_name specifies the ground pin name and net name to which to connect the SPICE subcircuit terminal.
Example :
The following is an example of a terminal mapping file:
Model Connection Protocol (MCP)

The MCP mapping file format can be used for location based pin mapping for package models. MCP is a block that is inserted into a host file as the comment section. It uses the host file's comment leading strings as its leading string. 
The format of the MCP package terminal mapping file is as follows:
Format Description :
[MCP Begin] and [MCP End] mark the MCP block.
The contents of the MCP block are described below.
[MCP Ver] 1.1 is a one-line item. It specifies the version of MCP. It is a required item.

[REM] comment is the MCP comment. It is a one-line item. The comment can also be in the form from the host, except in the net list section. In the net list sections, the comment must be in the form of MCP comments.
[Structure Type] PKG specifies the type of the extracted circuit model. It is a one-line item. It is optional. It can be a combination of the following (delimited by spaces) DIE, PKG, PCB.
[MCP Source] source specifies the origin of the circuit model. It is a one-line item. It can include the tool name, the tool version, and/or the date the model was created. This item is optional.
[Coordinate Unit] unit defines the unit used by the coordinates. unit can be one of the following, m, cm, mm, um, nm, mil. The default is m. This item is optional.

[Connection] compName modelName totalNumberOfPins starts a component connection section in the MCP block. It is a one-line item. This item is required. An MCP block should contain at least one component connection section. In the item, compName is the name of the component connection (or circuit name in SPD file, or reference designator in common layout tools); modelName is the model definition name of the component connection (or part number in common layout tools); and the totalNumberOfPins is the number of pins in this component connection. A new [Connection] line ends the previous component connection section and starts a new component connection section. 
 [Connection Type] DIE specifies the type of the component connection. It is a one-line item. It is optional. It can be a combination of the following (delimited by spaces) DIE, PKG, PCB, VRM, or SINK. 
 [Power Nets] starts the power pin section of the component. It is a one-line item. It is optional. 
 pin cktnode net x y lists the information for a pin. pin is the name of the pin. cktnode is the circuit node name used in the extracted circuit corresponding to the pin. net is the net of the pin. x y are optional and are the relative coordinates of the pin relative to the component origin. The five fields should be in one line. 
 [Ground Nets] starts the ground pin section of the component. It is a one-line item. It is optional. 
 [Signal Nets] starts the signal pin section (ends the [Power Nets] or [Ground Nets] section if exists) of the component connection. It is a one-line item. It is optional. 
In the Power/Ground/Signal pin sections, comments must be in the MCP comment form as [REM] comment.
Example :
The following is an example of an MCP file:
Power-Up Sequence File
A file containing the firing time for each power gate instance.
Sample file name : sequence.txt
Command : set_rail_analysis_mode -powerup_sequence_file filename
Format :
Format Description :
inst_name specifies the name of the full hierarchical power gate instance.
pin_name specifies the name of the enable pin.
always_on_net specifies the name of the always-on net.
switch_net specifies the name of the switched net.
rise_time specifies the rise time at the enable pin.
rise_slew specifies the rise slew at the enable pin.
fall_time specifies the fall time at the enable pin.
fall_slew specifies the fall slew at the enable pin.
Example :

- Overview
- Data Requirements for DEF Generation
- ESD Analysis using convert_gds_to_def
- Generating a DEF File

convert_gds_to_def is a GDS2DEF utility that converts a given GDSII file to a DEF file for power/rail signoff analysis. For some cells that only have a GDS format, it is not possible to show the design data inside the cells in Voltus GUI. In addition, it is not feasible to generate a desired DEF using the existing translators of Str2oa and oa2def because of missing connectivity information. Thus, it is necessary to have a GDS2DEF utility to generate a desired DEF file for rail analysis.

This utility enables you to perform rail analysis when the DEF file is not accessible. For example, certain data, such as the redistribution layer (RDL) flip-chip bump layer descriptions, is only available in the GDSII format. This type of data must be converted to the DEF/LEF format to be integrated into the DB data.
Cell-Level and Full-Chip Level Implementation

The utility can convert both cell-level GDS and full-chip level GDS to DEF. The generated DEF contains all the geometries for the P/G routing, the specified P/G pin information, component and logical connectivity information. Signal routing in not considered in this utility, that is, the signal nets will be filtered out in the DEF file. As an illustration of comparison between the new and original DEFs shown in the following figure, the signal nets visible in the image on the right side are not present on the left.

The utility can convert both cell-level GDS and full-chip level GDS to DEF. The generated DEF contains all the geometries for the P/G routing, the specified P/G pin information, component and logical connectivity information. Signal routing in not considered in this utility, that is, the signal nets will be filtered out in the DEF file. As an illustration of comparison between the new and original DEFs shown in the following figure, the signal nets visible in the image on the right side are not present on the left.
The table below lists the inputs that are required for the convert_gds_to_def utility:

data: Top cell name in the GDSII file is the design name in the generated DEF.
The layer mapping file between GDS and tech layers.
This file contains a list of coordinates and layer names as the starting point to trace.

For cells that need to be treated as an instance in the generated DEF and do not require to extract the P/G routing details, the Macro LEF information of these cells have to be provided. The utility traces the net connectivity using pin shape information in LEF and does not check the geometry inside the cell in GDS. If no LEF macros are provided, the utility flattens all the cell instances. 
 
 Tech file is also required for the utility to get the LEF layers. The .cl library contains the tech information.

lines: You can enable Electrostatic Discharge Analysis (ESD) analysis for macros through the convert_gds_to_def utility. The following parameters of the convert_ gds_to_def utility can be used to identify the bounding box of the ESD cells in the GDS file:

- -esd_marker_layer_file < filename > - specifies a file that includes a list of ESD cell/instance names and their corresponding GDS marker layers that represent the bounding box of the ESD cells. The format of the file is: 
 <cellname> <marker layer> [top layer] [bottom layer]
- -esd_cell_bbox_file < filename > - specifies a file that includes the bounding box for each ESD cell. The format of the file is: 
 <esd inst name> <x1 y1 x2 y2> [top layer] [bottom layer] 
 You can use the optional top layer and bottom layer arguments to specify the top and bottom technology layers of the ESD cell pin shape. This provides you with the flexibility to specify the lowest layers to be used for creating pins in the LEF.

When either of these parameters are specified, the software identifies the bounding box of the ESD cells in the GDS file, and collects all the GDS shapes inside the bounding box on layers between the specified top layer and bottom layer and considers them as ESD cell LEF pin shapes. If the top and bottom layers are not specified in these files, the shapes inside the bounding box of all layers are considered as ESD cell shapes.

convert_gds_to_def utility generates a LEF file for each ESD cell to enable PGV generation. The output DEF includes the user-defined nets and the ESD instances based on the specified marker layer or bounding box.
The use model of the convert_gds_to_def utility is given below:
The following command converts the specified GDS file new.gds to a DEF file top.def .
For detailed information on the command parameters, refer to the convert_gds_to_def command in the Voltus Text Command Reference .

title: Customizing GDS Layermap and XTC Command File for TRIM Metals Layers

lines: The purpose of the CUT/TRIM layer is to cut the metal layers so as to separate the different supplies. The following diagram illustrates how the CUT layers separate the power and ground supplies:

To ensure that Voltus is able to identify the CUT layers, you need to manually modify these layers in the GDS layermap file. After modifying the layermap file, you need to customize the XTC command file for supporting these layers during power-grid library generation.

This chapter gives examples for simple (without CUT layers) and advanced (with CUT layers) GDS layermap files, and the corresponding XTC command files. It gives sample XTC command files for stop@via layer 0, 1, and 2.
Simple GDS Layer Map File
Simple XTC Command File
Actual Layer Map with Mask Layers (Not Supported in Voltus)
Modified Layer Map with Renamed Metal (M0, M1, M2) and Mask Layers
XTC Command File with STOP@VIA0
XTC Command File with STOP@VIA1
XTC Command File with STOP@VIA2
AC
AC is an abbreviation for "alternating current," which is current that reverses direction at a regular rate. The current flow in signal wires is generally AC.
abstract power-grid view

An abstract power-grid view is a simplified form of the detailed power-grid view in which a block's or macro's internal current loading has been replaced by a mathematically equivalent impedance value assigned to the block's or macro's port pins.
activity
Number of signal transitions per clock period.
angled resistor
An angled resistor is any resistor that is not horizontal or vertical.
ASCII format
ASCII format is a human-readable format called American Standard Code for Information Interchange.
average current
Average current is the asymptotic average current flowing through each tap point.
block
A block describes a higher-level cell that can contain other blocks, cells, or transistors.
cell

A cell is a group of transistors that implements a primitive logic function. Typical examples of cells are NAND gates, NOR gates, XOR gates, and flip-flops. Typically, a cell does not contain placements of other cells.
chemical mechanical polishing (CMP)

Chemical mechanical polishing (CMP) is the process of removing surface irregularities and obtaining uniform planarization of a silicon wafer surface during the manufacturing of semiconductor devices below 0.35 microns.
cladding
Cladding is a barrier layer between the copper interconnect and low-k dielectric material to protect the interconnect from chemical reactions with the dielectric material.
clock signal jitter
Clock signal jitter is the variations in the delay, skew, and slew rates caused by supply voltage fluctuations.
clock skew

Clock skew is the difference between the time required for the clock signal to travel through the longest path from the clock origin to a latch and the time required for the clock signal to travel through the shortest path from the clock origin to a latch.
clock slew

Clock slew is the time required for the signal voltage at a buffer or receiver to rise from a lower voltage threshold to a higher voltage threshold (rising slew), or the reverse when measuring the falling slew rate. Typically, the lower voltage threshold is defined as 10 percent of the power supply voltage, and the higher voltage threshold is defined as 90 percent of the power supply voltage.
conductor layer
A conductor layer is a layer of material that carries electric current in an integrated circuit.
core
A core is a block that contains the main functionality of a design.
DC

DC is an abbreviation for "direct current," which is current that flows in only one direction. The current in a power grid is generally DC. DC is also a standard representation of a waveform that is typically used as a voltage source in circuit simulation. It displays a flat waveform.
decoupled capacitance
Decoupled capacitance is lumped capacitance that is used in place of coupled capacitance, typically to support static timing analysis.
decoupling capacitance
Added capacitance used to smooth the power rail. It is usually between power and ground.
decoupling cells
Decoupling cells are cells containing decoupling capacitance.
delay
Delay is the time that it takes a signal to propagate from a given input to a given output.
detailed power-grid view
A detailed power-grid view is a detailed electrical model of a cell's or macro's internal power routing extracted from the GDSII data. It provides resistance and current loading information.
dielectric layer
The dielectric layer is any layer deposited below the highest passivation layer.
diffusion layer
The diffusion layer is any layer that is below the surface of the substrate. It includes active areas, such as n-channel and p-channel areas, but not polysilicon, which is a conductor layer.
dishing

Dishing is the reduction of a cross-sectional area of wider interconnect lines because of chemical mechanical polishing (CMP). Dishing occurs when the copper recedes below or protrudes above the level of the adjacent dielectric. It is limited to a single wire.
DSPF

DSPF is an acronym for Detailed Standard Parasitic Format, which is a form of SPF that enables the file-based transfer of parasitic resistance and capacitance from physical design tools to timing analysis and other downstream analysis tools. DSPF cannot represent true coupling capacitance but models it as decoupled to ground.
duty cycle
The input duty cycle is a value between 0 and 1, representing the fraction of time that the signal is 1.
dynamic analysis
Dynamic analysis is a method of analyzing a circuit to obtain operating currents and voltages. It is time-based, analyzing the circuit netlist over a specified period, such as a clock cycle.
effective current source model (ECSM)
The effective current source model (ECSM) is a highly accurate, non-linear driver model used for delay calculation.
effective instance voltage (EIV)

The rail-to-rail voltage obtained during rail analysis. EIV is obtained by processing the voltage waveforms and finding the worse-case effective voltage between the power and ground pins during a specific window. EIV is measured over all the timing/switching windows (default), or the entire analysis (elapse). There are four possible windows to evaluate EIV:

- switching – any switching activity (current is non-leakage) reported in power analysis
- timing – timing window information from TWF or embedded timing engine
- both (Default) – both switching or timing window
- elapse – entire rail simulation, and not filtered to a specific window
The EIV windows are illustrated in the following diagram:
You can specify EIV windows using the following command:
effective instance voltage (EIV) method
There are different methods of computing EIV during rail analysis: worst (default), best , average , or worst-average .
Using the following, you can write out the best, worst, or average effective instance voltage between power and ground nets:

The worstavg EIV method computes the average instance voltage during each evaluation window defined by the -eiv_eval_window parameter, and reports the worst average instance voltage out of all the windows, as shown in the following diagram:
In this case:

- Best : Best drop/bounce inside the window
- Worst (default): Worst drop/bounce value inside the window
- Average : Average drop/bounce for all the windows
- Worst-average : Average of each window, and then report the worst average

If -eiv_method worstavg and -eiv_eval_window elapse (i.e. 1 evaluation window) are specified, then this method will report average voltage across all time steps in simulation, as shown in the following diagram:
In this case:

- Best : Best drop/bounce for the entire simulation
- Worst (default): Worst drop/bounce value for the entire simulation
- Average : Average drop/bounce for the entire simulation
- Worst-average : same as average since there is only 1 window (whole simulation)
electromigration

Electromigration is the mass transport of metal resulting from the transfer of momentum between conducting electrons and diffusing metal atoms. It occurs in metal wires that conduct high current densities. Conducting electrons collide with diffusing metal atoms and impel the atoms in the direction of electron flow. This collision produces a mass flux opposite to that of the current. Divergences in this mass flux can result in damage to the conductor in the form of voids or hillocks. This problem can be minimized by limiting the current density in metal conductor lines, vias, and contacts.
embedded block
An embedded block is a large custom block created for a specific design.
erosion
Erosion is the reduction of a cross-sectional area of interconnect lines and a localized thinning of the dielectric.
feedthrough

A feedthrough is a signal net within a cell that may be connected to the top-level routing at multiple locations simultaneously. It is commonly used in cells intended to be assembled by abutment to form a continuous net passing through the cells. The feedthrough may or may not connect to the circuitry in the cell.
In extraction, parasitics are not normally computed for cell ports, so long wires created by abutted feedthroughs can be modeled incorrectly if the resistance of the feedthroughs are not extracted.
The following figure shows a feedthrough in a single cell and a feedthrough in four abutting cell instances.
filler cell

A filler cell is a standard cell that only fills space rather than implementing any logical functionality. Filler cells are used to fill gaps in the power and ground rails in flows where the power grid does not include them or to act as placeholders in case functionality must be inserted at a late stage in the design process. They often include the ties that connect the wells to power or ground. Many flows require them to be inserted at intervals to make sure that the wells are tapped.
flat extraction
Flat extraction is the process of extracting the interconnect parasitic resistance and capacitance down to the transistor level.
flip chip

Flip chip refers to Direct Chip Attach (DCA), a type of microelectronic assembly in which an integrated circuit die is attached to the package by eutectic solder bumps of electrically conducting material placed on the chip bond pads across the top layer (called a redistribution layer, or RDL) of the chip. The chip is flipped face down on the substrate or board and the bumps attached to the package with reflow soldering. This type of packaging is an alternative to traditional packaging that uses perimeter bonding wires to attach the chip to the package. Flip-chip packaging offers improved signal integrity to processors, DSPs, SRAMs, chip sets, and other types of integrated circuits.
fusing

Fusing is the instant failure of a wire segment because of excessive instantaneous current. This failure mechanism is related to electromigration and is sometimes classified as a subset of Joule heating.
GDSII
GDSII is a binary file in graphical design system version 2.0 Stream format.
ground
Ground is a conducting body used as the electrical current return. It is also referred to as VSS or GND.
ground bounce
Ground bounce is an increase in voltage that occurs on the ground network of an integrated circuit because of the current flowing through the resistance of the ground network itself (V = I x R).
hierarchical design

A hierarchical design is a way of structuring logic at more than one level of abstraction; that is, a symbol at one level of abstraction represents the internal contents of a cell at a higher level of hierarchy.
hold time
The hold time is the period after the clocking edge for which the input data must be held constant.
hold-time violation
A hold-time violation is a change of the input signal earlier than the specified hold time.
horizontal resistor
A horizontal resistor is any resistor with an angle smaller than 30 degrees with respect to the X axis.
HSPICE
HSPICE is a specific commercial implementation of the SPICE simulator that has become an industry-standard tool for performing circuit simulation. It was developed by Meta Software.
input duty cycle
The input duty cycle is a value between 0 and 1, representing the fraction of time that the signal is 1.
interconnect
Interconnect refers to the physical wires and vias between the gates and cells in an integrated circuit.
ipeak analysis
Ipeak analysis is a static power-grid analysis based on peak transistor saturation currents.
IR drop
IR drop is a reduction in voltage that occurs on the VDD network of an integrated circuit because of the current flowing through the resistance of the VDD network itself (V = I x R).
Itaputil
Itaputil is a Cadence utility that either reports characteristics of tap current files or extracts portions of the data and places them into a new tap current file.
Joule heating
Joule heating is a cause of electromigration in which a specific segment of a wire becomes excessively heated because of high currents.
LEF/DEF

LEF/DEF is an acronym for Library Exchange Format/Design Exchange Format, which is a layout format widely used by place-and-route tools. LEF format stores the physical, timing, power, and related information of the cells used in a design. It can contain definitions at the polygon level or a pointer to a GDSII Stream file for data in the cell. DEF format contains all the instantiations of the cells and wiring, as determined by the router, and the port names and pin locations on the cells. LEF/DEF databases include node and instance names that match those of the schematic and gate-level netlists.
macro
A macro is a cell referenced in the LEF file. The geometric data for this cell is stored in either DEF or GDSII. If the cell is stored in GDSII, it may contain references to subcells.
Miller capacitance
Miller capacitance is coupling capacitance between nets associated with coupling between the gate and the source/drain of a transistor.
Moore's Law

Moore's Law refers to an observation made by Gordon Moore, former CEO of Intel, that the number of transistors on silicon integrated circuits has approximately doubled once every 18 months since 1962, the year in which the technology was invented.
netlist
A netlist is an ASCII or binary description of all the components in a design and their interconnections.
noise injection
Noise injection is the appearance of unexpected voltage values on a net resulting from transitioning nets or circuitry and coupling through interconnect or substrate parasitics.
optical proximity correction (OPC)
Optical proximity correction is the process of automatically generating mask features that compensate for subwavelength lithography effects.
panning
Panning is the process of obtaining a panoramic view of the design by selecting one point as the center of the view without changing the scale of the design.
parasitic capacitance
Parasitic capacitance is any capacitance causing unwanted effects between conductors or in devices.
passivation layer
The passivation layer is any layer deposited above the highest conductor of the process.
peak current
Peak current is the maximum value of a current waveform.
piecewise linear (PWL)
Piecewise linear is a standard representation of a waveform that is typically used as a voltage source in circuit simulation. The waveform displays no recurring pattern.
planar dielectric
Planar dielectric refers to a flat dielectric layer, as opposed to a conformal dielectric layer. See also "conformal dielectric."
port

A port is a named geometry that marks a location in certain output files. It can be a single point, marked by a text label on a special layer. It can be a collection of polygons on a special layer marked by text labels on that special layer. It can be a fully extracted node in the cell marked as a port by a text label on a special layer.
port power-grid view

A port power-grid view is a simple electrical view of a cell or macro containing no detail about the internal circuitry and power grid. Creation of the port power-grid view does not require the use of extraction.
power
Power is the voltage supply. It is also referred to as VDD or VCC.
power grid

A power grid is a large collection of interconnected wires on a chip that distributes power from the pins to all the gates and transistors on the chip. It is similar in function to a power distribution system that delivers power from a power station to residential and commercial facilities.
power-grid signoff (PGS)
Power-grid signoff (PGS) is the process of requiring a chip to pass static power-grid analysis before tapeout.
power-grid view
A power-grid view is a model of a cell's or block's power grid that contains power-port information providing the current loading to the power-grid networks.
promoted geometries
Promoted geometries are geometries within a cell or block that must be considered at the full-chip level to obtain accurate results when the power grid is extracted.
pulse
Pulse is a standard representation of a waveform that is typically used as a voltage source in circuit simulation. The waveform displays a recurring pattern.
setup time
The setup time is the period before the clocking edge for which the data must be held constant.
setup-time violation
A setup-time violation is a change of the input signal later than the specified setup time.
signal skew
Signal skew is the difference in signal arrival times across a design.
simultaneously switching output (SSO)
To switch the outputs at the same time. This is done to see what effect it has on the power grid.
skin effect

A skin effect is the concentration of a time-varying current near the surface of a conductor as opposed to a steady-state current flowing through a conductor that is distributed uniformly throughout the cross-section of the conductor. The penetration depth of the current is called the skin depth and is inversely proportional to the frequency of the time-varying current.
slotting

Slotting is the insertion of small holes, or slots, at regular intervals in wide wires to reduce the effect of dishing on wide wires. These slots insert a form of hard "posts" in the wire so that the CMP process removes less copper. This technique reduces the dishing, as well as the effective resistivity.
SPEF
SPEF is an acronym for Standard Parasitic Exchange Format, which is a form of SPF that allows true coupling capacitance to be carried between nodes and passed to downstream analysis tools.
SPICE

SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis) is a simulation program originally developed at UC Berkeley as a transistor-level circuit simulator. SPICE is now often used as a generic term for any transistor-level circuit simulator.
static analysis
Static analysis is a method of analyzing a circuit without using time-based simulation.
steady-state analysis
Steady-state analysis is an analysis of a system that assumes that the components have steady-state values.
Synopsys design constraints (SDC) file

The Synopsys design constraints (SDC) file is a set of data based on the tool command language (TCL) that specifies the timing and area constraints for a design. It can be submitted to Synopsys' static timing analysis tools for timing analysis.
substrate
The substrate is the supporting material on or within which an integrated circuit is fabricated or to which an integrated circuit is attached.
tap currents
Tap currents are currents arising from the connection of devices such as transistors to the power grid.
tapeout
Tapeout is the process of placing the final chip layout design files on media for delivery to a mask-making vendor.
toggle count format (TCF) file
The toggle count format file contains switching activity information that includes the toggle count information and the probability of the net or pin being in the logic 1 state.
transient analysis
Transient analysis is a time-based analysis of a system.
transition density
Number of signal transitions (up and down) per second.
transition time
The transition time is the time interval during which a signal switches from low to high or high to low.
value change dump (VCD) file
The value change dump (VCD) file is a standard Verilog file described in OVI documents and IEEE 1364 that contains the test vectors and switching activity information for all the nets in the design.
VDD
VDD is the common name of the power node, which is the voltage supply. It is the same as VCC.
vertical resistor
A vertical resistor is any resistor with an angle smaller than 30 degrees with respect to the Y axis.
via
A via is a connection point between two adjacent metal routing layers defined by a pad in each layer.
VSS
VSS is the common name of the ground node, which is a conducting body used as the electrical current return. It is the same as GND.
wire-edge enlargement
Wire-edge enlargement is the effective change to a wire's width and spacing because of its proximity to neighboring wires. This is an optical effect.
zooming
Zooming is the process of magnifying or shrinking the view of a selected area of the chip.
